text: “Why don’t you trade with me no more?” the Portuguese grocer asked Martin that evening, stepping out to hail him when he got off the car; and Martin explained that he wasn’t doing his own cooking any more, and then went in and had a drink of wine on the house. He noted it was the best wine the grocer had in stock.

"How come you don't shop with me anymore?" the Portuguese grocer asked Martin that evening, stepping outside to flag him down as he got off the streetcar. Martin explained that he wasn't preparing his own meals these days, then went inside to enjoy a complimentary glass of wine. He noted that it was the highest quality bottle the grocer kept in stock.

But before Mr. Direck could adjust his mind to this new phase of affairs, Cecily had passed the right honourable gentleman with the same mysterious ease with which she had flashed by Mr. Direck, and was bearing down upon the miscellaneous Landwehr which formed the "backs" of Mr. Direck's side.

But before Mr. Direck could get his bearings in this fresh turn of events, Cecily had already slipped past the right honourable gentleman with the same baffling smoothness she’d shown when she swept by Mr. Direck, and was now descending upon the motley Landwehr troops who made up the rearguard of Mr. Direck’s own side.

“It was not enchanted aforetime,” she said in a musing fashion, as if to herself. “And how strange is this marvel, and how awful --that to the one perception it is enchanted and dight in a base and shameful aspect; yet to the perception of the other it is not enchanted, hath suffered no change, but stands firm and stately still, girt with its moat and waving its banners in the blue air from its towers. And God shield us, how it pricks the heart to see again these gracious captives, and the sorrow deepened in their sweet faces! We have tarried along, and are to blame.”

“It bore no spell in former days,” she murmured, half lost in reverie, as though speaking to her own soul. “And what a strange and fearsome wonder this is—that to one beholder it appears bewitched and cloaked in a mean and shameful guise; while to the other’s sight it remains unaltered, steadfast and proud, encircled by its waters and flying its pennons from the towers against the high, blue sky. And Heaven preserve us, how it wounds the spirit to look once more upon these gentle prisoners, with grief etched deeper in their tender faces! We have lingered too long, and the fault is ours.”

Conversation with human Following previous work (Tang et al., 2019) , we also perform human evaluation for a more reliable system comparison. Given a model to be evaluated, we randomly select a dialogue from test set and pick its first utterance for the model to start a conversation with a human. Then the conversation will continue till 8 turns are reached. Finally, we obtain 50 dialogs for evaluation. For single-turn level evaluation, we randomly sample 100 message-response pairs from the dialogs for each model.

metadata.title: Conversational Graph Grounded Policy Learning for Open-Domain Conversation Generation

text: To complement prior studies (Tang et al., 2019), we conduct a human evaluation to achieve more dependable system comparisons. For each model under assessment, we randomly draw a dialogue from the test set and use its opening utterance to initiate a human–model conversation. The interaction proceeds until eight turns are completed. In total, we collect 50 dialogues for evaluation. At the single-turn level, we randomly sample 100 message–response pairs from these dialogues for each model.

The young tenant in the chair allowed these thoughts to file, soft-shod, through his mind, while there drifted into the room furnished sounds and furnished scents. He heard in one room a tittering and incontinent, slack laughter; in others the monologue of a scold, the rattling of dice, a lullaby, and one crying dully; above him a banjo tinkled with spirit. Doors banged somewhere; the elevated trains roared intermittently; a cat yowled miserably upon a back fence. And he breathed the breath of the house—a dank savour rather than a smell—a cold, musty effluvium as from underground vaults mingled with the reeking exhalations of linoleum and mildewed and rotten woodwork.

The young man seated in the chair let these reflections pass through his mind with silent, padded steps, as the room filled with borrowed noises and borrowed odors. From one chamber came a giggling, loose and unrestrained laughter; from others, a scold’s endless harangue, the clatter of dice, a cradle song, and a muffled weeping; overhead, a banjo twanged with energy. Doors slammed somewhere in the distance; elevated trains thundered at intervals; a cat wailed wretchedly on a back fence. And he inhaled the house’s exhalation—a damp essence more than a smell—a chill, stale vapor as from subterranean crypts blended with the pungent emissions of linoleum and decaying, moldy timber.

Dogs are not generally apt to revenge injuries inflicted upon them by their masters; but Mr. Sikes’s dog, having faults of temper in common with his owner, and labouring, perhaps, at this moment, under a powerful sense of injury, made no more ado but at once fixed his teeth in one of the half-boots. Having given in a hearty shake, he retired, growling, under a form; just escaping the pewter measure which Mr. Sikes levelled at his head.

Dogs are not often disposed to avenge the wrongs done them by their masters; yet Mr. Vane’s hound, inheriting, as it were, the ill-humour of his keeper, and perhaps at that instant oppressed by a very lively consciousness of being ill-used, wasted no time in resentment, but at once clamped his teeth upon one of the worn half-boots. After administering to it a vigorous shake, he withdrew beneath a settle, still growling, and so narrowly escaped the pewter measure which Mr. Vane flung at his head.

“While I was overcome by these feelings, I left the spot where I had committed the murder, and seeking a more secluded hiding-place, I entered a barn which had appeared to me to be empty. A woman was sleeping on some straw; she was young, not indeed so beautiful as her whose portrait I held, but of an agreeable aspect and blooming in the loveliness of youth and health. Here, I thought, is one of those whose joy-imparting smiles are bestowed on all but me. And then I bent over her and whispered, ‘Awake, fairest, thy lover is near—he who would give his life but to obtain one look of affection from thine eyes; my beloved, awake!’

While I was still swayed by these emotions, I left the place where I had committed the murder, and, in search of a more hidden refuge, entered a barn that had seemed to me deserted. There, upon a bed of straw, a woman was sleeping. She was young, not indeed so lovely as the one whose portrait I held, yet pleasing in appearance and radiant with the freshness of youth and health. Here, I thought, was one of those to whom those joyful smiles are granted that are denied to me. Then I bent over her and murmured, “Awake, fairest; thy lover is at hand—he who would give his life to win but one glance of affection from thine eyes. My beloved, awake!”

“The grand aristocrats,” Harry answered, “by which term I gather you mean my exceedingly dignified uncle, have sent me no word at all since I arrived; and at this season, it’s hardly probable that any matter would arise requiring my prompt presence in their company.”

Make ’em your models,” tapping the fire-shovel on the hearth to add force to his words; “do everything they bid you, and take their advice in all matters—especially the Dodger’s, my dear. He’ll be a great man himself, and will make you one too, if you take pattern by him.—Is my handkerchief hanging out of my pocket, my dear?” said the Jew, stopping short.

“Quite so,” the old man remarked; “that is, unless they happen to stumble upon something while they are out; and you can be certain they won’t overlook it if they do, my boy. Let them be your examples, my dear. Let them be your examples,” he added, striking the fire-shovel against the hearth to drive the point home. “Do exactly as they tell you and heed their advice in every matter—particularly the Dodger’s. He is bound for greatness himself, and he will make a man of you too, if you only follow his lead. Now, tell me, is my handkerchief trailing from my pocket?” he asked, stopping short.

We ventured out, and hurried cautiously away; and although we were worn out and sleepy, we kept on until we had put this place some miles behind us. Then we asked hospitality at the hut of a charcoal burner, and got what was to be had. A woman was up and about, but the man was still asleep, on a straw shake-down, on the clay floor. The woman seemed uneasy until I explained that we were travelers and had lost our way and been wandering in the woods all night. She became talkative, then, and asked if we had heard of the terrible goings-on at the manor-house of Abblasoure. Yes, we had heard of them, but what we wanted now was rest and sleep. The captain was a fishy-eyed Norwegian who somehow had fallen into possession of a complete Shakespeare, which he never read, and Martin had washed his clothes for him and in return been permitted access to the precious volumes. For a time, so steeped was he in the plays and in the many favorite passages that impressed themselves almost without effort on his brain, that all the world seemed to shape itself into forms of Elizabethan tragedy or comedy and his very thoughts were in blank verse. It trained his ear and gave him a fine appreciation for noble English; withal it introduced into his mind much that was archaic and obsolete.

The skipper, a Norwegian with dull, glassy eyes, had somehow acquired a full collection of Shakespeare that he ignored entirely. Martin had taken to laundering the man’s garments as payment for the privilege of poring over the treasured books. Before long, he became so saturated with the dramas and the numerous lines that lodged themselves naturally in his memory that the entire world appeared to take the shape of an Elizabethan play. He even found himself thinking in the steady rhythm of blank verse. While this immersion refined his sense of rhythm and instilled a deep respect for elegant English, it simultaneously filled his head with a great deal of antiquated and useless language.

The remainder of the dinner passed like a funeral, the judge and Mr. Morse confining their talk to each other, and the rest of the conversation being extremely desultory. Then afterward, when Ruth and Martin were alone, there was a scene.

Martin cut off mid-sentence sharply, the room falling completely still. Every member of Ruth’s family held Judge Blount in high regard as a formidable, highly accomplished man, so they were all appalled by Martin’s sudden outburst. The rest of the dinner dragged on with the stifling, somber energy of a funeral; the judge and Mr. Morse kept their conversation exclusively between themselves, and any other scraps of chat were scattered, half-hearted, and entirely aimless. Later, once the meal ended and Ruth and Martin were left alone together, a tense, explosive scene unfolded.

The clean, sleek creature arose from its fleece—how perfectly like Aphrodite rising from the foam should have been seen to be realized—looking startled and shy at the loss of its garment, which lay on the floor in one soft cloud, united throughout, the portion visible being the inner surface only, which, never before exposed, was white as snow, and without flaw or blemish of the minutest kind.

The smooth, spotless creature lifted itself free of its fleece—you would have to see the sight to fully grasp how perfectly it mirrored Aphrodite rising from the seafoam—looking startled and timid now that its covering was gone, the garment spread across the floor in a single, soft, unbroken cloud. Only the inner layer of the fleece was visible, a surface that had never been uncovered before, as white as fresh fallen snow, with not even the tiniest speck, scratch, or blemish to be found anywhere.

The Jew grinned; and, making a low obeisance to Oliver, took him by the hand, and hoped he should have the honour of his intimate acquaintance. Upon this, the young gentleman with the pipes came round him, and shook both his hands very hard—especially the one in which he held his little bundle. One young gentleman was very anxious to hang up his cap for him; and another was so obliging as to put his hands in his pockets, in order that, as he was very tired, he might not have the trouble of emptying them, himself, when he went to bed. These civilities would probably be extended much farther, but for a liberal exercise of the Jew’s toasting-fork on the heads and shoulders of the affectionate youths who offered them.

The old man smirked and, with a deep bow to Oliver, took his hand, expressing his hope for a long and close friendship. Immediately, the youth with the pipes stepped forward to shake both of Oliver's hands with great vigor—focusing particularly on the hand holding his small bundle. One boy was remarkably eager to hang up Oliver's cap, while another was kind enough to reach into his pockets so that, given his exhaustion, he wouldn't have to bother emptying them himself before bed. These displays of courtesy would likely have gone much further if the old man hadn't begun swinging his toasting-fork across the heads and shoulders of the overly affectionate boys.

With these exceptions, there was neither sight nor sound of any living thing; and often, when it grew dark, and he was tired of wandering from room to room, he would crouch in the corner of the passage by the street-door, to be as near living people as he could; and would remain there, listening and counting the hours, until the Jew or the boys returned.

Apart from these, there was neither sight nor sound of any living creature; and often, when darkness came and he was wearied of wandering from room to room, he would huddle in the corner of the passage by the street door, so as to be as close to living people as he could, and would stay there, listening and counting the hours until the Jew or the boys came back.

At the end of the time Mrs. Yeobright went in, and Venn sadly retraced his steps into the heath. When he had again regained his van he lit the lantern, and with an apathetic face at once began to pull off his best clothes, till in the course of a few minutes he reappeared as the confirmed and irretrievable reddleman that he had seemed before.

Rather than inviting him inside, she stepped out onto the porch. They spoke together in hushed, deliberate voices for ten minutes or longer. Afterwards, Mrs. Yeobright returned indoors, and Venn made his way sorrowfully back across the heath. Once he reached his van, he lighted the lantern and, his expression blank, immediately started stripping off his finest garments, so that within minutes he stood once more as the hopeless, dyed-in-the-wool reddleman he had always appeared to be.

“And oh, brother Anthony, don’t ever boast of the power of money again. A little emblem of true love—a little ring that symbolised unending and unmercenary affection—was the cause of our Richard finding his happiness. He dropped it in the street, and got out to recover it. And before they could continue the blockade occurred. He spoke to his love and won her there while the cab was hemmed in. Money is dross compared with true love, Anthony.”

And oh, brother Elias, never again boast of how all-conquering money is. A tiny token of genuine devotion—a thin little ring that symbolized endless, unmercenary affection—was the very reason our Jude found his lasting happiness. He dropped it onto the cobblestones mid-ride, and climbed out of the cab to retrieve it. Before they could nudge back into the street’s flow, the gridlock settled in. He spoke to the woman he’d loved from afar for ages, and won her over entirely while their cab was hemmed tight between wagons and pedestrians. All the gold in the world is dross compared to true love, Elias.

“I mean but this—that when I left you last, I left you with a firm determination to level all fancied barriers between yourself and me; resolved that if my world could not be yours, I would make yours mine; that no pride of birth should curl the lip at you, for I would turn from it. This I have done. Those who have shrunk from me because of this, have shrunk from you, and proved you so far right. Such power and patronage: such relatives of influence and rank: as smiled upon me then, look coldly now; but there are smiling fields and waving trees in England’s richest county; and by one village church—mine, Rose, my own!—there stands a rustic dwelling which you can make me prouder of, than all the hopes I have renounced, measured a thousandfold. This is my rank and station now, and here I lay it down!”

“I mean only this: when I left you last, I left you with a steadfast resolve to cast down every imagined barrier between us; determined that, if my world could not be yours, I would make yours mine; that no pride of birth should sneer at you, for I would turn away from it. And this I have done. Those who drew back from me because of it have also drawn back from you, and have thus proved you right in that at least. Such power and patronage, such kinsfolk of influence and degree, as smiled on me then, now look on me coldly; but there are fair fields and rustling trees in England’s richest county, and by one village church—mine, Rose, my own!—there stands a humble dwelling that can make me prouder than all the hopes I have surrendered, multiplied a thousand times. This is my rank and station now, and here I lay it down!”

Drawing from Li et al. (2018) , we propose a simple approach based on n-gram tf-idfs to estimate the set Γ v , which represents the style markers for style v. For a given corpus pair X 1 , X 2 in styles S 1 , S 2 respectively we first compute a probability distribution p 2 1 (w) over the n-grams w present in both the corpora (Eq. 2). Intuitively, p 2 1 (w) is proportional to the probability of sampling an n-gram present in both X 1 , X 2 but having a much higher tf-idf value in X 2 relative to X 1 . This is how we define the impactful style markers for style S 2 .

metadata.title: Politeness Transfer: A Tag and Generate Approach

text: Building on Li et al. (2018), we propose a straightforward method based on n-gram tf-idfs to estimate the set Γ_v, which denotes the style markers for style v. Given a corpus pair X_1, X_2 in styles S_1 and S_2, respectively, we first compute a probability distribution p_{21}(w) over the n-grams w that occur in both corpora (Eq. 2). Intuitively, p_{21}(w) is proportional to the probability of selecting an n-gram that appears in both X_1 and X_2 but has a much larger tf-idf value in X_2 than in X_1. We use this to define the influential style markers for style S_2.

However, my son William must have knowed the very man afore us—didn’t ye, Billy, afore ye left Norcombe?”

Of course," agreed his son, a young man of sixty-five with a balding head and a solitary tooth in the upper left of his jaw. This tooth made quite a show of itself, standing out as conspicuously as a milestone on a grassy bank. "But Joe was the one who dealt with him most. Even so, my son William must have known the fellow before the rest of us—didn't you, Billy, before you moved away from Norcombe?

“She’s blushing at the slight,” Bathsheba whispered, observing the rosy bloom that surfaced and swept across the ewe’s neck and shoulders, exposed now by the snipping blades—a blush so fine in its tenderness that many a socialite queen would covet it, and so swift in its rise that any woman alive might take pride in it.

The sluggish day began to break. Even its position terrestrially is one of the elements of a new interest, and for no particular reason save that the incident of the night had occurred there Oak went again into the plantation. Lingering and musing here, he heard the steps of a horse at the foot of the hill, and soon there appeared in view an auburn pony with a girl on its back, ascending by the path leading past the cattle-shed. She was the young woman of the night before. Gabriel instantly thought of the hat she had mentioned as having lost in the wind; possibly she had come to look for it. He hastily scanned the ditch and after walking about ten yards along it found the hat among the leaves. Gabriel took it in his hand and returned to his hut. Here he ensconced himself, and peeped through the loophole in the direction of the rider’s approach.

The slow day was beginning to dawn. Its very place on the earth was one of those things that had taken on a fresh significance, and for no reason other than that the night’s encounter had happened there, Oak found himself once more in the plantation. While he lingered there in thought, he heard the tread of a horse at the bottom of the hill, and presently an auburn pony came into sight, carrying a girl as it climbed the path that passed the cattle-shed. It was the young woman from the previous night. At once Gabriel remembered the hat she had said the wind had blown away; perhaps she had returned to search for it. He quickly looked along the ditch, and after going about ten yards found the hat lying among the leaves. Gabriel took it up and went back to his hut. There he settled himself and watched through the loophole in the direction from which the rider was approaching.

“My memory is still accurate, but I cannot write the words of our conversation. I suspect that I am watched more closely than I was, and I know not at what times I may be watched. She had in part suspected, and in part discovered, the main facts of the cruel story, of her husband’s share in it, and my being resorted to. She did not know that the girl was dead. Her hope had been, she said in great distress, to show her, in secret, a woman’s sympathy. Her hope had been to avert the wrath of Heaven from a House that had long been hateful to the suffering many.

My recollection of what passed between us is still perfectly sharp, but I dare not commit the exact words of our conversation to paper. I get the sense I’m under far closer watch than I used to be, and I have no way of knowing when I might be monitored. She’d guessed some parts of that brutal, terrible story on her own, and uncovered the rest of its core truths: how her husband was complicit, and why I’d been pulled into the whole affair. What she hadn’t yet learned was that the young woman was already dead. She was deeply distraught as she told me her greatest hope had been to quietly extend the gentle, understanding sympathy only another woman could offer the girl. She’d also hoped to ward off divine wrath from a household that had for so long been hated by scores of suffering, wronged people.

Never had he been at such an altitude of living, and he kept himself in the background, listening, observing, and pleasuring, replying in reticent monosyllables, saying, “Yes, miss,” and “No, miss,” to her, and “Yes, ma’am,” and “No, ma’am,” to her mother. He curbed the impulse, arising out of his sea-training, to say “Yes, sir,” and “No, sir,” to her brothers. He felt that it would be inappropriate and a confession of inferiority on his part—which would never do if he was to win to her. Also, it was a dictate of his pride. “By God!” he cried to himself, once; “I’m just as good as them, and if they do know lots that I don’t, I could learn ’m a few myself, all the same!” And the next moment, when she or her mother addressed him as “Mr. Eden,” his aggressive pride was forgotten, and he was glowing and warm with delight. He was a civilized man, that was what he was, shoulder to shoulder, at dinner, with people he had read about in books. He was in the books himself, adventuring through the printed pages of bound volumes.

He had never before reached such a peak of existence. He lingered on the edges, listening, watching, taking quiet pleasure, and answering in guarded monosyllables—"Yes, miss" and "No, miss" to her, "Yes, ma'am" and "No, ma'am" to her mother. He suppressed the sailor's reflex to say "Yes, sir" and "No, sir" to her brothers. It would strike the wrong note, he thought, a tacit admission of lower standing—and that would never serve if he meant to win her. His pride demanded it too. "By God!" he exclaimed inwardly at one point. "I'm every bit their equal, and if they know plenty I don't, I could teach them a thing or two myself, all the same!" Then, a moment later, when she or her mother called him "Mr. Eden," his bristling pride dissolved, and he glowed, suffused with pleasure. He was a man of cultivation now—that was the truth of it—sitting shoulder to shoulder at dinner with figures he had only met before in books. He himself had stepped into those books, adventuring through the printed pages of bound volumes.

“Pulled it off,” said Chunk with Elysium in his grin. “Rosy hit the fire-escape on time to a second, and we was under the wire at the Reverend’s at 9.30¼. She’s up at the flat—she cooked eggs this mornin’ in a blue kimono—Lord! how lucky I am! You must pace up some day, Ikey, and feed with us. I’ve got a job down near the bridge, and that’s where I’m heading for now.”

“Brought it off,” declared Barney, his smile radiating pure paradise. “Mabel reached the iron stairs precisely as planned, and we were before the parson at exactly nine-thirty and a fraction. She’s settled in the rooms now—whipped up a scramble this morning wearing a silk robe of cornflower blue—Lord, what a fortunate soul I've become!

. We discard the pairs with equal y s i and y s j . To improve the dialog feature representation, we fine-tune the model M (φ, f ) on sampled dialog pairs along with the derived labels from comparing the smoothed scores {x i , x j , z s i,j }. We note that z s i,j depends solely on the noisy self-reported ratings in the training set and does not depend on the expert annotations. Theoretically, we could iter-ate between label smoothing and model fine-tuning since the fine-tuned model provides better dialog feature representation. In practice, we find that one iteration is enough to reach good prediction performance.

metadata.title: Beyond User Self-Reported Likert Scale Ratings: A Comparison Model for Automatic Dialog Evaluation
metadata.section: Stage 2: Fine-tuning with smoothed self-reported user ratings

text: We discard pairs with equal y_{s_i} and y_{s_j}. To improve the dialog feature representation, we fine-tune the model M(\phi, f) on sampled dialog pairs together with the derived labels obtained by comparing the smoothed scores {x_i, x_j, z_{s_i,j}}. We note that z_{s_i,j} depends only on the noisy self-reported ratings in the training set and does not depend on the expert annotations. Theoretically, we could iterate between label smoothing and model fine-tuning, since the fine-tuned model yields a better dialog feature representation. In practice, we find that a single iteration is sufficient to achieve strong prediction performance.

As he surveyed the rooms he felt strongly disinclined for the alterations which would have to be made in the time-honoured furnishing of his parents and grandparents, to suit Eustacia’s modern ideas. The gaunt oak-cased clock, with the picture of the Ascension on the door panel and the Miraculous Draught of Fishes on the base; his grandmother’s corner cupboard with the glass door, through which the spotted china was visible; the dumb-waiter; the wooden tea trays; the hanging fountain with the brass tap—whither would these venerable articles have to be banished?

While Julian paced the apartments, he was seized by a sharp distaste for the removals necessary to reconcile his forebears’ hallowed furniture with Genevieve’s fashionable whims. The skeletal oak clock, its panel depicting the Assumption and its plinth the Gathering of the Nets; the corner hutch with its glazed door revealing the dapple-patterned china; the rotating server; the heavy wooden salvers; the wall-cistern with its burnished tap—to what exile would these venerable relics be consigned?

After placing a glass of hot gin-and-water on the mantelpiece, he pulled his chair closer to the fire and, interspersing the act with sundry moralisings on the pervasive vice of discontent and grumbling, settled down to peruse the newspaper.

Mrs. Nunsuch went and searched till she found a fragment of the narrowest red ribbon, which she took downstairs and tied round the neck of the image. Then fetching ink and a quilt from the rickety bureau by the window, she blackened the feet of the image to the extent presumably covered by shoes; and on the instep of each foot marked cross-lines in the shape taken by the sandalstrings of those days. Finally she tied a bit of black thread round the upper part of the head, in faint resemblance to a snood worn for confining the hair.

Mistress Craythorne hunted through her belongings until she unearthed a scrap of the slenderest crimson cord, carried it below, and fastened it about the figurine’s throat. Then, retrieving a pot of lampblack and a feather-stuffed counterpane from the tottering escritoire beside the casement, she darkened the feet of the little figure up to the line where slippers would have covered them, and upon the arch of each foot etched crossing strokes to mimic the crisscross thongs of the era’s sandals. At last she looped a strand of jet thread around the crown of the head, a ghost of the caul once used to bind the tresses.

“It could not be otherwise,” said the prisoner. “All things have worked together as they have fallen out. It was the always-vain endeavour to discharge my poor mother’s trust that first brought my fatal presence near you. Good could never come of such evil, a happier end was not in nature to so unhappy a beginning. Be comforted, and forgive me. Heaven bless you!”

“It had to be this way,” the prisoner said. “Everything has unfolded exactly as it was meant to. My doomed attempt to fulfill my poor mother’s trust is what first drew my cursed presence to you. No good could ever spring from such wickedness; a joyful conclusion was impossible from so wretched a start. Take solace, and pardon me. May Heaven keep you!”

In a paroxysm of fear, the boy closed the book, and thrust it from him. Then, falling upon his knees, he prayed Heaven to spare him from such deeds; and rather to will that he should die at once, than be reserved for crimes, so fearful and appalling. By degrees, he grew more calm, and besought, in a low and broken voice, that he might be rescued from his present dangers; and that if any aid were to be raised up for a poor outcast boy who had never known the love of friends or kindred, it might come to him now, when, desolate and deserted, he stood alone in the midst of wickedness and guilt.

Overwhelmed by a sudden surge of panic, the boy snapped the book shut and shoved it aside. Falling to his knees, he begged the heavens to keep him from such atrocities, pleading for a quick death rather than a future destined for such grisly and horrific acts. Slowly, his agitation subsided, and in a hushed, trembling voice, he prayed for deliverance from his current peril. He implored that if any mercy remained for a lonely orphan who had never known the love of kin or companions, it might reach him now, as he stood forsaken and isolated in the heart of such pervasive evil and guilt.

The current research on sentence similarity measures has made tremendous advances thanks to distributed word representations computed by neural nets. In this work, we use sent2vec 4 (Pagliardini et al., 2018) to generate sentence embeddings. The network implements a simple but efficient unsupervised objective to train distributed representations of sentences. The authors claim that the algorithm performs state-of-the-art sentence representations on multiple benchmark tasks in particular for unsupervised similarity evaluation. We define the similarity score EM (s i , s j ) between sentences s i and s j via cosine similarity of their distributed representations h i and h j :
