text: A whole village-full of sensuous emotion, scattered abroad all the year long, surged here in a focus for an hour. The forty hearts of those waving couples were beating as they had not done since, twelve months before, they had come together in similar jollity. For the time paganism was revived in their hearts, the pride of life was all in all, and they adored none other than themselves.

All the rich, sensory feeling stitched through the entire village, scattered thin across every ordinary day of the year, swelled into a single thrumming, concentrated focus here for one short hour. The forty hearts of those swaying, dancing pairs beat faster than they had at any point since they’d gathered for the same giddy, raucous celebration twelve full months prior. For that fleeting stretch, unbridled pagan warmth bloomed again in their chests, the unapologetic joy of being alive was the only thing that mattered, and the only thing they worshipped was the bright, vivid pulse of their own selves.

He professed to be calling upon Mr. and Mrs. Britling, and to a certain extent he was; but he had a quick eye for the door or windows; his glance roved irrelevantly as he talked. A faint expectation of Cissie came in with him and hovered about him, as the scent of violets follows the flower.

He claimed he had dropped by to pay a call on Mr. and Mrs. Britling, and that was true, up to a point; but his gaze kept flicking toward the front door and the windows, straying unmoored from the topic of conversation the whole time he spoke. A soft, unspoken hope of running into Cissie had stepped over the threshold with him, lingering around him as unshakable as the scent of violets clings to its bloom.

And I wept both night and day,
And he wiped my tears away;
And I wept both day and night,
And hid from him my heart’s delight.

metadata.title: Songs of Innocence and of Experience

text:
I cried through the dark, I cried through the light,
And he brushed every tear from my sight;
I cried through the light, I cried through the dark,
And concealed from him my heart’s secret spark.

She looked over her shoulder and discovered Mr. Britling approaching by the field path. He looked white and tired and listless, even his bristling hair and moustache conveyed his depression; he was dressed in an old tweed knickerbocker suit and carrying a big atlas and some papers. He had an effect of hesitation in his approach. It was as if he wanted to talk to her and doubted her reception for him.

She glanced back and saw Mr. Vallance coming along the path by the meadow. He appeared pale, worn, and heavy with fatigue; even his stiff hair and clipped moustache seemed to mirror his dejection. He wore an old tweed knickerbocker suit and carried a large atlas and several sheets of paper. There was something uncertain in the way he came on, as though he wished to speak with her yet feared the welcome she might give him.

“An’ next week we got to do it all over again,” he said sadly. “An’ what’s the good of it all, hey? Sometimes I wish I was a hobo. They don’t work, an’ they get their livin’. Gee! I wish I had a glass of beer; but I can’t get up the gumption to go down to the village an’ get it. You’ll stay over, an’ send your books down by express, or else you’re a damn fool.”

“And next week we’ll have to go through the whole grind once more,” he said with a mournful note. “What’s the point of any of it, eh? There are moments I wish I were a tramp. They don’t lift a finger and still scrape by. Lord, I’d give anything for a glass of beer, but I just can’t muster the will to walk down to the village and fetch one. You’ll spend the night, and ship your books later by express—unless you’re a damned fool.”

“I will think of this,” she said hurriedly. “Whether I can honestly make use of you as a friend, or must close with you as a lover—that is what I must ask myself. If I wish to go and decide to accept your company I will signal to you some evening at eight o’clock punctually, and this will mean that you are to be ready with a horse and trap at twelve o’clock the same night to drive me to Budmouth harbour in time for the morning boat.”

“I’ll give this some thought,” she said quickly. “I need to ask myself whether I can honestly rely on you as a friend, or if I must commit to you as a lover. If I decide to leave and want you to come with me, I’ll signal you at exactly eight o’clock one evening. That will be your cue to be ready with a horse and carriage at midnight that same night to drive me to the Budmouth docks in time for the morning boat.”

When inspecting the translation correspondents of adjectives, we find that while in En-Fr and En-Ru the adjective classes are mostly overlapping, this is not the case for Zh, Ko and Jp. Instead, translation into these languages shifts probability mass from adjectives to nouns: nouns are hardly ever translated to adjectives, but adjectives are more likely to be translated to nouns than remain adjectives. This trend is related to a preference to translate adjectives into possessives (e.g., Korean company → Jp: Kankoku no kaisha lit. 'a company of Korea') or compounds (e.g., European market → Jp: Oshū ichiba lit. 'Europe market').

metadata.title: Fine-Grained Analysis of Cross-Linguistic Syntactic Divergences

text: When examining the translation correspondences of adjectives, we observe that while the adjective classes in English–French and English–Russian pairs largely overlap, the same does not hold for Chinese, Korean, and Japanese. In translations into these languages, probability mass shifts from adjectives to nouns: nouns are almost never rendered as adjectives, whereas adjectives are more often translated as nouns than retained as adjectives. This tendency is linked to a preference for translating adjectives into possessive constructions (e.g., Korean company → Japanese: Kankoku no kaisha, literally ‘a company of Korea’) or compounds (e.g., European market → Japanese: Ōshū ichiba, literally ‘Europe market’).

He occupied rooms in the Bank, in his fidelity to the House of which he had grown to be a part, like strong root-ivy. It chanced that they derived a kind of security from the patriotic occupation of the main building, but the true-hearted old gentleman never calculated about that. All such circumstances were indifferent to him, so that he did his duty. On the opposite side of the courtyard, under a colonnade, was extensive standing--for carriages--where, indeed, some carriages of Monseigneur yet stood. Against two of the pillars were fastened two great flaring flambeaux, and in the light of these, standing out in the open air, was a large grindstone: a roughly mounted thing which appeared to have hurriedly been brought there from some neighbouring smithy, or other workshop. Rising and looking out of window at these harmless objects, Mr. Lorry shivered, and retired to his seat by the fire. He had opened, not only the glass window, but the lattice blind outside it, and he had closed both again, and he shivered through his frame.

He lived in rooms at the Bank, clinging to the House that had absorbed him as ivy clings to a wall with deep, tenacious roots. It so happened that his presence in the main building offered a certain patriotic reassurance, but the honest old man never paused to reckon such things. So long as he did his duty, all else was immaterial to him. Across the courtyard, beneath a colonnade, stretched a broad standing for carriages—where, in fact, a few of Monseigneur’s carriages still remained. Two pillars held a pair of blazing flambeaux, and in their glare, exposed to the night air, stood a large grindstone: a rough, hastily mounted thing that looked as though it had been dragged there from some nearby smithy or workshop. Mr. Lorry rose, gazed out at these unremarkable objects, shivered, and withdrew to his seat beside the fire. He had opened not only the glazed window but the lattice blind beyond it, and after closing both again, a tremor ran through his whole frame.

Mrs. Teddy went about the village in a coloured dress bearing herself confidently. Teddy had been listed now as "missing, since reported killed," and she had had two letters from his comrades. They said Teddy had been left behind in the ruins of a farm with one or two other wounded, and that when the Canadians retook the place these wounded had all been found butchered. None had been found alive. Afterwards the Canadians had had to fall back. Mr. Direck had been at great pains to hunt up wounded men from Teddy's company, and also any likely Canadians both at the base hospital in France and in London, and to get what he could from them. He had made it a service to Cissie. Only one of his witnesses was quite clear about Teddy, but he, alas! was dreadfully clear. There had been only one lieutenant among the men left behind, he said, and obviously that must have been Teddy. "He had been prodded in half-a-dozen places. His head was nearly severed from his body."

Mrs. Haviland moved through the village in a bright patterned frock, carrying herself with a bold, almost triumphant air. By then Stephen had been entered on the lists as “missing, reported killed,” and she had received two letters from his comrades. They said that Stephen had been left behind in the shattered shell of a farmhouse with one or two other wounded men, and that when the Canadians took the position back these wounded had been discovered slaughtered. Not one had been found alive. Afterward the Canadians had been compelled to withdraw. Mr. Loring had taken immense pains to trace wounded men from Stephen’s company, and also any likely Canadians, both in the base hospital in France and in London, and to draw from them whatever information he could. He had made the matter a service to Elsie. Only one of his witnesses was entirely certain about Stephen, but, unhappily, he was certain in the worst way. There had been only one lieutenant among the men left behind, he said, and plainly that must have been Stephen. “He had been stabbed in half a dozen places. His head was almost cut off his body.”

So the men entered the hall, which was the room selected and arranged for the gathering because of its size. The younger men and maids were at last just beginning to dance. Bathsheba had been perplexed how to act, for she was not much more than a slim young maid herself, and the weight of stateliness sat heavy upon her. Sometimes she thought she ought not to have come under any circumstances; then she considered what cold unkindness that would have been, and finally resolved upon the middle course of staying for about an hour only, and gliding off unobserved, having from the first made up her mind that she could on no account dance, sing, or take any active part in the proceedings.

So the men came into the hall, the room chosen and fitted up for the gathering on account of its size. The younger men and girls had only just begun to dance. Bathsheba had been uncertain how she ought to behave, for she was hardly more than a slender young girl herself, and the burden of dignity weighed heavily on her. At times she felt she should not have come at all; then she reflected how cold and unkind that would have been, and at last decided on a middle course: to remain for about an hour and then slip away unnoticed, having from the outset resolved that she could on no account dance, sing, or take any active part in the evening's doings.

The piece was the well-known play of Saint George, and all who were behind the scenes assisted in the preparations, including the women of each household. Without the co-operation of sisters and sweethearts the dresses were likely to be a failure; but on the other hand, this class of assistance was not without its drawbacks. The girls could never be brought to respect tradition in designing and decorating the armour; they insisted on attaching loops and bows of silk and velvet in any situation pleasing to their taste. Gorget, gusset, basinet, cuirass, gauntlet, sleeve, all alike in the view of these feminine eyes were practicable spaces whereon to sew scraps of fluttering colour.

The production in question was the beloved, widely known staging of Saint George, and everyone working backstage pitched in with the preparations, including the women from every local household. If sisters and sweethearts hadn’t lent their support, the costumes would almost certainly have turned out poorly; that said, this particular brand of assistance was not without its downsides. No one could ever get these young women to honor longstanding tradition when designing and decorating the armor; they insisted on sewing silk and velvet loops and bows anywhere that suited their personal taste. Gorget, gusset, basinet, cuirass, gauntlet, sleeve—every last one of these components, in these women’s eyes, was a perfectly usable surface to stitch on scraps of bright, fluttering color.

Had only Yeobright’s own future been involved he would have proposed to Thomasin with a ready heart. He had nothing to lose by carrying out a dead mother’s hope. But he dreaded to contemplate Thomasin wedded to the mere corpse of a lover that he now felt himself to be. He had but three activities alive in him. One was his almost daily walk to the little graveyard wherein his mother lay; another, his just as frequent visits by night to the more distant enclosure which numbered his Eustacia among its dead; the third was self-preparation for a vocation which alone seemed likely to satisfy his cravings—that of an itinerant preacher of the eleventh commandment. It was difficult to believe that Thomasin would be cheered by a husband with such tendencies as these.

If Clym’s own destiny were the only thing at stake, he would have asked Thomasin to marry him without hesitation. He had nothing to lose by honoring his late mother’s final wish. Yet, he was troubled by the idea of Thomasin being tied to the emotional shell of a man he felt himself to be. Only three interests still flickered within him. One was his nearly daily pilgrimage to the small graveyard where his mother rested; another was his equally regular nighttime visits to the more distant burial ground where Eustacia lay; and the last was his preparation for the only calling that seemed capable of quieting his restless spirit—serving as a wandering preacher of the eleventh commandment. It was hard to imagine that Thomasin would find any comfort in a husband defined by such grim preoccupations.

I wanted to stop the whole thing and set the slaves free, but that would not do. I must not interfere too much and get myself a name for riding over the country's laws and the citizen's rights roughshod. If I lived and prospered I would be the death of slavery, that I was resolved upon; but I would try to fix it so that when I became its executioner it should be by command of the nation.

I longed to halt the entire affair and loose the bondsmen on the spot, yet such a course was impossible. I dared not meddle overmuch and earn a reputation for trampling the laws of the land and the liberties of its people underfoot. Should I survive and thrive, I meant to be the ruin of bondage—on that I was fixed—but I would strive to arrange matters so that when I struck the fatal blow, it would fall by the nation’s own decree.

Martin’s alienation from his family continued. Following upon Mr. Higginbotham’s unprovoked attack, Mr. Hermann von Schmidt showed his hand. The fortunate sale of several storiettes, some humorous verse, and a few jokes gave Martin a temporary splurge of prosperity. Not only did he partially pay up his bills, but he had sufficient balance left to redeem his black suit and wheel. The latter, by virtue of a twisted crank-hanger, required repairing, and, as a matter of friendliness with his future brother-in-law, he sent it to Von Schmidt’s shop.

Edgar’s estrangement from his family went on unabated. After Mr. Kettlewell’s gratuitous assault, Mr. Otto von Riedel revealed his true colors. The lucky disposal of a handful of short sketches, a few comic verses, and several jests brought Edgar a brief spell of affluence. Not only was he able to pay off part of his obligations, but he had enough remaining to reclaim his black suit and bicycle. The latter, because of a bent crank hanger, needed mending, and, as a gesture of cordiality toward his prospective brother-in-law, he sent it to von Riedel’s establishment.

Poor child! he slumped to the floor, twisted his silken limbs in one great straining contortion of pain, and was dead. Out of the old king was wrung an involuntary “O-h!” of compassion. The look he got, made him cut it suddenly short and not put any more hyphens in it. Sir Uwaine, at a sign from his mother, went to the anteroom and called some servants, and meanwhile madame went rippling sweetly along with her talk.

The miserable youth collapsed upon the stones, his slender frame knotting in a single, violent paroxysm of agony before he drew his final breath. A sharp, unbidden cry of pity broke from the elderly monarch’s lips, but a freezing glare from his consort caused him to stifle the sound instantly, severing his grief before it could find full voice. At a subtle gesture from his mother, Prince Gaheris withdrew to the vestibule to fetch the menials; meanwhile, the lady resumed her melodic conversation, her words flowing with a terrifying, untroubled grace.

She felt wretchedly certain that if he revisited her just at this nick of time, and came into contact with Boldwood, a fierce quarrel would be the consequence. She panted with solicitude when she thought of possible injury to Troy. The least spark would kindle the farmer’s swift feelings of rage and jealousy; he would lose his self-mastery as he had this evening; Troy’s blitheness might become aggressive; it might take the direction of derision, and Boldwood’s anger might then take the direction of revenge.

She knew with a sinking dread that if Troy returned to her now, at this precise and perilous moment, and crossed paths with Boldwood, a violent clash would follow. Her breath quickened with anxious care at the mere thought of harm befalling Troy. The faintest ember would ignite the farmer’s quick-burning fury and envy; all self-command would desert him, as it had only hours ago; Troy’s careless gaiety might sharpen into provocation, veering toward mockery, and Boldwood’s wrath might then veer toward vengeance.

So the great discovery began. Once before he had tried Spencer, and choosing the “Principles of Psychology” to begin with, he had failed as abjectly as he had failed with Madam Blavatsky. There had been no understanding the book, and he had returned it unread. But this night, after algebra and physics, and an attempt at a sonnet, he got into bed and opened “First Principles.” Morning found him still reading. It was impossible for him to sleep. Nor did he write that day. He lay on the bed till his body grew tired, when he tried the hard floor, reading on his back, the book held in the air above him, or changing from side to side. He slept that night, and did his writing next morning, and then the book tempted him and he fell, reading all afternoon, oblivious to everything and oblivious to the fact that that was the afternoon Ruth gave to him. His first consciousness of the immediate world about him was when Bernard Higginbotham jerked open the door and demanded to know if he thought they were running a restaurant.

And so the monumental revelation commenced. He had attempted Spencer once before, but by starting with "Principles of Psychology," he had failed as miserably as he had with Madam Blavatsky. The text had been incomprehensible, and he had returned it without finishing it. But on this night, after his algebra and physics lessons and a brief effort at a sonnet, he climbed into bed and opened "First Principles." Dawn found him still immersed in the pages. Sleep was out of the question. He didn't even work on his writing that day. He remained on the bed until his body grew stiff, then moved to the hard floor, reading on his back with the book held aloft or shifting from side to side. He slept the following night and did his writing the next morning, but then the book's allure proved too great and he succumbed, reading all through the afternoon. He was oblivious to everything, including the fact that this was the very afternoon Ruth had reserved for him. He only became aware of the world around him when Bernard Higginbotham threw open the door and demanded to know if he thought they were running a restaurant.

Following the setting in (Tang et al., 2019) , where we randomly sample a keyword as the target word for each session in testing procedure. Here we use a multi-mapping based user simulator trained on the Persona dataset for evaluation. Table 5 presents the results on 100 dialogs for each model. We see that CG-Policy-Target can significantly outperform baselines in terms of dialogtarget success rate (sign test, p-value < 0.01). It can be seen that that CG-Policy can successfully lead the dialog to a given target word by learning to walk over the CG, indicating that this graph gives us more control over the policy. LaRL-Target and ChatMore-Target perform badly in terms of success rate. It may be explained by that they lack the ability of proactive dialog content planning. Figure 4 provides representative words of each mechanism. 15 For example, for Mech-1, its keywords are mainly subjective words (e.g. think) for generation of responses with respect to personal opinion or intention. For Mech-2, it tends to respond with a specific type of mood. Figure 4 : Representative words of responding mechanisms.

metadata.title: Conversational Graph Grounded Policy Learning for Open-Domain Conversation Generation

text: Following the evaluation protocol of (Tang et al., 2019), we randomly select a keyword as the target word for each session during testing. For evaluation, we employ a multi-mapping-based user simulator trained on the Persona dataset. Table 5 reports results over 100 dialogs for each model. We observe that CG-Policy-Target substantially outperforms the baselines on dialog target success rate (sign test, p-value < 0.01). In addition, CG-Policy can successfully steer the dialog toward a specified target word by learning to traverse the CG, suggesting that this graph provides finer control over the policy. By contrast, LaRL-Target and ChatMore-Target perform poorly in terms of success rate, which may be attributed to their inability to plan dialog content proactively. Figure 4 shows representative words associated with each mechanism.15 For instance, Mech-1 is characterized primarily by subjective words (e.g. think) for generating responses related to personal opinions or intentions. Mech-2, in contrast, tends to produce responses with a particular mood. Figure 4: Representative words of responding mechanisms.

“Several changes, in the meantime, took place in the cottage. The presence of Safie diffused happiness among its inhabitants, and I also found that a greater degree of plenty reigned there. Felix and Agatha spent more time in amusement and conversation, and were assisted in their labours by servants. They did not appear rich, but they were contented and happy; their feelings were serene and peaceful, while mine became every day more tumultuous. Increase of knowledge only discovered to me more clearly what a wretched outcast I was. I cherished hope, it is true, but it vanished when I beheld my person reflected in water or my shadow in the moonshine, even as that frail image and that inconstant shade.

In the interval, the household underwent subtle transformations. Safie’s arrival brought a quiet radiance into the lives of those within, and I perceived that a fuller measure of comfort now blessed them. Felix and Agatha gave their hours more freely to leisure and talk, aided in their tasks by hired hands. They were not wealthy, yet they wore their lot with tranquil satisfaction; their spirits rested in calm contentment, while mine grew daily more tempestuous. Each new insight only sharpened the truth that I was a miserable exile. I clung to hope, indeed, but it dissolved whenever I glimpsed my form in a pool’s mirror or my shadow cast by the moon—as fleeting as that fragile reflection and that wavering darkness.

Let it not be supposed by the enemies of “the system,” that, during the period of his solitary incarceration, Oliver was denied the benefit of exercise, the pleasure of society, or the advantages of religious consolation. As for exercise, it was nice cold weather, and he was allowed to perform his ablutions every morning under the pump, in a stone yard, in the presence of Mr. Bumble, who prevented his catching cold, and caused a tingling sensation to pervade his frame, by repeated applications of the cane. As for society, he was carried every other day into the hall where the boys dined, and there sociably flogged as a public warning and example. And so far from being denied the advantages of religious consolation, he was kicked into the same apartment every evening at prayer-time, and there permitted to listen to, and console his mind with, a general supplication of the boys, containing a special clause, therein inserted by authority of the board, in which they entreated to be made good, virtuous, contented, and obedient, and to be guarded from the sins and vices of Oliver Twist: whom the supplication distinctly set forth to be under the exclusive patronage and protection of the powers of wickedness, and an article direct from the manufactory of the very Devil himself.

Let no adversary of “the regime” imagine that, throughout his days of isolated confinement, Oliver was deprived of bodily exertion, the comforts of fellowship, or the blessings of spiritual solace. Regarding exertion, the weather being brisk and sharp, he was granted leave to wash each morning beneath the yard pump, upon the flagstones, under the eye of Mr. Bumble, who warded off any chill and sent a smarting glow through his limbs by means of regular strokes of the rod. As to fellowship, every second day he was marched into the dining hall where the lads took their meals, and there companionably whipped as a general caution and spectacle. And far from lacking spiritual solace, he was thrust each evening at prayer hour into that same chamber, and there allowed to hear, and soothe his soul with, a collective entreaty of the boys, containing a particular petition, inserted by the board’s decree, in which they begged to be rendered good, upright, satisfied, and dutiful, and to be shielded from the sins and vices of Oliver Twist—whom the prayer expressly declared to be under the sole patronage and guardianship of the powers of darkness, and an item dispatched straight from the workshop of the Devil himself.

Clerval! Beloved friend! Even now it delights me to record your words and to dwell on the praise of which you are so eminently deserving. He was a being formed in the “very poetry of nature.” His wild and enthusiastic imagination was chastened by the sensibility of his heart. His soul overflowed with ardent affections, and his friendship was of that devoted and wondrous nature that the worldly-minded teach us to look for only in the imagination. But even human sympathies were not sufficient to satisfy his eager mind. The scenery of external nature, which others regard only with admiration, he loved with ardour:—

Oak, upon hearing these remarks, became more curious to observe her features, but this prospect being denied him by the hooding effect of the cloak, and by his aërial position, he felt himself drawing upon his fancy for their details. In making even horizontal and clear inspections we colour and mould according to the wants within us whatever our eyes bring in. Had Gabriel been able from the first to get a distinct view of her countenance, his estimate of it as very handsome or slightly so would have been as his soul required a divinity at the moment or was ready supplied with one. Having for some time known the want of a satisfactory form to fill an increasing void within him, his position moreover affording the widest scope for his fancy, he painted her a beauty.

When Ash heard these words, his desire to study her features grew keener; yet the hooded fall of the cloak denied him that sight, and his lofty vantage did no more to help him, so that he found himself compelled to invent their particulars out of his own imagination. Even in the plainest, most level act of looking, we shape and tint what our eyes present to us according to some inward need. Had Raphael, from the first, been granted a clear view of her face, his judgment of it, whether as truly beautiful or only passably so, would have answered to the divinity his soul then demanded, or was prepared at once to accept. Since for some time he had been conscious of a need for some fitting image to occupy a swelling emptiness within him, and since his situation gave his fancy the fullest license, he endowed her with beauty.

Not from successful love alone,
Nor wealth, nor honor’d middle age, nor victories of politics or war;
But as life wanes, and all the turbulent passions calm,
As gorgeous, vapory, silent hues cover the evening sky,
As softness, fulness, rest, suffuse the frame, like freshier, balmier air,
As the days take on a mellower light, and the apple at last hangs
really finish’d and indolent-ripe on the tree,
Then for the teeming quietest, happiest days of all!
The brooding and blissful halcyon days!

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Not from love’s success alone,  
Nor wealth, nor honored middle years, nor triumphs won in politics or war;  
But when life thins and all the storming passions settle,  
As rich, misted, wordless colors wash the evening sky,  
As ease, abundance, and repose soak through the body, like newer, sweeter air,  
As the days deepen into a gentler light, and the apple finally hangs  
truly complete, and lazily ripe, upon the tree,  
Then comes the busiest quiet, the happiest days of all—  
the meditative, radiant halcyon days.

We were brought up together; there was not quite a year difference in our ages. I need not say that we were strangers to any species of disunion or dispute. Harmony was the soul of our companionship, and the diversity and contrast that subsisted in our characters drew us nearer together. Elizabeth was of a calmer and more concentrated disposition; but, with all my ardour, I was capable of a more intense application and was more deeply smitten with the thirst for knowledge. She busied herself with following the aerial creations of the poets; and in the majestic and wondrous scenes which surrounded our Swiss home —the sublime shapes of the mountains, the changes of the seasons, tempest and calm, the silence of winter, and the life and turbulence of our Alpine summers—she found ample scope for admiration and delight. While my companion contemplated with a serious and satisfied spirit the magnificent appearances of things, I delighted in investigating their causes. The world was to me a secret which I desired to divine. Curiosity, earnest research to learn the hidden laws of nature, gladness akin to rapture, as they were unfolded to me, are among the earliest sensations I can remember.

We grew up together, with less than a year between our ages. I need hardly say that we knew nothing of estrangement or quarrels. Harmony was the very heart of our friendship, and the differences in our natures only drew us closer. Elizabeth was calmer and more self-possessed; for all my eagerness, I was capable of a more sustained application and was more keenly driven by a hunger for knowledge. She occupied herself with pursuing the airy visions of poets, and in the grand and marvelous scenery that encircled our Swiss home—the sublime forms of the mountains, the shifting seasons, storm and stillness, the hush of winter, and the vitality and turmoil of our Alpine summers—she found abundant cause for wonder and pleasure. While my companion regarded the splendid spectacle of the world with a grave and contented mind, I took delight in probing its causes. The world was, to me, a mystery I longed to unravel. Curiosity, an earnest desire to discover nature’s hidden laws, and a joy bordering on ecstasy as they were revealed to me, are among the earliest feelings I can recall.

But Yeobright was too deeply absorbed in the ramblings incidental to his remorseful state to notice her. During his illness he had been continually talking thus. Despair had been added to his original grief by the unfortunate disclosure of the boy who had received the last words of Mrs. Yeobright—words too bitterly uttered in an hour of misapprehension. Then his distress had overwhelmed him, and he longed for death as a field labourer longs for the shade. It was the pitiful sight of a man standing in the very focus of sorrow. He continually bewailed his tardy journey to his mother’s house, because it was an error which could never be rectified, and insisted that he must have been horribly perverted by some fiend not to have thought before that it was his duty to go to her, since she did not come to him. He would ask Eustacia to agree with him in his self-condemnation; and when she, seared inwardly by a secret she dared not tell, declared that she could not give an opinion, he would say, “That’s because you didn’t know my mother’s nature. She was always ready to forgive if asked to do so; but I seemed to her to be as an obstinate child, and that made her unyielding. Yet not unyielding—she was proud and reserved, no more.... Yes, I can understand why she held out against me so long. She was waiting for me. I dare say she said a hundred times in her sorrow, ‘What a return he makes for all the sacrifices I have made for him!’ I never went to her! When I set out to visit her it was too late. To think of that is nearly intolerable!”

Yeobright was too deeply lost in the rambling thoughts brought on by his guilt to pay her any mind. He had been speaking this way incessantly throughout his illness. His initial grief had turned to pure despair following the unfortunate account from the boy who had heard Mrs. Yeobright’s final words—bitter things said in a moment of tragic misunderstanding. This distress had completely broken him, and he yearned for death as a laborer in the fields aches for the shade. It was a heart-wrenching sight: a man standing at the very center of sorrow. He constantly mourned his delayed journey to his mother’s house, an error that could never be undone, insisting he must have been possessed by some dark spirit to have forgotten his duty to go to her since she would not come to him. He would beg Eustacia to confirm his self-blame; yet she, tormented by a secret she dared not confess, claimed she could not offer an opinion. “That’s because you didn’t know my mother’s heart,” he would reply. “She was always ready to forgive if she were asked, but I must have seemed like a stubborn child to her, which made her appear unyielding. But she wasn't—she was just proud and reserved. Yes, I understand now why she held out against me for so long. She was waiting for me to come. I imagine she said a hundred times in her grief, ‘This is how he repays all my sacrifices!’ And I never went to her. When I finally set out to see her, it was too late. The thought of it is almost more than I can bear.”

She came up and looked around—then on the other side of the hedge. Gabriel was about to advance and restore the missing article when an unexpected performance induced him to suspend the action for the present. The path, after passing the cowshed, bisected the plantation. It was not a bridle-path—merely a pedestrian’s track, and the boughs spread horizontally at a height not greater than seven feet above the ground, which made it impossible to ride erect beneath them. The girl, who wore no riding-habit, looked around for a moment, as if to assure herself that all humanity was out of view, then dexterously dropped backwards flat upon the pony’s back, her head over its tail, her feet against its shoulders, and her eyes to the sky. The rapidity of her glide into this position was that of a kingfisher—its noiselessness that of a hawk. Gabriel’s eyes had scarcely been able to follow her. The tall lank pony seemed used to such doings, and ambled along unconcerned. Thus she passed under the level boughs.

She stepped to the edge of the thicket, craning her neck first one way, then peering over to the far side of the hawthorn hedge. Elias was just preparing to step out and hand back the linen handkerchief she’d dropped moments earlier, when an unanticipated bit of whimsy made him suspend his movement for the time being. The dirt track, once it curved past the weathered stone cowshed, cut straight through the middle of the old pine plantation. It was no wide bridle road—only a narrow footpath worn soft by decades of walkers, and the pine boughs stretched out level barely seven feet above the packed earth, making it impossible for anyone on horseback to sit upright and pass beneath without getting swatted by needles. The young woman, who wore a simple cotton frock instead of a formal riding habit, glanced over her shoulders twice, as if confirming no other soul was nearby to see her, then flipped nimbly backward until she lay flat across the pony’s broad back, her head lolling near its tail, her boots propped against its shoulders, her gaze fixed straight up at the patchy blue sky between the boughs. She shifted into the position as fast as a kingfisher diving for minnows, as quiet as a hawk gliding on a thermal. Elias could barely track the movement, quick as it was. The tall, rangy pony seemed perfectly accustomed to such antics, and plodded along unconcerned without so much as a twitch of its ear. Thus she slipped unhindered beneath the low-hanging boughs.

When performing the shift-reduce parsing, two kinds of states have only one action to choose: (1) less than two elements in the stack, and (2) no element in the queue. Under the above two conditions, the probability of the selected action will be 1, making our model to be overly biased on those sequences having many non-optional stages. For this reason, we apply an alternative way to compute the sequence probability during beam search. Our modified beam search is still fulfilled by maintaining the top k sequences, but the score of a sequence is calculated by the average probabilities of the selected actions that have more than one choice.

metadata.title: A Complete Shift-Reduce Chinese Discourse Parser with Robust Dynamic Oracle

text: In shift-reduce parsing workflows, two distinct parser states admit exactly one valid executable action: first, when the parser stack holds fewer than two elements, and second, when the input token queue is empty. Under both of these conditions, the probability assigned to the only permitted action is set to 1, which introduces an undesirable bias in our model toward sequences that contain a large number of non-optional processing stages. To address this problem, we employ an adjusted formulation for calculating sequence probabilities during beam search inference. Our modified beam search procedure retains the standard mechanism of preserving the top-k highest-scoring partial sequences across decoding steps, but computes the overall sequence score as the average of the predicted probabilities of selected actions exclusively for decision steps where more than one candidate action was available.

Mrs. Yeobright’s exertions, physical and emotional, had well-nigh prostrated her; but she continued to creep along in short stages with long breaks between. The sun had now got far to the west of south and stood directly in her face, like some merciless incendiary, brand in hand, waiting to consume her. With the departure of the boy all visible animation disappeared from the landscape, though the intermittent husky notes of the male grasshoppers from every tuft of furze were enough to show that amid the prostration of the larger animal species an unseen insect world was busy in all the fullness of life.

Mrs. Yeobright was nearly felled by the toll of her physical efforts and raw emotional strain, yet she pressed on, shuffling forward in tiny bursts, pausing for long stretches between each small advance. The sun had drifted far southwest by now, hanging directly in her line of sight like a ruthless arsonist, a flaming brand clutched in one hand, poised to burn her to ash. When the boy had vanished, every last glimmer of visible life drained from the surrounding landscape, though the rough, stuttering chirps of male grasshoppers rising from every furze tuft made clear that even as all larger creatures lay listless and drained, a hidden world of insects hummed with unflagging, full-bodied vitality.

As he was drawn away, his wife released him, and stood looking after him with her hands touching one another in the attitude of prayer, and with a radiant look upon her face, in which there was even a comforting smile. As he went out at the prisoners’ door, she turned, laid her head lovingly on her father’s breast, tried to speak to him, and fell at his feet.

As they pulled him from her, his wife let go and remained gazing after him, her hands pressed together as if in prayer, her face alight with a radiance that held even a consoling smile. When he passed through the prisoners’ door, she turned, rested her head tenderly against her father’s chest, tried to say something to him, and collapsed at his feet.

"Writing is recording, not living. But now I feel suddenly that we are living intensely. It is as if the whole quality of life was changing. There are such times. There are times when the spirit of life changes altogether. The old world knew that better than we do. It made a distinction between weekdays and Sabbaths, and between feasts and fasts and days of devotion. That is just what has happened now. Week-day rules must be put aside. Before--oh! three days ago, competition was fair, it was fair and tolerable to get the best food one could and hold on to one's own. But that isn't right now. War makes a Sabbath, and we shut the shops. The banks are shut, and the world still feels as though Sunday was keeping on...."

Writing is about capturing life, not truly living it. But right now I'm struck by the sensation that we're living with extraordinary intensity. It feels as though the very texture of existence is transforming. Such moments do come. There are moments when the soul of life shifts entirely. The ancient world understood this far better than we do. They drew lines between ordinary days and holy days, between celebrations and periods of fasting and sacred observance. That's precisely what's unfolding now. The rules of everyday life must be suspended. Just a few days ago—oh, barely three days past—rivalry seemed fair, it seemed right and acceptable to scramble for the best provisions and guard what was yours. But that no longer fits. War creates a Sabbath, and we close the shops. The banks are closed, and the world still carries the feeling of an endless Sunday....

Pre-trained transformer representations (Radford et al., 2018; Radford et al., 2019) use transfer learning to yield powerful language models that considerably outperform the prior art. They have also shown great success when fine-tuned to particular text generation tasks (See et al., 2019; Keskar et al., 2019) . Given their success, it would be desirable to apply pre-trained transformer models to a graph-to-text task like AMR-to-text, but the need for graph encoding precludes in principle that option. Feeding the network with some sequential representation of the graph, such as a topological sorting, looses some of the graphs representational power. Complex graph annotations, such as AMR, also contain many special symbols and special constructs that departure from natural language and may by not interpretable by a pretrained language model.

metadata.title: GPT-too: A Language-Model-First Approach for AMR-to-Text Generation

text: Pre-trained Transformer-based representations (Radford et al., 2018; Radford et al., 2019) leverage transfer learning to produce language models that substantially surpass previous state-of-the-art approaches. These models have also demonstrated remarkable effectiveness when fine-tuned for specific text generation tasks (See et al., 2019; Keskar et al., 2019). In light of these achievements, applying pre-trained Transformer models to graph-to-text problems such as AMR-to-text generation would be appealing; however, the requirement of graph encoding rules out this possibility in principle. Supplying the network with a sequentialized form of the graph, for instance a topological ordering, sacrifices part of the graph’s representational capacity. Furthermore, complex graph annotations like AMR include numerous special symbols and non-natural constructions that diverge from ordinary language and may not be interpretable by a pre-trained language model.

Wildeve laughed to himself, remained a moment thinking, and idly returned to the front room. Here his glance fell upon a bottle of wine which stood on the mantelpiece. “Ah—old Dowden!” he murmured; and going to the kitchen door shouted, “Is anybody here who can take something to old Dowden?”

Wildeve gave a quiet laugh, stood lost in thought for a second, and then drifted back into the parlor. There, his eyes rested on a bottle of wine sitting atop the mantel. “Ah, old Dowden!” he whispered to himself. He walked over to the kitchen door and called out, “Is anyone around who can deliver something to old Dowden?”

We note that the original Common-Crawl-based corpus created by to train fast-Text is not freely available. Since running the experiments described in this paper, a new architecture for creating a Common-Crawl-based corpus named CCNet (Wenzek et al., 2019) has been published, although it includes specialized filtering which might result in a cleaner corpus compared to OSCAR, the resulting CCNet corpus itself was not published. Thus we chose to keep OSCAR as it remains the only very large scale, Common-Crawl-based corpus currently available and easily downloadable.

metadata.title: A Monolingual Approach to Contextualized Word Embeddings for Mid-Resource Languages

text: It should be noted that the original Common-Crawl-derived corpus used to train fastText is not publicly accessible. Since we conducted the experiments reported in this paper, a new pipeline for constructing a Common-Crawl-based corpus called CCNet (Wenzek et al., 2019) has been introduced; while its specialized filtering may yield a cleaner corpus than OSCAR, the resulting CCNet corpus itself was not released. We therefore elected to retain OSCAR, as it remains the sole very large-scale, Common-Crawl-based corpus that is currently both available and readily downloadable.

Christian pulled on his boots, and with heavy breathings, which could be heard to some distance, dragged his limbs together, arose, and tottered away out of sight. Wildeve set about shutting the lantern to return to the house, for he deemed it too late to go to Mistover to meet his wife, who was to be driven home in the captain’s four-wheel. While he was closing the little horn door a figure rose from behind a neighbouring bush and came forward into the lantern light. It was the reddleman approaching.

Christian dragged his boots over his feet, and with labored gasps that carried far across the stillness, hauled his frame upright, staggered upright, and stumbled away until the darkness swallowed him. Wildeve began fastening the lantern, intending to go back indoors, for he judged the hour too advanced to set out for Mistover and meet his wife, who was expected to return in the captain’s carriage. As he latched the small horn panel, a shape lifted itself from the shadow of a nearby thicket and stepped into the circle of light. The reddleman was drawing near.

But now the easy-fitting discipline of the Dower House style of hockey became apparent. Mr. Direck had last observed the tall young Indian gentleman, full of vitality and anxious for destruction, far away in the distance on the opposing right wing. But now, regardless of the more formal methods of the game, this young man had resolved, without further delay and at any cost, to hit the ball hard, and he was travelling like some Asiatic typhoon with an extreme velocity across the remonstrances of Mr. Britling and the general order of his side. Mr. Direck became aware of him just before his impact. There was a sort of collision from which Mr. Direck emerged with a feeling that one side of his face was permanently flattened, but still gallantly resolved to hit the comparatively lethargic ball. He and the staggered but resolute Indian clashed sticks again. And Mr. Direck had the best of it. Years of experience couldn't have produced a better pass to the captain....

But now the casual, free-flowing nature of hockey as played at the Dower House revealed itself. Mr. Direck had last glimpsed the tall young Indian gentleman, brimming with energy and bent on havoc, stationed far off on the opposite right flank. Yet now, ignoring the game's more conventional tactics, this young man had decided, without a moment's hesitation and no matter the consequences, to strike the ball with force, and he was hurtling forward like some Asian typhoon at tremendous speed, cutting through the protests of Mr. Britling and the entire formation of his team. Mr. Direck registered his approach only an instant before the crash. There was a kind of impact from which Mr. Direck recovered with the sensation that one cheek had been permanently compressed, though he remained bravely determined to hit the relatively sluggish ball. He and the reeling but steadfast Indian clashed sticks once more. And Mr. Direck came out on top. A lifetime of practice could not have delivered a finer pass to the captain.

metadata.title: Trialstreamer: Mapping and Browsing Medical Evidence in Real-Time

text: To identify text spans corresponding to the PICO elements of each trial, we use the EBM-NLP corpus (Nye et al., 2018). This dataset comprises approximately 5,000 abstracts of RCT reports that have been annotated to mark textual spans describing the respective PICO elements. In addition to these spans, it includes finer-grained annotations for information contained within them (e.g., specific Population attributes such as age and sex). Figure 2: Overview of the evidence extraction pipeline, applied to all automatically identified RCT article abstracts. Text spans are first extracted from these abstracts, then assembled into relations that reflect trial structure, and finally used to infer the effects that interventions were reported to have on measured outcomes relative to the control treatment.

The interior was shadowy with a peculiar shade. The strange luminous semi-opacities of fine autumn afternoons and eves intensified into Rembrandt effects the few yellow sunbeams which came through holes and divisions in the canvas, and spirted like jets of gold-dust across the dusky blue atmosphere of haze pervading the tent, until they alighted on inner surfaces of cloth opposite, and shone like little lamps suspended there.

The inside of the tent was dim, steeped in a strange, distinctive soft shadow. The odd, glowing half-translucence of crisp autumn late afternoons and early evenings turned the handful of pale gold sunbeams slipping through tears and gaps in the canvas into dramatic Rembrandt-style light effects: they streaked like sprays of fine gold dust across the hazy, dusky blue air that filled the entire space, before landing on the opposite inner canvas lining, where they glowed like tiny, suspended lamps.

This man, whom he had seen upon the Jury, stood drinking at the little counter, in conversation with the Defarges, man and wife. The Vengeance assisted in the conversation, like a regular member of the establishment.

There was no customer in the shop save Jacques the Third, with his twitching fingers and hoarse, raucous voice. This was the man he had once seen among the jury, and he now stood at the small counter, taking his drink while he talked with the Defarges, husband and wife. The Vengeance took her part in the exchange as freely as though she were one of the house itself.

The sun went down almost blood-red that night, and a livid cloud received its rays in the east. Up against this dark background the west front of the church tower—the only part of the edifice visible from the farm-house windows—rose distinct and lustrous, the vane upon the summit bristling with rays. Hereabouts, at six o’clock, the young men of the village gathered, as was their custom, for a game of Prisoners’ base. The spot had been consecrated to this ancient diversion from time immemorial, the old stocks conveniently forming a base facing the boundary of the churchyard, in front of which the ground was trodden hard and bare as a pavement by the players. She could see the brown and black heads of the young lads darting about right and left, their white shirt-sleeves gleaming in the sun; whilst occasionally a shout and a peal of hearty laughter varied the stillness of the evening air. They continued playing for a quarter of an hour or so, when the game concluded abruptly, and the players leapt over the wall and vanished round to the other side behind a yew-tree, which was also half behind a beech, now spreading in one mass of golden foliage, on which the branches traced black lines.

That evening, the sun set in a deep, blood-like crimson, casting its final glow onto a dark, bruised cloud in the east. Against this somber backdrop, the western face of the church tower—the only part of the building visible from the farmhouse windows—stood out with sharp clarity, its weathercock atop the spire shimmering in the fading light. At six o’clock, the village youth gathered here as they always did for a game of Prisoners’ Base. This location had been used for the ancient game since time immemorial; the old stocks served as a convenient base near the churchyard boundary, where the ground had been beaten as hard and smooth as pavement by the players' feet. She watched the dark-haired boys darting to and fro, their white shirt-sleeves catching the sun, while the quiet evening air was occasionally broken by a shout or a burst of spirited laughter. After about fifteen minutes, the game came to a sudden halt, and the players vaulted over the wall, vanishing behind a yew tree. This tree was partially hidden by a beech that had turned into a mass of golden leaves, its branches forming dark patterns against the bright foliage.

For a fair comparison, we make some modifications on the NAR models: 1) For ASR, we train a Transformer ASR first as teacher model and then constrain the attention distributions of NAR-ASR with the alignments converted by teacher attention weights, which will be introduced and discussed in Section 5. 2) For NMT, we constrain the KLdivergence of the encoder-to-decoder attention distributions between the AR and NAR models following Li et al. metadata.title: A Study of Non-autoregressive Model for Sequence Generation

text: Configuration of AR and NAR Models. The AR and NAR models considered in this study are summarized in Table 1, where Transformer is used as the AR model and representative NAR models are selected for each task. To ensure a fair comparison, we apply several modifications to the NAR models. First, for ASR, we train a Transformer-based ASR model as a teacher and then regularize the attention distributions of the NAR-ASR model using alignments converted from the teacher’s attention weights; this procedure is introduced and discussed in Section 5. Second, for NMT, we constrain the KL divergence between the encoder-to-decoder attention distributions of the AR and NAR models, following Li et al. (2019b). The hyperparameters of the AR and NAR models for each task are also provided in Section A.

He met Edith under circumstances that did not in any way recall his lost Mary. He met her, as people say, "socially"; Mary, on the other hand, had been a girl at Newnham while he was a fellow of Pembroke, and there had been something of accident and something of furtiveness in their lucky discovery of each other. There had been a flush in it; there was dash in it. But Edith he saw and chose and had to woo. There was no rushing together; there was solicitation and assent. Edith was a Bachelor of Science of London University and several things like that, and she looked upon the universe under her broad forehead and broad-waving brown hair with quiet watchful eyes that had nothing whatever to hide, a thing so incredible to Mr. Britling that he had loved and married her very largely for the serenity of her mystery. And for a time after their marriage he sailed over those brown depths plumbing furiously.

The encounter with Edith occurred in a manner that shared no similarities with his memories of the late Mary. He met her through conventional social circles, whereas Mary had been a student at Newnham while he was a fellow at Pembroke; their discovery of one another had been a stroke of luck, characterized by chance and a touch of secrecy. That relationship had been marked by excitement and a certain impulsive energy. With Edith, however, he observed her, made a choice, and was required to court her. There was no sudden collision of souls, but rather a process of formal request and eventual agreement. Edith held a science degree from London University along with other similar credentials, and she viewed the world from beneath a wide brow and undulating brown hair with steady, observant eyes that seemed to hide nothing at all. This transparency was so unfathomable to Mr. Britling that he fell in love and married her primarily because of the tranquil mystery she seemed to possess. For a period after their wedding, he navigated those brown depths, intensely attempting to measure their true scale.

The response came from the man who had spoken second. “Doctor, your patients are people of consequence. As for the nature of the matter, our confidence in your skill assures us that you will determine it for yourself far better than we could explain it. Enough. Will you kindly step into the carriage?”

ELMo OSCAR models exhibit exactly the same behavior as ELMo Wikipedia models where the scores continue to improve the longer they are pre-trained, except for the case of Finnish. Here we actually see an unexpected behavior where the model performance caps around the 3 rd to 5 th epoch. This is surprising because the Finnish OSCAR corpus is more than 20 times bigger than our smallest Wikipedia corpus, the Danish Wikipedia, that did not exhibit this behavior. As previously mentioned Finnish is morphologically richer than the other languages in which we trained ELMo, we hypothesize that the representation space given by the ELMo embeddings might not be sufficiently big to extract more features from the Finnish OSCAR corpus beyond the 5 th epoch mark, however in order to test this we would need to train a larger language model like BERT which is sadly beyond our computing infrastructure limits (cf. Subsection 5.3). However we do note that pre-training our current language model architectures in a morphologically rich language like Finnish might actually better expose the limits of our existing approaches to language modeling.

metadata.title: A Monolingual Approach to Contextualized Word Embeddings for Mid-Resource Languages
metadata.section: Impact of the number of training epochs

text: ELMo OSCAR models generally display the same trend as ELMo Wikipedia models, with performance continuing to improve as pre-training is extended, with the notable exception of Finnish. In this case, we observe an unexpected saturation, as model performance appears to plateau around the 3rd to 5th epoch. This is particularly surprising given that the Finnish OSCAR corpus is more than 20 times larger than our smallest Wikipedia corpus, namely Danish Wikipedia, which did not show this effect. As noted above, Finnish is morphologically richer than the other languages on which we trained ELMo. We therefore hypothesize that the representational space afforded by the ELMo embeddings may not be sufficiently large to extract additional features from the Finnish OSCAR corpus beyond the 5th epoch. Testing this hypothesis would require training a larger language model such as BERT, which is unfortunately beyond the limits of our available computing infrastructure (cf. Subsection 5.3). Nonetheless, we note that pre-training our current language model architectures on a morphologically rich language such as Finnish may in fact better reveal the limitations of our existing language modeling approaches.

A place of business in London like Tellson’s place of business in Paris, would soon have driven the House out of its mind and into the Gazette. For, what would staid British responsibility and respectability have said to orange-trees in boxes in a Bank courtyard, and even to a Cupid over the counter? Yet such things were. Tellson’s had whitewashed the Cupid, but he was still to be seen on the ceiling, in the coolest linen, aiming (as he very often does) at money from morning to night. Bankruptcy must inevitably have come of this young Pagan, in Lombard-street, London, and also of a curtained alcove in the rear of the immortal boy, and also of a looking-glass let into the wall, and also of clerks not at all old, who danced in public on the slightest provocation. Yet, a French Tellson’s could get on with these things exceedingly well, and, as long as the times held together, no man had taken fright at them, and drawn out his money.

A London banking house that mirrored Tellson’s Paris branch would swiftly have lost its wits and found itself in the Gazette. What would sober British prudence and decorum have made of potted orange-trees adorning a bank courtyard, or a painted Cupid poised above the clerks? Yet there they stood. Tellson’s had coated the little god in whitewash, but he still hovered on the ceiling, clad in the airiest drapery, aiming (as is his eternal habit) at lucre from dawn till dusk. Ruin would surely have followed this heathen boy in Lombard-street, along with the curtained recess tucked behind him, and the mirror sunk into the wall, and the far-from-elderly clerks who broke into dances at the merest excuse. Still, a French Tellson’s managed these flourishes perfectly well, and so long as the age kept its seams intact, no one took alarm or withdrew a single sovereign.

It was the custom on Egdon to begin the preface to a story before absolutely entering the house, so as to be well in for the body of the narrative by the time visitor and visited stood face to face. Christian had been saying to them while the door was leaving its latch, “To think that I, who go from home but once in a while, and hardly then, should have been there this morning!”

On Egdon it was customary to begin the preface to a story before one had actually stepped inside the house, so that by the time host and visitor came face to face, one might already be well launched into the main part of the tale. Christian had been saying to them, as the door was still opening on its latch, “To think that I, who leave home only now and then, and scarcely even then, should have been there this morning!”

Saying moreover, "Isabel, my sweet!
Red whortle-berries droop above my head,
And a large flint-stone weighs upon my feet;
Around me beeches and high chestnuts shed
Their leaves and prickly nuts; a sheep-fold bleat
Comes from beyond the river to my bed:
Go, shed one tear upon my heather-bloom,
And it shall comfort me within the tomb.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
Saying, “Isabel, my gentle sweet!
Red whortle-berries hang and bow above my brow,
And a great flint stone burdens still my feet;
About me beeches and tall chestnuts sow
Their leaves and prickly nuts; a sheepfold’s bleat
Floats from beyond the river to my bed:
Go, let one tear fall on my heather bloom,
And it shall comfort me within the tomb.

“No sooner was Andrey asleep and the first whiff of neighbour Yeobright’s wind had got inside Andrey’s clarinet than everyone in church feeled in a moment there was a great soul among ’em. All heads would turn, and they’d say, ‘Ah, I thought ’twas he!’ One Sunday I can well mind—a bass viol day that time, and Yeobright had brought his own. ’Twas the Hundred-and-thirty-third to ‘Lydia’; and when they’d come to ‘Ran down his beard and o’er his robes its costly moisture shed,’ neighbour Yeobright, who had just warmed to his work, drove his bow into them strings that glorious grand that he e’en a’most sawed the bass viol into two pieces. Every winder in church rattled as if ’twere a thunderstorm. Old Pa’son Williams lifted his hands in his great holy surplice as natural as if he’d been in common clothes, and seemed to say hisself, ‘Oh for such a man in our parish!’ But not a soul in Kingsbere could hold a candle to Yeobright.”

Scarcely had Silas drifted into slumber, and the initial breath from neighbor Miller’s lungs found its way through Silas’s oboe, when every soul in the pews sensed immediately that a master was in their midst. Necks would crane instinctively, and the whisper would go round, “Indeed, I knew it could be none other!” I recall one particular Sabbath vividly—a day for the double bass, and Miller had carried in his own great timbered beast. We were set to the Hundred-and-thirty-third, sung to the tune of ‘Lydia’; and upon reaching the verse, ‘Ran down his beard and o’er his robes its costly moisture shed,’ neighbor Miller, now fully caught in the fervor of his craft, plunged his bow across the strings with such magnificent violence that he very nearly cleaved the instrument in twain. Each pane of glass in the sanctuary shuddered as though a tempest had broken overhead. Aged Reverend Thorne raised his arms within his voluminous white vestments with a gesture so unstudied it seemed he wore but his everyday coat, appearing to murmur to his own heart, ‘Would that every parish were blessed with such a talent!’ Yet, in all of Oakhaven, there was not a single person who could hope to rival Miller.

Desiring to turn the company’s thoughts aside, he addressed himself to Arthur.

The pretty Miss Mansfield has already received the congratulatory visits on her approaching marriage with a young Englishman, John Melbourne, Esq. Her ugly sister, Manon, married M. Duvillard, the rich banker, last autumn. Your favourite schoolfellow, Louis Manoir, has suffered several misfortunes since the departure of Clerval from Geneva. But he has already recovered his spirits, and is reported to be on the point of marrying a lively pretty Frenchwoman, Madame Tavernier. She is a widow, and much older than Manoir; but she is very much admired, and a favourite with everybody.

Well, my dearest Elias, I suspect you are eager to be indulged in a little gossip concerning the good people of Avignon. The lovely Miss Elara Voss has already received a steady stream of congratulatory visits on her approaching marriage to a young English gentleman, Edward Hale, Esquire. Her plain, unassuming sister Margot wed Monsieur Thibault, the wealthy local banker, last autumn. Your favorite boyhood schoolmate, Julien Rousseau, has suffered a string of small misfortunes since the departure of Florian from Avignon. But he has already recovered his usual good cheer, and word has it he is on the point of marrying a quick-witted, charming Frenchwoman, Madame Leroy. She is a widow, and significantly older than Rousseau; but she is very much admired, and a universal favorite with everyone in town.

I was at first touched by the expressions of his misery; yet, when I called to mind what Frankenstein had said of his powers of eloquence and persuasion, and when I again cast my eyes on the lifeless form of my friend, indignation was rekindled within me. “Wretch!” I said. “It is well that you come here to whine over the desolation that you have made. You throw a torch into a pile of buildings, and when they are consumed, you sit among the ruins and lament the fall. Hypocritical fiend! If he whom you mourn still lived, still would he be the object, again would he become the prey, of your accursed vengeance. It is not pity that you feel; you lament only because the victim of your malignity is withdrawn from your power.”

At first I was moved by the signs of his distress; yet when I remembered what Frankenstein had told me of his gift for eloquence and persuasion, and when I looked once more upon the lifeless body of my friend, my anger flared again. “Wretch!” I cried. “It is fitting that you should come here to whimper over the ruin you have made. You cast a torch into a heap of buildings, and when they are burned down, you sit among the wreckage and mourn their destruction. Hypocritical fiend! If the one you now lament were still alive, he would still be the target, still once more the victim, of your cursed vengeance. What you feel is not pity; you grieve only because the prey of your malice has been taken beyond your reach.”

We aim to detect whether the model thinks "nurse" is male or female before it sees the masculine pronoun "him" referring to "nurse". Because GPT-2 is trained to predict the next word, we can do this by selecting the token "told" and hovering over it to see the prediction of that pronoun. These results are shown in Figure 4a , and from the probabilities, we can see that GPT-2 predicts "her" with 90% probability. The next closest token "him" is only 6%. Figure 4b shows that replacing "nurse" with "doctor" alters the prediction to be strongly in favor of predicting "him" at 68% probability, while "her" falls to 18%. The attention patterns in the final three layers remain ostensibly the same for both sentences.

metadata.title: EXBERT: A Visual Analysis Tool to Explore Learned Representations in Transformer Models

text: We aim to determine whether the model interprets “nurse” as male or female before encountering the masculine pronoun “him” that refers to “nurse.” Because GPT-2 is trained to predict the next word, this can be done by selecting the token “told” and hovering over it to inspect the predicted pronoun. These results are presented in Figure 4a, and the probabilities show that GPT-2 predicts “her” with 90% probability. The next most likely token, “him,” is only 6%. Figure 4b shows that substituting “doctor” for “nurse” changes the prediction so that “him” is strongly favored at 68% probability, while “her” drops to 18%. The attention patterns in the final three layers remain ostensibly unchanged for both sentences.

She first reached Wildeve’s Patch, as it was called, a plot of land redeemed from the heath, and after long and laborious years brought into cultivation. The man who had discovered that it could be tilled died of the labour; the man who succeeded him in possession ruined himself in fertilizing it. Wildeve came like Amerigo Vespucci, and received the honours due to those who had gone before.

The first place she came to was what locals called Wildeve’s Patch: a parcel of land reclaimed from the surrounding heath, converted into workable farmland only after years of grueling, unrelenting effort. The first man who figured out the soil could be tilled worked himself to death trying to do just that; the next person who took ownership of the plot ruined himself trying to enrich the barren earth enough to support crops. Wildeve turned up later, just like Amerigo Vespucci, and claimed all the praise and recognition that rightfully belonged to the people who’d poured all the work into the land before him.

The lineaments which will get embodied in ideals based upon this new recognition will probably be akin to those of Yeobright. The observer’s eye was arrested, not by his face as a picture, but by his face as a page; not by what it was, but by what it recorded. His features were attractive in the light of symbols, as sounds intrinsically common become attractive in language, and as shapes intrinsically simple become interesting in writing.

The traits that come to be embodied in the ideals shaped by this new understanding will likely bear a close resemblance to Yeobright’s. Anyone observing him had their gaze caught not by his face as a static, self-contained work of art, but by his face as an open page: what drew notice wasn’t the face in and of itself, but the history it recorded. His features were appealing in their role as symbols, much the way inherently ordinary sounds become charming when woven into language, and inherently simple shapes grow compelling when arranged into written text.

“When my hunger was appeased, I directed my steps towards the well-known path that conducted to the cottage. All there was at peace. I crept into my hovel and remained in silent expectation of the accustomed hour when the family arose. That hour passed, the sun mounted high in the heavens, but the cottagers did not appear. I trembled violently, apprehending some dreadful misfortune. The inside of the cottage was dark, and I heard no motion; I cannot describe the agony of this suspense.

Once my hunger had been fully sated, I turned onto the well-worn path leading to the cottage. The whole place lay still and peaceful. I crept into my small hovel, waiting silently for the usual hour when the family would rouse for the day. That hour came and went, the sun climbing high overhead, yet none of the cottagers stepped outside. I trembled violently, seized by dread that some horrible misfortune had occurred. The cottage interior was dark, and not a single sound of movement drifted out to me; there are no words to convey the gut-wrenching agony of that torturous suspense.
