Be positive scientists, if you please; but ontology has no place in positive science, so leave it alone. Spencer is right in his agnosticism, but if Spencer—”

“You can’t put together a coherent refutation of Berkeley, even if you’re convinced you’ve completely dismantled Kant’s entire body of work. All the same, you automatically take Berkeley for granted as wrong when you insist science definitively proves God doesn’t exist, or, just as relevantly, that matter has an objective, independent existence. You know full well I only conceded the reality of matter in the first place to make my arguments accessible to your frame of reference. Stick to empirical, hard science if that’s the lane you want to stay in, but ontology has zero place in positive scientific inquiry, so quit trying to drag it in. Spencer’s agnostic position is entirely correct, but if Spencer—”

One week and another week wore on, and nothing seemed to lighten the gloom of the young couple. Dreadful imaginings occurred to Eustacia, but she carefully refrained from uttering them to her husband. Suppose he should become blind, or, at all events, never recover sufficient strength of sight to engage in an occupation which would be congenial to her feelings, and conduce to her removal from this lonely dwelling among the hills? That dream of beautiful Paris was not likely to cohere into substance in the presence of this misfortune. As day after day passed by, and he got no better, her mind ran more and more in this mournful groove, and she would go away from him into the garden and weep despairing tears.

Week after week dragged past, and no glimmer of relief cut through the heavy shadow hanging over the young married pair. Elowen was haunted by terrible, unspoken what-ifs, which she tucked tight behind her soft smiles whenever she spoke to Thorne. What if he lost his sight entirely? Or, even if he regained some vision, what if it never grew strong enough for him to take up the kind of work that would align with the life she longed for, and let them leave this isolated cottage tucked between the windswept hills? That soft, vivid dream of glittering Florence, the one they had whispered about over late-night fires, felt thinner than smoke now, unlikely to ever solidify into real life under the weight of this disaster. As each sun rose and set without any sign of his condition improving, her thoughts circled tighter and tighter around that same bitter groove. She would slip away from his side when he napped, out into the overgrown garden, and let silent, despairing sobs shake her shoulders where he could not see.

Under the over-swinging lamps--swinging ever brighter in the better streets, and ever dimmer in the worse--and by lighted shops, gay crowds, illuminated coffee-houses, and theatre-doors, to one of the city gates. Soldiers with lanterns, at the guard-house there. “Your papers, travellers!” “See here then, Monsieur the Officer,” said Defarge, getting down, and taking him gravely apart, “these are the papers of monsieur inside, with the white head. They were consigned to me, with him, at the--” He dropped his voice, there was a flutter among the military lanterns, and one of them being handed into the coach by an arm in uniform, the eyes connected with the arm looked, not an every day or an every night look, at monsieur with the white head. “It is well. Forward!” from the uniform. “Adieu!” from Defarge. And so, under a short grove of feebler and feebler over-swinging lamps, out under the great grove of stars.

Beneath the swaying streetlamps—glowing ever more brilliant in the finer quarters, and ever more feeble in the poorer—past bright shopwindows, lively throngs, lit-up cafés, and theatre entrances, they rolled on toward one of the city gates. At the guardhouse, soldiers stood holding lanterns. “Your documents, travellers!” “Here, if you please, Monsieur the Officer,” said Defarge, climbing down and drawing him soberly aside. “These belong to the gentleman inside, the one with the white hair. He was entrusted to me, along with the gentleman himself, at the—” He lowered his voice; the military lanterns bobbed and flickered, and one was passed into the carriage by a uniformed arm. The eyes above that arm fixed on the white-haired gentleman with a look that was no ordinary glance, by day or night. “All is in order. Proceed!” came from the uniform. “Farewell!” from Defarge. And so, beneath a brief avenue of ever-weakening, swaying lamps, they emerged into the vast avenue of stars.

After hailing the morn with this second salutation, he threw a boot at the woman as a third. It was a very muddy boot, and may introduce the odd circumstance connected with Mr. Cruncher’s domestic economy, that, whereas he often came home after banking hours with clean boots, he often got up next morning to find the same boots covered with clay.

After barking his second gruff salutation to the pale dawn, he hurled a work boot at the woman as a coarse third. It was caked thick with slimy mud, and this detail introduces the odd quirk tied to Mr. Tobin’s domestic arrangements: though he nearly always returned home after his warehouse closing shifts with clean, scuff-free boots, he would just as regularly wake the following morning to find the exact same pairs coated all over with heavy, wet clay.

Soon he was up again, and away,—not far into the country, but back towards London by the high-road—then back again—then over another part of the same ground as he already traversed—then wandering up and down in fields, and lying on ditches’ brinks to rest, and starting up to make for some other spot, and do the same, and ramble on again.

Before long he was on his feet and moving once more, heading not deep into the rural landscape but back toward London along the highway. He doubled back again, crossing over the very ground he had just walked, then drifted through the open fields. He would collapse by the edge of a ditch to rest, only to bolt upright and strike out for another spot, repeating the cycle as he wandered on.

And then she always dresses entirely in white, and that does get monotonous. But Gen. Pinkney is the dearest old man! I wish you could know him, Joe. He comes in sometimes when I am with Clementina at the piano—he is a widower, you know—and stands there pulling his white goatee. ‘And how are the semiquavers and the demisemiquavers progressing?’ he always asks.

“Every now and then,” she said, with a soft note of weariness lacing her tone, “Clementina really tries my patience. I’m afraid she never puts in nearly enough practice, so I end up going over the exact same corrections with her time and time again. What’s more, she wears nothing but head-to-toe white, which does get dreadfully monotonous after a while. But General Pinkney is the dearest old soul! I so wish you could know him, Joe. He stops by sometimes when I’m sitting with Clementina at the piano—he’s a widower, you know—and stands there plucking at his neat white goatee. ‘And how are the semiquavers and demisemiquavers coming along?’ he always asks.”

jiant supports over 50 tasks. Task types include classification, regression, sequence generation, tagging, masked language modeling, and span prediction. jiant focuses on NLU tasks like MNLI (Williams et al., 2018) , CommonsenseQA (Talmor et al., 2019), the Winograd Schema Challenge (Levesque et al., 2012) , and SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016) . A full inventory of tasks and task variants is available in the jiant/tasks module. jiant provides support for cutting-edge sentence encoder models, including support for Huggingface's Transformers. Supported models include: ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) , GPT (Radford, 2018) , BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) , XLM (Conneau and Lample, 2019), GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019) , XLNet , RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019b) , and ALBERT (Lan et al., 2019) . jiant also supports the from-scratch training of (bidirectional) LSTMs (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997) and deep bag of words models (Iyyer et al., 2015) , as well as syntax-aware models such as PRPN (Shen et al., 2018) and ON-LSTM (Shen et al., 2019) . jiant also supports word embeddings such as GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014) .

metadata.title: jiant: A Software Toolkit for Research on General-Purpose Text Understanding Models
metadata.section: Task and Model resources in jiant

text: jiant supports more than 50 tasks. These task types include classification, regression, sequence generation, tagging, masked language modeling, and span prediction. jiant places particular emphasis on NLU tasks such as MNLI (Williams et al., 2018), CommonsenseQA (Talmor et al., 2019), the Winograd Schema Challenge (Levesque et al., 2012), and SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016). A complete list of tasks and task variants is available in the jiant/tasks module. jiant provides support for state-of-the-art sentence encoder models, including integration with Hugging Face’s Transformers library. Supported models include ELMo (Peters et al., 2018), GPT (Radford, 2018), BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM (Conneau and Lample, 2019), GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), XLNet, RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019b), and ALBERT (Lan et al., 2019). jiant also supports training from scratch of (bidirectional) LSTMs (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997) and deep bag-of-words models (Iyyer et al., 2015), as well as syntax-aware models such as PRPN (Shen et al., 2018) and ON-LSTM (Shen et al., 2019). In addition, jiant supports word embeddings such as GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014).

In the next morning’s paper Martin learned a great deal more about himself that was new to him. “We are the sworn enemies of society,” he found himself quoted as saying in a column interview. “No, we are not anarchists but socialists.” When the reporter pointed out to him that there seemed little difference between the two schools, Martin had shrugged his shoulders in silent affirmation. His face was described as bilaterally asymmetrical, and various other signs of degeneration were described. Especially notable were his thuglike hands and the fiery gleams in his blood-shot eyes.

By the next morning’s edition, Julian was introduced to a version of himself he had never met. “We are the avowed adversaries of the commonweal,” the column quoted him as asserting during a brief interrogation. “Make no mistake: we are not insurrectionists, but communalists.” When the correspondent suggested that the two ideologies were essentially the same, Julian was depicted as having offered a mute, shrugging concession. The text went on to map the supposed irregularities of his skull, citing them as evidence of a primitive character. His fingers were described as those of a common ruffian, and much was made of the predatory, blood-rimmed glare that supposedly defined his expression.

After lunch Mr. Britling became more cheerful about his driving. The road from Market Saffron to Blandish, whence one turns off to Matching's Easy, is the London and Norwich high road; it is an old Roman Stane Street and very straightforward and honest in its stretches. You can see the cross roads half a mile away, and the low hedges give you no chance of a surprise. Everybody is cheered by such a road, and everybody drives more confidently and quickly, and Mr. Britling particularly was heartened by it and gradually let out Gladys from the almost excessive restriction that had hitherto marked the day. "On a road like this nothing can happen," said Mr. Britling.

After their midday meal, Mr. Britling grew more sanguine at the wheel. The highway running from Market Saffron toward Blandish, where one veers for Matching's Easy, follows the old London and Norwich route—an ancient Roman track, forthright and guileless in its long reaches. The crossroads announce themselves half a mile distant, and the clipped hedgerows afford no hiding place for sudden mishaps. Such a road lifts every driver's spirits, coaxing a bolder, swifter hand, and Mr. Britling in particular took courage from it, easing Gladys little by little from the almost stifling restraint that had defined the morning. "Nothing can go amiss on a road like this," he declared.

“I agree with you,” replied the stranger; “we are unfashioned creatures, but half made up, if one wiser, better, dearer than ourselves—such a friend ought to be—do not lend his aid to perfectionate our weak and faulty natures. I once had a friend, the most noble of human creatures, and am entitled, therefore, to judge respecting friendship. You have hope, and the world before you, and have no cause for despair. But I—I have lost everything and cannot begin life anew.”

“I concur with your sentiment,” the stranger answered. “We are incomplete beings, only partially realized, if someone more insightful, more virtuous, and more beloved than ourselves—the very definition of a true friend—does not offer their guidance to help refine our fragile and flawed characters. I once possessed such a friend, the most honorable of men, and so I feel qualified to speak on the nature of companionship. You are filled with hope and have your whole life ahead of you, with no reason to succumb to misery. But I—I have been stripped of everything and lack the strength to begin again.”

“Well, then, let me have my say. I have no fear of your repeating it. I believe the devil’s work was done last night upon those innocent, wretched people. That old baron got no more than he deserved. If it were left to me, all his kind would meet the same fate.”

In this section, we will elaborate on the system design of PHOTON. 1 We are continuously improving the performance of the neural semantic parser. Currently the semantic parser only accepts standalone question as input. We plan to also model the interaction context in future work. Figure 1 shows the overall workflow of our system. PHOTON is an end-to-end system that takes a user question and database schema as input, and output the query result after executing the generated SQL on the database. PHOTON is a modular framework designed towards practical industrial applications. The core modules in PHOTON are the SQL parser and confusion detection mechanism. The SQL parser parses the input question and database schema, maps them into executable SQL query via an encoder-decoder framework. The confusion detection module identifies the untranslatable questions and captures the confusing span of the untranslatable question. The confusing tokens together with the context are fed into the autocorrection module to make a prediction of user attempted question.

metadata.title: PHOTON: A Robust Cross-Domain Text-to-SQL System

text: This section details the architectural design of PHOTON. While we are iteratively refining the neural semantic parser's performance, the current implementation focuses on processing standalone queries, with the integration of interaction context reserved for future work. The comprehensive system workflow is illustrated in Figure 1. PHOTON is an end-to-end framework that accepts a natural language question and a database schema as input, ultimately returning query results by executing synthesized SQL code. Designed for practical industrial applications, the system employs a modular architecture centered on two primary components: a neural SQL parser and a confusion detection mechanism. The SQL parser leverages an encoder-decoder framework to map the input question and schema into an executable SQL query. Meanwhile, the confusion detection module identifies untranslatable queries and isolates the specific spans responsible for the failure. These problematic tokens, along with their context, are then passed to an autocorrection module to predict the user's intended question.

My life, as it passed thus, was indeed hateful to me, and it was during sleep alone that I could taste joy. O blessed sleep! Often, when most miserable, I sank to repose, and my dreams lulled me even to rapture. The spirits that guarded me had provided these moments, or rather hours, of happiness that I might retain strength to fulfil my pilgrimage. Deprived of this respite, I should have sunk under my hardships. During the day I was sustained and inspirited by the hope of night, for in sleep I saw my friends, my wife, and my beloved country; again I saw the benevolent countenance of my father, heard the silver tones of my Elizabeth’s voice, and beheld Clerval enjoying health and youth. Often, when wearied by a toilsome march, I persuaded myself that I was dreaming until night should come and that I should then enjoy reality in the arms of my dearest friends. What agonising fondness did I feel for them! How did I cling to their dear forms, as sometimes they haunted even my waking hours, and persuade myself that they still lived! At such moments vengeance, that burned within me, died in my heart, and I pursued my path towards the destruction of the dæmon more as a task enjoined by heaven, as the mechanical impulse of some power of which I was unconscious, than as the ardent desire of my soul.

The life I led during those days was utterly repugnant to me, and only in the sanctuary of sleep could I experience any sense of gladness. O, holy rest! Many times, when my despair was most profound, I would fall into a slumber where my dreams offered a comfort bordering on bliss. It seemed as if protective spirits had arranged these fleeting hours of peace to ensure I possessed the fortitude to finish my grim journey. Had I been denied such respite, I would have certainly collapsed beneath my burdens. During the daylight hours, I was buoyed by the promise of the dark, for in my dreams I walked again with my friends, my wife, and through the landscapes of my native land. I saw once more the gentle face of my father, heard the sweet, clear notes of Elizabeth’s voice, and beheld Clerval in all his youthful vitality. Often, when worn down by a grueling day's march, I told myself that the sunlit world was the illusion and that I would wake to the truth only when night restored me to the arms of those I held dear. What a piercing, desperate love I bore for them! I reached out to their beloved ghosts even when I was awake, convincing myself they had never truly left me. At such times, the vengeance that scorched my heart would grow cold, and I followed the trail of the monster more as a divinely appointed chore—a mechanical impulse of some hidden force—than as the fervent obsession of my own spirit.

Next, we discuss key factors of MSD. We take the E2L as the exemplar for discussion, as we have observed similar results for the opposing direction. Figure 4a shows the performance curves of BLEU and style accuracy. We choose the concept range to ensure they contain similar number of sentences. Along with the increasing number of concepts, we can see a downward BLEU trend. This is because it becomes more difficult to preserve content when the sentence is more professional. As for style accuracy, DeleteAndRetrieve achieves the peak around [8, 12) concepts, while the performance of other models drops gradually. Clearly, a lower number of concepts benefit the model for better understanding the sentences due to their correlated semantics, but a larger number of concepts requires knowledgeaware text understanding. Figure 4b presents the performance curves regarding the structure differences, where the edit distance is computed as mentioned in Section 3.2. Higher score denotes more heterogeneous structures. We see a similar trend with the curves of concepts. That is, existing models perform well in simple cases (fewer concepts and less structural differences), but becomes worse if the language is complex. We doubt that the encoder in each model is able to understand the domain-specific language sufficient well without considering knowledge. We thus propose a simple variant of ControlledGen by introducing terminology definitions, and observe some interesting findings in Section 4.10.

metadata.title: Expertise Style Transfer: A New Task Towards Better Communication between Experts and Laymen

text: We now turn to the central factors influencing MSD, using E2L as a representative case given that the reverse direction yields analogous outcomes. Figure 4a plots BLEU and style accuracy as functions of concept count, where the concept intervals are selected to maintain roughly equal sentence volumes. As the number of concepts grows, BLEU exhibits a consistent decline, reflecting the increased difficulty of content preservation when sentences become more specialized. Style accuracy for DeleteAndRetrieve peaks in the [8, 12) concept range, whereas other models show a gradual degradation. Evidently, a smaller concept set aids sentence comprehension through tighter semantic coherence, while a larger set demands knowledge-aware text understanding. Figure 4b displays performance trends relative to structural divergence, with edit distance computed as described in Section 3.2; higher values indicate more heterogeneous structures. The pattern mirrors that of the concept curves: existing models handle simple configurations—few concepts and limited structural disparity—reasonably well, but their performance deteriorates under complex language. We suspect that the encoder in each model struggles to adequately grasp domain-specific language without explicit knowledge integration. Motivated by this, we devise a straightforward variant of ControlledGen that injects terminology definitions, and report intriguing observations in Section 4.10.

From a pedigreed yellow pup I grew up to be an anonymous yellow cur looking like a cross between an Angora cat and a box of lemons. But my mistress never tumbled. She thought that the two primeval pups that Noah chased into the ark were but a collateral branch of my ancestors. It took two policemen to keep her from entering me at the Madison Square Garden for the Siberian bloodhound prize.

I began life as a yellow puppy of distinguished lineage, only to mature into a nameless yellow mongrel resembling something between an Angora cat and a crate of lemons. Yet my mistress never caught on. She believed those two primordial hounds that Noah herded onto the ark were merely a distant offshoot of my family tree. It required two officers to restrain her from entering me in the Madison Square Garden show for the Siberian bloodhound award.

“This is a common case--the enchanting of a thing to one eye and leaving it in its proper form to another. You have heard of it before, Sandy, though you haven't happened to experience it. But no harm is done. In fact, it is lucky the way it is. If these ladies were hogs to everybody and to themselves, it would be necessary to break the enchantment, and that might be impossible if one failed to find out the particular process of the enchantment. And hazardous, too; for in attempting a disenchantment without the true key, you are liable to err, and turn your hogs into dogs, and the dogs into cats, the cats into rats, and so on, and end by reducing your materials to nothing finally, or to an odorless gas which you can't follow--which, of course, amounts to the same thing. But here, by good luck, no one's eyes but mine are under the enchantment, and so it is of no consequence to dissolve it. These ladies remain ladies to you, and to themselves, and to everybody else; and at the same time they will suffer in no way from my delusion, for when I know that an ostensible hog is a lady, that is enough for me, I know how to treat her.”

This is a frequent occurrence—the way a spell might alter something in one man's sight while leaving it in its true form for another. You’ve heard of such things before, Sandy, even if you haven't witnessed it personally. But there is no real damage here. In truth, it’s a stroke of luck. If these ladies looked like swine to themselves and the rest of the world, we would be forced to break the enchantment, which might be impossible without knowing the exact ritual involved. It’s a dangerous business, too; if you try to reverse a spell without the correct formula, you're liable to make a mistake and turn your hogs into dogs, then cats, then rats, until you eventually reduce your subjects to nothing at all, or perhaps a scentless vapor that vanishes—which amounts to the same thing. Fortunately, only my vision is clouded, so there is no reason to undo the magic. These women remain noble ladies to you and everyone else; they won't suffer from my misunderstanding, for once I realize that a supposed pig is actually a lady, that is enough for me to treat her with the proper respect.

This work is also aimed to promote the development of grounded text generation. The exist-ing works focusing on the knowledge grounded text generation (Prabhumoye et al., 2019; Qin et al., 2019; usually assume the knowledge passage is given. However in practice this is not true. We provide the component to retrieve knowledge passage on-the-fly from web or customized document, to allow engineers or researchers test existing or new generation models. Keyphrase constrained generation (Hokamp and Liu, 2017) is another type of grounded generation, broadly speaking. Similarly the keyphrase needs to be provided to apply such constraints. We provide tools to extract constraints from knowledge passage or stylized corpus.

metadata.title: MIXINGBOARD: a Knowledgeable Stylized Integrated Text Generation Platform

text: This work is also intended to advance grounded text generation. Existing approaches to knowledge-grounded text generation (Prabhumoye et al., 2019; Qin et al., 2019) typically assume that the relevant knowledge passage is already available. However, this assumption does not hold in practical settings. To address this gap, we provide a component that retrieves knowledge passages on the fly from the web or from user-specified documents, enabling engineers and researchers to evaluate existing or newly proposed generation models. In a broader sense, keyphrase-constrained generation (Hokamp and Liu, 2017) is another form of grounded generation; likewise, the required keyphrases must be supplied in advance in order to enforce such constraints. To support this setting, we provide tools for extracting constraints from a knowledge passage or from a stylized corpus.

Create test suites Non-technical users can use SyntaxGym's browser-based interface to design and submit their own psycholinguistic test suites (Figure 3) . Separately, the site supports uploading pre-made test suites as a JSON-formatted file. This functionality may be useful for advanced users who prefer to automatically generate test suites. 2 Submit language models Users interested in evaluating their own language models first create a public Docker image conforming to the API specified by the SyntaxGym standard (Section 3.1.1). After users submit these language models on the SyntaxGym site, the models are automatically validated for conformity to the API by the SyntaxGym backend. Valid models are added to the SyntaxGym collection, and will be evaluated on all past and future available test suites in the Gym.

metadata.title: SyntaxGym: An Online Platform for Targeted Evaluation of Language Models

text:
1. Test suite construction
Non-technical users can leverage SyntaxGym’s browser-native interface to design and submit their custom psycholinguistic test suites (Figure 3). In parallel, the platform supports upload of pre-built test suites in JSON file format, a functionality intended for advanced users who prefer to generate test suites through automated workflows.
2. Language model submission
Users wishing to evaluate their own custom language models first produce a public Docker image that conforms to the API defined in the SyntaxGym standard (Section 3.1.1). Following submission of these language models via the SyntaxGym site, the platform’s backend automatically validates that the models meet the required API specifications. Validated models are added to the public SyntaxGym model collection, and will be run against all existing and future test suites available in the repository.

As Carton walked in, took his seat and asked (in very indifferent French) for a small measure of wine, Madame Defarge cast a careless glance at him, and then a keener, and then a keener, and then advanced to him herself, and asked him what it was he had ordered.

As Carton came in, took his seat, and asked—in very indifferent French—for a small measure of wine, Madame Defarge let her eyes wander over him carelessly, then more sharply, then sharper still, and finally came up to him herself to ask what he had ordered.

There was not so great a necessity for hurrying as Mr. Sowerberry had anticipated, however; for when they reached the obscure corner of the churchyard in which the nettles grew, and where the parish graves were made, the clergyman had not arrived; and the clerk, who was sitting by the vestry-room fire, seemed to think it by no means improbable that it might be an hour or so, before he came. So, they put the bier on the brink of the grave; and the two mourners waited patiently in the damp clay, with a cold rain drizzling down, while the ragged boys whom the spectacle had attracted into the churchyard played a noisy game at hide-and-seek among the tombstones, or varied their amusements by jumping backwards and forwards over the coffin. Mr. Sowerberry and Bumble, being personal friends of the clerk, sat by the fire with him, and read the paper.

There was, after all, not quite the need for haste that Mr. Sowerberry had expected; for when they reached that lonely corner of the churchyard where the nettles grew and the parish graves were dug, the clergyman had not yet come. The clerk, who was warming himself by the fire in the vestry-room, seemed to think it quite likely that he might be delayed for another hour or so. Accordingly, they set the bier down at the edge of the grave, and the two mourners waited quietly in the wet clay while a thin cold rain fell steadily. Meanwhile, the ragged boys drawn into the churchyard by the sight of it made a great noise at hide-and-seek among the tombstones, or changed their sport by leaping back and forth over the coffin. Mr. Sowerberry and Bumble, being on friendly terms with the clerk, sat with him by the fire and read the newspaper.

Ikey went behind the prescription desk. There he crushed to a powder two soluble tablets, each containing a quarter of a grain of morphia. To them he added a little sugar of milk to increase the bulk, and folded the mixture neatly in a white paper. Taken by an adult this powder would insure several hours of heavy slumber without danger to the sleeper. This he handed to Chunk McGowan, telling him to administer it in a liquid if possible, and received the hearty thanks of the backyard Lochinvar.

Ikey stepped behind the prescription counter. Once there, he ground two dissolvable tablets—each holding a quarter grain of morphine—into a fine powder. He mixed in a small pinch of milk sugar to bulk the dose up slightly, then folded the combined mixture tidily into a slip of white paper. For a full-grown adult, this powder would guarantee several hours of deep, uninterrupted sleep with no risk of harm to the person taking it. He handed the folded packet to Chunk McGowan, advised him to mix it into a drink if at all possible when giving it out, and got an effusive, heartfelt thank-you from that scrappy backyard Lochinvar.

text: Open-access scientific publications on SOFCs and related research, annotated by domain experts. For manual annotation, we use the InCeption annotation tool (Klie et al., 2018). Table 1 presents the key statistics of our corpus. Sentence segmentation was performed automatically. 4 As preparation for our experiments on the data, we manually removed all sentences belonging to the Acknowledgment and References sections. With her two brothers this fair lady dwelt,
Enriched from ancestral merchandize,
And for them many a weary hand did swelt
In torched mines and noisy factories,
And many once proud-quiver'd loins did melt
In blood from stinging whip;--with hollow eyes
Many all day in dazzling river stood,
To take the rich-ored driftings of the flood.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
With her two brothers dwelt this comely dame,  
Enriched by traffic from the elder line,  
And for their gain did many a weary hand  
Grow gaunt in firelit mines and clanging mills,  
And many a once proud, sinew-straining loins  
Were melted into blood beneath the lash;—  
And many, with hollow eyes, stood all day long  
In the bright river, gleaning golden drift.

The messenger rode back at an easy trot, stopping pretty often at ale-houses by the way to drink, but evincing a tendency to keep his own counsel, and to keep his hat cocked over his eyes. He had eyes that assorted very well with that decoration, being of a surface black, with no depth in the colour or form, and much too near together--as if they were afraid of being found out in something, singly, if they kept too far apart. They had a sinister expression, under an old cocked-hat like a three-cornered spittoon, and over a great muffler for the chin and throat, which descended nearly to the wearer’s knees. When he stopped for drink, he moved this muffler with his left hand, only while he poured his liquor in with his right; as soon as that was done, he muffled again.

The messenger rode back at an easy trot, pausing often at alehouses along the way to drink, yet seeming determined to keep his own counsel and to wear his hat pulled low over his eyes. Those eyes suited that fashion well: black on the surface, with no depth of color or expression, and set much too close together, as if they feared being detected in some misdeed if they kept too far apart. They gave him a sinister look beneath an old cocked hat like a three-cornered spittoon, and above a huge muffler for his chin and throat that fell almost to his knees. Whenever he stopped to drink, he lifted the muffler with his left hand only long enough to pour the liquor in with his right; once he had done so, he drew it up again.

Mr. Bumble walked on with long strides; little Oliver, firmly grasping his gold-laced cuff, trotted beside him, inquiring at the end of every quarter of a mile whether they were “nearly there.” To these interrogations Mr. Bumble returned very brief and snappish replies; for the temporary blandness which gin-and-water awakens in some bosoms had by this time evaporated; and he was once again a beadle.

Master Harrow strode forward in wide, swinging steps; young Pip clung tightly to the braided hem of his sleeve, scurrying to keep pace and asking after every few hundred yards if they had “almost arrived.” To these questions Master Harrow gave only curt and waspish answers, for the fleeting warmth that a draught of brandy kindles in certain hearts had now faded entirely, and he was once more a parish officer.

Services such as Google Scholar and Semantic Scholar cover a wide variety of academic disciplines. Wile there are benefits to this, the lack of focus on NLP literature has some drawbacks as well: e.g, the potential for too many search results that include many irrelevant papers. For example, if one is interested in NLP papers on emotion and privacy, searching for them on Google Scholar is less efficient than searching for them on a platform dedicated to NLP papers. Further, services such as Google Scholar provide minimal interactive visualizations. NLP Scholar with its focus on AA data, is not meant to replace these tools, but act as a complementary tool for dedicated visual search of NLP literature.

metadata.title: NLP Scholar: An Interactive Visual Explorer for Natural Language Processing Literature

text: Services such as Google Scholar and Semantic Scholar cover a wide range of academic disciplines. While this breadth has clear benefits, the lack of a specific focus on NLP literature also has drawbacks; for example, it can produce too many search results, many of which are irrelevant papers. If, for instance, one is interested in NLP papers on emotion and privacy, searching for them on Google Scholar is less efficient than using a platform dedicated to NLP papers. In addition, services such as Google Scholar provide only minimal interactive visualizations. NLP Scholar, with its focus on AA data, is not intended to replace these tools, but rather to serve as a complementary resource for the visual exploration of NLP literature.

"I don't make out he wasn't human. Often and often I have been angry with him, and disappointed in him. There were all sorts of weaknesses in him. We all knew them. And we didn't mind them. We loved him the better. And his odd queer cleverness!.... And his profound wisdom. And then all this beautiful and delicate fabric, all those clear memories in his dear brain, all his whims, his sudden inventions....

I never intended to portray him as anything beyond a mere man. I was frequently provoked by him, and just as often disillusioned. He was possessed of a thousand frailties, all of which were visible to our circle. Yet we did not resent them; rather, they served to anchor our devotion. And that peculiar, erratic brilliance! That bottomless well of sagacity. Then there was the exquisite, fragile architecture of his mind—the vivid recollections held within that beloved head, his fleeting vagaries, and those spontaneous sparks of invention....

He spoke to a postboy who was dozing under the gateway; and who, after hearing what he wanted, referred him to the ostler; who after hearing all he had to say again, referred him to the landlord; who was a tall gentleman in a blue neckcloth, a white hat, drab breeches, and boots with tops to match, leaning against a pump by the stable-door, picking his teeth with a silver toothpick.

He addressed a courier boy nodding off under the entry arch, who, after grasping the gist of his request, sent him to speak to the stable groom; the groom, after listening to his full query a second time, directed him to the inn’s landlord, a lanky gentleman wearing a navy cravat, a crisp white hat, taupe breeches, and boots with tops cut to the same neutral hue, propped against a water pump beside the stable threshold, working a silver toothpick between his teeth.

Well, I couldn't rack the executioner, neither would I overlook the just complaint of the priests. The man must be punished somehow or other, so I degraded him from his office and made him leader of the band--the new one that was to be started. He begged hard, and said he couldn't play--a plausible excuse, but too thin; there wasn't a musician in the country that could.

I could neither subject the executioner to torture nor brush off the priests’ fully justified grievances. The man had to be punished one way or another, so I stripped him of his official post and named him leader of the new musical band we were preparing to form. He begged profusely, insisting he did not know how to play—a plausible enough excuse at first glance, but far too thin to hold water; there was not a single musician in the whole country who could, anyway.

His machine-made storiettes, though he hated them and derided them, were successful. By means of them he redeemed all his pledges, paid most of his bills, and bought a new set of tires for his wheel. The storiettes at least kept the pot a-boiling and gave him time for ambitious work; while the one thing that upheld him was the forty dollars he had received from _The White Mouse_. He anchored his faith to that, and was confident that the really first-class magazines would pay an unknown writer at least an equal rate, if not a better one. But the thing was, how to get into the first-class magazines. His best stories, essays, and poems went begging among them, and yet, each month, he read reams of dull, prosy, inartistic stuff between all their various covers. If only one editor, he sometimes thought, would descend from his high seat of pride to write me one cheering line! No matter if my work is unusual, no matter if it is unfit, for prudential reasons, for their pages, surely there must be some sparks in it, somewhere, a few, to warm them to some sort of appreciation. And thereupon he would get out one or another of his manuscripts, such as “Adventure,” and read it over and over in a vain attempt to vindicate the editorial silence.

The formulaic, churned-out short tales he cranked out may have filled him with loathing and scorn, but they sold reliably enough. With the earnings, he made good on every outstanding promise, settled nearly all his debts, and picked up a fresh set of rubber tires for his scuffed secondhand bicycle. These throwaway stories at least kept food on the table and freed up hours for the serious, ambitious writing he cared about; the single bright spot holding his resolve together was the forty-dollar check he’d gotten from *The Copper Sparrow* a few weeks prior. He pinned all his hope to that small win, certain that top-tier literary journals would pay an untested writer at least that much per word, if not a far higher rate. The problem, of course, was breaking into those elite publications. His finest short fiction, personal essays, and verse circulated through their slush piles unbidden and unwanted, even as he pored through their issues each month and found page after page of flat, lifeless, unoriginal work filling their pages. If just one editor, he often mused, would climb down from their lofty, condescending perch to send him even a single line of encouragement! Even if his work was odd, even if it didn’t fit their editorial guidelines for practical, risk-averse reasons, there had to be a flicker of something worthwhile in it somewhere, enough to spark at least a glimmer of recognition from them. In those moments he’d yank one of his carefully drafted manuscripts off his shelf, *Wanderer’s Fall* among them, and reread it again and again, fruitlessly trying to work out why the editors had left him hanging without a word.

We pass rapidly on into the month of March, to a breezy day without sunshine, frost, or dew. On Yalbury Hill, about midway between Weatherbury and Casterbridge, where the turnpike road passes over the crest, a numerous concourse of people had gathered, the eyes of the greater number being frequently stretched afar in a northerly direction. The groups consisted of a throng of idlers, a party of javelin-men, and two trumpeters, and in the midst were carriages, one of which contained the high sheriff. With the idlers, many of whom had mounted to the top of a cutting formed for the road, were several Weatherbury men and boys—among others Poorgrass, Coggan, and Cain Ball.

Time moved forward quickly into the month of March, to a blustery day that lacked both the warmth of the sun and the crispness of frost or dew. At the summit of Yalbury Hill, halfway between Weatherbury and Casterbridge where the main road crosses the ridge, a large crowd had assembled, their gazes frequently fixed on the northern horizon. This gathering consisted of a local throng, a contingent of javelin-men, and two trumpeters; in their center stood several carriages, one of which held the high sheriff. Among the spectators, many of whom had climbed the steep embankments flanking the road, were several men and boys from Weatherbury—including Poorgrass, Coggan, and Cain Ball.

When the breakfast was cleared away; the merry old gentleman and the two boys played at a very curious and uncommon game, which was performed in this way. The merry old gentleman, placing a snuff-box in one pocket of his trousers, a note-case in the other, and a watch in his waistcoat pocket, with a guard-chain round his neck, and sticking a mock diamond pin in his shirt: buttoned his coat tight round him, and putting his spectacle-case and handkerchief in his pockets, trotted up and down the room with a stick, in imitation of the manner in which old gentlemen walk about the streets any hour in the day. Sometimes he stopped at the fire-place, and sometimes at the door, making believe that he was staring with all his might into shop-windows. At such times, he would look constantly round him, for fear of thieves, and would keep slapping all his pockets in turn, to see that he hadn’t lost anything, in such a very funny and natural manner, that Oliver laughed till the tears ran down his face. All this time, the two boys followed him closely about: getting out of his sight, so nimbly, every time he turned round, that it was impossible to follow their motions. At last, the Dodger trod upon his toes, or ran upon his boot accidently, while Charley Bates stumbled up against him behind; and in that one moment they took from him, with the most extraordinary rapidity, snuff-box, note-case, watch-guard, chain, shirt-pin, pocket-handkerchief, even the spectacle-case. If the old gentleman felt a hand in any one of his pockets, he cried out where it was; and then the game began all over again.

When breakfast had been cleared away, the merry old gentleman and the two boys engaged in a very strange and uncommon game, played in this manner. The merry old gentleman put a snuff-box into one pocket of his trousers, a note-case into the other, and a watch into the pocket of his waistcoat, with a guard-chain about his neck, while he stuck a sham diamond pin into his shirt. Then he buttoned his coat closely round him, and, placing his spectacle-case and handkerchief in his pockets, trotted to and fro across the room with a stick, imitating the way old gentlemen stroll about the streets at any hour of the day. Now and then he halted at the fireplace, and now and then at the door, pretending to peer with all his might into the shop-windows. At such moments he would keep glancing sharply about him, as though afraid of thieves, and would slap each of his pockets in turn to make sure that nothing was missing, all in so comical and natural a fashion that Oliver laughed until the tears came into his eyes. Meanwhile, the two boys kept close behind him, slipping out of his sight so quickly whenever he turned round that it was impossible to trace their movements. At last the Dodger trod on his toes, or perhaps accidentally ran against his boot, while Charley Bates stumbled up against him from behind; and in that single instant they removed from him, with astonishing quickness, the snuff-box, note-case, watch-guard, chain, shirt-pin, pocket-handkerchief, and even the spectacle-case. If the old gentleman felt a hand in any of his pockets, he called out which one it was; and then the game began again.

As it grew later the fire was made up in the large long hall into which the staircase descended, and all encumbrances were cleared out for dancing. The log which was to form the back-brand of the evening fire was the uncleft trunk of a tree, so unwieldy that it could be neither brought nor rolled to its place; and accordingly two men were to be observed dragging and heaving it in by chains and levers as the hour of assembly drew near.

As the shadows lengthened, the hearth was built up within the vast, low-beamed parlor where the main stairs terminated, and the floor was stripped of its furnishings to accommodate the feet of the dancers. The centerpiece of the night’s fire—a gargantuan, unsplit length of timber—proved so burdensome that no man could lift or roll it; consequently, as the guests’ arrival neared, a pair of workers could be seen laboring to drag it into position, plying levers and rattling chains to force the great weight home.

About this time we retired to our house at Belrive. This change was particularly agreeable to me. The shutting of the gates regularly at ten o’clock and the impossibility of remaining on the lake after that hour had rendered our residence within the walls of Geneva very irksome to me. I was now free. Often, after the rest of the family had retired for the night, I took the boat and passed many hours upon the water. Sometimes, with my sails set, I was carried by the wind; and sometimes, after rowing into the middle of the lake, I left the boat to pursue its own course and gave way to my own miserable reflections. I was often tempted, when all was at peace around me, and I the only unquiet thing that wandered restless in a scene so beautiful and heavenly—if I except some bat, or the frogs, whose harsh and interrupted croaking was heard only when I approached the shore—often, I say, I was tempted to plunge into the silent lake, that the waters might close over me and my calamities for ever. But I was restrained, when I thought of the heroic and suffering Elizabeth, whom I tenderly loved, and whose existence was bound up in mine. I thought also of my father and surviving brother; should I by my base desertion leave them exposed and unprotected to the malice of the fiend whom I had let loose among them?

It was during this season that we sought refuge at our villa in Clairmont. This transition brought a profound relief to my spirit; the rigid locking of the town gates at the stroke of ten and the prohibition against lingering upon the waves past twilight had made our urban dwelling feel like a stifling cage. Now, liberty was mine. Frequently, once my kin had surrendered to sleep, I would unmoor the skiff and spend the dark watches upon the great expanse of the water. On certain nights, I let the canvas catch the breeze to carry me where it would; on others, having pulled the oars until I reached the center of the lake, I allowed the vessel to drift aimlessly while I surrendered to the agony of my own dark reflections. 

Amidst the profound stillness of the world, I felt myself the sole discordant note in an otherwise celestial landscape—save perhaps for the erratic flight of a bat or the coarse, rhythmic pulse of the marsh-dwellers that fell silent as I neared the bank. In those moments, I was frequently seized by the urge to cast myself into the obsidian depths, seeking a final shroud beneath the waves to bury my griefs and my crimes eternally. Yet, the memory of the noble and enduring Isabella stayed my hand; I loved her with a desperate devotion, and her very life was intertwined with my own. I considered, too, my father and my remaining brother. Could I, through a coward's exit, abandon them to the cruel whims of the demon I had unleashed upon their world?

The House approached Mr. Lorry, and laying a soiled and unopened letter before him, asked if he had yet discovered any traces of the person to whom it was addressed? The House laid the letter down so close to Darnay that he saw the direction--the more quickly because it was his own right name. The address, turned into English, ran:

In crossing the bridge, she dropped the door key in the river. Arriving at the cathedral some few minutes before her escort, and waiting there, she thought, what if the key were already taken in a net, what if it were identified, what if the door were opened and the remains discovered, what if she were stopped at the gate, sent to prison, and charged with murder! In the midst of these fluttering thoughts, the escort appeared, took her in, and took her away.

As she crossed the bridge, she let the door key slip into the river. Reaching the cathedral a few minutes ahead of her escort, and waiting there, she thought: what if the key had already been caught in a net, what if someone recognized it, what if the door were opened and the remains found, what if she were arrested at the gate, carried off to prison, and accused of murder? While these restless thoughts kept fluttering through her mind, the escort arrived, received her, and led her away.

This formulation provides several benefits. First, since the data likelihood starts at the triple level, optimizing this likelihood corresponds to directly optimizing the final evaluation criteria at the triple level. Second, by making no assumption on how multiple triples may share entities in a sentence, it handles the overlapping triple problem by design. Third, the decomposition in Eq. (3) inspires a novel tagging scheme for triple extraction: we learn a subject tagger p(s|x j ) that recognizes subject entities in a sentence; and for each relation r, we learn an object tagger p r (o|s, x j ) that recognizes relationspecific objects for a given subject. In this way we can model each relation as a function that maps subjects to objects, as opposed to classifying relations for (subject, object) pairs.

metadata.title: A Novel Cascade Binary Tagging Framework for Relational Triple Extraction

text: This formulation offers several advantages. First, because the data likelihood begins at the triple level, maximizing this likelihood is equivalent to directly optimizing the final evaluation objective at the triple level. Second, by making no assumption about how multiple triples may share entities within a sentence, it addresses the overlapping triple problem by construction. Third, the decomposition in Eq. (3) motivates a new tagging scheme for triple extraction: we learn a subject tagger p(s|x j ) that identifies subject entities in a sentence, and for each relation r, we learn an object tagger p r (o|s, x j ) that identifies relation-specific objects for a given subject. In this way, each relation can be modeled as a function mapping subjects to objects, rather than as a classifier over (subject, object) pairs.

“You don’t know the social mill, dad. She’s part of the stream that turns it. Every hour and minute of her time is arranged for days in advance. I must have that girl, dad, or this town is a blackjack swamp forevermore. And I can’t write it—I can’t do that.”

“You don’t understand the social machine, father. She belongs to the current that keeps it moving. Every hour and every minute of her life is booked days ahead. I’ve got to have that girl, father, or this town will be nothing but a blackjack swamp from now on. And I can’t write it—I can’t do that.”

“This is the room,” said the housekeeper, from her furry throat. “It’s a nice room. It ain’t often vacant. I had some most elegant people in it last summer—no trouble at all, and paid in advance to the minute. The water’s at the end of the hall. Sprowls and Mooney kept it three months. They done a vaudeville sketch. Miss B’retta Sprowls—you may have heard of her—Oh, that was just the stage names—right there over the dresser is where the marriage certificate hung, framed. The gas is here, and you see there is plenty of closet room. It’s a room everybody likes. It never stays idle long.”

“This is the room,” the housekeeper announced, her voice burred deep in her throat. “A lovely room. Doesn’t come free often. Last summer I had some truly refined guests—no trouble whatsoever, and their payment arrived right on the dot. The water closet’s down the hall. Sprowls and Mooney had it for three months. They performed a vaudeville act. Miss B’retta Sprowls—the name might ring a bell—well, those were just their stage names—right there above the dresser is where the marriage certificate used to hang, all framed. The gas jet’s here, and you can see there’s ample closet space. It’s a room that charms everyone. It never stays empty long.”

My missionaries were taught to spell out the gilt signs on their tabards--the showy gilding was a neat idea, I could have got the king to wear a bulletin-board for the sake of that barbaric splendor--they were to spell out these signs and then explain to the lords and ladies what soap was; and if the lords and ladies were afraid of it, get them to try it on a dog. The missionary's next move was to get the family together and try it on himself; he was to stop at no experiment, however desperate, that could convince the nobility that soap was harmless; if any final doubt remained, he must catch a hermit--the woods were full of them; saints they called themselves, and saints they were believed to be. They were unspeakably holy, and worked miracles, and everybody stood in awe of them. If a hermit could survive a wash, and that failed to convince a duke, give him up, let him alone.

My envoys were drilled to decipher the gilded letters upon their surcoats—that flashy ornamentation was a clever stroke; I might have draped the monarch himself in a placard just to relish the primitive magnificence—they were to read these inscriptions aloud and then instruct the assembled nobles on the nature of soap; and should the nobles shrink from it, persuade them to test it upon a hound. The envoy’s subsequent task was to gather the household and demonstrate it upon his own person; he was to flinch at no trial, no matter how reckless, that might persuade the aristocracy of its safety; if any lingering suspicion persisted, he must seize a hermit—the forests abounded with them; they styled themselves holy men, and the world took them at their word. They were unutterably sacred, performed wonders, and all folk trembled before them. Should a hermit endure a scrubbing and still fail to sway a baron, abandon the fellow, leave him to his fate.

“All thinkers on general subjects, the greatest minds in the world, in fact, rely on the specialists. Herbert Spencer did that. He generalized upon the findings of thousands of investigators. He would have had to live a thousand lives in order to do it all himself. And so with Darwin. He took advantage of all that had been learned by the florists and cattle-breeders.”

Every philosopher of broad themes—the most profound intellects on earth—actually depends upon the specialist. Herbert Spencer followed this model. He drew his universal principles from the raw data of countless individual researchers. He would have required a thousand lifetimes to gather such evidence personally. Darwin was no different; he relied on the practical insights already gained by horticulturalists and livestock farmers.

“Don’t say it! don’t! I cannot bear you to feel so much, and me to feel nothing. And I am afraid they will notice us, Mr. Boldwood. Will you let the matter rest now? I cannot think collectedly. I did not know you were going to say this to me. Oh, I am wicked to have made you suffer so!” She was frightened as well as agitated at his vehemence.

“Do not speak it—pray, do not! I cannot endure that you should feel so deeply and I remain altogether unmoved. And I fear they may observe us, Mr. Ashcombe. Will you not leave the matter where it is, now? I cannot think calmly. I did not know you meant to say this to me. Oh, it is cruel of me to have caused you such pain!” She was as frightened as she was distressed by his intensity.

Moreover, he liked to see her casting her eye over the morning paper. She did it with a manner as though she thought the terrestrial globe a great fool, and quite beyond the reach of advice. And as though she understood and was rather amused at the way in which the newspaper people tried to keep back the real facts of the case from her.

Additionally, he enjoyed watching her scan the morning newspaper. She approached it as if she considered the entire planet an irredeemable idiot, utterly impervious to counsel. And as if she grasped, with a touch of amusement, how the journalists attempted to conceal the true state of affairs from her.

“Oh, I don’t know, pray let me go!” she said, her bosom beginning to rise. “I am afraid what to do! I want to be just to you, and to be that seems to be wronging myself, and perhaps it is breaking the commandments. There is considerable doubt of his death, and then it is dreadful; let me ask a solicitor, Mr. Boldwood, if I ought or no!”

“Oh, I cannot say—please, let me go!” she cried, her breath quickening as her chest began to heave. “I am terrified of what I should do! I truly wish to be fair to you, yet to do so feels like a betrayal of myself, and perhaps even a sin. There is such deep uncertainty regarding his death, and the thought is horrifying; let me consult a solicitor, Mr. Boldwood, to see if I am truly free to act!”

We support language modeling (LM), neural textto-speech (TTS) in addition to ASR, ST, and MT tasks. To the best of our knowledge, none of frameworks support all these tasks in a single toolkit. A comparison with other frameworks are summarized in Table 1 . Conceptually, it is possible to combine ASR and MT modules for Cascade-ST, but few frameworks provide such examples. Moreover, though some toolkits indeed support speechto-text tasks, it is not trivial to switch ASR and E2E-ST tasks since E2E-ST requires the auxiliary tasks (ASR/MT objectives) to achieve reasonable performance.

metadata.title: ESPnet-ST: All-in-One Speech Translation Toolkit

text: Our framework supports language modeling (LM) and neural text-to-speech (TTS) in addition to automatic speech recognition (ASR), speech translation (ST), and machine translation (MT) tasks. To the best of our knowledge, no other toolkit supports all of these tasks within a single framework. A comparison with existing frameworks is summarized in Table 1. While it is conceptually possible to combine ASR and MT modules for cascaded speech translation (Cascade-ST), few toolkits provide practical examples of such integration. Furthermore, although some frameworks support speech-to-text tasks, transitioning between ASR and end-to-end speech translation (E2E-ST) is often non-trivial, as E2E-ST typically requires auxiliary ASR or MT objectives to achieve competitive performance.

To resolve this issue, AlpacaTag, an annotation framework for sequence labeling (Lin et al., 2019) attempts to provide annotation recommendations from a learned sequence labeling model that is incrementally updated by batches of incoming human annotations. Its model training follows an active learning strategy (Shen et al., 2017) , which is shown to be a label-efficient, thus it attempts to minimize human annotation efforts. AlpacaTag selects the most informative batches of documents for humans to annotate and thus achieves a more cost-effective way of using human efforts. While active learning allows the model to achieve higher performance earlier in the learning process, model performance could be improved if additional supervision existed. It is imperative that provided annotation recommendations be as accurate as possible, as inaccurate annotation recommendations from the framework can push users towards generating noisy data, hindering instead of aiding the model training process.

metadata.title: LEAN-LIFE: A Label-Efficient Annotation Framework Towards Learning from Explanation

text: To address this issue, AlpacaTag, an annotation framework for sequence labeling (Lin et al., 2019), attempts to provide annotation recommendations from a learned sequence labeling model that is incrementally updated using batches of incoming human annotations. Its model training follows an active learning strategy (Shen et al., 2017), which has been shown to be label-efficient and therefore aims to minimize human annotation effort. AlpacaTag selects the most informative batches of documents for human annotation, thereby achieving a more cost-effective use of human labor. Although active learning enables the model to attain higher performance earlier in the learning process, model performance could be further improved if additional supervision were available. It is imperative that the annotation recommendations provided be as accurate as possible, since inaccurate recommendations from the framework may lead users to produce noisy data, thereby hindering rather than helping the model training process.

At length, a low cry of pain broke the stillness that prevailed; and uttering it, the boy awoke. His left arm, rudely bandaged in a shawl, hung heavy and useless at his side; the bandage was saturated with blood. He was so weak, that he could scarcely raise himself into a sitting posture; when he had done so, he looked feebly round for help, and groaned with pain. Trembling in every joint, from cold and exhaustion, he made an effort to stand upright; but, shuddering from head to foot, fell prostrate on the ground.

After a long stretch of unbroken quiet, a thin, pained whimper cut through the prevailing stillness, and the boy jolted awake. His left arm, crudely wrapped in a shawl, dangled heavy and useless at his side, the bandage soaked completely through with blood. He was so weak he could barely push himself up into a sitting position; once he’d managed the effort, he glanced dazedly around for help, groaning with sharp, throbbing pain. Every joint trembled with cold and total exhaustion, and he strained to pull himself upright, only to shudder violently from head to foot and collapse fully prone onto the ground.

It was now dark, and the hour must have been nearly ten. The only way to accomplish her purpose was to give up her idea of visiting Liddy at Yalbury, return to Weatherbury Farm, put the horse into the gig, and drive at once to Bath. The scheme seemed at first impossible: the journey was a fearfully heavy one, even for a strong horse, at her own estimate; and she much underrated the distance. It was most venturesome for a woman, at night, and alone.

Night had fallen, and it was likely nearly ten o'clock. To achieve her aim, she had to abandon her visit to Liddy at Yalbury, return to Weatherbury Farm, hitch the horse to the gig, and depart for Bath immediately. The plan seemed unfeasible at first; the journey was a grueling trek even for a sturdy horse, and she had significantly underestimated the distance. It was a remarkably bold venture for a woman to undertake alone in the dark.

On Friday night he finished the serial, twenty-one thousand words long. At two cents a word, he calculated, that would bring him four hundred and twenty dollars. Not a bad week’s work. It was more money than he had ever possessed at one time. He did not know how he could spend it all. He had tapped a gold mine. Where this came from he could always get more. He planned to buy some more clothes, to subscribe to many magazines, and to buy dozens of reference books that at present he was compelled to go to the library to consult. And still there was a large portion of the four hundred and twenty dollars unspent. This worried him until the thought came to him of hiring a servant for Gertrude and of buying a bicycle for Marian.

By Friday evening, he had completed the twenty-one-thousand-word serial. He calculated that at two cents per word, the piece would earn him four hundred and twenty dollars. It was an impressive return for a single week’s labor. This sum exceeded any amount of money he had ever held at once, and he was unsure how he could possibly exhaust it. He had struck a gold mine, and he knew he could return to the source for more whenever he wished. His plans involved upgrading his wardrobe, subscribing to numerous periodicals, and purchasing the dozens of reference volumes he currently had to visit the library to use. Even so, a significant portion of the four hundred and twenty dollars remained untouched. This surplus troubled him until he hit upon the idea of employing a domestic worker for Gertrude and getting a bicycle for Marian.

Prince Michael’s shoes were wrecked far beyond the skill of the carefullest cobbler. The ragman would have declined any negotiations concerning his clothes. The two weeks’ stubble on his face was grey and brown and red and greenish yellow—as if it had been made up from individual contributions from the chorus of a musical comedy. No man existed who had money enough to wear so bad a hat as his.

Prince Adrian’s shoes were ruined beyond the repair of the most meticulous cobbler. The ragpicker would have refused all discussion of his garments. The fortnight’s stubble on his face was grey and brown and rusty red and a sickly yellow, as though it had been assembled from separate offerings by the chorus of a comic opera. No man alive possessed sufficient means to wear a hat as dreadful as his.

“You are a noble heart. Did I say we could depend upon no better man? Tell her, to-night, what you know of her danger as involving her child and her father. Dwell upon that, for she would lay her own fair head beside her husband’s cheerfully.” He faltered for an instant; then went on as before. “For the sake of her child and her father, press upon her the necessity of leaving Paris, with them and you, at that hour. Tell her that it was her husband’s last arrangement. Tell her that more depends upon it than she dare believe, or hope. You think that her father, even in this sad state, will submit himself to her; do you not?”

“You’ve got such an honorable, good heart. Didn’t I say there’s no man we could depend on more than you? Tonight, tell her everything you know about the danger she’s facing, the threat that puts both her child and her father at risk right alongside her. Lean into that point, because she’d cheerfully lay down her own life without hesitation to stay by her husband’s side.” His voice wavered for a quick instant, then he continued just as he had before. “For the sake of her child and her father, make her understand how critical it is that she leave Paris at that exact hour, bringing both of them with you. Tell her this was her husband’s final arrangement for her. Let her know more rides on this than she could ever dare to believe, or even hope for. You think her father, even in this fragile, sorrowful state, will follow her lead, won’t you?”

“He!” the other man seemed to answer; “could I mistake him, think you? If a crowd of ghosts were to put themselves into his exact shape, and he stood amongst them, there is something that would tell me how to point him out. If you buried him fifty feet deep, and took me across his grave, I fancy I should know, if there wasn’t a mark above it, that he lay buried there?”

“He!” the other man appeared to reply. “Could I ever fail to know him, do you imagine? If a throng of spectres assumed his very likeness and he stood among them, something within me would still single him out. If you laid him fifty feet beneath the earth and led me across his grave, I believe I would sense it, even without a marker, that he was buried there.”

Again she looked. All the centuries of woman since sex began were eloquent in her eyes. And he measured her in a careless way, and knew, bold now, that she would begin to retreat, coyly and delicately, as he pursued, ever ready to reverse the game should he turn fainthearted. And, too, he was human, and could feel the draw of her, while his ego could not but appreciate the flattery of her kindness. Oh, he knew it all, and knew them well, from A to Z. Good, as goodness might be measured in their particular class, hard-working for meagre wages and scorning the sale of self for easier ways, nervously desirous for some small pinch of happiness in the desert of existence, and facing a future that was a gamble between the ugliness of unending toil and the black pit of more terrible wretchedness, the way whereto being briefer though better paid.

Again she looked. In her eyes were all the centuries of womanhood since sex first began to stir. And he measured her with a kind of careless confidence, knowing now, boldly, that as he advanced she would start to retreat, shyly and delicately, always ready to turn the game about again should he grow weak. Yet he was human too, and could not help feeling her attraction, while his vanity could not but relish the tribute of her kindness. Oh, he understood it all, knew them thoroughly from A to Z. Decent, as decency went among people of their sort; laboring hard for small wages and scorning to sell themselves for easier gain; anxiously hungry for some little scrap of happiness in the desert of life, and staring toward a future that was a gamble between the ugliness of endless drudgery and the black abyss of a more dreadful misery, the road to which was shorter, though better paid.

I was lost. There was no help for me. I was dazed, stupefied; I had no command over myself, I only wandered purposely about, like one out of his mind; so the soldiers took hold of me, and pulled me along with them, out of the cell and along the maze of underground corridors, and finally into the fierce glare of daylight and the upper world. As we stepped into the vast enclosed court of the castle I got a shock; for the first thing I saw was the stake, standing in the center, and near it the piled fagots and a monk. On all four sides of the court the seated multitudes rose rank above rank, forming sloping terraces that were rich with color. The king and the queen sat in their thrones, the most conspicuous figures there, of course.

I was utterly disoriented. There was no rescue coming for me. My head throbbed, my senses felt thick and unmoored; I had no mastery over my own body, only stumbled aimlessly back and forth as if robbed of my reason, until the guards seized me by the shoulders and dragged me along with them, out of the cold, narrow cell, through the winding labyrinth of underground corridors, and finally into the brutal, blinding glare of daylight and the world above. The second we stepped into the fortress’s vast walled courtyard, a jolt of terror shot through me; the first thing I laid eyes on was the wooden post driven into the earth at its center, piled stacks of dry kindling heaped beside it, and a robed friar standing a few paces off. On all four sides of the courtyard, seated crowds rose in tier after tier, forming sloping terraces stippled with the rich, bright colors of their garments. The sovereign and his consort sat on their carved thrones, naturally the most striking figures in the entire assembly.

heavenly bodies were supposed to make as they moved round the earth. Cf.
Merchant of Venice, V. i. 60.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
ll. 266–7
“Hold Thy turning spheres in perfect, tuned accord”
Speaks of the hymn all roaming heavenly bodies were fabled to pour
As they wheeled their arcs round earth’s still, steady core
Compare the *Merchant of Venice*, fifth act, first scene,
Line sixty, where that same star-song’s trace is seen.

“Yes, yes—I own it,” she cried under her breath, with a drowsy fervour of manner and tone which was quite peculiar to her. “Don’t begin speaking to me as you did, Damon; you will drive me to say words I would not wish to say to you. I had given you up, and resolved not to think of you any more; and then I heard the news, and I came out and got the fire ready because I thought that you had been faithful to me.”

“I know, I know—I won’t deny it,” she breathed, her voice thrumming with that slow, burning intensity that belonged only to her. “Don’t lecture me the way you did last time, Elias. You’ll push me to spit out things I’ll regret, words I never want to let fall between us. I’d already written you off, made up my mind to scrub every last thought of you from my head. Then I heard what happened, so I slipped out here and stoked the fire to wait—because I’d let myself believe you’d stayed true to me.”

“Because I didn’t know,” he answered. “I never dreamed of lepers. When I deserted the schooner and landed on the beach, I headed inland for some place of hiding. For three days I lived off guavas, _ohia_-apples, and bananas, all of which grew wild in the jungle. On the fourth day I found the trail—a mere foot-trail. It led inland, and it led up. It was the way I wanted to go, and it showed signs of recent travel. At one place it ran along the crest of a ridge that was no more than a knife-edge. The trail wasn’t three feet wide on the crest, and on either side the ridge fell away in precipices hundreds of feet deep. One man, with plenty of ammunition, could have held it against a hundred thousand.

I simply wasn't aware," he replied. "The possibility of lepers never entered my mind. After I abandoned the schooner and reached the shore, I struck out for the interior, looking for a place to hide. For three days, I sustained myself on wild guavas, *ohia*-apples, and bananas gathered from the jungle. On the fourth day, I stumbled upon a path—a simple foot-trail. It climbed steadily as it led further inland. This was the direction I intended to take, and the trail showed evidence of recent use. At one point, it followed the spine of a ridge that was as narrow as a knife's edge. The path atop the crest was less than three feet wide, with vertical cliffs on either side dropping hundreds of feet into the depths. A lone man, well-supplied with ammunition, could have defended that position against a hundred thousand.

Our model is trained with the standard maximum likelihood estimate. The optimization objective is to maximize the sum of the decomposed step-wise log-likelihood, where each is the sum of concept, edge, and edge label probabilities. To facilitate training, we create a reference generation order of nodes by running a breadth-first-traversal over target AMR graphs, as it is cognitively appealing (core-semantic-first principle, Cai and Lam, 2019) and the effectiveness of pre-order traversal is also empirically verified by Zhang et al. (2019a) in a depth-first setting. For the generation order for sibling nodes, we adopt the uniformly random order and the deterministic order sorted by the relation frequency in a 1 : 1 ratio at first then change to the deterministic order only in the final training steps. We empirically find that the deterministic-afterrandom strategy slightly improves performance.

metadata.title: AMR Parsing via Graph Sequence Iterative Inference *

text: We train our proposed model using standard maximum likelihood estimation. The core optimization objective is to maximize the sum of decomposed per-step log-likelihood values, where each per-step term is computed as the sum of individual probabilities for concepts, edges, and edge labels respectively. To streamline training, we derive a reference node generation order by performing breadth-first traversal over target Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR) graphs: this traversal choice is cognitively plausible as it adheres to the core-semantic-first principle (Cai and Lam, 2019), and the efficacy of pre-order traversal is additionally empirically validated by Zhang et al. (2019a) in a depth-first traversal setting. For sibling node generation order, we initially employ a 1:1 split between uniformly random ordering and deterministic ordering sorted by relation frequency, before shifting to exclusive use of the deterministic ordering regime only during the final training steps. We empirically observe that this random-then-deterministic scheduling strategy delivers a small performance improvement.

As I was occupied in fixing the boat and arranging the sails, several people crowded towards the spot. They seemed much surprised at my appearance, but instead of offering me any assistance, whispered together with gestures that at any other time might have produced in me a slight sensation of alarm. As it was, I merely remarked that they spoke English, and I therefore addressed them in that language. “My good friends,” said I, “will you be so kind as to tell me the name of this town and inform me where I am?”

While I busied myself with securing the vessel and adjusting the canvas, a small crowd began to gather near where I stood. Their expressions betrayed considerable astonishment at the sight of me, yet rather than stepping forward to help, they exchanged hushed words and motions that, under different circumstances, might have stirred a faint unease within me. In that moment, however, I simply noted that their murmurs were in English, so I spoke to them in that tongue. “Kind sirs,” I asked, “would you be good enough to give me the name of this place and tell me where I find myself?”
