She ran faster and still faster, stumbling and nearly falling. If she did not get to him speedily the world would burst.

She lurched into a run, legs that felt like they didn’t quite belong to her carrying her forward, her hands flailing wildly as she moved. With every pounding stride, she grew surer and surer that the injured man clad in odd, unrecognizable clothing was Teddy. She pushed herself faster and faster still, stumbling over ruts in the ground and nearly pitching to the dirt a half-dozen times. If she didn’t reach his side right away, she was convinced the entire world would burst apart at the seams.

Eustacia’s journey was at first as vague in direction as that of thistledown on the wind. She did not know what to do. She wished it had been night instead of morning, that she might at least have borne her misery without the possibility of being seen. Tracing mile after mile along between the dying ferns and the wet white spiders’ webs, she at length turned her steps towards her grandfather’s house. She found the front door closed and locked. Mechanically she went round to the end where the stable was, and on looking in at the stable door she saw Charley standing within.

Eustacia’s wandering at first was as uncertain in its course as thistledown drifting on the wind. She knew not what to do. She wished it had been night instead of morning, so that she might at least have endured her misery without the risk of being seen. Mile after mile she went on between the fading ferns and the damp, white spiders’ webs, until at last she turned her steps toward her grandfather’s house. The front door was shut and locked. Instinctively she went round to the end where the stable stood, and, peering in at the stable door, she saw Charley inside.

The gentleman with the white waistcoat was standing at the gate with his hands behind him, after having delivered himself of some profound sentiments in the board-room. Having witnessed the little dispute between Mr. Gamfield and the donkey, he smiled joyously when that person came up to read the bill, for he saw at once that Mr. Gamfield was exactly the sort of master Oliver Twist wanted. Mr. Gamfield smiled, too, as he perused the document; for five pounds was just the sum he had been wishing for; and, as to the boy with which it was encumbered, Mr. Gamfield, knowing what the dietary of the workhouse was, well knew he would be a nice small pattern, just the very thing for register stoves. So, he spelt the bill through again, from beginning to end; and then, touching his fur cap in token of humility, accosted the gentleman in the white waistcoat.

The man in the white waistcoat stood by the gate with his hands clasped behind his back, fresh from sharing his weighty opinions in the board-room. Having watched the brief scuffle between Mr. Gamfield and the donkey, he grinned broadly as the sweep approached the notice, sensing immediately that this was precisely the kind of master Oliver Twist required. Mr. Gamfield also smiled as he scanned the document; five pounds was exactly the windfall he had been hoping for. Regarding the child who came with the money, Gamfield was well aware of the meager workhouse rations and knew the boy would be suitably slight—the perfect size for navigating narrow chimney flues. He read the advertisement once more from start to finish, then tipped his fur cap in a show of modesty and addressed the gentleman in the white waistcoat.

He thought of the old parents away there in Pomerania--he believed but he was not quite sure, that Heinrich had been an only son--and of the pleasant spectacled figure that had now become a broken and decaying thing in a prisoner's shallow grave....

The missive’s contents seeped into the amber pool of lamplight; it demanded a reply, and the ramifications of that reply coursed through Mr. Aldridge’s mind, banishing any detached literary work. He pictured the elderly couple far off in Westphalia—he suspected, though he could not be certain, that Friedrich had been their sole child—and the genial, bespectacled form that had now dwindled into a shattered, rotting remnant in a captive’s meagre trench.

There was a slight noise from the direction of the dim corner where the ladder was. It was the king descending. I could see that he was bearing something in one arm, and assisting himself with the other. He came forward into the light; upon his breast lay a slender girl of fifteen. She was but half conscious; she was dying of smallpox. Here was heroism at its last and loftiest possibility, its utmost summit; this was challenging death in the open field unarmed, with all the odds against the challenger, no reward set upon the contest, and no admiring world in silks and cloth of gold to gaze and applaud; and yet the king's bearing was as serenely brave as it had always been in those cheaper contests where knight meets knight in equal fight and clothed in protecting steel. He was great now; sublimely great. The rude statues of his ancestors in his palace should have an addition--I would see to that; and it would not be a mailed king killing a giant or a dragon, like the rest, it would be a king in commoner's garb bearing death in his arms that a peasant mother might look her last upon her child and be comforted.

A faint rustle emanated from the gloom near the wooden rungs. It was the sovereign making his descent. I watched as he climbed down, one arm occupied by a heavy weight, the other guiding his passage. When he stepped into the pale light, I saw a girl of tender years resting against his chest, her consciousness fading as the pestilence claimed her. Here was the pinnacle of human courage; a confrontation with the reaper in the starkest of terms, where no prizes were offered and no banners waved. There were no throngs in velvet to cheer this lonely struggle, yet his resolve was as unshakable as when he faced an armed peer in the safety of his harness. In this hour, he reached a height of grandeur beyond any crown. I determined then that the hall of his forefathers needed a new monument—not another iron-clad warrior triumphing over a mythic foe, but a king in humble wool, carrying mortality itself so a mother’s heart might be stilled by one last glimpse of her child.

Three years ago Kraft, Bill Judkins (a poet), and I took our meals at Cypher’s, on Eighth Avenue. I say “took.” When we had money, Cypher got it “off of” us, as he expressed it. We had no credit; we went in, called for food and ate it. We paid or we did not pay. We had confidence in Cypher’s sullenness and smouldering ferocity. Deep down in his sunless soul he was either a prince, a fool or an artist. He sat at a worm-eaten desk, covered with files of waiters’ checks so old that I was sure the bottomest one was for clams that Hendrik Hudson had eaten and paid for. Cypher had the power, in common with Napoleon III. and the goggle-eyed perch, of throwing a film over his eyes, rendering opaque the windows of his soul. Once when we left him unpaid, with egregious excuses, I looked back and saw him shaking with inaudible laughter behind his film. Now and then we paid up back scores.

Three years back, Kraft, Bill Judkins—who wrote verse—and I used to eat at Cypher’s place on Eighth Avenue. I say “used to.” Whenever we had cash, Cypher, as he put it, “took it off of us.” We carried no tab; we simply walked in, ordered, and ate. Sometimes we settled up, sometimes we didn’t. We relied on Cypher’s brooding, pent-up rage. In the lightless depths of his spirit, he was either a nobleman, an idiot, or an artist. He sat behind a worm-gnawed desk buried under stacks of waiters’ checks so ancient I felt certain the bottommost one was for shellfish Hendrik Hudson himself had consumed and paid for. Like Napoleon III and the pop-eyed perch, Cypher could draw a veil across his eyes, clouding the windows of his soul. Once, when we’d walked out without paying, trailing flimsy excuses, I glanced back and saw him quaking with silent laughter behind that veil. Every so often, we cleared our old debts.

Nor were these my only visions. The raising of ghosts or devils was a promise liberally accorded by my favourite authors, the fulfilment of which I most eagerly sought; and if my incantations were always unsuccessful, I attributed the failure rather to my own inexperience and mistake than to a want of skill or fidelity in my instructors. And thus for a time I was occupied by exploded systems, mingling, like an unadept, a thousand contradictory theories and floundering desperately in a very slough of multifarious knowledge, guided by an ardent imagination and childish reasoning, till an accident again changed the current of my ideas.

These were far from the only strange fancies that held me in their grip. The authors I adored freely promised that any dedicated practitioner could summon ghosts or demons, a feat I chased with ravenous eagerness; when my incantations never once succeeded, I laid the failure entirely at my own door, chalking it up to inexperience and clumsy error rather than any lack of skill or fidelity in the works that guided me. For a stretch of time, I poured myself over these long-discredited schools of thought, fumbling like an utter novice as I tangled a thousand conflicting theories together and flailed desperately through a morass of disjointed, wide-ranging learning, led only by my burning imagination and childish reasoning, until a chance occurrence once again redirected the entire current of my ideas.

“Well, sir!” cried Mr. Lorry, thoroughly heated by his indifference, “business is a very good thing, and a very respectable thing. And, sir, if business imposes its restraints and its silences and impediments, Mr. Darnay as a young gentleman of generosity knows how to make allowance for that circumstance. Mr. Darnay, good night, God bless you, sir! I hope you have been this day preserved for a prosperous and happy life.--Chair there!”

“Now look here, sir!” exclaimed Mr. Hale, his cheeks flushed hot with frustration at the other man’s cool unconcern, “trade is an honorable calling, a thoroughly reputable one. And if, sir, trade demands its own measure of discretion, its unspoken reserve, its unavoidable hindrances, Mr. Thorne, as a young man of generous spirit, certainly understands how to make allowances for such a state of things. Mr. Thorne, a very good night to you, and may heaven smile on you, sir! I trust this day has found you well, and that a long, prosperous, joyful life lies ahead of you.--Bring the chair over here!”

But during this rendering young Bob Coggan exhibited one of those anomalies which will afflict little people when other persons are particularly serious: in trying to check his laughter, he pushed down his throat as much of the tablecloth as he could get hold of, when, after continuing hermetically sealed for a short time, his mirth burst out through his nose. Joseph perceived it, and with hectic cheeks of indignation instantly ceased singing. Coggan boxed Bob’s ears immediately.

During the performance, young Bob Coggan fell prey to one of those peculiar impulses that seize children whenever their elders are being especially solemn. In an effort to suppress his laughter, he stuffed a wad of the tablecloth into his mouth; however, after remaining tightly bottled up for a moment, his amusement finally erupted through his nostrils. Joseph noticed the disruption and, his face flushing with sudden resentment, stopped singing at once. Coggan promptly boxed Bob’s ears.

“See!” said the Doctor of Beauvais, raising his hand towards the moon. “I have looked at her from my prison-window, when I could not bear her light. I have looked at her when it has been such torture to me to think of her shining upon what I had lost, that I have beaten my head against my prison-walls. I have looked at her, in a state so dull and lethargic, that I have thought of nothing but the number of horizontal lines I could draw across her at the full, and the number of perpendicular lines with which I could intersect them.” He added in his inward and pondering manner, as he looked at the moon, “It was twenty either way, I remember, and the twentieth was difficult to squeeze in.”

“See!” said the Doctor of Beauvais, lifting his hand toward the moon. “I have watched her from my prison window when I could not endure her light. I have watched her when it tortured me to think of her shining on what I had lost, so that I struck my head against the prison walls. I have watched her in such a dull, stupefied state that I have thought of nothing except the number of horizontal lines I could draw across her at the full, and the number of upright lines with which I might cross them.” He added, in his inward, meditative way, as he gazed at the moon, “It was twenty each way, I remember, and the twentieth was hard to fit in.”

_But that we do struggle confusedly, with pitiful leaders and infinite waste and endless delay; that it is to our indisciplines and to the dishonesties and tricks our incompleteness provokes, that the prolongation of this war is to be ascribed, I readily admit. At the outbreak of this war I had hoped to see militarism felled within a year...._

I grant that we grapple in chaos, led by wretched commanders, squandering lives and time without measure; that the dragging on of this war owes itself to our lack of discipline and to the deceits and stratagems our half-formed nature breeds. When the conflict began, I believed I would witness militarism toppled within a year.

At length burst in the argent revelry,
With plume, tiara, and all rich array,
Numerous as shadows haunting fairily
The brain, new stuff'd, in youth, with triumphs gay
Of old romance. These let us wish away,
And turn, sole-thoughted, to one Lady there,
Whose heart had brooded, all that wintry day,
On love, and wing'd St. Agnes' saintly care,
As she had heard old dames full many times declare.

text:
At last, the silvered celebration broke,
In feathered caps and crowns of bright design,
As thick as phantom forms that softly woke
In young, fresh minds where olden legends shine
With gallant deeds. Let these we now decline,
To watch one Maiden with a steadfast gaze,
Who through the winter's chill did long incline
Toward love and Agnes' holy, winged ways,
As she from aged matrons heard through all her girlhood days.

That Oliver Twist was moved to resignation by the example of these good people, I cannot, although I am his biographer, undertake to affirm with any degree of confidence; but I can most distinctly say, that for many months he continued meekly to submit to the domination and ill-treatment of Noah Claypole: who used him far worse than before, now that his jealousy was roused by seeing the new boy promoted to the black stick and hat-band, while he, the old one, remained stationary in the muffin-cap and leathers. Charlotte treated him ill, because Noah did; and Mrs. Sowerberry was his decided enemy, because Mr. Sowerberry was disposed to be his friend; so, between these three on one side, and a glut of funerals on the other, Oliver was not altogether as comfortable as the hungry pig was, when he was shut up, by mistake, in the grain department of a brewery.

As Oliver Twist’s biographer, I can’t claim with any real confidence that the example of these virtuous people prompted Oliver to resign himself to his circumstances; but I can state unequivocally that for months on end he meekly endured the domination and abuse of Noah Claypole. Noah treated him far worse than he had before, his jealousy sparked when he saw the new boy promoted to the black walking stick and official hatband, while he, the longer-serving staff member, was stuck wearing the same old muffin cap and leather work gear he’d had for ages. Charlotte picked on him too, just because Noah did, and Mrs. Sowerberry made no secret of being his sworn enemy, all because Mr. Sowerberry was inclined to be kind to him. Caught between these three ganging up on him on one side, and a constant glut of funerals keeping him overworked on the other, Oliver was quite a bit less comfortable than that hungry pig that once got accidentally locked in a brewery’s grain storage room.

"Ah! your troubles in life haven't begun yet. Wait till you're a father. That cuts to the bone. You have the most delicate thing in the world in hand, a young kindred mind. You feel responsible for it, you know you are responsible for it; and you lose touch with it. You can't get at it. Nowadays we've lost the old tradition of fatherhood by divine right--and we haven't got a new one. I've tried not to be a cramping ruler, a director, a domestic tyrant to that lad--and in effect it's meant his going his own way.... I don't dominate. I hoped to advise. But you see he loves my respect and good opinion. Too much. When things go well I know of them. When the world goes dark for him, then he keeps his trouble from me. Just when I would so eagerly go into it with him.... There's something the matter now, something--it may be grave. I feel he wants to tell me. And there it is!--it seems I am the last person to whom he can humiliate himself by a confession of blundering, or weakness.... Something I should just laugh at and say, 'That's in the blood of all of us, dear Spit of myself. Let's see what's to be done.'..."

“Ah, your real troubles have scarcely begun. Wait until you have a child of your own. That is what goes to the quick. You are given the most fragile thing in existence, a young mind of your own blood. You know you are answerable for it, you feel the burden of that responsibility—and yet you lose hold of it. You cannot reach it. We have, nowadays, let slip the old notion of fatherhood by divine right, and nothing has come to take its place. I have tried not to be a narrowing master, a taskmaker, a household despot to that boy—only it has come to mean his taking his own path.... I do not impose myself. I had hoped to counsel. But you see, he values my regard and approval. Too much. When all goes well, I hear of it. When the sky lowers for him, he keeps his sorrow to himself. Just when I would most eagerly have gone into it with him.... Something is wrong now, something serious perhaps. I can feel that he wants to speak. And yet there it is—it seems I am the very last person before whom he could bring himself to confess folly, or frailty.... Something I ought only to laugh over and say, ‘That is in all our blood, my dear little replica. Let us see what can be done about it.’...”

His reason and his propensities had seldom any reciprocating influence, having separated by mutual consent long ago: thence it sometimes happened that, while his intentions were as honourable as could be wished, any particular deed formed a dark background which threw them into fine relief. The sergeant’s vicious phases being the offspring of impulse, and his virtuous phases of cool meditation, the latter had a modest tendency to be oftener heard of than seen.

His judgment and his inclinations had long since ceased to act in concert, having parted, as it were, by mutual agreement many years before; and so it came about that, although his purposes were of the purest kind, some single act would sometimes furnish a shadowy backdrop against which they shone out with uncommon brightness. The corporal’s corrupt moods, being born of sudden impulse, and his better ones the fruit of deliberate reflection, the latter possessed only a quiet habit of being more often spoken of than actually observed.

Not that, all this time, his mind was, for an instant, free from one oppressive overwhelming sense of the grave that opened at his feet; it was ever present to him, but in a vague and general way, and he could not fix his thoughts upon it. Thus, even while he trembled, and turned burning hot at the idea of speedy death, he fell to counting the iron spikes before him, and wondering how the head of one had been broken off, and whether they would mend it, or leave it as it was. Then, he thought of all the horrors of the gallows and the scaffold—and stopped to watch a man sprinkling the floor to cool it—and then went on to think again.

It wasn’t as if, the entire time, his mind ever stopped being hounded by the heavy, suffocating awareness of an open grave waiting right at his feet. That dread hung over him constantly, but it felt fuzzy and unfocused, like he couldn’t pin his mind down to really grapple with it. So even as he shook all over, his skin prickling with feverish heat at the thought of how soon he would die, he found himself counting the iron spikes set before him, puzzling over how the tip of one had snapped clean off, and if someone would eventually fix it or just leave it broken like that. Next, his mind drifted to every grisly detail of the gallows and the execution scaffold—until he paused to stare at a man sprinkling water across the floor to bring down the temperature—before his wandering thoughts pulled him right back to his grim ruminations.

text: It appeared that a caterpillar had come from the fern and chosen the front of her bodice as his resting place. She saw the point glisten towards her bosom, and seemingly enter it. Bathsheba closed her eyes in the full persuasion that she was killed at last. However, feeling just as usual, she opened them again.

A caterpillar had apparently crawled out of the nearby fern to settle on the front of her bodice. She watched its glistening pointed tip drift toward her bosom, seeming to sink straight into her skin. Bathsheba squeezed her eyes shut, fully convinced she had been fatally struck at last. When she registered that she felt exactly as well as she had a moment prior, however, she eased her eyes open again.

The Blue Light Drug Store is downtown, between the Bowery and First Avenue, where the distance between the two streets is the shortest. The Blue Light does not consider that pharmacy is a thing of bric-a-brac, scent and ice-cream soda. If you ask it for pain-killer it will not give you a bonbon.

Downtown, tucked where the Bowery and First Avenue nearly touch, stands the Lantern Chemist’s shop. The Lantern holds no brief for the notion that a pharmacy should traffic in curios, perfumes, and fountain treats. Ask it for a soporific, and you will not be handed a sugared almond.

I passed the night wretchedly. Sometimes my pulse beat so quickly and hardly that I felt the palpitation of every artery; at others, I nearly sank to the ground through languor and extreme weakness. Mingled with this horror, I felt the bitterness of disappointment; dreams that had been my food and pleasant rest for so long a space were now become a hell to me; and the change was so rapid, the overthrow so complete!

I passed the night in misery. At times my pulse throbbed so fast and feebly that I could feel the beating of every artery; at others, I seemed almost to sink to the ground from languor and utter weakness. Along with this terror, I suffered the sharp sting of disappointment. Dreams that had long been my sustenance and my pleasant repose had now become a hell to me; and so swift was the transformation, so total the ruin!

Then she broke away into the praises of serenity. Never had she thought so much of his beautiful "Silent Places" as she did now. How she longed to take refuge in some such dreamland from violence and treachery and foolish rumours! She was weary of every reality. She wanted to fly away into some secret hiding-place and cultivate her simple garden there--as Voltaire had done.... Sometimes at night she was afraid to undress. She imagined the sound of guns, she imagined landings and frightful scouts "in masks" rushing inland on motor bicycles....

Then she began to speak fervently about the virtues of tranquility. She had never appreciated his exquisite "Silent Places" as deeply as she did in that moment. She felt a profound longing to retreat into such a dreamscape, seeking shelter from the brutality, betrayal, and mindless gossip of the world. The weight of reality had become unbearable. She wanted to vanish into a private sanctuary and quietly tend her own garden, just as Voltaire had once suggested. At night, she was sometimes too terrified even to undress. Her mind was filled with the imagined roar of artillery, the threat of coastal invasions, and the sight of ghoulish, masked scouts on motorcycles racing through the countryside.

“It was evening when I arrived, and I retired to a hiding-place among the fields that surround it to meditate in what manner I should apply to you. I was oppressed by fatigue and hunger and far too unhappy to enjoy the gentle breezes of evening or the prospect of the sun setting behind the stupendous mountains of Jura.

I arrived at nightfall and withdrew into a secluded spot within the surrounding fields, contemplating how I might best approach you. I was weighed down by exhaustion and hunger, far too consumed by misery to take any pleasure in the mild evening air or the sight of the sun sinking behind the towering peaks of the Jura.

He felt a shock himself, and a blush of embarrassment shone faintly on his sunburned cheeks, though to him it burned as hotly as when his cheeks had been exposed to the open furnace-door in the fire-room. Such sordid things as stabbing affrays were evidently not fit subjects for conversation with a lady. People in the books, in her walk of life, did not talk about such things—perhaps they did not know about them, either.

A tremor of shock hit him, and a faint flush of shame colored his weather-beaten face, though in his own mind it burned with the intensity of an open furnace door in the engine room. Crude matters like knife fights were clearly unsuitable topics for a lady’s ears. The refined people in novels, those from her station in life, did not discuss such brutality—perhaps they were entirely unaware that such things even existed.

Now came a diversion. We heard shrieks and yells, and soon a woman came running and crying; and seeing our group, she flung herself into our midst and begged for protection. A mob of people came tearing after her, some with torches, and they said she was a witch who had caused several cows to die by a strange disease, and practiced her arts by help of a devil in the form of a black cat. This poor woman had been stoned until she hardly looked human, she was so battered and bloody. The mob wanted to burn her.

A sudden tumult arose. We detected wails and shouts, and presently a female emerged in a state of frantic flight; upon observing our assembly, she cast herself among us and pleaded for sanctuary. A riotous crowd pursued her closely, brandishing firebrands, and they proclaimed her a conjurer who had cursed the local herds with a mysterious plague, performing her spells with the aid of a fiend in the shape of an ink-colored cat. The wretched creature had been pelted with rocks until her features were scarcely recognizable, so mangled was she by the assault. The rabble intended to consign her to the pyre.

So Miss Corner went off to get two sticks and a ball and came back to instruct Mr. Direck. She said he had a good eye. The two small boys scenting play in the air got sticks and joined them. The overnight visitor's wife appeared from the house in abbreviated skirts, and wearing formidable shin-guards. With her abundant fair hair, which was already breaking loose, so to speak, to join the fray, she looked like a short stout dismounted Valkyr. Her gaze was clear and firm.

Miss Corner stepped off to fetch two sticks and a ball, then returned to teach Mr. Direck how to play, noting he had a sharp, capable eye for the game. The two small boys, catching wind of play nearby, grabbed their own sticks and darted over to join in. The wife of the overnight guest emerged from the house in cropped skirts, a set of formidable shin guards fastened to her legs. Her thick fair hair was already slipping free of its fastenings, as if itching to throw itself into the fray, and she looked for all the world like a short, stout dismounted Valkyrie, her gaze clear and unshakably steady.

The performer seemed quite at home anywhere between a horse’s head and its tail, and the necessity for this abnormal attitude having ceased with the passage of the plantation, she began to adopt another, even more obviously convenient than the first. She had no side-saddle, and it was very apparent that a firm seat upon the smooth leather beneath her was unattainable sideways. Springing to her accustomed perpendicular like a bowed sapling, and satisfying herself that nobody was in sight, she seated herself in the manner demanded by the saddle, though hardly expected of the woman, and trotted off in the direction of Tewnell Mill.

The rider appeared perfectly at ease on any part of her horse’s back. Once the plantation was behind her and there was no longer a need for her strange posture, she transitioned into a much more sensible stance. She lacked a side-saddle, and it was obvious that she could never maintain a secure grip on the polished leather by sitting sideways. Straightening up like a resilient young tree and ensuring she was unobserved, she took her seat astride the animal as the saddle required, despite the social impropriety, and trotted away toward Tewnell Mill.

“Now, if I had been stung by ten adders I should hardly have lost a day’s work for’t,” said Grandfer Cantle. “Such is my spirit when I am on my mettle. But perhaps ’tis natural in a man trained for war. Yes, I’ve gone through a good deal; but nothing ever came amiss to me after I joined the Locals in four.” He shook his head and smiled at a mental picture of himself in uniform. “I was always first in the most galliantest scrapes in my younger days!”

“If I’d been bitten by a dozen vipers, I doubt I’d have missed even a day’s labor over it,” Grandfer Cantle declared. “That’s the kind of fire I have when my pride’s at stake. Still, I suppose it comes naturally to a man drilled for battle. Lord knows, I’ve weathered plenty—but nothing ever truly rattled me once I signed up with the Local Volunteers in ’04.” He gave a slow shake of his head, smiling as he conjured an image of himself in regimentals. “Back in my youth, I was always the first to leap into the very thickest of the fray!”

Of all things in the world Hugh was the one that could most easily rouse Mr. Britling's unhappy aptitude for distressing imaginations. Hugh was nearer by far to his heart and nerves than any other creature. In the last few years Mr. Britling, by the light of a variety of emotional excursions in other directions, had been discovering this. Whatever Mr. Britling discovered he talked about; he had evolved from his realisation of this tenderness, which was without an effort so much tenderer than all the subtle and tremendous feelings he had attempted in his--excursions, the theory that he had expounded to Mr. Direck that it is only through our children that we are able to achieve disinterested love, real love. But that left unexplained that far more intimate emotional hold of Hugh than of his very jolly little step-brothers. That was a fact into which Mr. Britling rather sedulously wouldn't look....

Among all the world’s influences, Julian possessed the singular power to awaken Mr. Sterling’s unfortunate penchant for morbid foreboding. The boy resided closer to the marrow of his being and the sensitivity of his nerves than any other living soul. Over recent years, illuminated by the flickering torches of several romantic diversions, Sterling had gradually come to recognize this truth. Whatever Sterling perceived, he felt compelled to articulate; from this profound well of affection—an effortless warmth that surpassed the choreographed passions of his past—he had forged the philosophy he once shared with Mr. Thorne: that only through one’s offspring can a man touch the hem of selfless, unadulterated devotion. Yet this doctrine failed to account for the visceral, singular grip Julian held over him, a bond far more potent than the cheerful regard he felt for his younger step-sons. It was a discrepancy that Sterling, with a deliberate and practiced caution, chose never to scrutinize too closely.

Twice the long hands of the clocks went round, Royalties thinned from their _al fresco_ thrones, and buzzed or clattered away in their vehicles of state. The music retired into cases of wood and bags of leather and baize. Waiters removed cloths pointedly near the plain figure sitting almost alone.

Twice the long hands of the clocks made their circuit, and the royalties dwindled on their _al fresco_ thrones, then buzzed and clattered away in their state carriages. The music was packed back into its wooden cases and leather and baize bags. The waiters drew away the cloths with marked deliberation near the plain figure seated almost alone.

Summer passed away in these occupations, and my return to Geneva was fixed for the latter end of autumn; but being delayed by several accidents, winter and snow arrived, the roads were deemed impassable, and my journey was retarded until the ensuing spring. I felt this delay very bitterly; for I longed to see my native town and my beloved friends. My return had only been delayed so long, from an unwillingness to leave Clerval in a strange place, before he had become acquainted with any of its inhabitants. The winter, however, was spent cheerfully; and although the spring was uncommonly late, when it came its beauty compensated for its dilatoriness.

The warmer months dissolved amidst these pursuits, and I had intended to depart for my ancestral home as the leaves turned gold; however, unforeseen complications intervened, and before I could set out, the frost descended with such fury that the mountain passes were rendered impassable. Thus, my homecoming was deferred until the thaw of the following year. This postponement weighed heavily upon my spirit, for my heart yearned for the familiar streets of my youth and the society of those I held dear. I had tarried primarily from a sense of duty toward Adrian, being loath to abandon him to a city of strangers before he had established any local ties. Nevertheless, the winter months were endured with a certain lightness of heart; and though the vernal season was exceptionally sluggish in its approach, its eventual magnificence more than atoned for its long hesitation.

The restaurant was not so showy or pretentious as the one further down Broadway, which he always preferred, but it was nearly so. The tables were well filled with prosperous-looking diners, there was a good orchestra, playing softly enough to make conversation a possible pleasure, and the cuisine and service were beyond criticism. His companion, even in her cheap hat and dress, held herself with an air that added distinction to the natural beauty of her face and figure. And it is certain that she looked at Chandler, with his animated but self-possessed manner and his kindling and frank blue eyes, with something not far from admiration in her own charming face.

The bistro wasn’t quite as ostentatious or overdone as the spot three blocks further down Michigan Avenue that Elias usually favored, but it came remarkably close. Every table was occupied by well-heeled patrons, a small chamber ensemble played low enough that easy, unstrained conversation never felt like a chore, and both the menu and the waitstaff’s attentiveness were unimpeachable. His dining companion, even in her thrifted wool hat and simple, unadorned cotton dress, carried herself with a quiet grace that lent an extra edge of elegance to her already striking features and willowy frame. And there was no mistaking the warm, near-admiring glint in her own soft, expressive face as she watched Elias, his manner lively but never unsteady, his bright, honest blue eyes sparkling as he spoke.

The light raised by her breath had been very fitful, and a momentary irradiation of flesh was all that it had disclosed of her face. That consisted of two matchless lips and a cheek only, her head being still enveloped. She threw away the stick, took the glass in her hand, the telescope under her arm, and moved on.

The flame kindled by her respiration flickered with great instability, and a transitory gleaming of flesh was the only portion of her visage it managed to unveil. This consisted merely of two peerless lips and a solitary cheek, her head being yet muffled in its coverings. She tossed the splinter aside, took the goblet in her hand, the optic tube beneath her arm, and resumed her walk.

text: The impact of m. To investigate the effect of m, the limit on the number of translation candidates, we conduct an experiment examining how model performance changes as m varies from 10 to 5. Table 4 presents the ATS results on Zh2EnSum and En2ZhSum under different hyperparameter settings, where m denotes the cap on translation candidates. RG and MVS refer to ROUGE and MoverScore, respectively. We adopt “subword-subword” and “word-character” segmentation granularities in the Zh2En and En2Zh models, respectively. Table 5 reports the results of models on Zh2EnSum with different segmentation granularities. “Unit” denotes the granularity combination of text units. w and sw denote “word” and “subword” (Sennrich et al., 2016), respectively. The improvements of all ATS models over TNCLS are statistically significant (p < 0.01).

Certain small packets were made and given to him. He put them, one by one, in the breast of his inner coat, counted out the money for them, and deliberately left the shop. “There is nothing more to do,” said he, glancing upward at the moon, “until to-morrow. I can’t sleep.”

A set of small wrapped packages were prepared and handed over to him. He tucked each one individually into the inner breast pocket of his coat, counted out the exact sum owed for them, and walked out of the shop at a slow, deliberate pace. “There’s nothing else left to do,” he said, tilting his head up to glance at the moon, “not until tomorrow. I can’t sleep.”

I write to you, encompassed by peril and ignorant whether I am ever doomed to see again dear England and the dearer friends that inhabit it. I am surrounded by mountains of ice which admit of no escape and threaten every moment to crush my vessel. The brave fellows whom I have persuaded to be my companions look towards me for aid, but I have none to bestow. There is something terribly appalling in our situation, yet my courage and hopes do not desert me. Yet it is terrible to reflect that the lives of all these men are endangered through me. If we are lost, my mad schemes are the cause.

I am writing to you while besieged by danger, uncertain if I shall ever again behold my beloved England or the even dearer friends who dwell there. I am hemmed in by towering peaks of ice that offer no path of escape and threaten to shatter my vessel at any moment. The valiant men I persuaded to join me look to me for some sign of rescue, yet I have no help to offer them. There is something profoundly horrific about our predicament, and yet my spirit and my hopes have not yet failed me. Still, it is an agonizing reflection that the lives of all these souls are placed in such jeopardy because of me. Should we perish, my own reckless ambitions are the cause.

It was curious to observe some faces which stood out prominently from among the group. There was the chairman himself, (the landlord of the house,) a coarse, rough, heavy built fellow, who, while the songs were proceeding, rolled his eyes hither and thither, and, seeming to give himself up to joviality, had an eye for everything that was done, and an ear for everything that was said—and sharp ones, too. Near him were the singers: receiving, with professional indifference, the compliments of the company, and applying themselves, in turn, to a dozen proffered glasses of spirits and water, tendered by their more boisterous admirers; whose countenances, expressive of almost every vice in almost every grade, irresistibly attracted the attention, by their very repulsiveness. Cunning, ferocity, and drunkeness in all its stages, were there, in their strongest aspect; and women: some with the last lingering tinge of their early freshness almost fading as you looked: others with every mark and stamp of their sex utterly beaten out, and presenting but one loathsome blank of profligacy and crime; some mere girls, others but young women, and none past the prime of life; formed the darkest and saddest portion of this dreary picture.

It was strange to pick out certain visages that seemed to rise distinctly from the crowd. At the center sat the host himself—the tavern’s proprietor—a thick-set, vulgar, heavy-handed man, who, while the ditties rang on, let his gaze wander freely around, and, though he feigned full surrender to merriment, missed no gesture enacted nor whisper uttered—and those senses were keen. Close beside him clustered the performers: accepting, with the practiced indifference of their trade, the crowd’s flattery, and drinking one by one from a dozen offered tumblers of gin and water, thrust forward by their louder, rowdier partisans—faces so stamped with nearly every shade and species of vice that their very ugliness seized the eye. Guile, savagery, and intoxication in every degree were on display in their most vivid forms; and women: some with the final ghost of youthful bloom fading even as you watched; others with every trace and sign of womanhood so thoroughly hammered out that nothing remained but a sickening vacancy of debauchery and guilt; a few mere girls, others scarcely grown, none beyond the height of their years—these composed the gloomiest, most pitiable fragment of the whole bleak scene.

“We’re lucky that most of them are here,” Brissenden whispered to Martin. “There’s Norton and Hamilton; come on and meet them. Stevens isn’t around, I hear. I’m going to get them started on monism if I can. Wait till they get a few jolts in them and they’ll warm up.”

“We’re fortunate that most of them are here,” Brissenden whispered to Martin. “There’s Norton and Hamilton; come along and meet them. I hear Stevens isn’t around. I’m going to see if I can get them started on monism. Just wait until they’ve had a few jolts and you’ll see them begin to thaw.”

He went downstairs and out into the street, breathing great breaths of air. He had been suffocating in that atmosphere, while the apprentice’s chatter had driven him frantic. There had been times when it was all he could do to refrain from reaching over and mopping Jim’s face in the mush-plate. The more he had chattered, the more remote had Ruth seemed to him. How could he, herding with such cattle, ever become worthy of her? He was appalled at the problem confronting him, weighted down by the incubus of his working-class station. Everything reached out to hold him down—his sister, his sister’s house and family, Jim the apprentice, everybody he knew, every tie of life. Existence did not taste good in his mouth. Up to then he had accepted existence, as he had lived it with all about him, as a good thing. He had never questioned it, except when he read books; but then, they were only books, fairy stories of a fairer and impossible world. But now he had seen that world, possible and real, with a flower of a woman called Ruth in the midmost centre of it; and thenceforth he must know bitter tastes, and longings sharp as pain, and hopelessness that tantalized because it fed on hope.

He descended the narrow stairwell and stepped into the open air, drawing deep, desperate lungfuls of the evening. The stagnant atmosphere inside had begun to choke him, and Arthur’s relentless, vapid prattle had pushed his nerves to a jagged edge. More than once, he had felt a violent impulse to seize the boy by the scruff and bury his grinning face in the bowl of cold porridge. Every mindless syllable Arthur uttered seemed to widen the gulf between himself and Elena. How could a man mired in such common company ever hope to stand beside her?

The sheer scale of his predicament struck him with a heavy dread, the leaden weight of his birthright pinning him to the earth. It was as if every thread of his life—his sister’s cramped parlor, her noisy brood, the apprentice’s idiocy, and every familiar face—conspired to drag him back into the mud. The very flavor of his life had turned to ash. Until this moment, he had moved through his days with a blunt acceptance, viewing his lot as natural and sufficient. Doubts had only ever flickered when he retreated into novels, but those had been mere fables—glimpses of a porcelain reality that could never truly exist. Now, however, he had glimpsed that world in the flesh, a tangible paradise with Elena at its radiant heart. From this night on, his bread would taste of bile, his desires would cut like glass, and he would be haunted by a despair that was all the more cruel for its refusal to let go of hope.

text:
Or, if man’s superior might
Dare invade your native right,
On the lofty ether borne,
Man with all his pow’rs you scorn;
Swiftly seek, on clanging wings,
Other lakes and other springs;
And the foe you cannot brave,
Scorn at least to be his slave.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Or, should human strength and pride
Storm the home where you abide,
Through the vaulted heavens you’ll soar,
Mocking all their vaunted power;
On resounding pinions flee
To some far, untainted sea;
And the tyrant you can’t fight,
Spurn in chains—reclaim your flight.

As Ruth’s front door closed behind them and he came down the steps with her, he found himself greatly perturbed. It was not unalloyed bliss, taking her to the lecture. He did not know what he ought to do. He had seen, on the streets, with persons of her class, that the women took the men’s arms. But then, again, he had seen them when they didn’t; and he wondered if it was only in the evening that arms were taken, or only between husbands and wives and relatives.

As Ruth’s front door shut behind them and he descended the steps beside her, he felt himself deeply unsettled. Taking her to the lecture was not the pure delight he had imagined. He did not know what was proper for him to do. On the streets, among people of her class, he had observed that women took men by the arm. Yet he had also seen them when they did not, and he wondered whether such arm-taking was done only in the evening, or only among husbands and wives and relations.

They cooper’d at e’en, they cooper’d at morn,
Till our gudeman has gotten the scorn;
On ilka brow she’s planted a horn,
And swears that there they sall stan’ O.
We’ll hide the Cooper, &c.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
At dusk they hammered, at dawn they shaped the wood,
Until our goodman found himself with shame imbued;
On every forehead she set a horn to brood,
And vows that there they must remain.
We’ll conceal the Cooper, &c.

He reached home damp and weary enough after his ten-mile walk. It had hardly been a propitious beginning, but he had chosen his course, and would show no swerving. The evening and the following morning were spent in concluding arrangements for his departure. To stay at home a minute longer than necessary after having once come to his determination would be, he felt, only to give new pain to his mother by some word, look, or deed.

He arrived at his doorstep soaked and spent from the ten-mile tramp. The outset had been anything but auspicious, yet he had fixed his path and resolved not to waver. That night and the next morning passed in tying up final threads for his leaving. Lingering at home even a moment beyond what was needful now that his mind was made up would, he sensed, merely inflict fresh hurt on his mother through some stray phrase, glance, or gesture.

“No, no, now—that’s too much!” said the timid man, becoming a man of brazen courage all of a sudden. “I didn’t say _sir_. I’ll take my oath I didn’t say ‘Joseph Poorgrass o’ Weatherbury, sir.’ No, no; what’s right is right, and I never said sir to the bird, knowing very well that no man of a gentleman’s rank would be hollering there at that time o’ night. ‘Joseph Poorgrass of Weatherbury,’—that’s every word I said, and I shouldn’t ha’ said that if ’t hadn’t been for Keeper Day’s metheglin.... There, ’twas a merciful thing it ended where it did.”

“No, no, now—that’s going too far!” cried the shrinking fellow, who had all at once grown as bold as brass. “I never said _sir_. I’ll swear I never called out, ‘Abel Mugford o’ Nethercombe, sir.’ No, no; fairness is fairness, and I never put the title on the bird, being well aware that no gentleman of standing would be bawling there at that hour o’ the night. ‘Abel Mugford of Nethercombe’—that was the whole of it, every syllable; and I’d never ha’ spoken so much, if it hadn’t been for old Gaffin’s barley-wine.... There now, it was a merciful thing the matter stopped where it did.”

“Then come with me,” said Mrs. Sowerberry: taking up a dim and dirty lamp, and leading the way upstairs; “your bed’s under the counter. You don’t mind sleeping among the coffins, I suppose? But it doesn’t much matter whether you do or don’t, for you can’t sleep anywhere else. Come; don’t keep me here all night!”

“Come along with me, then,” said Mrs. Sowerberry, picking up a dim, grimy lamp and leading the way up the stairs. “Your bed’s stowed under the counter. I suppose you don’t mind sleeping surrounded by coffins, do you? But it hardly makes a difference whether you mind or not, because there’s nowhere else for you to sleep. Hurry up now—don’t make me hang around here all night!”

“There is so much that is necessary.” She looked at him gently, and he was a brute to oppose her. “Physics and chemistry—you can’t do them without laboratory study; and you’ll find algebra and geometry almost hopeless without instruction. You need the skilled teachers, the specialists in the art of imparting knowledge.”

“There’s so much that’s non-negotiable when it comes to this.” She gazed at him softly, and he felt like an absolute brute for pushing back against her. “Physics and chemistry—you can’t get a real grasp on either without hands-on lab work; you’ll also find algebra and geometry nearly impossible to work through without proper guidance. You need skilled, knowledgeable teachers, specialists who know exactly how to convey that kind of complex material effectively to learners.”

Coggan furnished a suitable reply. Oak went on with his shearing, and said not another word. Pestilent moods had come, and teased away his quiet. Bathsheba had shown indications of anointing him above his fellows by installing him as the bailiff that the farm imperatively required. He did not covet the post relatively to the farm: in relation to herself, as beloved by him and unmarried to another, he had coveted it. His readings of her seemed now to be vapoury and indistinct. His lecture to her was, he thought, one of the absurdest mistakes. Far from coquetting with Boldwood, she had trifled with himself in thus feigning that she had trifled with another. He was inwardly convinced that, in accordance with the anticipations of his easy-going and worse-educated comrades, that day would see Boldwood the accepted husband of Miss Everdene. Gabriel at this time of his life had out-grown the instinctive dislike which every Christian boy has for reading the Bible, perusing it now quite frequently, and he inwardly said, “‘I find more bitter than death the woman whose heart is snares and nets!’” This was mere exclamation—the froth of the storm. He adored Bathsheba just the same.

Coggan gave a fitting response. Oak kept shearing, and didn’t speak another word. Sour, gnawing restlessness had settled over him, chasing off his usual quiet calm. Bathsheba had dropped hints that she might elevate him above the rest of the workers, naming him the bailiff the farm so desperately needed. The position itself, as just a role managing the farm, didn’t appeal to him at all: what he’d coveted was the closeness it would bring to her, the woman he loved, who was not yet promised to anyone else. All his previous guesses about her feelings and intentions felt foggy and untrustworthy now. That sharp lecture he’d given her, he thought, was one of the most idiotic mistakes he’d ever made. She hadn’t been flirting with Boldwood at all; instead, she’d been toying with him, putting on a whole show of leading another man on just to rattle him. He was convinced deep down that his laid-back, less educated coworkers had been right all along—by the end of the day, Boldwood would be Miss Everdene’s accepted fiancé. At this point in his life, Gabriel had outgrown the automatic, instinctive dislike of reading the Bible that nearly every Christian boy carries through childhood, and he pored over its pages fairly often now. In his head, he bit out a verse he knew well: “‘I find more bitter than death the woman whose heart is snares and nets!’” That was just a burst of angry frustration, nothing more—froth floating on the surface of a roiling storm. He adored Bathsheba just as fiercely as he always had.

I can see no other solution that is not offensive to God. It does not matter in the least what we owe to Serbia or what we owe to Italy. We have got to set this world on a different footing. We have got to set up the world at last--on justice and reason."

The sole conceivable regime for Illyria," he declared, his gaze fixed unwaveringly on the valley below the slope, "is a federation of free communes modeled on the Helvetic system. I perceive no alternative that does not affront the Almighty. Our debts to Dalmatia or to Rome are of no consequence whatsoever. We must place this earth upon a new foundation. We must finally establish the world—upon fairness and rationality.

“When I begin to feel drowsy, I change the heavy book I am reading for a lighter one. And when I doze over that, I beat my head with my knuckles in order to drive sleep away. Somewhere I read of a man who was afraid to sleep. Kipling wrote the story. This man arranged a spur so that when unconsciousness came, his naked body pressed against the iron teeth. Well, I’ve done the same. I look at the time, and I resolve that not until midnight, or not until one o’clock, or two o’clock, or three o’clock, shall the spur be removed. And so it rowels me awake until the appointed time. That spur has been my bed-mate for months. I have grown so desperate that five and a half hours of sleep is an extravagance. I sleep four hours now. I am starved for sleep. There are times when I am light-headed from want of sleep, times when death, with its rest and sleep, is a positive lure to me, times when I am haunted by Longfellow’s lines:

Lighter and lighter, until at last the sun touched the tops of the still trees, and poured its radiance over the hill. In the glow, the water of the chateau fountain seemed to turn to blood, and the stone faces crimsoned. The carol of the birds was loud and high, and, on the weather-beaten sill of the great window of the bed-chamber of Monsieur the Marquis, one little bird sang its sweetest song with all its might. At this, the nearest stone face seemed to stare amazed, and, with open mouth and dropped under-jaw, looked awe-stricken.

The dawn grew ever more luminous, until at last the sun grazed the crowns of the motionless trees and spilled its brilliance across the slope. In that light, the water of the manor’s fountain appeared to darken into wine, and the sculpted visages flushed scarlet. The chorus of birds rang out, piercing and fervent, and upon the weathered ledge of the vast casement in the sleeping chamber of the Seigneur, a single small bird poured forth its most honeyed melody with all its strength. At this, the closest stone countenance seemed to gape in wonder, its mouth agape and jaw hanging slack, as though stricken with awe.

What place is besieged, and vainly tries to raise the siege?
Lo, I send to that place a commander, swift, brave, immortal,
And with him horse and foot, and parks of artillery,
And artillery-men, the deadliest that ever fired gun.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
What ground is under siege, and stumbles in a failed attempt to break it?
Look, I send to that ground a commander—swift, fearless, undying—
with cavalry and infantry, and whole batteries of cannon,
and gunners more lethal than any who ever touched a fuse.

I presently gave up looking, the night shut down so black, but I kept my ears strained to catch the least suspicious sound, for I judged I had only to wait, and I shouldn't be disappointed. However, I had to wait a long time. At last I caught what you may call indistinct glimpses of sound--dulled metallic sound. I pricked up my ears, then, and held my breath, for this was the sort of thing I had been waiting for. This sound thickened, and approached--from toward the north. Presently, I heard it at my own level--the ridge-top of the opposite embankment, a hundred feet or more away. Then I seemed to see a row of black dots appear along that ridge--human heads? I couldn't tell; it mightn't be anything at all; you can't depend on your eyes when your imagination is out of focus. However, the question was soon settled. I heard that metallic noise descending into the great ditch. It augmented fast, it spread all along, and it unmistakably furnished me this fact: an armed host was taking up its quarters in the ditch. Yes, these people were arranging a little surprise party for us. We could expect entertainment about dawn, possibly earlier.

I soon abandoned the search, for the darkness fell like a curtain of pitch, but I kept my hearing sharpened for the faintest hint of danger, reckoning I had only to be patient, and my vigil would be rewarded. Yet the wait stretched on and on. Eventually I caught what might be called blurred fragments of noise—a muffled, metallic clinking. I straightened my ears then and stilled my breath, for this was precisely the token I had anticipated. The noise swelled and drew nearer—from the direction of the north. Before long, I perceived it at my own elevation—the crest of the facing ridge, a hundred feet distant or more. Then I fancied a line of dark specks emerging along that ridge—human crowns? I could not be sure; it might be nothing whatsoever; one cannot trust the eyes when the mind’s focus falters. But the riddle was soon resolved. I caught that metallic clatter descending into the vast trench. It quickened rapidly, it rippled along the whole line, and it plainly delivered me this truth: a body of armed men was settling into the ditch. Indeed, these folk were preparing a modest ambuscade for us. We might look for amusement around daybreak, perhaps sooner.

The despatch from General French put him into a kindlier frame of mind. He wrote instead briefly but affectionately. As a gentleman should. "How could you doubt our fleet or our army?" was the gist of his letter. He ignored completely every suggestion of a visit to Pyecrafts that her letter had conveyed. He pretended that it had contained nothing of the sort.... And with that she passed out of his mind again under the stress of more commanding interests....

The message from Commander Ashford softened his temper. He replied with brevity yet warmth. As a man of honour would. “How could you question our navy or our troops?” formed the core of his note. He overlooked entirely every hint of a journey to the Whitmores that her correspondence had implied. He feigned that it had held no such suggestion at all.... And with that, she slipped from his thoughts once more beneath the weight of more pressing concerns....

There is a subject I have determined to put off no longer. Your sentiment on the wisdom of _Carpe diem_ does not impress me today. Our present mode of life must shortly be brought to an end.”

“You are exactly like all women. You are always willing to build your life on whatever chance position happens to present itself, while men would rather shape the whole world to fit them. Listen to me, Eustacia. There is something I have resolved not to delay any longer. Your view of the wisdom of _carpe diem_ does not move me today. Our present way of living must soon be brought to an end.”

Relations in WordNet are mainly of a hierarchical, and thus paradigmatic nature, with the most frequently encoded relation being the super-subordinate relation (instantiated in terms of hypernymy and hyponymy; see also Section 1). Other relations linking concepts in WordNet include part-whole relations (meronymy, e.g. between wheel n and car n ), antonymy relations and cross-part-of-speech relations holding among semantically similar words sharing a stem with the same meaning (e.g. between speed n and speedy a ). As of today, WordNet is the most widely used and de facto standard sense inventory for the WSD task (Raganato et al., 2017a) .
