To date, WordNet remains the most widely used and de facto standard sense inventory for the WSD task (Raganato et al., 2017a).

The hill was covered on its northern side by an ancient and decaying plantation of beeches, whose upper verge formed a line over the crest, fringing its arched curve against the sky, like a mane. To-night these trees sheltered the southern slope from the keenest blasts, which smote the wood and floundered through it with a sound as of grumbling, or gushed over its crowning boughs in a weakened moan. The dry leaves in the ditch simmered and boiled in the same breezes, a tongue of air occasionally ferreting out a few, and sending them spinning across the grass. A group or two of the latest in date amongst the dead multitude had remained till this very mid-winter time on the twigs which bore them and in falling rattled against the trunks with smart taps.

The ridge was cloaked on its eastern face by a venerable and moribund grove of oaks, the highest reaches of which etched a dark border over the summit, crowning the pale arc of the firmament like a bristling fringe. This night, the timber served to screen the western incline from the biting gusts that struck the copse and labored through the branches with a heavy muttering, or surged across the topmost limbs in a weary sigh. Down in the trench, the brittle leaves seethed and hissed in the restless air, a sudden draft occasionally prying a few loose to send them skittering wildly over the turf. A final scattering of the autumn’s dead had endured upon their native twigs until this deep mid-winter hour, and in their eventual fall, they struck the boles with a succession of crisp, percussive raps.

Since then the appellation which I had thought to reserve to the horizons and landscapes of a merely realistic dream-country, has become more and more popular as a practical definition; and the dream-country has, by degrees, solidified into a utilitarian region which people can go to, take a house in, and write to the papers from. But I ask all good and gentle readers to be so kind as to forget this, and to refuse steadfastly to believe that there are any inhabitants of a Victorian Wessex outside the pages of this and the companion volumes in which they were first discovered.

Since that time, the name I once meant to keep for the distant views and countryside of a purely imagined realm has steadily gained currency as a handy label; and that dream-realm has gradually hardened into a practical district where folk can settle, lease a cottage, and send letters to the press. Yet I entreat every kind and courteous reader to put this out of mind, and to resist with firm conviction the notion that any denizens of a Victorian Wessex exist beyond the covers of this book and the companion volumes where they first came to light.

I pray you let the drama halt while Chorus stalks to the footlights and drops an epicedian tear upon the fatness of Mr. Hoover. Tune the pipes to the tragedy of tallow, the bane of bulk, the calamity of corpulence. Tried out, Falstaff might have rendered more romance to the ton than would have Romeo’s rickety ribs to the ounce. A lover may sigh, but he must not puff. To the train of Momus are the fat men remanded. In vain beats the faithfullest heart above a 52-inch belt. Avaunt, Hoover! Hoover, forty-five, flush and foolish, might carry off Helen herself; Hoover, forty-five, flush, foolish and fat is meat for perdition. There was never a chance for you, Hoover.

I beg you, let the action pause while Chorus steps down to the footlights and sheds a dirge-like tear over the ample girth of Mr. Hoover. Let the pipes be tuned to the tragedy of tallow, to the curse of heaviness, the disaster of corpulence. Trimmed down, Falstaff might have lent more romance to the ton than Romeo’s frail ribs could ever yield to the pound. A lover may sigh, but he must not wheeze. The fat men are consigned to the retinue of Momus. In vain does the most faithful heart beat above a fifty-two-inch belt. Begone, Hoover! Hoover, at forty-five, flushed and foolish, might have borne Helen herself away; Hoover, forty-five, flushed, foolish, and fat is food for damnation. You never had a chance, Hoover.

They went out, leaving Lucie, and her husband, her father, and the child, by a bright fire. Mr. Lorry was expected back presently from the Banking House. Miss Pross had lighted the lamp, but had put it aside in a corner, that they might enjoy the fire-light undisturbed. Little Lucie sat by her grandfather with her hands clasped through his arm: and he, in a tone not rising much above a whisper, began to tell her a story of a great and powerful Fairy who had opened a prison-wall and let out a captive who had once done the Fairy a service. All was subdued and quiet, and Lucie was more at ease than she had been.

They stepped outside, leaving Lucie alone with her husband, her father, and the child beside a glowing hearth. Mr. Lorry was due back shortly from the bank. Miss Pross had lit the lamp but pushed it into a shadowy corner so they could savor the firelight without interruption. Young Lucie nestled next to her grandfather, her small fingers laced around his arm, while he, in a voice barely above a murmur, spun her a tale about a mighty Fairy who had shattered a prison wall to free a prisoner who had once helped the Fairy. Everything felt hushed and serene, and Lucie felt more at peace than she had in some time.

Yet they contriv'd to steal the Basil-pot,
And to examine it in secret place:
The thing was vile with green and livid spot,
And yet they knew it was Lorenzo's face:
The guerdon of their murder they had got,
And so left Florence in a moment's space,
Never to turn again.--Away they went,
With blood upon their heads, to banishment.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
Still, they managed to pilfer the basil-jar,
To scrutinize it in a hidden nook:
The object was foul, stained with sickly hues,
Yet they recognized Lorenzo’s visage there:
The prize of their dark homicide was won,
And so they fled from Florence instantly,
Never to return.—They hurried forth,
To exile, with the weight of blood upon them.

His uplifted hand descended, and thereafter rose and fell in a swift and steady rhythm. The cub struggled and cursed and squirmed, but did not offer to bite. Brissenden looked on gravely, though once he grew excited and gripped the whiskey bottle, pleading, “Here, just let me swat him once.”

His raised hand came down and then continued its rapid, even motion. The young man writhed and swore and twisted, yet made no move to bite. Brissenden watched with a solemn expression, though at one point he became agitated, seized the whiskey bottle, and begged, “Here, just let me hit him once.”

When the quiet of the garret had been long undisturbed, and his heaving breast and shaken form had long yielded to the calm that must follow all storms--emblem to humanity, of the rest and silence into which the storm called Life must hush at last--they came forward to raise the father and daughter from the ground. He had gradually dropped to the floor, and lay there in a lethargy, worn out. She had nestled down with him, that his head might lie upon her arm; and her hair drooping over him curtained him from the light.

After the attic had remained silent for some time, and his heavy breathing and trembling frame had finally surrendered to the stillness that inevitably follows a crisis—a symbol of the ultimate peace and silence that must eventually quiet the storm of human life—the others moved closer to lift the father and daughter from the floor. He had slowly collapsed onto the boards, lying in a state of utter, worn-out lethargy. She had lowered herself with him, cushioning his head upon her arm, while her loose hair fell over him like a curtain to shield him from the light.

In contrast, we show that BPE itself incorporates the ability to produce multiple segmentations of the same word. BPE builds a vocabulary of subwords and a merge table, which specifies which subwords have to be merged into a bigger subword, as well as the priority of the merges. During segmentation, words are first split into sequences of characters, then the learned merge operations are applied to merge the characters into larger, known symbols, till no merge can be done (Figure 1(a) ). We introduce BPE-dropout -a subword regularization method based on and compatible with conventional BPE. It uses a vocabulary and a merge table built by BPE, but at each merge step, some merges are randomly dropped. This results in different segmentations for the same word (Figure 1(b) ). Our method requires no segmentation training in addition to BPE and uses standard BPE at test time, therefore is simple. BPE-dropout is superior compared to both BPE and Kudo (2018) on a wide range of translation tasks, therefore is effective.

metadata.title: BPE-Dropout: Simple and Effective Subword Regularization

text: Conversely, we demonstrate that BPE inherently possesses the capacity to generate multiple segmentations for a single word. BPE constructs a subword vocabulary alongside a merge table, which delineates the specific subwords to be combined into larger units and establishes the precedence of these operations. In the segmentation phase, words are initially decomposed into character sequences; subsequently, the acquired merge operations are executed to aggregate these characters into increasingly larger, recognized symbols until no further merges are possible (Figure 1(a)). We propose BPE-dropout, a subword regularization technique that is both derived from and fully compatible with standard BPE. While it utilizes the vocabulary and merge table generated by BPE, it introduces stochasticity by randomly omitting certain merges at each step of the process. This stochastic approach yields diverse segmentations for the same word (Figure 1(b)). Our approach is characterized by its simplicity, as it necessitates no additional segmentation training beyond the standard BPE procedure and employs conventional BPE during inference. BPE-dropout demonstrates its effectiveness by outperforming both standard BPE and the method proposed by Kudo (2018) across a broad spectrum of translation tasks.

Identifying the central theme and concepts in scientific texts is a time-consuming task for experts and a hard task for laymen (Alper et al., 2004; El-Arini and Guestrin, 2011; Pain, 2016) . This problem is even more pronounced in inter-disciplinary fields of study, where experts in a target domain often lack the deeper knowledge of a source domain (Carr et al., 2018) . A specific example is biomimetics, an engineering problem-solving process in which one draws on analogous biological solutions (Kruiper et al., 2016) . A major issue is that engineers (target domain) know little biology (source domain) or characteristics of plants or Figure 1 : Example of Semi-Open Relation Extraction in the Biology domain (Burgess et al., 2006) . We first extract the TRADE-OFF expressed between the central concepts 'safety' and 'efficiency' (in blue), that takes place specifically in 'conifer species' (in green). We further explore the content of a paper by investigating the results of an Open Information Extraction (OIE) system (in red). The central concepts captured by a TRADE-OFF mechanism enable the filtering of many irrelevant OIE extractions, which are found to be errorprone in scientific texts. Further OIE extractions found in the document can shed light on the semantic meaning of relevant concepts, e.g., ' xylem', as depicted at the top of the Figure ( in the red box).

metadata.title: In Layman's Terms: Semi-Open Relation Extraction from Scientific Texts

text: Locating core themes and key concepts within scientific publications is a resource-intensive activity for subject-matter experts, and an inaccessible challenge for non-specialist audiences (Alper et al., 2004; El-Arini and Guestrin, 2011; Pain, 2016). This difficulty is particularly acute in cross-disciplinary research areas, where practitioners working in a target field frequently lack in-depth familiarity with the foundational knowledge of the source domain being integrated (Carr et al., 2018). One concrete illustration of this dynamic occurs in biomimetics, an engineering-based problem-solving framework that leverages preexisting analogous solutions developed in biological systems (Kruiper et al., 2016). A core limitation in this use case is that engineers (the target domain) have minimal working knowledge of biology (the source domain) or specific structural and functional traits of plant species, as demonstrated in Figure 1: Example of Semi-Open Relation Extraction in the Biology domain (Burgess et al., 2006). For the illustrated use case, we first identify the TRADE-OFF relation articulated between the core concepts of ‘safety’ and ‘efficiency’ (marked in blue), which occurs exclusively in the context of ‘conifer species’ (marked in green). We then conduct deeper exploration of the target paper’s content by analyzing outputs generated by an Open Information Extraction (OIE) system, which are highlighted in red in the figure. The core concepts linked by the TRADE-OFF relation support the removal of a large volume of extraneous OIE outputs, which have been widely documented to have high error rates when applied to scientific textual corpora. Additional OIE extractions recovered from the same document can clarify the semantic definition of domain-relevant concepts, such as ‘xylem’, as shown in the red box positioned at the top of the figure.

“I thought my mistress would have married you,” said Gabriel, not knowing enough of the full depths of Boldwood’s love to keep silence on the farmer’s account, and determined not to evade discipline by doing so on his own. “However, it is so sometimes, and nothing happens that we expect,” he added, with the repose of a man whom misfortune had inured rather than subdued.

"I truly believed she would have married you," Gabriel said. He didn't quite grasp the sheer intensity of Boldwood’s feelings—which might have otherwise made him hold his tongue—and he was determined not to avoid a hard truth just to spare himself the discomfort. "But that's how it is sometimes; life rarely follows our expectations," he added, speaking with the quiet composure of a man who had been hardened by misfortune without being broken by it.

A favourite at the Old Bailey, and eke at the Sessions, Mr. Stryver had begun cautiously to hew away the lower staves of the ladder on which he mounted. Sessions and Old Bailey had now to summon their favourite, specially, to their longing arms; and shouldering itself towards the visage of the Lord Chief Justice in the Court of King’s Bench, the florid countenance of Mr. Stryver might be daily seen, bursting out of the bed of wigs, like a great sunflower pushing its way at the sun from among a rank garden-full of flaring companions.

Mr. Stryver, a darling of the Old Bailey and the Sessions alike, had started carefully to kick away the lower rungs of the ladder he was climbing. Now the Sessions and Old Bailey were compelled to beckon their cherished advocate into their eager embrace with special summons; and thrusting itself toward the face of the Lord Chief Justice in the Court of King’s Bench, Mr. Stryver’s ruddy visage could be spotted daily, erupting from the wig-beds like an enormous sunflower straining toward the sun amid a dense garden of garish blooms.

That night Mrs. Britling made the supreme effort of her life to burst the prison of self-consciousness and inhibition in which she was confined. Never before in all her life had she so desired to be spontaneous and unrestrained; never before had she so felt herself hampered by her timidity, her self-criticism, her deeply ingrained habit of never letting herself go. She was rent by reflected distress. It seemed to her that she would be ready to give her life and the whole world to be able to comfort her husband now. And she could conceive no gesture of comfort. She went out of the dining-room into the hall and listened. She went very softly upstairs until she came to the door of her husband's room. There she stood still. She could hear no sound from within. She put out her hand and turned the handle of the door a little way, and then she was startled by the loudness of the sound it made and at her own boldness. She withdrew her hand, and then with a gesture of despair, with a face of white agony, she flitted along the corridor to her own room.

That evening, Eleanor summoned a desperate strength to shatter the cage of reserve that had always entombed her soul. Never before had she yearned so fiercely for a moment of unstudied grace; never had she felt so paralyzed by her own caution and the iron-clad habits of a lifetime’s restraint. She was ravaged by a borrowed sorrow. It seemed to her that she would gladly have surrendered her very life to offer her husband some measure of peace, yet she could conceive of no gesture that might bridge the distance between them. She slipped from the dining room into the dim hallway and ascended the stairs with ghostly quiet until she reached his door. There, she waited. No sound emerged from the room beyond. She extended a trembling hand and began to turn the latch, but the sudden, sharp click of the mechanism struck her like a physical blow. Appalled by her own daring, she recoiled. With a face of blanched despair, she turned and fled through the shadows to the sanctuary of her own chamber.

This sound disturbed an old woman who was sleeping in a chair beside me. She was a hired nurse, the wife of one of the turnkeys, and her countenance expressed all those bad qualities which often characterise that class. The lines of her face were hard and rude, like that of persons accustomed to see without sympathising in sights of misery. Her tone expressed her entire indifference; she addressed me in English, and the voice struck me as one that I had heard during my sufferings.

The sound roused an aged woman who had been dozing in a chair beside me. She was a hired nurse, the wife of one of the jailers, and her face bore all those ill features so often seen in such women. The hard, coarse lines of her countenance were those of one long accustomed to look on suffering without pity. Her manner showed her complete indifference; she spoke to me in English, and the voice seemed one I had already heard in the midst of my own misery.

Mr. Oak carried about him, by way of watch, what may be called a small silver clock; in other words, it was a watch as to shape and intention, and a small clock as to size. This instrument being several years older than Oak’s grandfather, had the peculiarity of going either too fast or not at all. The smaller of its hands, too, occasionally slipped round on the pivot, and thus, though the minutes were told with precision, nobody could be quite certain of the hour they belonged to. The stopping peculiarity of his watch Oak remedied by thumps and shakes, and he escaped any evil consequences from the other two defects by constant comparisons with and observations of the sun and stars, and by pressing his face close to the glass of his neighbours’ windows, till he could discern the hour marked by the green-faced timekeepers within. It may be mentioned that Oak’s fob being difficult of access, by reason of its somewhat high situation in the waistband of his trousers (which also lay at a remote height under his waistcoat), the watch was as a necessity pulled out by throwing the body to one side, compressing the mouth and face to a mere mass of ruddy flesh on account of the exertion required, and drawing up the watch by its chain, like a bucket from a well.

Mr. Vale wore, for a watch, what might more properly have been called a little silver clock; that is to say, it was a watch in shape and purpose, and a clock only in its smallness. This piece of mechanism was older than his grandfather by several years, and possessed the singular habit of either running far too quickly or else refusing to run at all. The lesser hand, moreover, had a trick of sometimes slipping upon its pivot; so that while the minutes were shown with exactness, no one could be altogether sure to what hour they belonged. Vale cured the stopping by means of knocks and jolts, and escaped the inconvenience of the other two imperfections by perpetual reference to the sun and stars, together with such observations as he could make by pressing his face close against his neighbours’ panes, until he could distinguish the hour displayed in the green faces of the clocks within. It should be added that, his fob being hard of access by reason of its rather lofty position in the waistband of his trousers, which itself lay at a yet more remote elevation beneath his waistcoat, the watch had to be produced, of necessity, by throwing the body over to one side, compressing mouth and countenance into a mere crimson lump under the strain, and hauling the watch up by its chain as one might draw a bucket from a well.

She saw him stoop to the grass, pick up the winding lock which he had severed from her manifold tresses, twist it round his fingers, unfasten a button in the breast of his coat, and carefully put it inside. She felt powerless to withstand or deny him. He was altogether too much for her, and Bathsheba seemed as one who, facing a reviving wind, finds it blow so strongly that it stops the breath. He drew near and said, “I must be leaving you.”

She watched him bend toward the turf, gather up the curling strand he had cut from her abundant hair, wind it about his fingers, undo a button at the breast of his coat, and with grave care place it within. She felt no strength left in her to resist or refuse him. He was altogether beyond her, and Clara seemed like one who, meeting a freshening wind, finds it rise so fiercely that it checks the breath. He came closer and said, “I must leave you now.”

“Why, you’ll be safe to meet the reddleman that I told ye of,” said Fairway. “He’s only gone back to get his van. We heard that your niece and her husband were coming straight home as soon as they were married, and we are going down there shortly, to give ’em a song o’ welcome.”

“You’ll be in good time to meet the reddleman I spoke of,” said Hatherley. “He has only turned back for his van. We had word that your niece and her husband meant to come home at once after the wedding, and we shall be going down there presently to give them a welcoming song.”

After some words apart between the last comer and Fagin, they drew their chairs towards the fire; and the Jew, telling Oliver to come and sit by him, led the conversation to the topics most calculated to interest his hearers. These were, the great advantages of the trade, the proficiency of the Dodger, the amiability of Charley Bates, and the liberality of the Jew himself. At length these subjects displayed signs of being thoroughly exhausted; and Mr. Chitling did the same: for the house of correction becomes fatiguing after a week or two. Miss Betsy accordingly withdrew; and left the party to their repose.

Following a brief private exchange between Fagin and the newcomer, they pulled their seats toward the warmth of the fire. Beckoning Oliver to sit at his side, Fagin steered the talk toward subjects he knew would capture their attention: the lucrative nature of their business, the Dodger’s remarkable talent, the pleasant company of Charley Bates, and Fagin’s own generous spirit. In time, these topics were fully spent, as was Mr. Chitling, who found that a few weeks in the house of correction took a heavy toll on one's energy. Miss Betsy soon took her leave, allowing the rest of the company to seek their rest.

We consider four previously published datasets. Two of them, Riloff (Riloff et al., 2013) and SemEval-2018 (Van Hee et al., 2018 , were labeled via a hybrid approach of distant supervision for initial collection and manual annotation for actual labelling. The other two datasets, Ptacek (Ptáček et al., 2014) and SARC (Khodak et al., 2018) , are labeled using distant supervision. As mentioned earlier, we managed to collect 1,832 tweets from the Riloff dataset. SemEval-2018 is a balanced dataset consisting of 4,792 tweets. For the Ptacket dataset, we collected 27,177 tweets out of the 50K published tweet IDs. Finally, The SARC datasets consists of Reddit comments. In a setting similar to Hazarika et al. (2018) who publish state-of-theart results on this dataset, we consider two variants of SARC. SARC-balanced contains 154,702 comments with the same number of sarcastic and nonsarcastic comments, while SARC-imbalanced contains 103,135 comments with a ratio of about 20:80 between sarcastic and non-sarcastic comments.

metadata.title: iSarcasm: A Dataset of Intended Sarcasm

text: This study analyzes four datasets published in prior peer-reviewed work. Two of these corpora—the Riloff dataset (Riloff et al., 2013) and SemEval-2018 (Van Hee et al., 2018)—were annotated via a hybrid pipeline that uses distant supervision to generate an initial candidate pool, followed by manual annotation to produce final gold-standard labels. The remaining two datasets, the Ptáček corpus (Ptáček et al., 2014) and the Self-Annotated Reddit Corpus (SARC; Khodak et al., 2018), are labeled using only distant supervision. As noted in earlier sections, we recovered 1,832 valid tweets from the Riloff dataset for inclusion in our analyses. SemEval-2018 is a class-balanced corpus consisting of 4,792 total tweets. For the Ptáček dataset, we successfully retrieved 27,177 tweets from the 50,000 publicly released tweet IDs associated with the original publication. Finally, the SARC corpus comprises public Reddit comments rather than Twitter posts. Following the experimental setup of Hazarika et al. (2018), who report state-of-the-art performance on this corpus, we evaluate on two SARC variants: SARC-balanced, which contains 154,702 comments split evenly between sarcastic and non-sarcastic instances, and SARC-imbalanced, which includes 103,135 comments with a roughly 20:80 ratio of sarcastic to non-sarcastic samples.

Filtering For each word w i in the text, we retrieve from our wordlists (see §4) a set of possible diacritizations D i = d i 1 , ..., d i and their corresponding morphological analyses. This set is then further refined by intersecting it with the predicted morphological analysis for the word. Words that are not in our list get an empty set, indicating that their diacritization is not constrained. This stage leaves us with an average of 1.2 diacritic sequences for each known word. If we were to perform random selection from this list, we would achieve 87.1% exact-match word-level diacritization accuracy on our Modern Hebrew test corpus.

metadata.title: Nakdan: Professional Hebrew Diacritizer

text: Filtering: For each word \(w_i\) in the input text, we retrieve from our precompiled wordlists (see §4) a set of candidate diacritizations \(D_i = \{d_{i1}, ..., d_{ik}\}\) and their corresponding morphological analyses. This candidate set is then further refined by intersecting it with the predicted morphological analysis generated for the target word. Words absent from our wordlists are assigned an empty set, indicating that no constraints are placed on their diacritization. Following this stage, we retain an average of 1.2 valid diacritic sequences for each word present in the wordlists. If we were to select uniformly at random from these filtered candidate sets, we would obtain an 87.1% word-level exact-match diacritization accuracy on our Modern Hebrew test corpus.

Heaven opened then, indeed. The flash was almost too novel for its inexpressibly dangerous nature to be at once realized, and they could only comprehend the magnificence of its beauty. It sprang from east, west, north, south, and was a perfect dance of death. The forms of skeletons appeared in the air, shaped with blue fire for bones—dancing, leaping, striding, racing around, and mingling altogether in unparalleled confusion. With these were intertwined undulating snakes of green, and behind these was a broad mass of lesser light. Simultaneously came from every part of the tumbling sky what may be called a shout; since, though no shout ever came near it, it was more of the nature of a shout than of anything else earthly. In the meantime one of the grisly forms had alighted upon the point of Gabriel’s rod, to run invisibly down it, down the chain, and into the earth. Gabriel was almost blinded, and he could feel Bathsheba’s warm arm tremble in his hand—a sensation novel and thrilling enough; but love, life, everything human, seemed small and trifling in such close juxtaposition with an infuriated universe.

The sky truly split open right then. The burst of light was so startlingly unfamiliar that they didn’t immediately register its unspeakable peril, all they could grasp first was the staggering, unearthly beauty of it. It arced out from every point of the compass—east, west, north, south—a flawless, terrible dance of death. Skeleton shapes flickered into view in the open air, their bones forged from searing blue flame: dancing, leaping, striding, darting in circles, all blending together into a chaos no one could have imagined. Wriggling, coiling streaks of green lightning wove through them, and behind the whole swirling display loomed a wide, dimmer bank of diffused light. At the exact same moment, a sound tore out of every corner of the roiling sky that could only be called a shout; no human yell could ever match its power, but it aligned more with the raw, feral energy of a shout than any other earthly phenomenon. Right as that sound hit, one of those ghoulish, glowing shapes landed on the tip of Gabriel’s rod, snaking invisibly down its length, along the chain at its base, and straight into the soil. Gabriel was nearly blinded, and he could feel Bathsheba’s warm arm trembling where he clutched it, a jolt of something new and sharp and thrilling shooting through him at the contact; but love, life, every small, human concern felt trivial and tiny, pressed up so close against the unbridled rage of the universe itself.

Meantime, it was getting hotter and hotter in there. You see, the sun was beating down and warming up the iron more and more all the time. Well, when you are hot, that way, every little thing irritates you. When I trotted, I rattled like a crate of dishes, and that annoyed me; and moreover I couldn't seem to stand that shield slatting and banging, now about my breast, now around my back; and if I dropped into a walk my joints creaked and screeched in that wearisome way that a wheelbarrow does, and as we didn't create any breeze at that gait, I was like to get fried in that stove; and besides, the quieter you went the heavier the iron settled down on you and the more and more tons you seemed to weigh every minute. And you had to be always changing hands, and passing your spear over to the other foot, it got so irksome for one hand to hold it long at a time.

Meanwhile, it was growing hotter and hotter inside. The sun was beating down and steadily heating the iron more and more. And when you are that hot, everything begins to fret you. If I trotted, I clattered like a box of dishes, and that vexed me; besides, I could not bear that shield of mine slapping and banging, now against my breast, now about my back. If I fell into a walk, my joints would creak and squeal in that weary way a wheelbarrow does, and since we made no breeze at that pace, I was in danger of being roasted in that furnace. Worse still, the more quietly you went, the more the iron seemed to settle on you, and the more tons you appeared to weigh with every passing minute. And all the while you had to keep shifting your hands and changing your spear from one foot to the other, for it became so tiresome to hold it in one hand very long.

Martin talked for fifteen minutes with him, nor could Ruth have wished better behavior on her lover’s part. Not once did his eyes flash nor his cheeks flush, while the calmness and poise with which he talked surprised her. But in Martin’s estimation the whole tribe of bank cashiers fell a few hundred per cent, and for the rest of the evening he labored under the impression that bank cashiers and talkers of platitudes were synonymous phrases. The army officer he found good-natured and simple, a healthy, wholesome young fellow, content to occupy the place in life into which birth and luck had flung him. On learning that he had completed two years in the university, Martin was puzzled to know where he had stored it away. Nevertheless Martin liked him better than the platitudinous bank cashier.

Martin chatted with him for a full fifteen minutes, and Ruth couldn’t have asked for better behavior from her boyfriend. He never once let his eyes flash with irritation or his cheeks flush with frustration, and the steady, unflappable poise he carried through the whole conversation caught her completely off guard. But as far as Martin was concerned, the entire profession of bank cashiers plummeted a good few hundred points in his esteem, and for the rest of the evening he couldn’t shake the belief that “bank cashier” was just another term for someone who spouts empty, overused platitudes. The army officer, by contrast, struck him as good-natured and unpretentious, a healthy, well-adjusted young man who was perfectly content occupying the spot in life that birth and luck had tossed him into. When he learned the officer had completed two years of university, Martin was puzzled to figure out where he’d stored all that education, since he gave no obvious sign of it in casual interaction. Even so, Martin still liked him far better than the trite, cliché-ridden bank cashier.

Neural Machine Translation (NMT) (Sutskever et al., 2014; Vaswani et al., 2017) has significantly improved the translation quality due to its end-to-end modeling and continuous representation. While conventional NMT performs single pair translation well, training a separate model for each language pair is resource consuming, considering there are thousands of languages in the world. Therefore multilingual NMT is introduced to handle multiple language pairs in one model, reducing the online serving and offline training cost. Furthermore, the multilingual NMT framework facilitates the cross-lingual knowledge transfer to improve translation performance on low resource language pairs (Wang et al., 2019) .

metadata.title: Language-aware Interlingua for Multilingual Neural Machine Translation

text: Neural Machine Translation (NMT) (Sutskever et al., 2014; Vaswani et al., 2017) has achieved substantial gains in translation quality through its end-to-end learning paradigm and dense continuous representations. Although conventional NMT attains strong results on individual language pairs, dedicating a distinct model to each pair imposes considerable resource demands, especially given the existence of thousands of languages worldwide. To address this, multilingual NMT has been proposed to accommodate multiple language pairs within a single model, thereby curtailing both online serving overhead and offline training expenditure. Moreover, this multilingual framework enables cross-lingual transfer, boosting translation performance on low-resource pairs (Wang et al., 2019).

"That diary-hunting strategy is just the sort of thing that makes this war intellectually fascinating. Everything is being thought out and then tried over that can possibly make victory. The Germans go in for psychology much more than we do, just as they go in for war more than we do, but they don't seem to be really clever about it. So they set out to make all their men understand the war, while our chaps are singing 'Tipperary.' But what the men put down aren't the beautiful things they ought to put down; most of them shove down lists of their meals, some of the diaries are all just lists of things eaten, and a lot of them have written the most damning stuff about outrages and looting. Which the French are translating and publishing. The Germans would give anything now to get back these silly diaries. And now they have made an order that no one shall go into battle with any written papers at all.... Our people got so keen on documenting and the value of chance writings that one of the principal things to do after a German attack had failed had been to hook in the documentary dead, and find out what they had on them.... It's a curious sport, this body fishing. You have a sort of triple hook on a rope, and you throw it and drag. They do the same. The other day one body near Hooghe was hooked by both sides, and they had a tug-of-war. With a sharpshooter or so cutting in whenever our men got too excited. Several men were hit. The Irish--it was an Irish regiment--got him--or at least they got the better part of him....

“That diary-hunting business is exactly the kind of thing that makes this war so interesting to think about. Everything that might possibly help bring victory is being worked out and tested. The Germans are much more interested in psychology than we are, just as they are more interested in war itself, though they don’t seem especially clever at it. So they try to make all their men understand the war, while our fellows are singing ‘Tipperary.’ But what the men actually write down isn’t the lovely material it ought to be; most of them just make lists of their meals, and some of the diaries are nothing but inventories of what was eaten, while many have set down the most damaging accounts of outrages and looting. The French are translating and publishing them. The Germans would give anything now to have those foolish diaries back. And now they’ve issued an order that no one is to go into battle carrying any written papers at all.... Our people became so eager about keeping records and about the value of accidental writings that one of the main jobs after a German attack had failed was to hook in the documentary dead and see what they were carrying.... It’s a strange sport, this body fishing. You have a kind of triple hook on a rope, and you cast it and drag it along. They do the same. The other day a body near Hooghe was hooked by both sides, and they ended up in a tug-of-war, with a sharpshooter or two taking shots whenever our men got too worked up. Several men were hit. The Irish—it was an Irish regiment—got him, or at least they got the larger part of him....”

metadata.title: Learning to Update Natural Language Comments Based on Code Changes

text: Results: Table 3 reports the percentage of annotations (out of 500) for which users selected comment suggestions produced by each model. Using Krippendorff’s α (Krippendorff, 2011) with MASI distance (Passonneau, 2006), which is appropriate for our multi-label setting, inter-annotator agreement is 0.64, indicating satisfactory agreement. The reranked edit model outperforms the strongest baseline as well as reranked generation by wide, statistically significant margins. From the rationales supplied by two annotators, we observe that certain options were not chosen because they removed relevant information from the existing comment, and, unsurprisingly, these options often came from the comment generation model.

metadata.title: PHOTON: A Robust Cross-Domain Text-to-SQL System

text: GNN (Bogin et al., 2019a) 40.7, Global-GNN (Bogin et al., 2019b) 52.7, EditSQL + BERT 57.6, GNN+Bertrand-DR † (Kelkar et al., 2020) 57.9, EditSQL+Bertrand-DR † (Kelkar et al., 2020) 58.5, IRNet + BERT (Guo et al., 2019) 61.9, RYANSQL + BERT † (Choi et al., 2020) 66.6, and PHOTON 63.2. Here, † indicates unpublished work available on arXiv. As shown in Table 3, PHOTON attains a highly competitive text-to-SQL result on the Spider benchmark, reaching 63.2% exact set match accuracy on the Spider dev set, thereby confirming the effectiveness of our neural semantic parser in converting an input question into a syntactically and semantically valid SQL query.

He strove to stir himself and find something to interest him. He ventured the petty officers’ mess, and was glad to get away. He talked with a quartermaster off duty, an intelligent man who promptly prodded him with the socialist propaganda and forced into his hands a bunch of leaflets and pamphlets. He listened to the man expounding the slave-morality, and as he listened, he thought languidly of his own Nietzsche philosophy. But what was it worth, after all? He remembered one of Nietzsche’s mad utterances wherein that madman had doubted truth. And who was to say? Perhaps Nietzsche had been right. Perhaps there was no truth in anything, no truth in truth—no such thing as truth. But his mind wearied quickly, and he was content to go back to his chair and doze.

He tried to rouse himself and find some interest to occupy him. He went into the petty officers’ mess, but was glad enough to leave it. He spoke with a quartermaster who was off duty, an intelligent fellow who at once assailed him with socialist propaganda and pressed a handful of leaflets and pamphlets into his hands. He listened while the man laid out the doctrine of slave morality, and, as he did, he half-heartedly thought of his own Nietzschean ideas. But what were they worth, after all? He recalled one of Nietzsche’s wild pronouncements, in which that same madman had cast doubt on truth. And who could say? Perhaps Nietzsche had been right. Perhaps there was no truth in anything, no truth even in truth—no such thing as truth at all. But his mind tired soon enough, and he was content to return to his chair and drowse.

We evaluate our error hypotheses on a baseline and three of the most recent state-of-the-art RE models. None of the models were part of the set of models used for selecting challenging instances (Section 3.1), so as not to bias the automatic evaluation. As the baseline we use a single layer CNN (Zeng et al., 2014 ; Nguyen and Grishman, 2015) with max-pooling and 300-dimensional GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014) embeddings as input. The state-of-the-art models use pre-trained language models (LM) fine-tuned to the RE task and include: TRE (Alt et al., 2019) , which uses the unidirectional OpenAI Generative Pre-Trained Transformer (GPT) (Radford et al., 2018) ; SpanBERT , which employs a bidirectional LM similar to BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) but is pre-trained on span-level; and KnowBERT (Peters et al., 2019) , which is an extension to BERT that integrates external knowledge. In particular, we use KnowBERT-W+W, which is trained by joint entity linking and language modelling on Wikipedia and WordNet.

metadata.title: TACRED Revisited: A Thorough Evaluation of the TACRED Relation Extraction Task

text: We assess our error hypotheses using a baseline and three recent state-of-the-art relation extraction (RE) models. To avoid biasing the automatic evaluation, none of these models were included in the set employed for selecting challenging instances (Section 3.1). Our baseline consists of a single-layer CNN (Zeng et al., 2014; Nguyen and Grishman, 2015) with max-pooling that takes 300-dimensional GloVe embeddings (Pennington et al., 2014) as input. The state-of-the-art models leverage pre-trained language models (LMs) fine-tuned for RE: TRE (Alt et al., 2019), which relies on the unidirectional OpenAI Generative Pre-Trained Transformer (GPT) (Radford et al., 2018); SpanBERT, a bidirectional LM akin to BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) but pre-trained on span-level representations; and KnowBERT (Peters et al., 2019), an augmented BERT variant that incorporates external knowledge. Specifically, we adopt KnowBERT-W+W, which is trained via joint entity linking and language modeling on Wikipedia and WordNet.

Dulcie went up to her room—the third floor back in a West Side brownstone-front. She lit the gas. Scientists tell us that the diamond is the hardest substance known. Their mistake. Landladies know of a compound beside which the diamond is as putty. They pack it in the tips of gas-burners; and one may stand on a chair and dig at it in vain until one’s fingers are pink and bruised. A hairpin will not remove it; therefore let us call it immovable.

Dulcie climbed the stairs to her room, the rear unit on the third floor of a West Side brownstone. She turned on the gas. Any scientist will tell you diamond is the hardest substance known to humankind. They’re wrong. Landladies are familiar with a compound next to which diamond is as soft as putty. They pack this stuff right into the tips of gas burners; you can stand on a chair and dig at it fruitlessly until your fingers are pink and bruised. Not even a hairpin can pry it loose, so we might as well call it entirely immovable.

I beseech you, sir, do not say you mean to banish me!" Julian cried, struck with terror by the elder man’s grave delivery. "Do not cast me back to the wilderness of the streets. Let me stay and serve you. Only do not return me to that den of misery. Have pity, I implore you, on a friendless boy!

I was at the well next day betimes. Merlin was there, enchanting away like a beaver, but not raising the moisture. He was not in a pleasant humor; and every time I hinted that perhaps this contract was a shade too hefty for a novice he unlimbered his tongue and cursed like a bishop--French bishop of the Regency days, I mean.

I showed up at the weathered stone well bright and early the next morning. Kaelen was already there, chanting incantations as busily as a beaver piling dam sticks, but not a single drop of moisture rose from the dry basin. His mood was thoroughly sour; every time I dropped a gentle hint that the contract he’d signed on for might be a little too hefty for an inexperienced spellcaster, he let loose a volley of curses coarse enough to match a bishop – a French Regency bishop, that is, the sort far fonder of wine and debauchery than pious prayer.

He went away vexed with himself, and ashamed of having for this one time in his life done anything which could be called underhand. Poor Boldwood had no more skill in finesse than a battering-ram, and he was uneasy with a sense of having made himself to appear stupid and, what was worse, mean. But he had, after all, lighted upon one fact by way of repayment. It was a singularly fresh and fascinating fact, and though not without its sadness it was pertinent and real. In little more than six years from this time Bathsheba might certainly marry him. There was something definite in that hope, for admitting that there might have been no deep thought in her words to Liddy about marriage, they showed at least her creed on the matter.

He stormed off furious at his own choices, mortified that for the first time in his entire life he’d done something anyone could label sneaky or underhanded. Boldwood was never any good at subtle maneuvering, after all—he had as much tact as a sledgehammer. He squirmed under the conviction that he’d come off not just foolish, but far worse: petty and small. Still, he’d stumbled on one small truth as a sort of consolation prize. It was such a vivid, thrilling little detail, and even if it carried a twinge of melancholy, it was unshakably concrete and relevant. A little over six years from that day, Bathsheba could very well agree to marry him. That hope had a real, tangible weight to it; even if she hadn’t put much careful thought into what she’d told Liddy about her marriage plans, those offhand words still revealed her core beliefs on the subject.

The finish beyond which philosophy cannot go and does not wish to go,
The justified mother of men.

text:
The resonant nature of the soil,  
The edge past which thought cannot reach and has no hunger to reach,  
The rightful mother of humankind.

As my sickness quitted me, I was absorbed by a gloomy and black melancholy that nothing could dissipate. The image of Clerval was for ever before me, ghastly and murdered. More than once the agitation into which these reflections threw me made my friends dread a dangerous relapse. Alas! Why did they preserve so miserable and detested a life? It was surely that I might fulfil my destiny, which is now drawing to a close. Soon, oh, very soon, will death extinguish these throbbings and relieve me from the mighty weight of anguish that bears me to the dust; and, in executing the award of justice, I shall also sink to rest. Then the appearance of death was distant, although the wish was ever present to my thoughts; and I often sat for hours motionless and speechless, wishing for some mighty revolution that might bury me and my destroyer in its ruins.

When my illness had at last departed, I became possessed by a heavy and funereal melancholy from which nothing could deliver me. The figure of Hartwell was ever before my eyes, pale, distorted, and slain. More than once the turmoil into which such meditations drove me caused my companions to fear a fatal relapse. Alas! Why had they spared so wretched and loathed a life? Surely only that I might complete the fate appointed me, which now seemed near its end. Soon, ah, very soon, death would extinguish these palpitations and release me from the vast burden of sorrow that pressed me to the earth; and, while the sentence of justice was fulfilled, I too should descend into repose. At that time death still appeared remote, though the desire for it was never absent from my mind; and I would often remain for hours without motion or speech, longing for some mighty convulsion that might bury both me and my persecutor beneath its ruins.

Two weeks had slipped by since Martin last encountered him, and he racked his mind in vain for any slight he might have given. The dime took Martin on the ferry to San Francisco, and as he strode up Market Street he considered what would become of him if he couldn’t obtain the money. He’d have no means to get back to Oakland, and he was acquainted with no one in San Francisco who could lend him another dime.

“Boys, there's a good many curious things about law, and custom, and usage, and all that sort of thing, when you come to look at it; yes, and about the drift and progress of human opinion and movement, too. There are written laws--they perish; but there are also unwritten laws--_they_ are eternal. Take the unwritten law of wages: it says they've got to advance, little by little, straight through the centuries. And notice how it works. We know what wages are now, here and there and yonder; we strike an average, and say that's the wages of to-day. We know what the wages were a hundred years ago, and what they were two hundred years ago; that's as far back as we can get, but it suffices to give us the law of progress, the measure and rate of the periodical augmentation; and so, without a document to help us, we can come pretty close to determining what the wages were three and four and five hundred years ago. Good, so far. Do we stop there? No. We stop looking backward; we face around and apply the law to the future. My friends, I can tell you what people's wages are going to be at any date in the future you want to know, for hundreds and hundreds of years.”

“Boys, there are a good many curious things about law, and custom, and usage, and all that sort of business, when you come to study them; yes, and about the drift and progress of human opinion and action, too. There are written laws—they die; but there are also unwritten laws—they endure forever. Take the unwritten law of wages: it declares that wages must rise, little by little, through the centuries. And see how it operates. We know what wages are now, here and there, everywhere; we strike an average and call that the wages of today. We know what wages were a hundred years ago, and what they were two hundred years ago; that is as far back as we can go, but it is enough to give us the law of progress, the rate and measure of the recurring increase; and so, without a single document to guide us, we can get pretty close to knowing what wages were three hundred, four hundred, and five hundred years ago. Very well, so far. Do we stop there? No. We turn from the past and apply the law to the future. My friends, I can tell you what men’s wages are going to be at any date in the future you care to name, for hundreds and hundreds of years.”

Maryann, who had been afraid to shout in the robber’s presence, having seen him depart had no fear. She hastily slipped on her clothes, stumped down the disjointed staircase with its hundred creaks, ran to Coggan’s, the nearest house, and raised an alarm. Coggan called Gabriel, who now again lodged in his house as at first, and together they went to the paddock. Beyond all doubt the horse was gone.

Maryann, who had been too afraid to cry out while the robber stood there, now that she had seen him go, felt no fear at all. She hurriedly dressed, clattered down the broken staircase, which gave a hundred creaks beneath her feet, ran to Coggan’s, the nearest house, and sounded the alarm. Coggan called Gabriel, who was once more lodging in his house as he had at first, and the two of them went together to the paddock. There could be no question of it: the horse had been taken.

metadata.title: Improving Truthfulness of Headline Generation

text: The experimental setup adopts the identical data partitioning into training (3.8 million instances), development (390,000 instances), and test (380,000 instances) sets as employed by Rush et al. (2015). For evaluation in this work, we draw a sample of 10,000 instances from the test set, which were excluded from the analysis presented in Section 3. No replacement operations—such as digit masking, mapping rare words to UNK, or lowercasing—are applied to the English Gigaword corpus. The dataset is tokenized using WordPiece (Wu et al., 2016) with the same vocabulary as that utilized in UniLM.

“Tell the gentleman that I need not repeat to him a description of my ideals. He knows what they have been and what they still are. So far as they touch on this case, absolute loyalty and truth are the ones paramount. Tell him that I have studied my own heart as well as one can, and I know its weakness as well as I do its needs. That is why I decline to hear his pleas, whatever they may be. I did not condemn him through hearsay or doubtful evidence, and that is why I made no charge. But, since he persists in hearing what he already well knows, you may convey the matter.

“Inform the gentleman that there is no need for me to restate my principles once more. He is fully aware of what they were and what they remain today. In relation to this matter, unwavering loyalty and honesty are my highest priorities. Tell him that I have scrutinized my own soul as thoroughly as one can, and I understand its vulnerabilities just as clearly as its requirements. This is precisely why I refuse to listen to his petitions, no matter what they might be. I did not judge him based on rumors or questionable proof, which is why I never leveled a formal accusation. Yet, since he insists on hearing what he already understands perfectly well, you may relay the facts to him.”

Troy was full of activity, but his activities were less of a locomotive than a vegetative nature; and, never being based upon any original choice of foundation or direction, they were exercised on whatever object chance might place in their way. Hence, whilst he sometimes reached the brilliant in speech because that was spontaneous, he fell below the commonplace in action, from inability to guide incipient effort. He had a quick comprehension and considerable force of character; but, being without the power to combine them, the comprehension became engaged with trivialities whilst waiting for the will to direct it, and the force wasted itself in useless grooves through unheeding the comprehension.

Troy was always on the move, but his constant activity was less purposeful than a steam engine and more like the unplanned spread of a climbing plant; he never chose a clear core aim or direction for his efforts, so he simply poured energy into whatever random thing chance dropped in front of him. That’s why, while he could occasionally turn out brilliantly sharp, off-the-cuff lines when speaking, his actions were far worse than unremarkable—he simply couldn’t guide the first flickers of effort into anything coherent. He had a quick, perceptive mind and no shortage of inherent force of character, but he lacked the ability to make those two strengths work in tandem. His sharp perception would fixate on meaningless small details while it waited for his will to give it a clear goal, and all that inherent grit burned away on pointless, repetitive dead ends because he never let his good judgment steer it.

_When I bring these charges against Germany I have little disposition to claim any righteousness for Britain. There has been small splendour in this war for either Germany or Britain or Russia; we three have chanced to be the biggest of the combatants, but the glory lies with invincible France. It is France and Belgium and Serbia who shine as the heroic lands. They have fought defensively and beyond all expectation, for dear land and freedom. This war for them has been a war of simple, definite issues, to which they have risen with an entire nobility. Englishman and German alike may well envy them that simplicity. I look to you, as an honest man schooled by the fierce lessons of this war, to meet me in my passionate desire to see France, Belgium and Serbia emerge restored from all this blood and struggle, enlarged to the limits of their nationality, vindicated and secure. Russia I will not write about here; let me go on at once to tell you about my own country; remarking only that between England and Russia there are endless parallelisms. We have similar complexities, kindred difficulties. We have for instance an imported dynasty, we have a soul-destroying State Church which cramps and poisons the education of our ruling class, we have a people out of touch with a secretive government, and the same traditional contempt for science. We have our Irelands and Polands. Even our kings bear a curious likeness...._

Oliver Twist’s ninth birthday found him a pale thin child, somewhat diminutive in stature, and decidedly small in circumference. But nature or inheritance had implanted a good sturdy spirit in Oliver’s breast. It had had plenty of room to expand, thanks to the spare diet of the establishment; and perhaps to this circumstance may be attributed his having any ninth birth-day at all. Be this as it may, however, it _was_ his ninth birthday; and he was keeping it in the coal-cellar with a select party of two other young gentleman, who, after participating with him in a sound thrashing, had been locked up for atrociously presuming to be hungry, when Mrs. Mann, the good lady of the house, was unexpectedly startled by the apparition of Mr. Bumble, the beadle, striving to undo the wicket of the garden-gate.

No one could have imagined that such a mode of cultivation would yield a thriving or abundant harvest. On the morning of his ninth birthday, Oliver Twist was a pallid, slender boy, short for his age and alarmingly narrow around the middle. Still, either through birth or some hidden bequest, a tenacious spirit had taken root inside him. It had been granted ample space to grow, owing to the meager rations of the workhouse, and it may well have been this very fact that allowed him to reach a ninth birthday at all. Whatever the truth of it, that day was indeed his ninth birthday, and he was spending it in the coal-cellar alongside a chosen company of two other boys, who, after sharing a vigorous beating with him, had been shut away for the outrageous crime of daring to feel hunger. Just then, Mrs. Mann, the household’s kindly matron, was suddenly alarmed by the sight of Mr. Bumble the beadle, struggling to unfasten the latch of the garden gate.

But, in the composure of his manner he was unaltered, except that to the shrewd glance of Mr. Lorry it disclosed some shadowy indication that the old air of avoidance and dread had lately passed over him, like a cold wind.

The door to the doctor’s chambers swung open, and he stepped out alongside Charles Darnay. His face was ghastly pale, drained of every last trace of color—a stark change from how he’d looked when the two of them had gone inside together only moments earlier. His steady, unruffled manner was otherwise entirely unchanged, save for a faint, fleeting tell that only Mr. Lorry’s shrewd, practiced eye caught: the old, familiar air of avoidance and dread had just passed over him, sharp and quick as a gust of icy wind.

The book was closed on his forefinger, and before he turned he was thrilling to the first new impression, which was not of the girl, but of her brother’s words. Under that muscled body of his he was a mass of quivering sensibilities. At the slightest impact of the outside world upon his consciousness, his thoughts, sympathies, and emotions leapt and played like lambent flame. He was extraordinarily receptive and responsive, while his imagination, pitched high, was ever at work establishing relations of likeness and difference. “Mr. Eden,” was what he had thrilled to—he who had been called “Eden,” or “Martin Eden,” or just “Martin,” all his life. And “_Mister_!” It was certainly going some, was his internal comment. His mind seemed to turn, on the instant, into a vast camera obscura, and he saw arrayed around his consciousness endless pictures from his life, of stoke-holes and forecastles, camps and beaches, jails and boozing-kens, fever-hospitals and slum streets, wherein the thread of association was the fashion in which he had been addressed in those various situations.

The book rested closed upon his forefinger, and before he turned he felt a tremor from a fresh impression—not of the girl herself, but of her brother’s phrasing. Beneath that muscular frame he was a knot of trembling sensitivity. At the lightest touch of the outer world upon his awareness, his thoughts, sympathies, and feelings would leap and flicker like dancing flame. He was exceptionally open and reactive, and his high-strung imagination constantly wove connections of similarity and contrast. “Mr. Eden”—that was what had stirred him, he who had been called “Eden,” or “Martin Eden,” or simply “Martin,” his entire life. And “_Mister_!” That’s really something, he remarked inwardly. His mind seemed to shift, in a flash, into a vast dark chamber, and around his consciousness he glimpsed endless scenes from his past: stokeholds and forecastles, camps and shorelines, jails and drinking dens, fever wards and slum alleys, all threaded together by the way he had been addressed in each of those places.

The training process illustrated in Figure 1 (a) consists of a mutual back-translation procedure. The anchored source sentence "s 1 s 2 t s 3 t s 4 " is translated into target sentence "t 1 t 2 t 3 " by using source-to-target decoding, then "t 1 t 2 t 3 " and "s 1 s 2 t s 3 t s 4 " constitute a sentence pair for training the target-to-source translation model. In contrast, the target sentence "t 1 t 2 t 3 t 4 t 5 " is translated into anchored source sentence "s 1 s 2 s 3 t s 4 " by using target-to-source decoding, then both sen-tences constitute a sentence pair for training the source-to-target translation model. Note that during training the translation model, the input sentences are always pseudo sentences generated by decoding an MT model, while the output sentences are always true or anchored true sentences. Beside this mutual back-translation procedure, a denoising procedure used in unsupervised MT (Lample et al., 2018b ) is also adopted. The deletion and permutation noises are added to the source/target sentence, and the translation model is also trained to denoise them into the original source/target sentence.

metadata.title: Bilingual Dictionary Based Neural Machine Translation without Using Parallel Sentences

text: The training framework, as depicted in Figure 1 (a), is centered on a mutual back-translation objective. Specifically, an anchored source sentence "$s_1 s_2 t s_3 t s_4$" is mapped to a target sequence "$t_1 t_2 t_3$" via source-to-target decoding; the resulting pair is then utilized to optimize the target-to-source translation model. Conversely, the target sentence "$t_1 t_2 t_3 t_4 t_5$" is translated into the anchored source "$s_1 s_2 s_3 t s_4$" through target-to-source decoding, forming a training pair for the source-to-target model. It should be noted that during this training phase, the model inputs are consistently pseudo-sentences generated by the MT system, whereas the target outputs are always ground-truth or anchored ground-truth sentences. In addition to this mutual back-translation scheme, we incorporate a denoising procedure standard in unsupervised MT (Lample et al., 2018b). This involves applying deletion and permutation noise to the source and target sentences, requiring the translation model to reconstruct the original, noise-free sequences.

It was not a reckless manner, the manner in which he said these words aloud under the fast-sailing clouds, nor was it more expressive of negligence than defiance. It was the settled manner of a tired man, who had wandered and struggled and got lost, but who at length struck into his road and saw its end.

It was not a wild gesture, the way he spoke those words into the racing sky, nor did it betray carelessness any more than rebellion. It was the steady bearing of a weary man, who had roamed and fought and lost his way, but who had finally found his path and glimpsed its close.

But there was no longer any Herr Heinrich--and somehow German games were already out of fashion. The two philosophers admitted that they had already considered skat to be complicated without subtlety, and that its chief delight for them had been the pink earnestness of Herr Heinrich, his inability to grasp their complete but tacit comprehension of its innocent strategy, and his invariable ill-success to bring off the coups that flashed before his imagination.

They used to play skat regularly with Herr Heinrich, who had taught them the rules with painstaking clarity. But Herr Heinrich was gone now—and, in any case, German card games had fallen from favor. The two philosophers confessed that they had long found skat elaborate yet lacking in real depth, and that its main pleasure had lain in Herr Heinrich’s flushed seriousness, his failure to perceive that they fully yet silently grasped its guileless tactics, and his unfailing inability to execute the brilliant moves his mind kept conjuring.

Miss Vye’s family is a good one on her mother’s side; and her father was a romantic wanderer—a sort of Greek Ulysses.”

“Hate you—no,” Thomasin said in a gentle tone. “It’s simply that he cares for her too deeply. Consider it calmly—please do. It’s not such a terrible fault in him. You know, I didn’t think it was the poorest choice he could have made. Miss Vye’s lineage is respectable through her mother, and her father was a fanciful rover—a kind of Grecian Ulysses.”

• Impact of semantic graph. When we do not employ the semantic graph (A2, -w/o Semantic Graph), the BLEU-4 score of our model dramatically drops to 13.85, which indicates the necessity of building semantic graphs to model semantic relations between relevant content for deep QG. Despite its vital role, result of A1 shows that generating questions purely from the semantic graph is unsatisfactory. We posit three reasons: 1) the semantic graph alone is insufficient to convey the meaning of the entire document, 2) sequential information in the passage is not captured by the graph, and that 3) the automatically built semantic graph inevitably contains much noise. These reasons ne-cessitate the composite document representation.

metadata.title: Semantic Graphs for Generating Deep Questions

text: The influence of the semantic graph is substantial. Excluding the semantic graph (A2, -w/o Semantic Graph) causes the model’s BLEU-4 score to fall sharply to 13.85, underscoring its essential role in capturing semantic relationships among relevant content for deep question generation. Nevertheless, the outcome for A1 demonstrates that generating questions solely from the semantic graph yields inadequate results. We attribute this to three factors: 1) the semantic graph by itself cannot fully represent the meaning of the whole document, 2) it fails to encode sequential information present in the passage, and 3) the automatically constructed semantic graph inevitably introduces considerable noise. These limitations make the composite document representation indispensable.

“Heh-heh! well, I wish to noise nothing abroad—nothing at all,” murmured Poorgrass, diffidently. “But we be born to things—that’s true. Yet I would rather my trifle were hid; though, perhaps, a high nater is a little high, and at my birth all things were possible to my Maker, and he may have begrudged no gifts.... But under your bushel, Joseph! under your bushel with ’ee! A strange desire, neighbours, this desire to hide, and no praise due. Yet there is a Sermon on the Mount with a calendar of the blessed at the head, and certain meek men may be named therein.”

“Ah—well, I’ve no wish to trumpet anything abroad—nothing whatever,” murmured Moulden, falteringly. “Yet we are born to our lots—that much is certain. Still, I’d sooner my small portion stayed concealed; though, perhaps, a lofty nature is a trifle lofty, and at my making all gifts lay within the Creator’s power, and He may have withheld none.... But keep it beneath your hat, Thomas! beneath your hat, I say! A queer instinct, friends, this urge to hide, and no credit in it. And yet there stands the Beatitudes with a roll of the favoured at the start, and certain unassuming souls may find their place there.”

He won thoughtfully and chiefly through the ardour of the younger brother, whose enthusiastic returns invariably went out. Instantly the boys attacked Mrs. Britling with a concerted enthusiasm. "Mummy! Is it to be dressing-up supper?"

They came upon the German tutor in a small courtyard, playing badminton with the two younger boys. He was a stout young fellow with spectacles and neat, economical movements; the game proceeded largely by missed strokes, and the tally was kept in German. He won in a deliberate way, owing chiefly to the younger boy’s zeal, for his ardent returns were forever going wide. At once the children assailed Mrs. Darrell with a united burst of eagerness. “Mummy! Is it going to be fancy-dress supper?”

“Certainly,” replied the shorter man; “and whatever Mr. Giles says, it isn’t our place to contradict him. No, no, I know my sitiwation! Thank my stars, I know my sitiwation.” To tell the truth, the little man _did_ seem to know his situation, and to know perfectly well that it was by no means a desirable one; for his teeth chattered in his head as he spoke.

“Assuredly,” answered the smaller of the two; “and whatever Mr. Bell says, it is not for us to gainsay him. No, no, I understand my position well enough! Heaven be praised, I understand my position.” In truth, the little fellow did appear to understand it, and to know very thoroughly that it was anything but an enviable one; for his teeth were actually rattling in his head as he spoke.

"And another thing I find noteworthy is the way in which what I might call mature people seem to go on having a good time instead of standing by and applauding the young people having a good time.... And the young people do not seem to have set out to have a good time at all.... Now in America, a charming girl like Miss Corner would be distinctly more aware of herself and her vitality than she is here, distinctly more. Her peculiarly charming sidelong look, if I might make so free with her--would have been called attention to. It's a perfectly beautiful look, the sort of look some great artist would have loved to make immortal. It's a look I shall find it hard to forget.... But she doesn't seem to be aware in the least of it. In America she would be aware of it. She would be distinctly aware of it. She would have been _made_ aware of it. She would have been advised of it. It would be looked for and she would know it was looked for. She would _give_ it as a singer gives her most popular song. Mamie Nelson, for example, used to give a peculiar little throw back of the chin and a laugh.... It was talked about. People came to see it....

Another thing that strikes me as worth pointing out is how grown adults, for lack of a better term, still seem to prioritize enjoying themselves instead of hanging on the sidelines cheering on young people as they have fun. And the young folks here don’t even seem like they’re trying to have a good time in the first place.

Take someone as lovely as Miss Corner, for instance: back in the States, she’d be so much more conscious of her own energy and appeal than she is here, no question. That little sideways glance of hers, if I can be so forward as to mention it, would’ve been pointed out to her ages ago. It’s such a gorgeous expression, the kind a master painter would jump at the chance to capture forever. I don’t think I’ll ever be able to get it out of my head, honestly.

But she has no idea she even does it, not a clue. In the US, she’d know. She’d know full well. People would’ve made sure she knew. Someone would’ve mentioned it to her outright. Everyone would be waiting for that look, and she’d know they were waiting. She’d serve it up on purpose, the same way a singer trots out her most beloved hit for an adoring crowd. Take Mamie Nelson, for example: she had this little habit of tilting her chin back and laughing a very specific way. Everyone talked about it. People would turn up to events just to see her do it.

"I'm content now in my mind--in a way. He wasn't anyhow there--unless he was dead. But if I saw Teddy coming over the hedge there to me--It would be just natural.... No, don't stare at me. I know really he is dead. And it is a comfort. It is peace.... All the thoughts of him being crushed dreadfully or being mutilated or lying and screaming--or things like that--they've gone. He's out of his spoilt body. He's my unbroken Teddy again.... Out of sight somewhere.... Unbroken.... Sleeping."

“I’m content now in my mind—in a way. He wasn’t there at all, really—unless he was dead. But if I saw Teddy coming over that hedge toward me—it would seem quite natural.... No, don’t stare like that. I know he is dead, truly. And it is a comfort. It brings peace.... All those thoughts of him being horribly crushed or mutilated or lying there screaming—or things like that—they’ve gone now. He’s out of that spoiled body. He’s my Teddy again, whole and untouched.... Somewhere out of sight.... Whole.... Sleeping.”
