At length, in a fit of professional enthusiasm, he insisted upon producing his box of housebreaking tools: which he had no sooner stumbled in with, and opened for the purpose of explaining the nature and properties of the various implements it contained, and the peculiar beauties of their construction, than he fell over the box upon the floor, and went to sleep where he fell.

Once these preliminaries were adjusted, Mr. Sikes set to drinking brandy at a furious pace, while he brandished the crowbar in a most alarming fashion, all the while bellowing out most unmusical snatches of song, thickly interspersed with wild curses. At length, in a burst of professional zeal, he insisted on producing his box of housebreaking tools; but he had scarcely staggered in with it, and opened it to explain the use and qualities of the several instruments it held, together with the special merits of their workmanship, when he toppled over the box onto the floor and fell asleep where he lay.

Finally, we observe that using translations from online MT systems also outperforms referencebased evaluation. The differences are larger for Estonian-English. This could be because for into-English translation the quality of pseudo-references is higher, making them as good as actual reference translations, while yet closer to the MT output under evaluation. For English-Czech, pseudoreferences are closer to mt-ref and generally worse than hyp-mt-avg-ref. Table 3 illustrates the advantage of our uncertainty-aware evaluation over standard reference-based scoring. We show MC dropout and top beam hypotheses for a high quality and for a low quality MT output. First, note that MC dropout hypotheses are very different for a low-quality MT output and fairly similar for good-quality translation. By contrast, beam hypotheses are similar or the same in both cases. Second, the evaluation scores obtained using MC dropout hypotheses result in a large difference between low-quality and high-quality MT outputs, whereas Meteor assigns a higher score to the low-quality example due to surface word and synonym matches that are in this case not indicative of MT quality.

metadata.title: Multi-Hypothesis Machine Translation Evaluation

text: Finally, we observe that utilizing translations from online MT systems surpasses reference-based evaluation, with these discrepancies being more pronounced for the Estonian-English language pair. This may be attributed to the superior quality of pseudo-references in into-English translation, which renders them as effective as gold-standard references while remaining more similar to the evaluated MT output. In the case of English-Czech, pseudo-references align more closely with mt-ref and typically underperform compared to hyp-mt-avg-ref. Table 3 demonstrates the benefits of our uncertainty-aware evaluation approach relative to traditional reference-based metrics. We present MC dropout and top beam hypotheses for both high-quality and low-quality MT outputs. First, it is noteworthy that MC dropout hypotheses exhibit significant diversity for low-quality outputs but remain relatively consistent for high-quality translations. Conversely, beam hypotheses remain largely uniform across both quality levels. Second, scores derived from MC dropout hypotheses clearly distinguish between low- and high-quality translations; in contrast, Meteor yields a higher score for the low-quality sample because of lexical and synonym overlaps that, in this instance, do not accurately reflect translation quality.

Restless, shifting, fugacious as time itself is a certain vast bulk of the population of the red brick district of the lower West Side. Homeless, they have a hundred homes. They flit from furnished room to furnished room, transients forever—transients in abode, transients in heart and mind. They sing “Home, Sweet Home” in ragtime; they carry their _lares et penates_ in a bandbox; their vine is entwined about a picture hat; a rubber plant is their fig tree.

Unquiet, mutable, and as evanescent as time itself is a certain great multitude of the inhabitants of the red-brick quarter of the lower West Side. Homeless, yet lodged in a hundred dwellings, they pass from furnished room to furnished room, eternal wanderers—wanderers in their roofs, wanderers in their hearts and thoughts. They croon “Home, Sweet Home” to a syncopated tune; they bear their _lares et penates_ in a hatbox; their vine winds itself about a feathered hat; a rubber plant serves them for a fig tree.

“Don’t ye play no more shepherd” said Susan Tall’s husband, the young married man who had spoken once before. “I must be moving and when there’s tunes going on I seem as if hung in wires. If I thought after I’d left that music was still playing, and I not there, I should be quite melancholy-like.”

“Don’t play any more, shepherd,” said Susan Tall’s husband, the young man who had spoken once before. “I must be on my way, and while the music continues, I feel as though I am suspended by wires. If I were to leave and knew the tunes were still playing without me there to hear them, I would feel quite dejected.”

I had gazed on him while unfinished; he was ugly then, but when those muscles and joints were rendered capable of motion, it became a thing such as even Dante could not have conceived.

Oh! No mortal could have borne the horror of that face. Not even a mummy restored to life could have been as ghastly as that miserable creature. I had looked at him when he was still unfinished; he had been ugly enough then, but once those muscles and joints were made capable of movement, he became a being such as even Dante could never have imagined.

Thus situated, employed in the most detestable occupation, immersed in a solitude where nothing could for an instant call my attention from the actual scene in which I was engaged, my spirits became unequal; I grew restless and nervous. Every moment I feared to meet my persecutor. Sometimes I sat with my eyes fixed on the ground, fearing to raise them lest they should encounter the object which I so much dreaded to behold. I feared to wander from the sight of my fellow creatures lest when alone he should come to claim his companion.

So placed, trapped in the most loathsome of tasks, sunk in a loneliness that offered not a single moment’s reprieve from the scene before me, my mood grew fractured; I turned edgy and unsettled. At every instant I dreaded encountering my tormentor. At times I sat staring rigidly at the ground, afraid to lift my gaze lest it meet the very thing I most feared to see. I grew afraid to stray from the presence of other people, lest in solitude he should appear to reclaim his companion.

I am in trouble.’”

“I can’t say much by way of explaining, ma’am. All I know is that, as I was walking along the road this morning, near a mile beyond Anglebury, I heard something coming after me at a trot, light as a doe’s step; and when I turned, there she was, pale as death. ‘Oh, Diggory Venn!’ she said, ‘I thought it was you—will you help me? I am in trouble.’”

“Yes, a little bit,” he confessed. “That is part of the adventure. It is not the being famous, but the process of becoming so, that counts. And after all, to be famous would be, for me, only a means to something else. I want to be famous very much, for that matter, and for that reason.”

“I suppose I do,” he admitted. “That uncertainty is what gives the journey its weight. It is not the state of being known, but the arduous climb toward it, that truly signifies. Besides, for me, such acclaim would be nothing more than a tool for a different end. I crave that distinction deeply, for that specific reason and that alone.”

She was sunk in a pit of blackness, with but that small square of pallid light framing the star that she had so whimsically and oh, so ineffectually named. Miss Longnecker must be right; it was Gamma, of the constellation Cassiopeia, and not Billy Jackson. And yet she could not let it be Gamma.

For Leo Marlow was shining down on her, calm, bright, unwavering, through the slanted glass skylight. The rest of the world had vanished entirely. She was sunk in a hollow of thick, unbroken blackness, with only that small square of pallid light framing the star she’d named on such a whimsical, and oh, so futile impulse. Ms. Hale must be right; it was Gamma, of the constellation Cassiopeia, and not Leo Marlow. And yet she could not bear to let it be Gamma.

We use the publicly available pretrained word embeddings GoogleNews word vectors (word2vec) , Flair (Akbik et al., 2018) , and BERT 3 (Devlin et al., 2018) to represent words. Flair embeddings were trained on the 1-billion word corpus used in Chelba et al. (2013) and the embedding size (both forward and backward) was 2048. As for BERT, we utilized bert-base-uncased model which is trained on the English Wikipedia (2.5G words) and BooksCorpus (0.8G words). We employed 2-layer Bi-LSTM with CRF to develop for GED model and set the hidden layer size of Bi-LSTM to 256. We used SGD optimizer with learning rate 0.01, with maximum gradient norm set to 1. We applied dropout on both embedding and Bi-LSTM layers with dropout probabilities 0.5. We trained the network for 150 epochs and selected the best model with the highest F1 score on the development set.

metadata.title: LinggleWrite: a Coaching System for Essay Writing

text: We use the publicly available pretrained word embeddings GoogleNews word vectors (word2vec), Flair (Akbik et al., 2018), and BERT 3 (Devlin et al., 2018) to represent words. The Flair embeddings were trained on the 1-billion-word corpus used in Chelba et al. (2013), and the embedding size in each direction, forward and backward, was 2048. For BERT, we used the bert-base-uncased model, which is trained on English Wikipedia (2.5G words) and BooksCorpus (0.8G words). We employed a 2-layer Bi-LSTM with CRF to develop the GED model and set the Bi-LSTM hidden-layer size to 256. We used the SGD optimizer with a learning rate of 0.01 and set the maximum gradient norm to 1. We applied dropout to both the embedding and Bi-LSTM layers with a dropout probability of 0.5. We trained the network for 150 epochs and selected the best model according to the highest F1 score on the development set.

“I thank you,” replied I; “listen, therefore, to the deposition that I have to make. It is indeed a tale so strange that I should fear you would not credit it were there not something in truth which, however wonderful, forces conviction. The story is too connected to be mistaken for a dream, and I have no motive for falsehood.” My manner as I thus addressed him was impressive but calm; I had formed in my own heart a resolution to pursue my destroyer to death, and this purpose quieted my agony and for an interval reconciled me to life. I now related my history briefly but with firmness and precision, marking the dates with accuracy and never deviating into invective or exclamation.

“I am grateful,” I answered. “Please attend, then, to the statement I must deliver. It is a narrative so extraordinary that I would fear you might find it unbelievable, were it not for the inherent power of truth which, however incredible, demands belief. The events are too logically linked to be dismissed as a dream, and I have no reason to lie.” My demeanor as I spoke was solemn yet composed; I had already vowed within my heart to hunt my tormentor to the death, and this resolution stilled my agony, briefly reconciling me to existence. I then recounted my history concisely but with steady precision, noting each date accurately and never lapsing into bitter insults or emotional outbursts.

“Pretty fair--pretty fair. Chin a little lower, please--there, very good. Eyes too high; pray don't look at the horizon, look at the ground, ten steps in front of you. Ah--that is better, that is very good. Wait, please; you betray too much vigor, too much decision; you want more of a shamble. Look at me, please--this is what I mean.... Now you are getting it; that is the idea--at least, it sort of approaches it.... Yes, that is pretty fair. _But!_ There is a great big something wanting, I don't quite know what it is. Please walk thirty yards, so that I can get a perspective on the thing.... Now, then--your head's right, speed's right, shoulders right, eyes right, chin right, gait, carriage, general style right--everything's right! And yet the fact remains, the aggregate's wrong. The account don't balance. Do it again, please.... _Now_ I think I begin to see what it is. Yes, I've struck it. You see, the genuine spiritlessness is wanting; that's what's the trouble. It's all _amateur_--mechanical details all right, almost to a hair; everything about the delusion perfect, except that it don't delude.”

“Not bad, really not bad at all. Tuck your chin just a little lower, please—there, that’s perfect. Your eyes are tilted too far up, c’mon, don’t stare off at the horizon. Fix your gaze on the ground ten steps ahead of you, yeah? Ah, that’s way better, that works great. Hold on a second, though—you’re coming off too energized, too sure of yourself. You need to shamble more, drag your feet a little. Watch me, okay? This is what I’m talking about… Oh, you’re getting the hang of it now, that’s exactly the vibe—well, it’s close enough, anyway… Yeah, that’s pretty decent. But! There’s still something big missing, I can’t quite put my finger on it yet. Walk thirty yards for me, would you? So I can get a proper perspective on how it reads from a distance… Okay, let’s break this down—your head position’s right, pace is right, shoulders are right, eyes are right, chin is right, your gait, your posture, the whole general energy checks out—every last little detail is correct! But the fact remains, the full package is off. It doesn’t add up at all. Do it again, please… Now I think I finally see what the issue is. Yeah, I figured it out. Look, you don’t have that real, bone-deep lack of spirit, that’s what’s wrong. This is all total amateur hour—all the mechanical details are almost perfect, down to the smallest tiny thing, every part of the act is flawless, except for the fact that it doesn’t fool anyone for a second.”

PASCAL layer When we introduced our model, we motivated our design choice of placing PASCAL heads in the first layer in order to enrich the representations of words from their isolated embeddings by introducing contextualization from their parents. We ran an ablation study on the NC11 data in order to verify our hypothesis. As shown in Table 3a , the performance of our model on the validation sets is lower when placing Pascal heads in upper layers; a trend that we also observed with the LISA mechanism. These results corroborate the findings of Raganato and Tiedemann (2018) who noticed that, in the first layer, more attention heads solely focus on the word to be translated itself rather than its context. We can then deduce that enforcing syntactic dependencies in the first layer effectively leads to better word representations, which further enhance the translation accuracy of the Transformer model. Investigating the performance of multiple syntax-aware layers is left as future work.

metadata.title: Enhancing Machine Translation with Dependency-Aware Self-Attention

text: We introduced our model by motivating the decision to place PASCAL heads in the first layer, arguing that this design enriches isolated word embeddings with contextualization drawn from their syntactic parents. To test this hypothesis, we conducted an ablation study on the NC11 dataset. As reported in Table 3a, model performance on the validation sets declines when PASCAL heads are situated in higher layers—a pattern we also observed with the LISA mechanism. These results align with the findings of Raganato and Tiedemann (2018), who noted that in the first layer a greater proportion of attention heads focus on the word being translated rather than on its surrounding context. We therefore infer that enforcing syntactic dependencies in the first layer yields improved word representations, which in turn boost the translation accuracy of the Transformer model. We leave the investigation of multiple syntax-aware layers for future work.

However, there is still a huge gap between high performance on the leader-board and poor practical user experience. Chatbot engines often generate responses that are logically incorrect or violate commonsense knowledge (Shum et al., 2018) . A likely reason is that current dialogue systems do not have strong reasoning skills, and most of the cases in previous benchmarks can be tackled by linguistic information matching. Previous work has demonstrated that neural encoders capture a rich hierarchy of syntactic and semantic information (Jawahar et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2019) . However, reasoning capability and commonsense knowledge are not captured sufficiently (Young et al., 2018) .

metadata.title: MuTual: A Dataset for Multi-Turn Dialogue Reasoning

text: However, a substantial gap remains between strong leaderboard performance and unsatisfactory real-world user experience. Chatbot engines frequently produce responses that are logically flawed or inconsistent with commonsense knowledge (Shum et al., 2018). One plausible explanation is that current dialogue systems lack robust reasoning abilities, and that many instances in prior benchmarks can be resolved through linguistic information matching alone. Prior studies have shown that neural encoders encode a rich hierarchy of syntactic and semantic information (Jawahar et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2019). Nevertheless, reasoning capacity and commonsense knowledge are not sufficiently captured (Young et al., 2018).

Its owner went downstairs into the courtyard, got into his carriage, and drove away. Not many people had talked with him at the reception; he had stood in a little space apart, and Monseigneur might have been warmer in his manner. It appeared, under the circumstances, rather agreeable to him to see the common people dispersed before his horses, and often barely escaping from being run down. His man drove as if he were charging an enemy, and the furious recklessness of the man brought no check into the face, or to the lips, of the master. The complaint had sometimes made itself audible, even in that deaf city and dumb age, that, in the narrow streets without footways, the fierce patrician custom of hard driving endangered and maimed the mere vulgar in a barbarous manner. But, few cared enough for that to think of it a second time, and, in this matter, as in all others, the common wretches were left to get out of their difficulties as they could.

The Count descended to the stone quadrangle, stepped into his waiting coach, and sped off. He had found few companions at the gala; he had stood in a pocket of isolation, and the Cardinal’s reception of him had been notably frigid. In light of this, it seemed to afford him a certain grim satisfaction to see the dregs of the city flee before his galloping horses, their lives held by a thread as they scrambled from his path. His driver steered with the ferocity of a cavalryman in mid-charge, yet this lethal speed provoked neither a frown nor a word of caution from the master. A murmur of protest was sometimes heard, even in that unhearing city and muted century, suggesting that in these tight thoroughfares lacking walkways, the haughty tradition of high-speed travel brutally crippled the poor. However, no one of consequence gave the matter a second thought, and in this, as in all things, the wretched populace was left to endure their misery as best they could.

Bathsheba loved Troy in the way that only self-reliant women love when they abandon their self-reliance. When a strong woman recklessly throws away her strength she is worse than a weak woman who has never had any strength to throw away. One source of her inadequacy is the novelty of the occasion. She has never had practice in making the best of such a condition. Weakness is doubly weak by being new.

Elspeth adored Torin with the fierce, unmoored devotion that only fiercely independent women know when they cast aside their own hard-won self-sufficiency. When a woman accustomed to holding her own carelessly discards that well-earned fortitude, she fares far worse than the soft, untested woman who never possessed such strength to squander in the first place. A core root of her helplessness lies in how entirely unacquainted she is with this state of being. She has never had cause to practice navigating, let alone making the most of, such vulnerable disarray. Fragility is doubly feeble when it is still new, unrooted in long habit.

Nearly half an hour was spent thus, and on returning to the house Wildeve had no thought of Thomasin being anywhere but in bed. He had told the stable lad not to stay up, leading the boy to understand that his departure would be at three or four in the morning; for this, though an exceptional hour, was less strange than midnight, the time actually agreed on, the packet from Budmouth sailing between one and two.

He made his way next to the carriage-house and stalls, wanting to be certain the mare, the trap, and all the tack were ready for a lengthy journey. A full thirty minutes passed in this inspection, and when he came back into the house Wildeve never imagined Thomasin would be anywhere save her bedroom. He had instructed the groom not to remain awake, giving the lad the impression they would set out at three or four in the morning—an odd hour, to be sure, yet far less peculiar than midnight, which was the hour truly fixed, for the Budmouth vessel sailed between one and two.

Second, we base our experiments on a dataset derived from the Enron corpus (Klimt and Yang, 2004) which consists of email exchanges in an American corporation. Thus, we restrict our attention to the notion of politeness as widely accepted by the speakers of North American English in a formal setting.

metadata.title: Politeness Transfer: A Tag and Generate Approach

text: For the purposes of this study, we explicitly delimit the operational scope of politeness per two guiding constraints: First, we adopt the data-driven definition of politeness proposed in Danescu-Niculescu-Mizil et al. (2013). Second, our experiments are run on a custom dataset derived from the Enron corpus (Klimt and Yang, 2004), a collection of internal email exchanges from employees of a United States-based corporation. Taken together, these constraints restrict our analysis exclusively to politeness norms as broadly accepted by speakers of North American English in formal professional settings.

Mr. Britling was full of the heady draught of liberal optimism he had been brewing upstairs. "I am not sorry I have lived to see this war," he said. "It may be a tremendous catastrophe in one sense, but in another it is a huge step forward in human life. It is the end of forty years of evil suspense. It is crisis and solution."

Mr. Britling was still buzzing off the heady rush of liberal optimism he’d been stewing on upstairs. “I don’t regret having lived long enough to see this war,” he said. “On one hand, it might be an unimaginable catastrophe, but on the other, it’s a massive leap forward for human existence. It wraps up forty years of poisonous, hanging uncertainty. It’s both the crisis and the resolution.”

They lunched precariously. Then they went into the study to smoke.

For a period they spoke of Hugh, yet Mr. Britling remained restless and ill-tempered, seemingly ready to make Mr. Direck answer for the War Office’s failures, the betrayal by Bulgaria, the evasiveness of Romania, or any other frustration currently vexing his spirit. They shared an uneasy, hurried lunch. Afterward, they withdrew to the study to smoke.

SUGILITE's approach is to ask users to verbally explain their intent for the demonstrated actions using speech. Our formative study (Li et al., 2018a) found that end users were able to provide useful and generalizable explanations for the intents of their demonstrated actions. They also commonly used in their utterances semantic references to GUI contents (e.g., "the close by restaurant" for an entry showing the text "596 ft") and implicit spatial references (e.g., "the score for Lakers" for a text object that contains a numeric value and is right-aligned to another text object "Lakers").

metadata.title: Interactive Task Learning from GUI-Grounded Natural Language Instructions and Demonstrations
metadata.section: Spoken Intent Clarification for Demonstrated Actions

text: The SUGILITE framework adopts an approach that prompts end users to verbally articulate the underlying intent behind their demonstrated actions via spoken input. Results from our earlier formative study (Li et al., 2018a) confirm that end users are consistently able to produce useful, generalizable explanations for the intents driving the interaction sequences they demonstrate. Analysis of collected user utterances further reveals that speakers frequently leverage two distinct reference patterns for interface elements: semantic references to underlying GUI content, such as referring to an entry displaying the text “596 ft” as “the close by restaurant”, and implicit spatial references to element positioning, such as describing a numeric text object that is right-aligned adjacent to the text element “Lakers” as “the score for Lakers.”

“--any fancies, any reasons, any apprehensions, anything whatsoever, new or old, against the man she really loved--the direct responsibility thereof not lying on his head--they should all be obliterated for her sake. She is everything to me; more to me than suffering, more to me than wrong, more to me--Well! This is idle talk.”

“--any whims, any justifications, any misgivings, anything at all, recent or ancient, concerning the man she truly cherished--provided the immediate blame did not rest upon him--they would all be erased for her benefit. She is my whole world; greater to me than agony, greater to me than injustice, greater to me--Ah! This is foolish chatter.”

“Poor little thing, her feelings got the better of her, no doubt. ’Tis nature. Well, they may call him what they will—he’ve several acres of heth-ground broke up here, besides the public house, and the heth-croppers, and his manners be quite like a gentleman’s. And what’s done cannot be undone.”

The poor girl was simply overcome by her feelings, no doubt. It is only natural. People may say what they wish about him—he has cultivated several acres of heath, owns the local inn, and keeps a stock of ponies. His manners are those of a true gentleman. Besides, what is done is done, and it cannot be altered now.

Strange sound it was, when the pale shadow spake;
For there was striving, in its piteous tongue,
To speak as when on earth it was awake,
And Isabella on its music hung:
Languor there was in it, and tremulous shake,
As in a palsied Druid's harp unstrung;
And through it moan'd a ghostly under-song,
Like hoarse night-gusts sepulchral briars among.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
An eerie voice arose from that wan wraith,  
Its mournful speech contending to express  
The waking tones it once possessed on earth,  
When Isabella clung to its caress:  
A weary falter, and a trembling dearth,  
Like some unstrung harp of a druidess;  
And through it sighed a spectral undertone,  
As rough night winds through tomb-entangled stone.

Now you mustn't take it unkindly if I remind you that as we don't know you, we must go a little slow. You may be all right, of course, and we'll hope that you are; but to take it for granted isn't business. _You_ understand that. I'm obliged to ask you a few questions; just answer up fair and square, and don't be afraid. Where do you live, when you are at home?”

“Well, I didn't suppose you had, but I wanted to ask just to be certain—that's how I was brought up. Now don't take offense if I point out that, since we're strangers to you, we need to proceed with some caution. You might be perfectly fine, and we certainly hope so, but assuming so without proof isn't sensible. _You_ see that. I have to put a few questions to you; just answer honestly and openly, and don't worry. Where do you make your home, when you're there?”

Five or ten minutes later, another knock sounded at the door. We will surely learn the truth of the matter in a day or two.”

This might slow our progress or make the objective more difficult, but it won't change the final result. We are going to win, of course. No other outcome is even conceivable. I never truly believed they were serious, but I see now that they were. This arrogant buildup and constant drilling, forty years of nationalistic posturing—sooner or later, it was bound to spill over into actual war.

It struck him motionless. The gaoler standing at his side, and the other gaolers moving about, who would have been well enough as to appearance in the ordinary exercise of their functions, looked so extravagantly coarse contrasted with sorrowing mothers and blooming daughters who were there--with the apparitions of the coquette, the young beauty, and the mature woman delicately bred--that the inversion of all experience and likelihood which the scene of shadows presented, was heightened to its utmost. Surely, ghosts all. Surely, the long unreal ride some progress of disease that had brought him to these gloomy shades!

It froze him where he stood. The jailer beside him, and the others drifting through their duties—men whose looks would have passed without remark in the common run of their work—now seemed grotesquely rough set against the grieving mothers and fresh-faced daughters gathered there, against the spectres of the flirt, the youthful beauty, the gently reared matron. The shadowy scene, already a reversal of all that was natural and probable, sharpened into something absolute. Surely, every one a phantom. Surely, the long hallucinatory journey some fevered advance of illness that had delivered him into these sombre depths.

Cotterell et al. (2014)'s corpus was collected in 2012 from an Algerian newspaper's web forums and covers a wide range of topics (from discussion about football events to politics). We collected the 9973 raw sentences from its GitHub repository 4 and sampled about 1300 sentences. In addition, because they were available with translations in French and English, we included lyrics from a few dozen recent popular songs of various genres (Raï, hip-hop, etc.), leading to an additional set of 200 sentences. These 1500 sentences form the core of our NArabizi treebank annotation project. In order to make our corpus usable by modern, resource-hungry natural language processing techniques, we also used data-driven language identification models to extract NArabizi samples among the whole collection of the Common-Crawl-based OSCAR corpora (Ortiz Suárez et al., 2019) as well as 2 millions sentences of additional crawled webdata, resulting in 50k NArabizi sentences of high quality, to date the largest corpus of this language. This makes this collection a valuable test bed for low-resource NLP research.

metadata.title: Building a User-Generated Content North-African Arabizi Treebank: Tackling Hell

text: Cotterell et al. (2014)’s corpus was gathered in 2012 from the web forums of an Algerian newspaper and spans a broad set of topics, ranging from discussions of football matches to politics. From its GitHub repository4, we obtained the 9,973 raw sentences and sampled roughly 1,300 of them. Moreover, since translations in French and English were available, we incorporated lyrics from several dozen recent popular songs across multiple genres, including Raï and hip-hop, which yielded an additional 200 sentences. Together, these 1,500 sentences constitute the core of our NArabizi treebank annotation project. To further render the corpus suitable for contemporary, data-intensive NLP methods, we also applied data-driven language identification models to identify NArabizi examples within the full Common-Crawl-based OSCAR corpora (Ortiz Suárez et al., 2019) as well as 2 million additional web-crawled sentences, producing 50k high-quality NArabizi sentences, which is, to our knowledge, the largest corpus of this language to date. As such, this collection provides a valuable test bed for low-resource NLP research.

This problem is most clearly illustrated with an example. In the following sections, we introduce Prasad et al.'s (2019) novel psycholinguisticsinspired template-based probe of relative clause types, which was taken as evidence in support of syntactic representation in LSTMs. We then pass PvSL's test with two non-syntactic baselines: an n-gram LM which can only capture short-distance word order of concrete types (Section 3), and an LSTM trained on scrambled inputs (Section 4). These baselines show that a combination of collocation and lexical representation can account for PvSL's results, which highlights a critical flaw in that experimental design. Following that, we argue that it is unlikely that LSTMs induce syntactic representations given current evidence and suggest an alternative angle for the question (Section 5).

metadata.title: Overestimation of Syntactic Representation in Neural Language Models

text: This issue is best demonstrated through a concrete case. In what follows, we first describe the psycholinguistically motivated template-based probing method for relative clause constructions proposed by Prasad et al. (2019), which was interpreted as evidence that LSTMs encode syntactic structure. We then challenge PvSL’s findings using two non-syntactic baselines: an n-gram language model that only captures local word-order patterns over specific lexical forms (Section 3), and an LSTM trained on scrambled input sequences (Section 4). These baselines reveal that a combination of collocational information and lexical representations suffices to reproduce PvSL’s outcomes, exposing a fundamental weakness in the original experimental setup. Subsequently, we contend that existing evidence does not support the claim that LSTMs learn syntactic representations and propose a different perspective on the inquiry (Section 5).

“Elena, I summon the memory of those former griefs only for the sake of the love I bear you this evening—a devotion far beyond the reach of speech—and to praise the Almighty for the bounty of my joy. My spirit, in its most frantic wanderings, never climbed toward a bliss so profound as that which I have shared with you, nor dreamed of the radiant path that lies ahead for us both.”

Our main conclusions are as follows: First, intrinsic and extrinsic hallucinations happen frequently -in more than 70% of single-sentence summaries. Second, the majority of hallucinations are extrinsic, which potentially could be valid abstractions that use background knowledge. However, our study found that over 90% of extrinsic hallucinations were erroneous. Thus, hallucinations happen in most summaries and the majority of these are neither faithful nor factual. Third, models initialized with pretrained parameters perform best both on automatic metrics and human judgments of faithfulness/factuality. Furthermore, they have the highest percentage of extrinsic hallucinations that are factual. This suggests that while some studies argue that large-scale pretrained models are merely better at learning data-specific regularities (Niven and Kao, 2019) , at least on in-domain summarization the gains in automatic metrics are realized in observable differences by humans. Fourth, ROUGE (Lin and Hovy, 2003) and BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020) correlates less with faithfulness/factuality than metrics derived from automatic semantic inference systems, specifically the degree to which a summary is entailed by the source document. This presents an opportunity for improved automatic evaluation measures as well as model training and decoding objectives. We show preliminary experiments in this direction.

metadata.title: On Faithfulness and Factuality in Abstractive Summarization

text: Our principal findings are as follows. First, both intrinsic and extrinsic hallucinations occur frequently—in over 70% of single-sentence summaries. Second, the majority of hallucinations are extrinsic, which in principle could represent valid abstractions drawing on background knowledge. However, our analysis revealed that more than 90% of extrinsic hallucinations were factually incorrect. Consequently, hallucinations appear in most summaries, and most of these are neither faithful nor factual. Third, models initialized from pretrained parameters achieve the best performance according to both automatic metrics and human evaluations of faithfulness and factuality. Moreover, they produce the highest proportion of extrinsic hallucinations that are factually accurate. This indicates that, although some research contends that large-scale pretrained models merely learn dataset-specific regularities more effectively (Niven and Kao, 2019), in in-domain summarization at least, the improvements captured by automatic metrics correspond to discernible differences perceived by human judges. Fourth, ROUGE (Lin and Hovy, 2003) and BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020) exhibit weaker correlation with faithfulness and factuality than metrics derived from automatic semantic inference systems, particularly the extent to which a summary is entailed by the source document. This finding highlights an opportunity to develop better automatic evaluation measures, as well as enhanced model training and decoding objectives. We present preliminary experiments pursuing this direction.

By an effort, I contained my joy when Clarence brought me the news. But he--he could not contain his. His mouth gushed delight and gratitude in a steady discharge--delight in my good fortune, gratitude to the king for this splendid mark of his favor for me. He could keep neither his legs nor his body still, but pirouetted about the place in an airy ecstasy of happiness.

I managed to hide my excitement with some effort when Clarence delivered the news. But he—he was unable to restrain himself at all. A constant stream of joy and gratitude poured from his lips; he was thrilled by my good luck and deeply thankful to the king for such a grand gesture of favor. He couldn't keep his body still for even a moment, spinning around the room in a lighthearted state of pure bliss.

I instantly wrote to Geneva; nearly two months have elapsed since the departure of my letter. But you are ill; even now you tremble; you are unfit for agitation of any kind.”

“The moment you were seized with illness, all the papers found upon you were brought to me, and I examined them in the hope of discovering some clue by which I might inform your relations of your misfortune and sickness. I found several letters, and among them one which I knew at once, from its opening lines, to be from your father. I wrote immediately to Geneva; nearly two months have passed since I sent that letter. But you are ill; even now you tremble; you are in no state for any disturbance.”

She pulled Liora into a tight, trembling embrace, her voice thick with half-swallowed emotion as she spoke: “Farewell, my sweet girl, dearest Liora, my one and only beloved friend. May the heavens in their boundless generosity bless and keep you; may this be the last sorrow you ever have to bear. Live, be full of joy, and share that happiness with everyone you meet.”

Look into the world; think how many hearts you would be proud to gain, are there. Confide some other passion to me, if you will; I will be the truest, warmest, and most faithful friend you have.”

“It is this,” Rose replied, “that you must strive to forget me—not as your old, deeply cherished companion, for that would cut me to the quick—but as the object of your romantic love. Look out into the world, and think of how many hearts there are whose regard you would be proud to earn. If you wish to confide any other feelings to me, I will be the truest, warmest, most faithful friend you could ever have.”

The Boss has never stirred--sleeps like a stone. If he does not wake to-day we shall understand what kind of a sleep it is, and his body will then be borne to a place in one of the remote recesses of the cave where none will ever find it to desecrate it. As for the rest of us--well, it is agreed that if any one of us ever escapes alive from this place, he will write the fact here, and loyally hide this Manuscript with The Boss, our dear good chief, whose property it is, be he alive or dead.

Our leader has not moved; he remains in a slumber as heavy as stone. If he does not rouse himself by nightfall, we will finally understand the true nature of his rest, and we shall carry his body to a secluded hollow deep within the cave where it can never be found or disturbed. As for the survivors, we have resolved that if any one of us manages to escape this place alive, he will record the event here and faithfully conceal this manuscript with our beloved chief, for it is his rightful property, whether he breathes or not.

The havoc continued an hour and a half, and unimaginable was the destruction of substantials. Of the chief feature of the feast --the huge wild boar that lay stretched out so portly and imposing at the start--nothing was left but the semblance of a hoop-skirt; and he was but the type and symbol of what had happened to all the other dishes.

The carnage lasted for an hour and a half, leaving behind a level of culinary devastation that was hard to fathom. Of the banquet’s centerpiece—the massive wild boar that had looked so grand and stout when the meal began—nothing remained but a skeletal frame resembling a hoop-skirt. This ruin was merely a symbol of the wreckage that had overtaken every other dish on the table.

l. 224. willow, the weeping willow, so-called because its branches
with their long leaves droop to the ground, like dropping tears. It has
always been sacred to deserted or unhappy lovers. Cf. Othello, IV.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
The willow, the mournful willow, named for the way its boughs,
draped in slender leaves, sweep downward to the earth like falling tears.
It has forever been hallowed by forsaken or sorrowful hearts. See Othello, IV.
iii. 24 seq.

I passed the bridge of Pélissier, where the ravine, which the river forms, opened before me, and I began to ascend the mountain that overhangs it. Soon after, I entered the valley of Chamounix. This valley is more wonderful and sublime, but not so beautiful and picturesque as that of Servox, through which I had just passed. The high and snowy mountains were its immediate boundaries, but I saw no more ruined castles and fertile fields. Immense glaciers approached the road; I heard the rumbling thunder of the falling avalanche and marked the smoke of its passage. Mont Blanc, the supreme and magnificent Mont Blanc, raised itself from the surrounding _aiguilles_, and its tremendous _dôme_ overlooked the valley.

I crossed the span of Pélissier, where the chasm carved by the river yawned before me, and I started to climb the slope that loomed above it. Not long after, I came into the vale of Chamounix. This vale was more awe-inspiring and majestic, yet lacked the loveliness and charm of Servox, which I had lately traversed. Towering, snow-clad peaks formed its nearest walls, but I beheld no further remnants of fortresses or verdant meadows. Vast glaciers crept close to the path; I caught the distant roar of a tumbling avalanche and glimpsed the haze of its descent. Mont Blanc, the sovereign and resplendent Mont Blanc, soared above the encircling _aiguilles_, its immense _dôme_ commanding the entire valley.

And so Martin’s rôle was arranged for him, while he, led on by Arthur and Norman, was meditating an extravagance. They were going out for a ride into the hills Sunday morning on their wheels, which did not interest Martin until he learned that Ruth, too, rode a wheel and was going along. He did not ride, nor own a wheel, but if Ruth rode, it was up to him to begin, was his decision; and when he said good night, he stopped in at a cyclery on his way home and spent forty dollars for a wheel. It was more than a month’s hard-earned wages, and it reduced his stock of money amazingly; but when he added the hundred dollars he was to receive from the _Examiner_ to the four hundred and twenty dollars that was the least _The Youth’s Companion_ could pay him, he felt that he had reduced the perplexity the unwonted amount of money had caused him. Nor did he mind, in the course of learning to ride the wheel home, the fact that he ruined his suit of clothes. He caught the tailor by telephone that night from Mr. Higginbotham’s store and ordered another suit. Then he carried the wheel up the narrow stairway that clung like a fire-escape to the rear wall of the building, and when he had moved his bed out from the wall, found there was just space enough in the small room for himself and the wheel.

Elias’s part in the weekend plan was already settled for him, even as he, egged on by Finn and Jasper, was plotting a little splurge of his own. The two boys had planned a Sunday morning bicycle ride out into the foothills, a plan that had not piqued Elias’s interest at all until he heard Lila would be joining them, and that she rode a bike herself. Elias had never learned to ride, nor did he own a bicycle, but if Lila rode, he decided, there was no question he would learn too. He said his goodnights to the group, then detoured to a bike shop on his walk home and dropped forty dollars on a new model. That sum was more than he earned in a full month of backbreaking work, and it cut his available cash sharply, but when he tallied the hundred dollars coming to him from the _Herald_ alongside the minimum four hundred twenty he would get from _Young Folks' Quarterly_, he was relieved to whittle down the awkward surplus of money that had been weighing on him as of late. He didn’t even care that he tore and stained his good suit terribly while fumbling to learn to ride the bike the rest of the way home. That same night, he called his tailor from the pay phone at Mr. Hollister’s general store and put in an order for a new suit. After that, he hauled the bicycle up the narrow staircase bolted like a fire escape to the back of his apartment building, and once he shifted his bed a few inches away from the wall, he found his tiny room had just enough floor space left for both him and his new purchase.

Discourse relation describes how two adjacent text units (e.g., clauses, sentences, and larger sentence groups) are connected logically to one another. A discourse relation instance is usually defined as a connective taking two arguments (as Arg1 and Arg2, respectively). Implicit discourse relation recognition without explicit connectives (Pitler et al., 2009) is still a challenging problem of discourse analysis, which needs to infer the discourse relation from a specific context. It is beneficial to many downstream natural language processing (NLP) applications, such as machine translation (Meyer and Popescu-Belis, 2012 ) and text summarization (Gerani et al., 2014) .

metadata.title: TransS-Driven Joint Learning Architecture for Implicit Discourse Relation Recognition

text: Discourse relations characterize the logical connections holding between two contiguous textual spans, which may take the form of individual clauses, standalone sentences, or larger multi-sentence segments. Standard formalizations of discourse relation instances frame each relation as structured around a connective that takes two ordered arguments, conventionally labelled Arg1 and Arg2 respectively. A particularly persistent open challenge in the field of discourse analysis is implicit discourse relation recognition, the task of identifying relations when no explicit connective is present (Pitler et al., 2009), which requires inferring the underlying logical link exclusively from the surrounding contextual information. Progress on this task delivers concrete benefits to a broad suite of downstream natural language processing (NLP) applications, including machine translation (Meyer and Popescu-Belis, 2012) and text summarization (Gerani et al., 2014).

The wretched wife of the innocent man thus doomed to die, fell under the sentence, as if she had been mortally stricken. But, she uttered no sound; and so strong was the voice within her, representing that it was she of all the world who must uphold him in his misery and not augment it, that it quickly raised her, even from that shock.

The devastated wife of the innocent man newly condemned to die crumpled the moment the sentence was read, as though she had been struck by a mortal blow. Yet she uttered not a sound; the quiet voice deep within her was too firm, insisting that of all people on earth, she was the one who must uphold him in his misery rather than add to it, and that unshakable conviction lifted her almost at once, even out of the throes of that devastating shock.

The only ghost I ever saw
Was dressed in mechlin, -- so;
He wore no sandal on his foot,
And stepped like flakes of snow.
His gait was soundless, like the bird,
But rapid, like the roe;
His fashions quaint, mosaic,
Or, haply, mistletoe.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
The only spirit I have known  
Wore lace of mechlin thread;  
No shoe or sole was ever shown,  
Like snowdrift was his tread.  
His pace was mute as a bird in flight,  
Yet swift as a deer instead;  
His garments strange, inlaid with light,  
Or woven of mistletoe.

As he said this he led the way across the ice; I followed. My heart was full, and I did not answer him, but as I proceeded, I weighed the various arguments that he had used and determined at least to listen to his tale. I was partly urged by curiosity, and compassion confirmed my resolution. I had hitherto supposed him to be the murderer of my brother, and I eagerly sought a confirmation or denial of this opinion. For the first time, also, I felt what the duties of a creator towards his creature were, and that I ought to render him happy before I complained of his wickedness. These motives urged me to comply with his demand. We crossed the ice, therefore, and ascended the opposite rock. The air was cold, and the rain again began to descend; we entered the hut, the fiend with an air of exultation, I with a heavy heart and depressed spirits. But I consented to listen, and seating myself by the fire which my odious companion had lighted, he thus began his tale.

With these words, he struck out across the frozen wasteland, and I pursued him in silence. My mind was a tumult of conflicting thoughts; I pondered the claims he had made and resolved, at the very least, to grant him an audience. A dark curiosity propelled me, but it was a burgeoning sense of mercy that finally fixed my purpose. Until this moment, I had viewed him only as the butcher of my young brother, and I sought desperately to know the truth of that bloody deed. Furthermore, I felt for the first time the heavy burden of a progenitor’s debt—the realization that I owed some modicum of solace to my handiwork before I dared denounce his cruelty. Bound by these considerations, I followed his lead. We navigated the slick ice and scaled the opposing crag. The atmosphere grew frigid as a grey rain began to fall; we retreated into the shelter, the wretch wearing a mask of victory while I felt only a profound despondency. I sat by the flames my loathsome host had stoked, and he began his narration.

“Oh, Anthony,” sighed Aunt Ellen, “I wish you would not think so much of money. Wealth is nothing where a true affection is concerned. Love is all-powerful. If he only had spoken earlier! She could not have refused our Richard. But now I fear it is too late. He will have no opportunity to address her. All your gold cannot bring happiness to your son.”

My dear Arthur," Aunt Beatrice sighed, "I wish you would not dwell so upon finance. Opulence is a trifle against a sincere bond; love is invincible. Had he but voiced his intentions sooner! She could never have turned our Julian away. But I fear the hour has slipped by, leaving him no occasion to speak. All your hoarded treasures cannot purchase the contentment your son requires.

But such thoughts and visions did not prevent him from following Professor Caldwell closely. And as he followed, comprehendingly and critically, he noted the unbroken field of the other’s knowledge. As for himself, from moment to moment the conversation showed him gaps and open stretches, whole subjects with which he was unfamiliar. Nevertheless, thanks to his Spencer, he saw that he possessed the outlines of the field of knowledge. It was a matter only of time, when he would fill in the outline. Then watch out, he thought—’ware shoal, everybody! He felt like sitting at the feet of the professor, worshipful and absorbent; but, as he listened, he began to discern a weakness in the other’s judgments—a weakness so stray and elusive that he might not have caught it had it not been ever present. And when he did catch it, he leapt to equality at once.

Still, none of those wandering thoughts and daydreams stopped him from keeping pace right at Professor Caldwell’s side. As he walked along, engaging with every word both thoughtfully and skeptically, he couldn’t help but notice how seamlessly the professor’s expertise spanned every topic they touched on. For his own part, the discussion kept turning up gaps in his own learning, vast blank swathes of entire subject areas he’d never even dabbled in. Even so, his deep dive into Spencer’s work had given him a clear sketch of the full landscape of all there was to know, he realized. Filling in every empty spot on that sketch was just a matter of putting in the time. When that day came, everyone better watch their step, he smirked to himself—shallow waters ahead for anyone who thought they could outmatch him. For a while he felt like he could kneel right there at the professor’s feet, eager to soak up every last word, reverent of the older man’s smarts. But the longer he listened, the more he picked up on a faint flaw in the professor’s reasoning, a quirk so scattered and hard to pin down he never would have spotted it if it didn’t pop up again and again. The second he pinned that flaw down, he felt himself standing on even ground with the man immediately.

Sydney had not gone far out of sight, when he stopped in the middle of the street under a glimmering lamp, and wrote with his pencil on a scrap of paper. Then, traversing with the decided step of one who remembered the way well, several dark and dirty streets--much dirtier than usual, for the best public thoroughfares remained uncleansed in those times of terror--he stopped at a chemist’s shop, which the owner was closing with his own hands. A small, dim, crooked shop, kept in a tortuous, up-hill thoroughfare, by a small, dim, crooked man.

Julian had scarcely slipped from view when he paused beneath the weak radiance of a guttering lamp to mark a scrap of paper with his lead. Resuming his pace with the unwavering gait of one who knew every turn by heart, he threaded through a succession of grim, fouled streets—alleys far more wretched than their wont, as even the primary arteries of the city lay abandoned to filth during those seasons of dread. He eventually halted at a druggist’s door just as the master was barring it with his own weary hands. It was a narrow, murky, warped little vault, tucked into a twisting, rising wynd, and kept by a narrow, murky, warped little man.

It was the stubborn unreasoning of the time. It was useless to argue with her. Arguments have no chance against petrified training; they wear it as little as the waves wear a cliff. And her training was everybody's. The brightest intellect in the land would not have been able to see that her position was defective.

That age was marked by an obstinate irrationality. There was no point in reasoning with her. Reason stands powerless against hardened conditioning, just as the sea makes no impression on a cliff. And her conditioning was shared by all. Even the most brilliant mind in the country would have failed to recognize the flaw in her stance.

Clym retired to his lodging at the housetop much relieved, and occupied himself during the afternoon in noting down the heads of a sermon, with which he intended to initiate all that really seemed practicable of the scheme that had originally brought him hither, and that he had so long kept in view under various modifications, and through evil and good report. He had tested and weighed his convictions again and again, and saw no reason to alter them, though he had considerably lessened his plan. His eyesight, by long humouring in his native air, had grown stronger, but not sufficiently strong to warrant his attempting his extensive educational project. Yet he did not repine—there was still more than enough of an unambitious sort to tax all his energies and occupy all his hours.

Clym returned to his lodging under the roof much relieved, and spent the afternoon jotting down the outline of a sermon, by which he meant to begin all that now appeared genuinely feasible of the scheme that had first brought him there, and which he had so long kept before him in one altered form or another, through praise and blame alike. He had examined and reexamined his convictions many times, and saw no cause to change them, though he had greatly scaled back his plan. His sight, strengthened by long indulgence in his native air, had improved, but not enough to justify his undertaking so broad an educational venture. Yet he did not complain—there was still, in a modest way, more than enough to call for every effort and fill all his time.

I sat reflecting upon my evident cosmopolite and wondering how the poet had managed to miss him. He was my discovery and I believed in him. How was it? “The men that breed from them they traffic up and down, but cling to their cities’ hem as a child to the mother’s gown.”

I sat mulling over the unfeigned, far-roaming cosmopolitan I’d crossed paths with, wondering how generations of poets had never stumbled on his type to immortalize. He was my own unshared discovery, and I held full belief in his singular character. How had he slipped through the cracks? “The men born of that stock drift back and forth trading across borders, yet cling to the frayed edge of their home city’s skirt as a small child clutches at the fabric of its mother’s gown.”

"I'm going out--anywhere." She turned, coat in hand. "Can't I go out if I like?" she asked. "It's a beautiful day.... Mustn't I go out?... I suppose you think I ought to take in what you have told me in a moment. Just smile and say '_Indeed!_' ... Abandoned!--while his men retreated! How jolly! And then not think of it any more.... Besides, I must go out. You two want to be left together. You want to canoodle. Do it while you can!"

“I’m leaving—any place but here.” She spun on her heel, wool coat slung over one arm. “What, I can’t step out whenever I feel like it?” she snapped. “The sun’s shining, for Christ’s sake… Am I not allowed to breathe fresh air?… I guess you expect me to swallow everything you just dumped on me all at once, right? Just bat my eyes and chirp ‘How fascinating!’ Deserted his post! Left his troops to fall back alone! Isn’t that just darling? And then I’m supposed to wipe it from my mind like it never happened… And anyway, I need to get out. You two want the place to yourselves. Want to make goo-goo eyes at each other. Go ahead, enjoy it while it lasts!”

The first thing Martin did next morning was to go counter both to Brissenden’s advice and command. “The Shame of the Sun” he wrapped and mailed to _The Acropolis_. He believed he could find magazine publication for it, and he felt that recognition by the magazines would commend him to the book-publishing houses. “Ephemera” he likewise wrapped and mailed to a magazine. Despite Brissenden’s prejudice against the magazines, which was a pronounced mania with him, Martin decided that the great poem should see print. He did not intend, however, to publish it without the other’s permission. His plan was to get it accepted by one of the high magazines, and, thus armed, again to wrestle with Brissenden for consent.

The first thing Elias did when he woke the next morning was act directly counter to both Caspar Voss’s casual advice and his firm, explicit command. *The Weight of the Daylight*, his full-length manuscript, he wrapped carefully and posted off to *The High Citadel*. He was confident he could secure magazine serialization for the work, and suspected that formal endorsement from a reputable periodical would make hard-to-impress book publishers far more receptive to his pitches. He also wrapped *Mayflies*, his sprawling long poem, and dropped it in the post to a different prominent literary magazine. For all Caspar’s rabid, near-obsessive loathing of mainstream periodicals, Elias was determined that the celebrated verse piece should see print eventually. He had no intention of running the poem without his friend’s permission, though. His gambit was to get it accepted by one of the most prestigious magazines first, and with that proof of its merit in hand, argue Caspar around to giving his consent.

For language models, we use a Transformer decoder with the same hyperparameters used in the translation task to effectively measure the contribution given by a translation. These models were trained, using label smoothing ls = 0.1, for 10,000 steps on sequences consisting of separate sentences in our corpus. Analogously to translation models, the checkpoints corresponding to the lowest validation losses were used for evaluation. Table 3 presents the results when applying bootstrap re-sampling (Koehn, 2004) on either training or test sets to the systems achieving the highest and the lowest BLEU scores in the validation set for each direction. In our experiments, we observe a general trend where the performance of different models varies similarly. For instance, when we bootstrap test sets, we see that the average BLEU scores are equal to the ones seen in Table 1 , and that all the models have similar confidence intervals. 5 When bootstrapping the training data, we observe a consistent drop in mean performance of 2 − 3 BLEU points across the translation tasks. The drop in performance is not surprising as the resulting training sets are more redundant, having fewer unique sentences than the original sets, but it is interesting to see that all models are similarly affected. The standard deviation over 5 runs is also similar across all models but slightly larger on the high-performing ones. text: For, the time was to come, when the gaunt scarecrows of that region should have watched the lamplighter, in their idleness and hunger, so long, as to conceive the idea of improving on his method, and hauling up men by those ropes and pulleys, to flare upon the darkness of their condition. But, the time was not come yet; and every wind that blew over France shook the rags of the scarecrows in vain, for the birds, fine of song and feather, took no warning.

The hour had not yet arrived when the starved effigies of that countryside, hollow-eyed and motionless, would study the lamplighter so obsessively in their barren waiting that they would dream of refining his craft—of hoisting men by those same cords and wheels to blaze against the blackness of their plight. But the hour was still distant, and every gust that swept across the land stirred the scarecrows’ tatters uselessly, for the birds, elegant in plume and melody, heeded nothing.

The force of the farmer’s threats lay in their relation to a circumstance known at present only to herself: her lover was coming back to Weatherbury in the course of the very next day or two. Troy had not returned to his distant barracks as Boldwood and others supposed, but had merely gone to visit some acquaintance in Bath, and had yet a week or more remaining to his furlough.

The weight of the farmer’s menacing words rested on a secret she alone possessed: her beloved was due to return to Weatherbury within the next day or two. Troy had not, as Boldwood and the rest imagined, gone back to his remote barracks; he had merely travelled to Bath to see some friends, and still had a week or more left of his leave.

“As the night advanced, a fierce wind arose from the woods and quickly dispersed the clouds that had loitered in the heavens; the blast tore along like a mighty avalanche and produced a kind of insanity in my spirits that burst all bounds of reason and reflection. I lighted the dry branch of a tree and danced with fury around the devoted cottage, my eyes still fixed on the western horizon, the edge of which the moon nearly touched. A part of its orb was at length hid, and I waved my brand; it sank, and with a loud scream I fired the straw, and heath, and bushes, which I had collected. The wind fanned the fire, and the cottage was quickly enveloped by the flames, which clung to it and licked it with their forked and destroying tongues.

As the night wore on, a brutal, howling wind gusted up from the deep woods, scattering the slow-loitering clouds that had hung heavy across the sky in seconds. The gale tore through the air like a crashing avalanche, tipping my mind into a rabid frenzy that shattered every last boundary of reason and careful thought. I lit a dry, gnarled tree branch and danced in a wild rage around the cottage I’d marked for destruction, my eyes locked tight to the western horizon where the moon hovered just a hair’s breadth above the edge. When the first sliver of its disc dipped out of view, I swung my burning torch high; the moment it sank fully out of sight, I let out a deafening, ragged scream and touched the flame to the pile of straw, heath, and dry brush I’d stashed beforehand. The wind fanned the fire to a roar, and the cottage was quickly wrapped in roiling flames, which coiled tight to its walls and lapped at every inch of it with their sharp, forked, ravenous tongues.

Thus--a vivid fact as yet only in a few hundred skulls or so--the vast catastrophe of the Great War gathered behind the idle, dispersed and confused spectacle of an indifferent world, very much as the storms and rains of late September gathered behind the glow and lassitudes of August, and with scarcely more of set human intention. For the greater part of mankind the European international situation was at most something in the papers, no more important than the political disturbances in South Africa, where the Herzogites were curiously uneasy, or the possible trouble between Turkey and Greece. The things that really interested people in England during the last months of peace were boxing and the summer sales. A brilliant young Frenchman, Carpentier, who had knocked out Bombardier Wells, came over again to defeat Gunboat Smith, and did so to the infinite delight of France and the whole Latin world, amidst the generous applause of Anglo-Saxondom. And there was also a British triumph over the Americans at polo, and a lively and cultured newspaper discussion about a proper motto for the arms of the London County Council. The trial of Madame Caillaux filled the papers with animated reports and vivid pictures; Gregori Rasputin was stabbed and became the subject of much lively gossip about the Russian Court; and Ulivi, the Italian impostor who claimed he could explode mines by means of an "ultra-red" ray, was exposed and fled with a lady, very amusingly. For a few days all the work at Woolwich Arsenal was held up because a certain Mr. Entwhistle, having refused to erect a machine on a concrete bed laid down by non-unionists, was rather uncivilly dismissed, and the Irish trouble pounded along its tiresome mischievous way. People gave a divided attention to these various topics, and went about their individual businesses.

Thus—a vivid reality known, as yet, to only a few hundred minds—the immense disaster of the Great War was massing behind the scattered, distracted, and casual spectacle of an unconcerned world, much as the storms and rains of late September gather behind the brilliance and languor of August, and with scarcely more deliberate human purpose. For most of mankind, the European crisis was, at best, just something in the newspapers, no more significant than the political unrest in South Africa, where the Herzogites were oddly restless, or the possibility of trouble between Turkey and Greece. What truly occupied people in England in those last months of peace were boxing and the summer sales. A dazzling young Frenchman, Carpentier, who had knocked out Bombardier Wells, came back to defeat Gunboat Smith as well, to the boundless joy of France and the entire Latin world, amid the hearty cheers of Anglo-Saxondom. There was also a British victory over the Americans at polo, and a lively, cultivated newspaper debate over the proper motto for the London County Council’s coat of arms. The trial of Madame Caillaux filled the papers with energetic reports and striking images; Gregori Rasputin was stabbed and became the center of much spirited gossip about the Russian court; and Ulivi, the Italian fraud who claimed he could detonate mines by means of an “ultra-red” ray, was unmasked and fled away with a lady, very amusingly. For a few days all work at Woolwich Arsenal was brought to a halt because a certain Mr. Entwhistle, after refusing to erect a machine on a concrete foundation laid by non-union men, was rather rudely dismissed, and the Irish trouble trudged on in its tedious, troublesome fashion. People divided their attention among these various matters and carried on with their own affairs.

When I was about fifteen years old we had retired to our house near Belrive, when we witnessed a most violent and terrible thunderstorm. It advanced from behind the mountains of Jura, and the thunder burst at once with frightful loudness from various quarters of the heavens. I remained, while the storm lasted, watching its progress with curiosity and delight. As I stood at the door, on a sudden I beheld a stream of fire issue from an old and beautiful oak which stood about twenty yards from our house; and so soon as the dazzling light vanished, the oak had disappeared, and nothing remained but a blasted stump. When we visited it the next morning, we found the tree shattered in a singular manner. It was not splintered by the shock, but entirely reduced to thin ribbons of wood. I never beheld anything so utterly destroyed.

In my sixteenth year, during our retreat to the family estate overlooking the lake, I bore witness to a tempest of unparalleled ferocity. It gathered behind the dark crags of the Jura, and the thunder erupted with a sudden, deafening roar that seemed to shake the very foundations of the world. I stood upon the threshold, captivated by the chaotic splendor of the elements. Without warning, a pillar of celestial fire descended upon a majestic elm that had long graced our lawn; once the brilliance faded, the great tree had vanished, leaving only a scorched and skeletal ruin. The following morning revealed a strange sight: the trunk had not merely split, but had been teased into a thousand silken filaments of timber. Never before had I seen such total annihilation.

“Ah!” returned the man, with a relish; “he’ll be drawn on a hurdle to be half hanged, and then he’ll be taken down and sliced before his own face, and then his inside will be taken out and burnt while he looks on, and then his head will be chopped off, and he’ll be cut into quarters. That’s the sentence.”

“Ah!” the man replied with grim satisfaction. “He’ll be tied to a hurdle and dragged out to be half strangled, then cut down and slit open before his own eyes. His entrails will be pulled out and burned while he watches, and then his head will be struck off, and his body hewn into four parts. That’s the judgment.”

As soon as they were alone, and the door closed, Thomasin said, turning up her pale, tearful face to him, “It is killing me, this, Damon! I did not mean to part from you in anger at Anglebury this morning; but I was frightened and hardly knew what I said. I’ve not let Aunt know how much I suffered today; and it is so hard to command my face and voice, and to smile as if it were a slight thing to me; but I try to do so, that she may not be still more indignant with you. I know you could not help it, dear, whatever Aunt may think.”

The second the door clicked shut behind them and they were fully alone, Thomasin tilted her pale, tear-streaked face up to Damon. “This whole mess is eating me alive, Damon! I never wanted to leave you on bad terms when we were at Anglebury this morning. I was just so shaken I barely knew what was coming out of my mouth. I haven’t told Aunt a thing about how much I’ve suffered today, either. It’s so hard to keep my expression and voice steady, to smile like this is just some trivial annoyance that doesn’t bother me at all, but I’m forcing myself to do it so she doesn’t get even angrier with you. I know you couldn’t help what happened, dear, no matter what Aunt thinks.”

So saying, he stepped aside and wrote down a list of several books treating of natural philosophy which he desired me to procure, and dismissed me after mentioning that in the beginning of the following week he intended to commence a course of lectures upon natural philosophy in its general relations, and that M.
