Fair or not," Sikes snapped, "just hand it over! Do you think Nancy and I have nothing better to do with our time than spend it tracking down and snatching back every kid who gets caught because of you? Hand it over, you greedy old bag of bones—give it here!

There Mr. Direck was unfortunate enough to notice a copy of that innocent American publication _The New Republic_, lying close to two or three numbers of _The Fatherland_, a pro-German periodical which at that time inflicted itself upon English writers with the utmost determination. Mr. Direck remarked that _The New Republic_ was an interesting effort on the part of "_la Jeunesse Américaine_." Mr. Britling regarded the interesting effort with a jaded, unloving eye.

There, to his misfortune, Mr. Direck spotted an issue of that guileless American journal _The New Republic_, resting beside a few copies of _The Fatherland_, a German-leaning magazine that in those days pressed itself upon British authors with relentless resolve. Mr. Direck observed that _The New Republic_ represented a worthy endeavor from "_la Jeunesse Américaine_." Mr. Britling surveyed the worthy endeavor with a weary, indifferent gaze.

It turned out to be one of those rich and rare fall days which is just a June day toned down to a degree where it is heaven to be out of doors. Toward noon the guests arrived, and we assembled under a great tree and were soon as sociable as old acquaintances. Even the king's reserve melted a little, though it was some little trouble to him to adjust himself to the name of Jones along at first. I had asked him to try to not forget that he was a farmer; but I had also considered it prudent to ask him to let the thing stand at that, and not elaborate it any. Because he was just the kind of person you could depend on to spoil a little thing like that if you didn't warn him, his tongue was so handy, and his spirit so willing, and his information so uncertain.

It ended up being one of those lush, uncommon autumn days—exactly the kind of sun-kissed June day softened just enough that being outside feels like pure bliss. By midday, the guests started showing up, and we all gathered beneath a massive old tree, chatting easily with one another like we’d been close friends for years. Even the king’s usual standoffishness thawed a little, though he stumbled at first when he had to get used to answering to the plain, ordinary name of Jones. I’d told him to do his best to remember he was supposed to be a regular farmer, but I’d also thought it was smart to warn him not to add any extra details beyond that basic backstory. He was exactly the sort of person who’d absolutely ruin a small, simple setup like that if you didn’t lay down ground rules first: he talked too quickly, was too eager to jump into any conversation, and half the facts he spouted were unreliable at best.

"My Madeline! sweet dreamer! lovely bride!
Say, may I be for aye thy vassal blest?
Thy beauty's shield, heart-shap'd and vermeil dyed?
Ah, silver shrine, here will I take my rest
After so many hours of toil and quest,
A famish'd pilgrim,--saved by miracle.
Though I have found, I will not rob thy nest
Saving of thy sweet self; if thou think'st well
To trust, fair Madeline, to no rude infidel."

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
My Madeline! soft visionary, darling wife!
Tell me, may I forever be your favored knight?
Your beauty’s guard, heart-formed and crimson-bright?
Oh, silver altar, here I’ll pause from strife,
After long toil and searching, saved to life,
A starving pilgrim—spared by sacred spell.
Though I have found, I’ll plunder not your bower
But take your gentle self alone; if you agree
To trust, dear Madeline, no coarse disloyalty.

“I thought so. Now, I care a little for your good opinion, and I want to explain something—I have longed to do it ever since I returned, and you looked so gravely at me. For if I were to die—and I may die soon—it would be dreadful that you should always think mistakenly of me. Now, listen.”

“I thought as much. Now, your good opinion matters to me, just a little, and I need to make something clear—I’ve been aching to do this ever since I came back and you met me with that solemn gaze. Because if I were to die—and I might die soon—it would be unbearable for you to go on thinking wrongly of me. So now, listen.”

But it seemed to him there was something in the recital that jarred upon his sense of beauty and life. He could not find an adequate motive in Mr. Butler’s life of pinching and privation. Had he done it for love of a woman, or for attainment of beauty, Martin would have understood. God’s own mad lover should do anything for the kiss, but not for thirty thousand dollars a year. He was dissatisfied with Mr. Butler’s career. There was something paltry about it, after all. Thirty thousand a year was all right, but dyspepsia and inability to be humanly happy robbed such princely income of all its value.

Yet it seemed to him that something in the story struck harshly against his sense of beauty and of life. He could find no sufficient motive in Mr. Butler’s existence of stint and deprivation. Had he done it for the sake of a woman, or in pursuit of beauty, Martin could have understood. God’s own mad lover might do anything for the kiss, but not for thirty thousand dollars a year. He was unsatisfied with Mr. Butler’s career. There was something, after all, mean and diminished about it. Thirty thousand a year was very well, but dyspepsia and an inability to be humanly happy stripped so princely an income of all its worth.

Everyone loved Elizabeth. The passionate and almost reverential attachment with which all regarded her became, while I shared it, my pride and my delight. On the evening previous to her being brought to my home, my mother had said playfully, “I have a pretty present for my Victor—tomorrow he shall have it.” And when, on the morrow, she presented Elizabeth to me as her promised gift, I, with childish seriousness, interpreted her words literally and looked upon Elizabeth as mine—mine to protect, love, and cherish. All praises bestowed on her I received as made to a possession of my own. We called each other familiarly by the name of cousin. No word, no expression could body forth the kind of relation in which she stood to me—my more than sister, since till death she was to be mine only.

All adored Clara. The fervent, almost worshipful affection with which she was regarded by everyone became, while I partook in it, at once my pride and my happiness. On the evening before she was brought beneath my roof, my mother had said, in playful mood, “I have a lovely gift for my Adrian; tomorrow he shall receive it.” And when, on the following day, she delivered Clara to me as that promised gift, I, with the grave simplicity of childhood, took her at her word and looked upon Clara as mine—mine to shield, to love, to cherish. Every compliment bestowed upon her I accepted as though addressed to a possession of my own. We called one another by the familiar title of cousin. Yet no phrase, no description, could rightly convey the nature of the tie that bound her to me—my more than sister, for until death she was to belong to me alone.

“Just look at it!” he cried. “An’ it’s all mine! It’s free. I can lie down under them trees an’ sleep for a thousan’ years if I want to. Aw, come on, Mart, let’s chuck it. What’s the good of waitin’ another moment. That’s the land of nothin’ to do out there, an’ I got a ticket for it—an’ it ain’t no return ticket, b’gosh!”

“Look at that!” he shouted. “And it’s all mine! It’s wide open. I could lie down under those trees and sleep for a thousand years if I felt like it. Come on, Mart, let's just leave it all behind. What’s the point in waiting another minute? That’s the land of leisure out there, and I’ve got my ticket in hand—and believe me, I’m never coming back!”

“If it be painful to him,” she thought, “to come back here, how painful it will be to me! But perhaps he will not come; he may write, or he may come himself, and studiously abstain from meeting me—he did when he went away. I hardly thought he would; but it was better for us both.” And here Rose dropped the pen, and turned away, as though the very paper which was to be her messenger should not see her weep.

“Should this place bring him torment,” she reflected, “what torment it shall bring me! Yet maybe he will stay away; he might send a letter, or arrive and carefully avoid my presence—as he did upon his departure. I scarcely believed he would; yet it proved kinder for the pair of us.” And here Iris let the quill fall, and averted her face, as if even the sheet destined to carry her words must not witness her tears.

As illustrated in Figure 1 (a), given the source sentence "s 1 s 2 s 3 s 4 " with words of s 2 and s 3 being covered by the bilingual dictionary, we replace the two words with their translation words according to the dictionary. This results in the source sentence "s 1 s 2 t s 3 t s 4 ", of which s 2 t and s 3 t serve as the anchors which are actually the target language words obtained by translating s 2 and s 3 according to the dictionary, respectively. Through the anchors, some words on the source side share the same word embeddings with the corresponding words on the target side. The AT process will strengthen the consistency of embedding spaces of both languages based on these anchors.

metadata.title: Bilingual Dictionary Based Neural Machine Translation without Using Parallel Sentences

text: As shown in Figure 1 (a), starting from the source sentence “s 1 s 2 s 3 s 4 ” in which the words s 2 and s 3 are covered by the bilingual dictionary, we substitute those two words with their dictionary translations. This yields the sentence “s 1 s 2 t s 3 t s 4 ”, where s 2 t and s 3 t function as anchors—target-language words obtained by translating s 2 and s 3 via the dictionary, respectively. By means of these anchors, certain source-side words share the same word embeddings with their corresponding target-side words. The anchor training (AT) procedure then reinforces the consistency of the two languages’ embedding spaces on the basis of these anchors.

We stood there awhile, in the thick darkness and stillness, looking toward the red blur in the distance, and trying to make out the meaning of a far-away murmur that rose and fell fitfully on the night. Sometimes it swelled up and for a moment seemed less remote; but when we were hopefully expecting it to betray its cause and nature, it dulled and sank again, carrying its mystery with it. We started down the hill in its direction, and the winding road plunged us at once into almost solid darkness--darkness that was packed and crammed in between two tall forest walls. We groped along down for half a mile, perhaps, that murmur growing more and more distinct all the time. The coming storm threatening more and more, with now and then a little shiver of wind, a faint show of lightning, and dull grumblings of distant thunder. I was in the lead. I ran against something--a soft heavy something which gave, slightly, to the impulse of my weight; at the same moment the lightning glared out, and within a foot of my face was the writhing face of a man who was hanging from the limb of a tree! That is, it seemed to be writhing, but it was not. It was a grewsome sight. Straightway there was an ear-splitting explosion of thunder, and the bottom of heaven fell out; the rain poured down in a deluge. No matter, we must try to cut this man down, on the chance that there might be life in him yet, mustn't we? The lightning came quick and sharp now, and the place was alternately noonday and midnight. One moment the man would be hanging before me in an intense light, and the next he was blotted out again in the darkness. I told the king we must cut him down. The king at once objected.

We lingered for a duration in the dense shadow and silence, watching a ruby smudge on the horizon and straining to interpret a distant hum that ebbed and flowed fitfully through the night. At times it swelled, seeming for a moment more proximate; yet, when we anticipated it might reveal its source, it grew dull and subsided, its secret still guarded. We set off down the slope toward it, the winding track casting us at once into a nearly palpable darkness—an obscurity packed tight between two high ramparts of timber. We groped forward for perhaps half a mile, the murmur growing increasingly audible. The impending storm became more threatening, bringing occasional tremors of wind, pale flickers of lightning, and the low growls of remote thunder. I was at the front. I collided with an object—a soft, heavy mass that gave way slightly under my momentum; in that same instant, the lightning blazed, and a foot from my face hung the distorted visage of a man dangling from a branch! It seemed to twitch, though it was actually still. It was a morbid sight. Immediately, an ear-splitting crash of thunder followed, and the very floor of heaven gave way; the rain fell in a deluge. Regardless, we had to attempt to cut him down, for there was a chance that a spark of life remained, was there not? The lightning became rapid and piercing, the woods alternating between the clarity of midday and the void of midnight. One heartbeat the man was suspended before me in a fierce light, and the next he was vanished into the gloom. I told the Sovereign we must lower him. The Sovereign at once demurred.

The combat shall be to the uttermost, for the said affront was of a mortal nature and admits of no common terms.

“Humph! I see one thing,” said Carton. “I hold another card, Mr. Barsad. Impossible, here in raging Paris, with Suspicion filling the air, for you to outlive denunciation, when you are in communication with another aristocratic spy of the same antecedents as yourself, who, moreover, has the mystery about him of having feigned death and come to life again! A plot in the prisons, of the foreigner against the Republic. A strong card--a certain Guillotine card! Do you play?”

“Well, I’ll tell you what I notice,” Carton remarked. “I’ve got another card to play, Mr. Barsad. In this feverish Paris, thick with mistrust, you can’t possibly survive being exposed—not while you’re dealing with another noble-born informer just like yourself, one who carries the added puzzle of having pretended to die and then reappeared alive! A foreign conspiracy brewing inside the prisons, aimed at the Republic. A powerful card—a sure ticket to the guillotine! Will you take the gamble?”

In great families, when an advantageous place cannot be obtained, either in possession, reversion, remainder, or expectancy, for the young man who is growing up, it is a very general custom to send him to sea. The board, in imitation of so wise and salutary an example, took counsel together on the expediency of shipping off Oliver Twist, in some small trading vessel bound to a good unhealthy port. This suggested itself as the very best thing that could possibly be done with him: the probability being, that the skipper would flog him to death, in a playful mood, some day after dinner, or would knock his brains out with an iron bar; both pastimes being, as is pretty generally known, very favourite and common recreations among gentleman of that class. The more the case presented itself to the board, in this point of view, the more manifold the advantages of the step appeared; so, they came to the conclusion that the only way of providing for Oliver effectually, was to send him to sea without delay.

In prominent households, when no suitable position—whether immediate, future, contingent, or anticipated—can be secured for a boy coming of age, it is quite customary to pack him off to sea. The board, following this prudent and time-honored precedent, deliberated on the wisdom of dispatching Oliver Twist aboard some modest merchant vessel bound for a reliably insalubrious port. The idea presented itself as the finest possible solution for the boy: the odds being that the captain, in a fit of whimsy after dinner one day, would either whip him to death or stove in his skull with an iron rod—both diversions being, as is widely acknowledged, especially cherished and habitual among gentlemen of that profession. The more the board turned the matter over from this angle, the more numerous the benefits of the measure appeared; thus they resolved that the sole effective means of settling Oliver's future was to send him to sea forthwith.

I went swiftly back, saying not a word to anybody. I told Sandy this ghastly news. We could imagine no explanation that would begin to explain. Had there been an invasion? an earthquake? a pestilence? Had the nation been swept out of existence? But guessing was profitless. I must go--at once. I borrowed the king's navy--a “ship” no bigger than a steam launch--and was soon ready.

I returned at once, speaking to no one. When I told Sandy the dreadful tidings, we found ourselves utterly unable to account for it. Had some invasion struck? An earthquake? A plague? Had the entire kingdom been blotted from the earth? Speculation was useless. I had to leave immediately. I requisitioned the king’s navy—a vessel no larger than a steam launch—and made ready without delay.

“Ah!” said the gentleman. “A Turk turns his face, after washing it well, to the East, when he says his prayers; these good people, after giving their faces such a rub against the World as to take the smiles off, turn with no less regularity, to the darkest side of Heaven. Between the Mussulman and the Pharisee, commend me to the first!”

“Ah,” the gentleman remarked. “A Muslim, after a thorough washing, faces the East to say his prayers. These pious folks, however, after scouring their expressions against the world until they’ve rubbed away every smile, turn just as consistently toward the gloomiest side of the heavens. If I must choose between the devotee and the hypocrite, I would much prefer the former!”

An oil painting caught and held him. A heavy surf thundered and burst over an outjutting rock; lowering storm-clouds covered the sky; and, outside the line of surf, a pilot-schooner, close-hauled, heeled over till every detail of her deck was visible, was surging along against a stormy sunset sky. There was beauty, and it drew him irresistibly. He forgot his awkward walk and came closer to the painting, very close. The beauty faded out of the canvas. His face expressed his bepuzzlement. He stared at what seemed a careless daub of paint, then stepped away. Immediately all the beauty flashed back into the canvas. “A trick picture,” was his thought, as he dismissed it, though in the midst of the multitudinous impressions he was receiving he found time to feel a prod of indignation that so much beauty should be sacrificed to make a trick. He did not know painting. He had been brought up on chromos and lithographs that were always definite and sharp, near or far. He had seen oil paintings, it was true, in the show windows of shops, but the glass of the windows had prevented his eager eyes from approaching too near.

An oil painting seized his attention. A massive surf roared and shattered against a jutting rock; dark storm clouds smothered the sky; and beyond the breaking waves, a pilot schooner, sails hauled tight, leaned so steeply that every line of her deck lay exposed as she drove forward against a turbulent sunset. There was beauty in it, and it pulled him with irresistible force. He forgot his clumsy gait and moved nearer to the painting, very near. The beauty dissolved from the canvas. His face clouded with bewilderment. He peered at what now looked like a careless smear of paint, then stepped back. Instantly all the beauty flared back into the canvas. “A trick picture,” he thought, dismissing it, though amid the countless impressions flooding in on him he still found room for a stab of resentment that so much beauty should be wasted on a trick. He knew nothing of painting. He had been raised on chromolithographs that stayed crisp and distinct, whether viewed up close or from a distance. He had seen oil paintings, it was true, in shop windows, but the glass had always kept his hungry eyes from pressing too near.

text: It was as if he perceived the beauty of youth for the first time in Hugh's slender, well balanced, khaki-clad body. There was infinite delicacy in his clear complexion, his clear eyes; the delicately pencilled eyebrow that was so exactly like his mother's. And this thing of brightness and bravery talked as gravely and as wisely as any weather-worn, shop-soiled, old fellow....

It was as though, in Hugh’s slim, well-proportioned figure, dressed in khaki, he were seeing for the first time the loveliness of youth. There was an exquisite frailty in his clear skin, his candid eyes; in the finely drawn eyebrow that was so unmistakably like his mother’s. And this creature of radiance and courage spoke with the same gravity and wisdom as any weather-beaten, shop-worn old man....

Thus encouraged, the matron drew near to the brink; and even Mr. Bumble himself, impelled by curiousity, ventured to do the same. The turbid water, swollen by the heavy rain, was rushing rapidly on below; and all other sounds were lost in the noise of its plashing and eddying against the green and slimy piles. There had once been a water-mill beneath; the tide foaming and chafing round the few rotten stakes, and fragments of machinery that yet remained, seemed to dart onward, with a new impulse, when freed from the obstacles which had unavailingly attempted to stem its headlong course.

Encouraged by these words, the matron stepped toward the river's edge, and even Mr. Bumble, driven by a growing inquisitiveness, dared to follow her. The murky water, bloated by the heavy rains, surged rapidly below, and every other sound was lost to the roar of its churning and splashing against the slick, mossy pilings. A water-mill had once stood there; the current, foaming and agitated as it struck the few rotting stakes and broken fragments of machinery that remained, seemed to leap forward with a sudden burst of speed once it was clear of the obstacles that had unsuccessfully tried to block its frantic path.

In pursuance of this determination, little Oliver, to his excessive astonishment, was released from bondage, and ordered to put himself into a clean shirt. He had hardly achieved this very unusual gymnastic performance, when Mr. Bumble brought him, with his own hands, a basin of gruel, and the holiday allowance of two ounces and a quarter of bread. At this tremendous sight, Oliver began to cry very piteously: thinking, not unnaturally, that the board must have determined to kill him for some useful purpose, or they never would have begun to fatten him up in that way.

Following through on this decision, little Oliver, to his utter amazement, was freed from his confinement and told to change into a fresh shirt. He had barely completed this remarkably unfamiliar physical feat when Mr. Bumble personally delivered a bowl of gruel and the special holiday portion of two and a quarter ounces of bread. Confronted by this overwhelming sight, Oliver began to weep most sorrowfully, convinced, not unreasonably, that the board must have resolved to dispatch him for some practical end—otherwise they would never have started to fatten him up in such a manner.

“I spent the winter in this manner. The gentle manners and beauty of the cottagers greatly endeared them to me; when they were unhappy, I felt depressed; when they rejoiced, I sympathised in their joys. I saw few human beings besides them, and if any other happened to enter the cottage, their harsh manners and rude gait only enhanced to me the superior accomplishments of my friends. The old man, I could perceive, often endeavoured to encourage his children, as sometimes I found that he called them, to cast off their melancholy. He would talk in a cheerful accent, with an expression of goodness that bestowed pleasure even upon me. Agatha listened with respect, her eyes sometimes filled with tears, which she endeavoured to wipe away unperceived; but I generally found that her countenance and tone were more cheerful after having listened to the exhortations of her father. It was not thus with Felix. He was always the saddest of the group, and even to my unpractised senses, he appeared to have suffered more deeply than his friends. But if his countenance was more sorrowful, his voice was more cheerful than that of his sister, especially when he addressed the old man.

I passed the winter in this way. The cottagers’ gentle bearing and beauty greatly won my affection; when sorrow weighed upon them, I felt my own spirits sink, and when they were glad, I shared in their gladness. I saw few other human beings besides them, and whenever anyone else entered the cottage, their rough manners and uncouth gait only made the superior grace of my friends seem greater still. I could see that the old man often tried to hearten his children, as I sometimes heard him call them, and to drive away their sadness. He would speak in a cheerful voice, with such a look of kindness that it gave pleasure even to me. Agatha listened with reverence, her eyes at times brimming with tears, which she tried to brush away unseen; yet I usually found her face and manner brighter after she had heard her father’s words of encouragement. It was not so with Felix. He remained the saddest of them all, and even to my inexpert judgment, he seemed to have endured more deeply than the others. Yet if his face was more mournful, his voice was brighter than his sister’s, especially when he spoke to the old man.

He had laid aside his coat and waistcoat; his shirt was open at the throat, as it used to be when he did that work; and even the old haggard, faded surface of face had come back to him. He worked hard--impatiently--as if in some sense of having been interrupted.

He’d shed his coat and waistcoat, his shirt gaping open at the throat just as it always was when he did this particular work; even that old haggard, faded cast to his features had settled back across his face. He labored fiercely, sharp with impatience, as if he carried the quiet sense that he’d been pulled away from the task before he could finish it.

Let us compare our MSD dataset against both publicly available ST and TS datasets. Simp-Wiki (Van den Bercken et al., 2019) is a TS dataset derived from the linked articles between Simple Wikipedia and Normal Wikipedia. It focuses on the medical domain and extracts parallel sentences automatically by computing their BLEU scores. GYAFC (Rao and Tetreault, 2018) is the largest ST dataset on formality in the domains of Entertainment & Music (E&M) and Family & Relationships (F&R) from Yahoo Answers. It contains more than 50,000 training sentences (non-parallel) for each domain, and over 1,000 parallel sentences for testing, obtained by rewriting informal answers via Amazon Mechanical Turk. Yelp and Amazon are sentiment ST datasets by rewriting reviews based on crowdsourcing. They both contain over 270k training sentences (non-parallel) and 500 parallel sentences for evaluation. Authorship (Xu et al., 2012) aims at transferring styles between modern English and Shakespearean English. It contains 18,395 sentences for training (non-parallel) and 1,462 sentence pairs for testing. Table 2 presents the statistics of expertise and layman sentences in our dataset as well as Sim-pWiki. We split the sentences using NLTK, and compute the ratio of layman to expert in each metric to denote the gap between the two styles (a lower value implies a smaller gap expect that for #Sentence). Three standard readability indices are used to evaluate the simplicity levels: FleshKincaid (Kincaid et al., 1975) , Gunning (Gunning, 1968 ) and Coleman (Coleman and Liau, 1975) . The lower the indices are, the simpler the sentence is. Note that SimpWiki does not provide a train/test split, and thus we randomly sample 350 sentence pairs for evaluation. We follow the same strategy in our experiments.

metadata.title: Expertise Style Transfer: A New Task Towards Better Communication between Experts and Laymen

text: We situate our MSD dataset in relation to publicly available style transfer (ST) and text simplification (TS) corpora. Simp-Wiki (Van den Bercken et al., 2019) is a TS dataset constructed from linked articles in Simple Wikipedia and standard Wikipedia, concentrating on the medical domain and automatically extracting parallel sentences via BLEU score computation. GYAFC (Rao and Tetreault, 2018) constitutes the largest ST dataset targeting formality in the Entertainment & Music (E&M) and Family & Relationships (F&R) domains sourced from Yahoo Answers; it supplies over 50,000 non-parallel training sentences per domain and more than 1,000 parallel test sentences obtained by crowdsourcing informal-to-formal rewrites on Amazon Mechanical Turk. The Yelp and Amazon datasets are sentiment-oriented ST corpora in which reviews are rewritten through crowdsourcing, each comprising over 270k non-parallel training sentences and 500 parallel evaluation pairs. Authorship (Xu et al., 2012) addresses style transfer between modern English and Shakespearean English, providing 18,395 non-parallel training sentences and 1,462 parallel test pairs. Table 2 reports the statistics of expert and layman sentences in our dataset alongside Simp-Wiki. We tokenize sentences with NLTK and compute the layman-to-expert ratio for each metric to capture the stylistic gap (a lower value indicates a smaller gap, except for #Sentence). Three standard readability formulas assess simplicity: Flesch–Kincaid (Kincaid et al., 1975), Gunning (Gunning, 1968), and Coleman–Liau (Coleman and Liau, 1975); lower index values correspond to simpler sentences. Because Simp-Wiki lacks a predefined train/test partition, we randomly sample 350 sentence pairs for evaluation and adopt the same procedure in our experiments.

It was a perfectly appointed, small steam laundry, wherein the most modern machinery did everything that was possible for machinery to do. Martin, after a few instructions, sorted the great heaps of soiled clothes, while Joe started the masher and made up fresh supplies of soft-soap, compounded of biting chemicals that compelled him to swathe his mouth and nostrils and eyes in bath-towels till he resembled a mummy. Finished the sorting, Martin lent a hand in wringing the clothes. This was done by dumping them into a spinning receptacle that went at a rate of a few thousand revolutions a minute, tearing the water from the clothes by centrifugal force. Then Martin began to alternate between the dryer and the wringer, between times “shaking out” socks and stockings. By the afternoon, one feeding and one stacking up, they were running socks and stockings through the mangle while the irons were heating. Then it was hot irons and underclothes till six o’clock, at which time Joe shook his head dubiously.

The laundry was a compact, immaculate establishment, fitted with the latest machines to perform every task a machine could undertake. After a brief orientation, Martin set to sorting the mountainous piles of soiled linen, while Joe fired up the masher and prepared a fresh batch of soft-soap—a brew so acrid with harsh chemicals that he had to wrap his mouth, nose, and eyes in bath-towels until he looked like a mummy. Once the sorting was done, Martin helped with the wringing. They heaved the wet garments into a spinning drum that whirled at several thousand revolutions per minute, centrifugal force slinging the water out of the cloth. From then on, Martin shuttled between the dryer and the wringer, pausing now and then to “shake out” socks and stockings. By afternoon, one man feeding and the other stacking, they were running hosiery through the mangle while the irons heated. Then came hot irons and undergarments until six o’clock, when Joe gave a doubtful shake of his head.

Strangely enough he began to feel now that it would not be so hard to persuade her who was his best friend that comparative poverty was essentially the higher course for him, as to reconcile to his feelings the act of persuading her. From every provident point of view his mother was so undoubtedly right, that he was not without a sickness of heart in finding he could shake her.

Curiously, it dawned on him at last that the true difficulty lay not in convincing his dearest ally that a modest station was, for him, the nobler path, but in making peace with his own conscience for having swayed her. By every prudent measure her judgment was so faultless that a quiet ache settled in his chest when he realized he possessed the power to unsettle it.

text: PvSL observe that this procedure can also be applied to neural networks. Since a model’s surprisal when it receives an input decreases as it is subsequently exposed to similar inputs, one can cumulatively “prime” a model by adapting it toward a particular class of input (van Schijndel and . The reasoning is that if a model can be primed for a particular syntactic structure, this implies that it is able to recognize that structure and, therefore, has learned a representation of it.

With the next morning, there came a rumour, that two men and a boy were in the cage at Kingston, who had been apprehended over night under suspicious circumstances; and to Kingston Messrs. Blathers and Duff journeyed accordingly. The suspicious circumstances, however, resolving themselves, on investigation, into the one fact, that they had been discovered sleeping under a haystack; which, although a great crime, is only punishable by imprisonment, and is, in the merciful eye of the English law, and its comprehensive love of all the King’s subjects, held to be no satisfactory proof, in the absence of all other evidence, that the sleeper, or sleepers, have committed burglary accompanied with violence, and have therefore rendered themselves liable to the punishment of death; Messrs. Blathers and Duff came back again, as wise as they went.

At daybreak, word spread that three strangers—two grown men and a lad—had been locked in the Kingston jail, seized the previous evening on questionable grounds. Accordingly, Messrs. Crane and Tully set off for Kingston. Yet the questionable grounds, upon closer scrutiny, dwindled to a single detail: they had been found asleep beneath a hayrick. Now, though this is indeed a serious offense, it warrants only confinement, and in the charitable view of English law, with its ample affection for every subject of the Crown, mere slumber under straw, absent any further proof, cannot be taken as certain evidence that the slumberer or slumberers have perpetrated a violent burglary and thus made themselves answerable to the gallows. Crane and Tully, therefore, returned no wiser than when they departed.

This dialogue had taken place at Mr. Lorry’s usual desk, with Monseigneur swarming within a yard or two of it, boastful of what he would do to avenge himself on the rascal-people before long. It was too much the way of Monseigneur under his reverses as a refugee, and it was much too much the way of native British orthodoxy, to talk of this terrible Revolution as if it were the only harvest ever known under the skies that had not been sown--as if nothing had ever been done, or omitted to be done, that had led to it--as if observers of the wretched millions in France, and of the misused and perverted resources that should have made them prosperous, had not seen it inevitably coming, years before, and had not in plain words recorded what they saw. Such vapouring, combined with the extravagant plots of Monseigneur for the restoration of a state of things that had utterly exhausted itself, and worn out Heaven and earth as well as itself, was hard to be endured without some remonstrance by any sane man who knew the truth. And it was such vapouring all about his ears, like a troublesome confusion of blood in his own head, added to a latent uneasiness in his mind, which had already made Charles Darnay restless, and which still kept him so.

This exchange unfolded at Mr. Lorry’s regular work desk, with a crowd of refugee aristocrats clustered barely three feet away, loudly bragging about how they would soon take brutal revenge on the lowborn rabble they blamed for their troubles. That kind of talk was par for the course for these deposed nobles navigating their exile, and just as common among rigid, conventional British thinkers of the time: they spoke of the horrific Revolution as if it were the only harvest in human history that had sprung up entirely unseeded. They acted as if no choice, no failure to act, had paved the way for the uprising; as if the people who’d watched France’s millions of destitute citizens suffer, who’d seen the resources that should have made them thrive hoarded and misused for the elite, hadn’t seen the collapse coming for years, and hadn’t documented their observations in clear, unambiguous writing. All this empty posturing, paired with the aristocrats’ absurd schemes to restore a regime that had already completely devoured itself, alienating every last person on earth and even defying all sense of divine justice in the process, was almost impossible for any sensible person who knew the full truth to tolerate without speaking up. And it was this endless stream of loud, foolish rhetoric swirling right around him, buzzing in his ears like the disorienting thud of blood rushing to his own skull, layered over the quiet, underlying anxiety he already held in his chest, that had first left Charles Darnay restless – and that still kept him that way.

“I’ll tell you,” said Sydney. “I lighted on you, Mr. Barsad, coming out of the prison of the Conciergerie while I was contemplating the walls, an hour or more ago. You have a face to be remembered, and I remember faces well. Made curious by seeing you in that connection, and having a reason, to which you are no stranger, for associating you with the misfortunes of a friend now very unfortunate, I walked in your direction. I walked into the wine-shop here, close after you, and sat near you. I had no difficulty in deducing from your unreserved conversation, and the rumour openly going about among your admirers, the nature of your calling. And gradually, what I had done at random, seemed to shape itself into a purpose, Mr. Barsad.”

“Let me enlighten you,” Julian said softly. “My gaze chanced upon you, Mr. Vane, as you departed the shadows of the Châtelet while I stood observing its heavy walls some time ago. Your features possess a distinct quality, and I have a keen faculty for recollection. Spurred by the sight of you at that particular threshold, and mindful of your past link to the calamities of a friend who now languishes in sorrow, I took it upon myself to trail you. I followed you into this very cellar, keeping close, and found a place beside you. From your own reckless speech and the common talk of the ruffians who surround you, your true profession was easily unmasked. And so, what started as a wandering impulse has begun to take the form of a design, Mr. Vane.”

When Mr. Britling, after a strenuous morning among high ideals, descended for lunch, he found Mr. Lawrence Carmine had come over to join him at that meal. Mr. Carmine was standing in the hall with his legs very wide apart reading _The Times_ for the fourth time. "I can do no work," he said, turning round. "I can't fix my mind. I suppose we are going to war. I'd got so used to the war with Germany that I never imagined it would happen. Gods! what a bore it will be.... And Maxse and all those scaremongers cock-a-hoop and 'I told you so.' Damn these Germans!"

When Mr. Audley, after a laborious morning in the company of lofty principles, came down to luncheon, he discovered that Mr. Lionel Carfax had ridden over to share that meal with him. Mr. Carfax was standing in the hall, his feet planted very wide apart, reading _The Times_ for the fourth time. “I can do no work,” he said, turning about. “I can’t collect my thoughts. I suppose we are going to war. I had grown so accustomed to the war with Germany that I never imagined it would really come. Heaven help us! What a nuisance it will be.... And Havelock and all those alarmists crowing and saying, ‘I told you so.’ Curse these Germans!”

While he trotted back with the message he was to deliver to the night watchman in his box at the door of Tellson’s Bank, by Temple Bar, who was to deliver it to greater authorities within, the shadows of the night took such shapes to him as arose out of the message, and took such shapes to the mare as arose out of _her_ private topics of uneasiness. They seemed to be numerous, for she shied at every shadow on the road.

As he jogged back with the note he had been charged to carry to the night watchman in his little shelter at the gate of Mellor’s Bank, near Temple Bar, from whom it was to pass to higher powers inside, the night’s dimness assumed for him whatever forms the errand suggested, and for the mare whatever shapes sprang from her own particular anxieties. They appeared to be many indeed, for she started at every darkness that crossed the road.

I have felt it myself for years and said so in private.... Knowing what I did.... To such friends as I could trust not to misunderstand me.... Now at least I can speak out.

“Take my word for it,” said Aunt Wetherby in her most solemn manner. “The man is mad. It will be shown in the end. He will finish his life in an asylum—as a lunatic. I have known it in my bones for years, and I have said as much in confidence.... I was aware of things.... To those few friends I could depend on not to take me amiss.... At last, at any rate, I am free to speak plainly.”
