“But it isn’t necessary for us to have one. We benefit from the work of specialists. That is what they are there for. When I came in, I saw the chimney sweeps hard at work. They are specialists, and once they have finished, you will have clean chimneys without needing to know anything about how chimneys are built.”

“I have not fallen in love with you, Mr. Boldwood—certainly I must say that.” She allowed a very small smile to creep for the first time over her serious face in saying this, and the white row of upper teeth, and keenly-cut lips already noticed, suggested an idea of heartlessness, which was immediately contradicted by the pleasant eyes.

“I haven’t fallen for you, Mr. Boldwood—I have to make that perfectly clear.” As she spoke, a tiny smile tugged across her usually solemn face for the very first time. The flash of her even white upper teeth, paired with the sharply defined lips everyone had already taken note of, gave a fleeting impression of cold heartlessness, an impression that was immediately erased by the warm, pleasant glow of her eyes.

One thing was certain: the Morses had not cared to have him for himself or for his work. Therefore they could not want him now for himself or for his work, but for the fame that was his, because he was somebody amongst men, and—why not?—because he had a hundred thousand dollars or so. That was the way bourgeois society valued a man, and who was he to expect it otherwise? But he was proud. He disdained such valuation. He desired to be valued for himself, or for his work, which, after all, was an expression of himself. That was the way Lizzie valued him. The work, with her, did not even count. She valued him, himself. That was the way Jimmy, the plumber, and all the old gang valued him. That had been proved often enough in the days when he ran with them; it had been proved that Sunday at Shell Mound Park. His work could go hang. What they liked, and were willing to scrap for, was just Mart Eden, one of the bunch and a pretty good guy.

This much was beyond dispute: the Morses had never wanted him for his own sake or for his writing. So they could hardly want him now for his own sake or for his writing, but only for the renown that clung to him, because he counted among men, and—why pretend otherwise?—because he had a hundred thousand dollars or thereabouts. That was how the middle class weighed a man, and what right had he to imagine anything different? Yet he was proud. He scorned such reckoning. He longed to be prized for himself, or for his writing, which was, in the end, a part of himself. That was how Lizzie prized him. With her, the writing did not even enter. She prized him, the man. That was how Jimmy, the plumber, and all the old crew prized him. They had shown it often enough in the days when he ran beside them; they had shown it that Sunday at Shell Mound Park. His writing could be damned. What they loved, and would fight for, was simply Mart Eden, one of their own and a decent fellow.

Apart from translators' preference, productivity gains of 36% when using modern neural MT for PE (Toral et al., 2018) already result in substantial changes in translation workflows (Zaretskaya and Seghiri, 2018) and will probably continue to do so the better MT becomes. Thus, PE requires thorough investigation in terms of interface design, since the task changes from mostly text production to comparing and adapting MT and translation memory (TM) proposals, or put differently, from control to supervision. Previous elicitation-based research (Herbig et al., 2019a) investigated how translation environments could better support the PE process and found that translators envision PE interfaces relying on touch, pen, and speech input combined with mouse and keyboard as particularly useful. A small number of prototypes exploring some of these modalities also showed promising results (Teixeira et al., 2019) . This paper presents MMPE, the first translation environment combining standard mouse & keyboard input with touch, pen, and speech interactions for PE of MT. The results of a study with 11 professional translators show that participants are enthusiastic about having these alternatives, even though time measurements and subjective ratings do not always agree. Overall, pen and touch modalities are well suited for deletion and reordering operations, while speech and multi-modal interaction are suitable for insertions and replacements.

metadata.title: MMPE: A Multi-Modal Interface for Post-Editing Machine Translation

text: Beyond individual translator preferences, the 36% productivity increase reported for neural machine translation post-editing (Toral et al., 2018) is already reshaping translation workflows (Zaretskaya and Seghiri, 2018) and will likely exert growing influence as MT quality continues to improve. Consequently, post-editing demands careful scrutiny from an interface design perspective, because the activity shifts from predominantly generating text to evaluating and refining suggestions from MT and translation memory—or, more succinctly, from active control to supervisory oversight. Prior work employing elicitation methods (Herbig et al., 2019a) explored how translation environments could more effectively assist post-editing and identified that translators consider interfaces integrating touch, pen, and speech input alongside mouse and keyboard especially promising. A handful of prototypes examining some of these modalities have also yielded encouraging outcomes (Teixeira et al., 2019). This paper introduces MMPE, the first translation environment that unites conventional mouse-and-keyboard input with touch, pen, and speech modalities for MT post-editing. Findings from a study involving 11 professional translators indicate that participants respond enthusiastically to having these alternative modalities available, even when temporal metrics and subjective assessments are not fully aligned. Broadly, pen and touch modalities prove well-suited for deletion and reordering tasks, whereas speech and multimodal interaction excel at insertions and substitutions.

Eustacia turned, entered the house, and ascended to the front bedroom, where a shaded light was burning. In the bed lay Clym, pale, haggard, wide awake, tossing to one side and to the other, his eyes lit by a hot light, as if the fire in their pupils were burning up their substance.

Eustacia turned around, went inside, and climbed the stairs to the front bedroom, where a lamp glowed behind a shade. On the bed rested Clym, pallid, worn, fully conscious, shifting restlessly from side to side, his eyes gleaming with a fierce brightness, as though the blaze within them was consuming his very being.

“Now, let me recommend you,” pursued Stryver, “to look it in the face. I have looked it in the face, in my different way; look it in the face, you, in your different way. Marry. Provide somebody to take care of you. Never mind your having no enjoyment of women’s society, nor understanding of it, nor tact for it. Find out somebody. Find out some respectable woman with a little property--somebody in the landlady way, or lodging-letting way--and marry her, against a rainy day. That’s the kind of thing for _you_. Now think of it, Sydney.”

“Now, I advise you,” persisted Thorne, “to confront the matter directly. I have faced it after my own fashion; you must face it after yours. Take a wife. Secure someone to oversee your comfort. Pay no heed to your lack of relish for feminine company, nor your ignorance of their nature, nor your want of social grace. Seek someone out. Find a reputable woman with a modest inheritance—someone of the domestic or house-managing sort—and wed her as a hedge against hard times. That is precisely the arrangement for a man like you. Reflect upon it, Elias.”

“It is just as I tell you. I did not find out these things at once, but I found them out finally. Did you send me verbal information, by the commander of the ship, to the effect that upon his return to you, with supplies, you were going to leave Cadiz--”

“It’s exactly as I’ve said. I didn’t uncover these matters straightaway, but in the end I did piece them together. Did you dispatch word to me, through the ship’s captain, that once he came back to you with provisions, you intended to depart from Cadiz—”

The people that ran to him were stricken dumb to find that the life was actually gone out of the man and no reason for it visible, no hurt upon his body, nothing like a wound. There was a hole through the breast of his chain-mail, but they attached no importance to a little thing like that; and as a bullet wound there produces but little blood, none came in sight because of the clothing and swaddlings under the armor. The body was dragged over to let the king and the swells look down upon it. They were stupefied with astonishment naturally. I was requested to come and explain the miracle. But I remained in my tracks, like a statue, and said:

When I brought my procession of human bats up into the open world and the glare of the afternoon sun--previously blindfolding them, in charity for eyes so long untortured by light--they were a spectacle to look at. Skeletons, scarecrows, goblins, pathetic frights, every one; legitimatest possible children of Monarchy by the Grace of God and the Established Church. I muttered absently:

Some say it is the spheres at play!
Some say that bright majority
Of vanished dames and men!
Some think it service in the place
Where we, with late, celestial face,
Please God, shall ascertain!

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
Some claim it’s the music of the stars!
Some claim that radiant company
Of lost women and men!
Some hold it’s duty in the realm
Where we, at last, with heaven-lit face,
Shall, God willing, know for certain!

The farmers were bound to throw in something, to sort of offset my liberality, whether I would or no; so I let them give me a flint and steel; and as soon as they had comfortably bestowed Sandy and me on our horse, I lit my pipe. When the first blast of smoke shot out through the bars of my helmet, all those people broke for the woods, and Sandy went over backwards and struck the ground with a dull thud. They thought I was one of those fire-belching dragons they had heard so much about from knights and other professional liars. I had infinite trouble to persuade those people to venture back within explaining distance. Then I told them that this was only a bit of enchantment which would work harm to none but my enemies. And I promised, with my hand on my heart, that if all who felt no enmity toward me would come forward and pass before me they should see that only those who remained behind would be struck dead. The procession moved with a good deal of promptness. There were no casualties to report, for nobody had curiosity enough to remain behind to see what would happen.

The farmers felt compelled to contribute something, as a sort of return for my generosity, whether I wanted it or not; so I allowed them to give me a flint and steel. And as soon as they had snugly seated Sandy and me on the horse, I struck a light for my pipe. When the first plume of smoke burst out through the bars of my helmet, the whole crowd bolted for the woods, and Sandy toppled backward and hit the ground with a heavy thump. They took me for one of those fire-belching dragons they had heard so much about from knights and other professional liars. I had endless difficulty persuading them to come back within speaking range. At last I told them this was merely a bit of enchantment that would harm no one except my enemies. Then I declared, hand over my heart, that if all who bore me no ill will would come forward and pass in front of me, they should see that only those who stayed behind would be struck dead. The procession got under way with surprising speed. There were no losses to report, for no one was curious enough to linger and see what would happen.

The attendant did as she was told: shaking her head meanwhile, to intimate that the woman would not die so easily; having done so, she resumed her seat by the side of the other nurse, who had by this time returned. The mistress, with an expression of impatience, wrapped herself in her shawl, and sat at the foot of the bed.

The servant performed the task as bid, shaking her head to suggest the patient would not expire so quickly; having finished, she sat back down beside the fellow-watcher, who had by then returned. The mistress, with a look of irritation, pulled her shawl tight and took a seat at the bed’s end.

“A good many of us knew as well as He before long,” said the captain, chuckling. “I lived seven years under water on account of it in my boyhood—in that damned surgery of the Triumph, seeing men brought down to the cockpit with their legs and arms blown to Jericho.... And so the young man has settled in Paris. Manager to a diamond merchant, or some such thing, is he not?”

“A good many of us came to know it just as well as he did before long,” said the captain with a chuckle. “I spent seven years under water because of it when I was a boy—in that cursed surgery on the Triumph, watching men carried down to the cockpit with their arms and legs blown to bits.... So the young fellow has settled in Paris. He’s manager to a diamond merchant, or something of the sort, isn’t he?”

And yet, somehow, he had a feeling that Brissenden had been right, after all. “The Shame of the Sun” had been the cause of his success more than the stuff he had written. That stuff had been merely incidental. It had been rejected right and left by the magazines. The publication of “The Shame of the Sun” had started a controversy and precipitated the landslide in his favor. Had there been no “Shame of the Sun” there would have been no landslide, and had there been no miracle in the go of “The Shame of the Sun” there would have been no landslide. Singletree, Darnley & Co. attested that miracle. They had brought out a first edition of fifteen hundred copies and been dubious of selling it. They were experienced publishers and no one had been more astounded than they at the success which had followed. To them it had been in truth a miracle. They never got over it, and every letter they wrote him reflected their reverent awe of that first mysterious happening. They did not attempt to explain it. There was no explaining it. It had happened. In the face of all experience to the contrary, it had happened.

Still, he couldn’t shake the sense that Brissenden had been correct all along. The triumph belonged less to his own writing than to “The Shame of the Sun.” His work had merely tagged along. Magazine after magazine had turned it down. It was the publication of “The Shame of the Sun” that ignited a firestorm and set off the avalanche in his favor. Without that piece, there would have been no avalanche; without the inexplicable magic of its release, no avalanche either. Singletree, Darnley & Co. could vouch for that magic. They had printed a cautious first run of fifteen hundred copies, doubtful they’d ever sell them. Seasoned publishers, they were more stunned than anyone by the ensuing success. To them it was a genuine marvel. They never fully recovered from the shock, and every letter they sent him quivered with reverent wonder at that first uncanny event. They offered no explanation. There was none to give. It had simply occurred. Against every known precedent, it had occurred.

"I think it is because the affair has surprised him right out of acting Ortheris and Tommy Atkins for a bit, into his proper self. He's frightfully like some sort of mongrel with a lot of wiry-haired terrier and a touch of Airedale in it. A mongrel you like in spite of the flavour of all the horrid things he's been nosing into. And he's as hard as nails and, my dear daddy! he can't box for nuts."

I imagine the ordeal has startled him out of his soldierly performance and back into his genuine self. He is remarkably like a scruffy mongrel, a mix of wiry terrier with a touch of Airedale. He is the sort of stray you find yourself liking, despite the foulness of everything he’s been poking his nose into. He is as tough as iron, but—good heavens—he is an absolutely pathetic boxer.

“His rooms we’re going to. One time professor—fired from university—usual story. A mind like a steel trap. Makes his living any old way. I know he’s been a street fakir when he was down. Unscrupulous. Rob a corpse of a shroud—anything. Difference between him and the bourgeoisie is that he robs without illusion. He’ll talk Nietzsche, or Schopenhauer, or Kant, or anything, but the only thing in this world, not excepting Mary, that he really cares for, is his monism. Haeckel is his little tin god. The only way to insult him is to take a slap at Haeckel.”

We’re heading to his place right now. He used to be a professor, got canned from the university, same old run-of-the-mill story. He’s got a mind as sharp as a steel trap, never lets a single detail slip past him. He scrapes by doing whatever odd work he can scare up, and I know for a fact he worked as a cheap street con artist when he hit rock bottom. The man’s completely unscrupulous—would steal the burial shroud right off a corpse if it suited him, no questions asked, no guilt whatsoever. The only real difference between him and your typical bourgeois type is that he doesn’t hide behind self-serving illusions when he takes what he wants. He’ll hold forth for hours about Nietzsche, Schopenhauer, Kant, any philosopher you bring up, but the only thing in the entire world he actually cares about—not even Mary is an exception—is his monist philosophy. Haeckel is his personal little tin god, the one figure he reveres without question. The only surefire way to insult him is to talk trash about Haeckel.

“I thought that till half an hour ago. But, after all, why should her going off with him to Anglebury for a few hours do her any harm? Anybody who knows how pure she is will feel any such thought to be quite unjust. I have been trying this morning to help on this marriage with Wildeve—yes, I, ma’am—in the belief that I ought to do it, because she was so wrapped up in him. But I much question if I was right, after all. However, nothing came of it. And now I offer myself.”

“I held that conviction until this very hour. Yet, in truth, why should a mere afternoon’s excursion to Oakhaven in his company work her any lasting injury? To any witness of her inherent virtue, such a suspicion must seem a grave injustice. Only this morning, I labored to encourage her union with Sterling—yes, I, madam—believing it my duty to honor her devotion. But I find myself questioning the rectitude of that course now. In any case, the effort failed. And thus, I offer myself in his stead.”

Again she started to remove her hand. He felt a momentary curiosity. Now that she was out of danger she was afraid. She was in almost a panic to be quit of him. He could see no reason for it and attributed it to her nervousness. So he restrained her withdrawing hand and started to walk on with her. Halfway down the block, he saw a man in a long overcoat shrink back into a doorway. He shot a glance in as he passed by, and, despite the high turned-up collar, he was certain that he recognized Ruth’s brother, Norman.

Once more, she tried to pull her hand away. He felt a flicker of curiosity; now that she was no longer in danger, fear seemed to take hold of her. She appeared almost desperate to get away from him. Finding no logical reason for it, he assumed it was just her nerves. He held her hand firmly to stop her from withdrawing and continued walking beside her. Halfway down the block, he noticed a man in a long coat ducking back into a doorway. He stole a quick look as he passed and, despite the high, upturned collar, he was certain he recognized Norman, Ruth’s brother.

The girl had taken a few restless turns to and fro—closely watched meanwhile by her hidden observer—when the heavy bell of St. Paul’s tolled for the death of another day. Midnight had come upon the crowded city. The palace, the night-cellar, the jail, the madhouse: the chambers of birth and death, of health and sickness, the rigid face of the corpse and the calm sleep of the child: midnight was upon them all.

The young woman paced back and forth in short, uneasy strides—all the while under the keen scrutiny of the one who watched from the shadows—as the great bell of St. Paul’s rang out, mourning the passing of yet another day. Midnight had descended upon the teeming metropolis. The royal court, the underground tavern, the prison, the asylum: the rooms where lives began and ended, where vigor met decay, the stiffened mask of the dead and the untroubled slumber of the infant: midnight covered them all.

We no longer groan and heap ashes upon our heads when the flames of Tophet are mentioned. For, even the preachers have begun to tell us that God is radium, or ether or some scientific compound, and that the worst we wicked ones may expect is a chemical reaction. This is a pleasing hypothesis; but there lingers yet some of the old, goodly terror of orthodoxy.

We no longer lament or scatter ashes upon our brows at the mention of hell’s eternal fires. Even our ministers have begun to suggest that the Creator is composed of radium or ether—some scientific substance—and that the most a sinner need fear is a simple chemical transformation. While this is a comforting theory, a remnant of that old, righteous terror still haunts our modern hearts.

After a short return of the stupor in which he had been so long plunged, Oliver: urged by a creeping sickness at his heart, which seemed to warn him that if he lay there, he must surely die: got upon his feet, and essayed to walk. His head was dizzy, and he staggered to and fro like a drunken man. But he kept up, nevertheless, and, with his head drooping languidly on his breast, went stumbling onward, he knew not whither.

Oliver drifted back from the stupor that had held him so long, only to feel a slow, sickening dread coil around his heart—a dread that whispered he would surely die if he stayed motionless. He forced himself upright and tried to walk. Dizziness swam through his head, and he reeled and swayed like a drunkard. Yet he remained on his feet, and with his head lolling heavily against his chest, he staggered forward, blind to where his steps might lead.

"You see that old felled tree there. I was sitting on it the day before yesterday and thinking of you. Will you come there and sit with me on it? When you sit on it you get a view, oh! a perfectly lovely English view, just a bit of the house and those clumps of trees and the valley away there with the lily pond. I'd love to have you in my memory of it...."

Do you see that fallen timber over there? I rested upon it only the day before yesterday, my mind filled with thoughts of you. Won't you accompany me to that spot and sit a while? From that seat, one is granted a view—oh, a most charming English prospect!—offering a glimpse of the manor, those stands of trees, and the valley stretching toward the lily pond. I should so like to include you in my remembrance of it.

They had not talked much upon the way; for Oliver was in a flutter of agitation and uncertainty which deprived him of the power of collecting his thoughts, and almost of speech, and appeared to have scarcely less effect on his companions, who shared it, in at least an equal degree. He and the two ladies had been very carefully made acquainted by Mr. Brownlow with the nature of the admissions which had been forced from Monks; and although they knew that the object of their present journey was to complete the work which had been so well begun, still the whole matter was enveloped in enough of doubt and mystery to leave them in endurance of the most intense suspense.

They exchanged very few words along the way. Oliver was caught in a flurry of restlessness and unresolvable doubt that left him unable to collect his thoughts, barely even able to speak at all, and that same frayed unease seemed to weigh just as heavily on his two female traveling companions, if not more. Mr. Brownlow had already carefully walked all three of them through the full substance of the confessions Monks had been pressured into making, and while they understood their present journey was meant to see through the final steps of the work that had already been so successfully launched, the whole affair was still shrouded in enough ambiguity and secrecy to leave them caught in a state of nearly unbearable, taut suspense.

The two sat down very quietly to tea in Bathsheba’s parlour in the evening of the same day, for it had been arranged that Farmer Oak should go there to live, since he had as yet neither money, house, nor furniture worthy of the name, though he was on a sure way towards them, whilst Bathsheba was, comparatively, in a plethora of all three.

The pair shared a hushed evening tea in Elara’s sitting-room later that same day, for it was settled that Silas should take up residence there. He was, as yet, without capital, a dwelling, or any effects of significant merit—notwithstanding his steady progress toward their acquisition—whereas Elara possessed a comparative surfeit of all three.

“Why, what the blazes is in the wind now!” growled a deep voice. “Who pitched that ’ere at me? It’s well it’s the beer, and not the pot, as hit me, or I’d have settled somebody. I might have know’d, as nobody but an infernal, rich, plundering, thundering old Jew could afford to throw away any drink but water—and not that, unless he done the River Company every quarter. Wot’s it all about, Fagin? D—me, if my neck-handkercher an’t lined with beer! Come in, you sneaking warmint; wot are you stopping outside for, as if you was ashamed of your master! Come in!”

“What in the nine hells is going on now?” rumbled a gravel-throated growl. “Who slung that mess at my head? Lucky for ’em it was just the beer, not the mug, that connected, or I’d have cracked some fool’s skull open. I oughta guessed, only a cursed, hoarding, swindling, roaring old grifter like you could afford to toss good drink away when even water’s too precious unless you cheat the water company out of their dues every quarter. What’s the big idea, Ezra? Blimey, my whole neckerchief’s soaked through with suds! Get in here, you skulking little rat—what’re you loitering out there for, like you’re ashamed to be seen with your master? Get in!”

In addition to those four transformations, Penman adds a few more methods. The rearrange method operates on a tree and sorts the order of branches by their branch labels. Besides changing the order of branches, their structure is unchanged by this method. Van Noord and Bos (2017) similarly rearranged tree branches based on surface alignments. The reconfigure method configures a tree from a graph after discarding the layout markers in the epigraph and sorting the triples based on their roles. Unlike the rearrange method, reconfigure affects the entire structure of the graph except for which node is the graph's top. For both of these, the sorting methods are defined by the model, and Penman includes three such methods: original order, random order, and canonical order. For rearrange there are additional sorting methods applicable to trees: alphanumeric order, attributesfirst order, and inverted-last order. Since node variables in AMR are conventionally assigned in order of their appearance and the above methods can change this order, the reset-variables method reassigns the variables based on the new tree.

metadata.title: Penman: An Open-Source Library and Tool for AMR Graphs

text: Beyond those four transformations, Penman introduces several additional methods. The rearrange method acts on a tree and reorders its branches according to their branch labels. While the ordering of branches is altered, the internal structure of the tree remains intact. Van Noord and Bos (2017) employed a comparable approach, reordering tree branches based on surface alignments. The reconfigure method constructs a tree from a graph by removing the layout markers in the epigraph and sorting the triples according to their roles. In contrast to rearrange, reconfigure modifies the entire structure of the graph except for the designation of the top node. For both methods, the sorting criteria are specified by the model, and Penman provides three such options: original order, random order, and canonical order. For rearrange, additional tree-specific sorting methods are available: alphanumeric order, attributes-first order, and inverted-last order. Because node variables in AMR are conventionally assigned in the order they appear and the above methods can alter this sequence, the reset-variables method reassigns variables based on the newly ordered tree.

The graph language described above is expressive enough to support many interesting queries, but it is also very tedious to specify query graphs G, especially for non-expert users. We propose a simple syntax that allows to easily specify a graph query G (constrained nodes connected by labeled edges) using a textual query q that takes the form of an example sentence and lightweight markup. Let s = w 1 , ..., w n be a proper English sentence. Let D be its dependency graph, with nodes w i and labeled edges (w i , w j , ). A corresponding textual query q takes the form q = q 1 , ..., q n , where each q i is either a word q i = w i , or a marked word Each of these corresponds to a node v qi in the query graph above.

metadata.title: Syntactic Search by Example

text: The graph language described above is expressive enough to support many interesting queries, but specifying query graphs G is also very tedious, especially for non-expert users. We therefore propose a simple syntax that makes it easy to specify a graph query G—namely, constrained nodes connected by labeled edges—using a textual query q composed of an example sentence and lightweight markup. Let s = w1, ..., wn be a well-formed English sentence. Let D denote its dependency graph, with nodes wi and labeled edges (wi, wj, ). A corresponding textual query q has the form q = q1, ..., qn, where each qi is either a word, qi = wi, or a marked word. Each of these corresponds to a node vqi in the query graph above.

“Marry, we shall have it again,” sighed the boy; “that same old weary tale that he hath told a thousand times in the same words, and that he _will_ tell till he dieth, every time he hath gotten his barrel full and feeleth his exaggeration-mill a-working. Would God I had died or I saw this day!”

“Indeed, we shall hear it again,” the boy sighed. “That same old, tiresome story he has told a thousand times in exactly the same words, and will go on telling until the day he dies, every time he gets his barrel filled and feels his exaggeration machine set to work. God grant I had died, or that I had never lived to see this day!”

Such views of life were to some extent the natural begettings of her situation upon her nature. To dwell on a heath without studying its meanings was like wedding a foreigner without learning his tongue. The subtle beauties of the heath were lost to Eustacia; she only caught its vapours. An environment which would have made a contented woman a poet, a suffering woman a devotee, a pious woman a psalmist, even a giddy woman thoughtful, made a rebellious woman saturnine.

Her outlook on life was, in part, the inevitable offspring of her circumstances acting upon her character. To inhabit a heath without deciphering its language was akin to marrying a stranger without ever learning his speech. The heath’s delicate loveliness escaped Eustacia entirely; she perceived only its mists. A setting that might have shaped a contented soul into a poet, a sorrowful one into a mystic, a devout one into a hymnist, or even a frivolous one into a thinker, instead turned a defiant woman sullen.

Once they were seated in the living-room, he began to get on easily—more easily by far than he had expected. She made it easy for him; and the gracious spirit with which she did it made him love her more madly than ever. They talked first of the borrowed books, of the Swinburne he was devoted to, and of the Browning he did not understand; and she led the conversation on from subject to subject, while she pondered the problem of how she could be of help to him. She had thought of this often since their first meeting. She wanted to help him. He made a call upon her pity and tenderness that no one had ever made before, and the pity was not so much derogatory of him as maternal in her. Her pity could not be of the common sort, when the man who drew it was so much man as to shock her with maidenly fears and set her mind and pulse thrilling with strange thoughts and feelings. The old fascination of his neck was there, and there was sweetness in the thought of laying her hands upon it. It seemed still a wanton impulse, but she had grown more used to it. She did not dream that in such guise new-born love would epitomize itself. Nor did she dream that the feeling he excited in her was love. She thought she was merely interested in him as an unusual type possessing various potential excellencies, and she even felt philanthropic about it.

Once they’d settled into the faded velvet armchairs of her front parlor, the conversation unfolded far more easily than he’d dared to anticipate. She met him halfway at every turn, the unforced warmth of her manner stoking his adoration to a giddy, dizzying pitch he’d never thought possible. They first chatted about the stack of books he’d borrowed the week prior: the wild, throbbing verse of Dowson he’d memorized half of by heart, and the layered, allusive poems of Rossetti he still couldn’t parse. She steered the talk gently from topic to topic, all while turning over the question of how she might lift him up, a problem she’d mulled constantly since their first meeting by the town’s frozen mill pond. She ached to help him. He tugged at a vein of pity and tenderness no other person had ever reached in her, and that softness was not belittling to him so much as it was gently maternal, rooted in care, not condescension. It could never be the ordinary sort of pity, not when the man who inspired it was so unapologetically vividly male that he roused fluttering, girlish alarms in her, sent her thoughts spinning and her pulse thrumming with odd, unrecognizable feelings. She was still drawn to the strong, sun-browned slope of his neck, and the thought of pressing her palms flat to it hummed with quiet sweetness. It still felt like a bold, unruly impulse, but she’d grown far more comfortable with the thought of it lately. She never guessed that new, fledgling love so often dressed itself in exactly these small, unnameable, half-wanton urges. She never guessed that the warm, thrumming feeling he roused in her was love at all, instead convincing herself she was merely curious about him, an unusual, rough-edged figure brimming with untapped potential, and she even framed the whole interest as a sort of quiet, philanthropic project.

“When the news reached Leghorn that Felix was deprived of his wealth and rank, the merchant commanded his daughter to think no more of her lover, but to prepare to return to her native country. The generous nature of Safie was outraged by this command; she attempted to expostulate with her father, but he left her angrily, reiterating his tyrannical mandate.

When word reached the port of Trieste that Elias had been stripped of his family fortune and noble rank, the wealthy spice merchant commanded his daughter to banish all thoughts of her lover from her mind, and prepare at once to return to their home country. Lila’s generous, unbowed nature rebelled fiercely at this order; she tried to reason and plead with her father, but he stormed away from her in a rage, repeating his harsh, non-negotiable mandate as he went.

The men and women being all busily engaged in saving the hay—even Liddy had left the house for the purpose of lending a hand—Bathsheba resolved to hive the bees herself, if possible. She had dressed the hive with herbs and honey, fetched a ladder, brush, and crook, made herself impregnable with armour of leather gloves, straw hat, and large gauze veil—once green but now faded to snuff colour—and ascended a dozen rungs of the ladder. At once she heard, not ten yards off, a voice that was beginning to have a strange power in agitating her.

Every man and woman on the property was fully occupied with the urgent work of saving the hay crop—even Liddy had stepped out of the house to pitch in—so Bathsheba decided she would hive the bees herself, if she could pull it off. She’d coated the inside of the empty hive with herbs and honey to draw the swarm in, fetched a ladder, soft bristle brush, and beekeeping crook, then armored herself against stings with thick leather gloves, a wide-brimmed straw hat, and an oversized gauze veil that had once been a vibrant green, now faded to a murky snuff shade. She’d just climbed a dozen rungs up the ladder when she heard a voice no more than ten yards away, one that was starting to hold a strange, unsettling power to rattle her completely.

Mr. Direck had read a very great deal of all this expressed opiniativeness of Mr. Britling: he found it entertaining and stimulating stuff, and it was with genuine enthusiasm that he had come over to encounter the man himself. On his way across the Atlantic and during the intervening days, he had rehearsed this meeting in varying keys, but always on the supposition that Mr. Britling was a large, quiet, thoughtful sort of man, a man who would, as it were, sit in attentive rows like a public meeting and listen. So Mr. Direck had prepared quite a number of pleasant and attractive openings, and now he felt was the moment for some one of these various simple, memorable utterances. But in none of these forecasts had he reckoned with either the spontaneous activities of Mr. Britling or with the station-master of Matching's Easy. Oblivious of any conversational necessities between Mr. Direck and Mr. Britling, this official now took charge of Mr. Direck's grip-sack, and, falling into line with the two gentlemen as they walked towards the exit gate, resumed what was evidently an interrupted discourse upon sweet peas, originally addressed to Mr. Britling.

Mr. Direck had consumed a considerable volume of that printed assertiveness which belonged to Mr. Britling; he deemed it both amusing and provocative matter, and it was with unfeigned eagerness that he had journeyed to meet the originator in the flesh. Throughout his Atlantic crossing and in the days that followed, he had staged their encounter in shifting tones, yet always upon the assumption that Mr. Britling would prove a tall, subdued, contemplative figure, a man disposed to arrange himself in attentive tiers like a civic assembly and listen. Accordingly, Mr. Direck had fashioned a store of genial and engaging preludes, and now he sensed that the hour had arrived for one of those various plain but resonant declarations. What none of these anticipations had allowed for, however, was either the unscripted energies of Mr. Britling or the stationmaster of Matching’s Easy. Unmindful of any conversational obligation hanging between Mr. Direck and Mr. Britling, this functionary now assumed custody of Mr. Direck’s valise, and, falling into step beside the pair as they moved toward the platform gate, continued what was plainly a suspended disquisition on sweet peas, originally directed at Mr. Britling.

The unseen force was drawing him fast to itself, now, and all the tides and winds were setting straight and strong towards it. He left his two letters with a trusty porter, to be delivered half an hour before midnight, and no sooner; took horse for Dover; and began his journey. “For the love of Heaven, of justice, of generosity, of the honour of your noble name!” was the poor prisoner’s cry with which he strengthened his sinking heart, as he left all that was dear on earth behind him, and floated away for the Loadstone Rock.

A hidden power now pulled him swiftly toward its core, every current and gust aligning with fierce purpose in that single direction. He entrusted his two letters to a reliable porter, with strict orders they be handed over no earlier than half an hour before midnight; then he mounted his horse at Dover and set out. “For the love of Heaven, for justice, for mercy, for the honour of your noble name!”—that desperate prisoner’s plea was what he used to steady his faltering spirit as he abandoned everything precious in this world and drifted toward the Loadstone Rock.

Then from his Lordship I shall learn,
Henceforth to meet with unconcern
One rank as weel’s another;
Nae honest, worthy man need care
To meet with noble youthful Daer,
For he but meets a brother.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Then from his Lordship I’ll take heed,
To greet with calm and equal speed
All stations, high or lowly;
No upright, decent soul should dread
To face young Daer, nobly bred,
For he but meets kin solely.

“Maria,” Martin announced that night, “I’m going to leave you. And you’re going to leave here yourself soon. Then you can rent the house and be a landlord yourself. You’ve a brother in San Leandro or Haywards, and he’s in the milk business. I want you to send all your washing back unwashed—understand?—unwashed, and to go out to San Leandro to-morrow, or Haywards, or wherever it is, and see that brother of yours. Tell him to come to see me. I’ll be stopping at the Metropole down in Oakland. He’ll know a good milk-ranch when he sees one.”

“Maria,” Martin declared that night, “I am moving on. And you will be leaving this place yourself before long. You’ll be the one renting the house out, a landlady in your own right. You have a brother in the dairy trade over in San Leandro or Haywards. I want you to return all this laundry just as it is—unwashed, do you understand?—and go see him tomorrow. Tell him to come find me at the Metropole in Oakland. He’ll be able to recognize a quality milk ranch when he sees one.”

Mr. Direck changed again, and after using some embrocation that Mrs. Britling recommended very strongly, came down in a black jacket and a cheerfully ample black tie. He had a sense of physical well-being such as he had not experienced since he came aboard the liner at New York. The curious thing was that it was not quite the same sense of physical well-being that one had in America. That is bright and clear and a little dry, this was--humid. His mind quivered contentedly, like sunset midges over a lake--it had no hard bright flashes--and his body wanted to sit about. His sense of intimacy with Cecily increased each time he looked at her. When she met his eyes she smiled. He'd caught her style now, he felt; he attempted no more compliments and was frankly her pupil at hockey and Badminton. After supper Mr. Britling renewed his suggestion of an automobile excursion on the Monday.

Mr. Sterling emerged transformed, having applied a pungent liniment at Mrs. Thorne’s insistent suggestion, now attired in a somber dinner jacket and a generously knotted silk tie. He was suffused with a bodily contentment he had not tasted since departing the New York docks. Yet, this vigor possessed a quality distinct from the transatlantic variety. Where the American spirit felt crystalline and parched, this English vitality was—moist. His thoughts hovered with a quiet restlessness, like evening flies above a pool—devoid of sharp brilliance—and his limbs craved only repose. His bond with Elara matured with every shared look. Whenever their gazes locked, she offered a grin. He had grasped her tempo at last; abandoning his clumsy flatteries, he became her humble apprentice in the arts of the court and green. Following the evening meal, Mr. Thorne revisited his proposal for a motorcar outing on Monday.

But in the afternoon Mr. Manning telephoned to comfort his friend. A reassuring despatch from General French had been published and--all was well--practically--and the British had been splendid. They had been fighting continuously for several days round and about Mons; they had been attacked at odds of six to one, and they had repulsed and inflicted enormous losses on the enemy. They had established an incontestable personal superiority over the Germans. The Germans had been mown down in heaps; the British had charged through their cavalry like charging through paper. So at last and very gloriously for the British, British and German had met in battle. After the hard fighting of the 26th about Landrecies, the British had been comparatively unmolested, reinforcements covering double the losses had joined them and the German advance was definitely checked ... Mr. Britling's mind swung back to elation. He took down the entire despatch from Mr. Manning's dictation, and ran out with it into the garden where Mrs. Britling, with an unwonted expression of anxiety, was presiding over the teas of the usual casual Sunday gathering.... The despatch was read aloud twice over. After that there was hockey and high spirits, and then Mr. Britling went up to his study to answer a letter from Mrs. Harrowdean, the first letter that had come from her since their breach at the outbreak of the war, and which he was now in a better mood to answer than he had been hitherto.

It was mid-afternoon when Mr. Manning telephoned to buoy his friend’s spirits. A reassuring dispatch from General French had just been made public, and all was, for all practical purposes, well; the British troops had performed superbly. They had been locked in nonstop combat for days in and around Mons, attacked by forces outnumbering them six to one, yet they had repulsed the assault and inflicted devastating losses on the enemy. They had established an undeniable edge in individual fighting prowess over the Germans: enemy troops were cut down in heaps, and British soldiers charged through German cavalry formations as easily as if they were tearing through paper. This was the first full pitched battle between British and German forces, and it had ended in resounding British glory. After the brutal fighting on the 26th near Landrecies, the British had faced barely any harassment, fresh reinforcements numbering twice their recent losses had joined their ranks, and the German advance was definitively halted.

Mr. Britling’s mood swung sharply back to elation. He scribbled down the full dispatch as Mr. Manning dictated it over the line, then hurried out to the garden, where Mrs. Britling was overseeing tea for the usual casual Sunday gathering of guests, a rare expression of anxiety tugging at her features. The dispatch was read aloud twice to the group. After that came a game of hockey and raucous high spirits, before Mr. Britling headed up to his study to reply to a letter from Mrs. Harrowdean. It was the first correspondence he had received from her since their falling out the moment war broke out, and he was far better disposed to answer it warmly now than he had been at any point prior.

So he took his wings, and fled;
Then the morn blushed rosy red.
I dried my tears, and armed my fears
With ten thousand shields and spears.

metadata.title: Songs of Innocence and of Experience

text:
So he spread his wings and flew;
Then the dawn grew rosy through.
I wiped my tears, and braced my fears
With ten thousand shields and spears.

“Poor Bet! She went to see the Body, to speak to who it was,” replied Chitling, his countenance falling more and more, “and went off mad, screaming and raving, and beating her head against the boards; so they put a strait-weskut on her and took her to the hospital—and there she is.”

“Poor Bet! She went to view the body, to see if she could identify it,” Chitling replied, his expression sinking further into gloom. “She went completely out of her mind, screaming and shouting, slamming her head against the floorboards. They had to force her into a straightjacket and take her to the hospital—and that’s where she is now.”

When a trigger is inputted into the system, we generate weak labels for our training data via softmatching between trigger representations and unlabeled sentences . Each sentence may contain one or more triggers, but each trigger is associated with only one label. Our framework jointly learns a mapping between triggers and their label using a linear layer with a soft-max output and a log-likelihood loss, as well as the semantic similarity between the triggers and their associated sentences using contrastive loss--we weigh both objectives equally. Through this joint learning, our trigger representations can capture label knowledge as well as semantic information. We use these representations to improve model training by generating weakly labeled data via soft matching on the unlabeled sentences. More specifically, for each unlabeled sentence, we first calculate the semantic similarity between the sentence and all collected triggers and then filter out all triggers where the similarity distance is larger than our fixed threshold. We then generate a trigger-aware sentence encoding for each threshold-passing trigger and feed these encodings into a downstream classifier for label inference. Finally, we conduct majority vote over outputted label sequences to finalize our weak labels for the unlabeled sentence. In this manner we are able to train over more data, where a good portion of it is weakly labeled.

metadata.title: LEAN-LIFE: A Label-Efficient Annotation Framework Towards Learning from Explanation

text: Upon entering a trigger into the system, we produce weak labels for the training data by soft-matching trigger representations with unlabeled sentences. A sentence may include one or more triggers, yet each trigger corresponds to a single label. Our framework jointly learns a mapping from triggers to their labels via a linear layer with a soft-max output and a log-likelihood loss, while simultaneously capturing semantic similarity between triggers and their associated sentences through a contrastive loss; both objectives are weighted equally. Through this joint learning, the trigger representations acquire both label knowledge and semantic information. We leverage these representations to enhance model training by generating weakly labeled data through soft matching on the unlabeled sentences. Concretely, for each unlabeled sentence, we compute the semantic similarity between the sentence and all collected triggers, then discard any triggers whose similarity distance exceeds a fixed threshold. For each trigger that passes the threshold, we construct a trigger-aware sentence encoding and pass these encodings to a downstream classifier for label inference. Finally, we apply a majority vote over the resulting label sequences to determine the weak labels for the unlabeled sentence. In this way, we can train on a larger dataset, a substantial portion of which is weakly labeled.

We conduct an ablation study for CG-Policy on Weibo corpus to investigate why CG-Policy performs better. First, to evaluate the contribution of CG, we remove the CG from CG-Policy, denoted as CG-Policy-noCG, where we do not use graph structure information for action space pruning and reward design. Moreover, we attempt to use the CG (without how-vertices) to augment the ChatMore model for action space pruning and reward design, denoted as Chatmore-CG. As shown in Table 4 , the performance of CG-Policy-noCG drops dramatically in terms of coherence, Dist-2 and appropriateness when compared to the original model. Moreover, CG can boost the performance of ChatMore in terms of most of metrics. It indicates that the use of CG is crucial to the superior performance of CG-Policy, and it can also help other models, e.g., ChatMore. Second, to evaluate the contribution of CG for action space pruning or reward design respectively, we implement two system variants: (1) we use all the what-vertices in CG as action candidates at each turn, denoted as CG-Policy-noCGact;

text: "She won't.... Some years ago I used to believe in the inevitable European war," Mr. Britling explained to Mr. Direck, "but it's been threatened so long that at last I've lost all belief in it. The Powers wrangle and threaten. They're far too cautious and civilised to let the guns go off. If there was going to be a war it would have happened two years ago when the Balkan League fell upon Turkey. Or when Bulgaria attacked Serbia...."

She won't.... Years ago, I was convinced a European war was unavoidable," Mr. Britling told Mr. Direck, "but it's been looming for so long that I've finally stopped believing in it. The Great Powers bicker and bluster. They're much too careful and refined to actually start shooting. If war were truly coming, it would have broken out two years ago when the Balkan League descended on Turkey. Or when Bulgaria struck at Serbia....

Eustacia Vye was the raw material of a divinity. On Olympus she would have done well with a little preparation. She had the passions and instincts which make a model goddess, that is, those which make not quite a model woman. Had it been possible for the earth and mankind to be entirely in her grasp for a while, she had handled the distaff, the spindle, and the shears at her own free will, few in the world would have noticed the change of government. There would have been the same inequality of lot, the same heaping up of favours here, of contumely there, the same generosity before justice, the same perpetual dilemmas, the same captious alteration of caresses and blows that we endure now.

Eustacia Vye was made of the very stuff of the divine. Given a little polish, she would have been perfectly at home on Olympus. She possessed the temperament and drives that define an ideal goddess—which is to say, the very traits that make for a less-than-ideal woman. Had she been granted total dominion over the earth and its people, free to spin and sever the threads of fate at her whim, the world would scarcely have registered the change in power. There would have been the same unfairness of fortune, the same piling of rewards in one place and abuse in another, the same impulsive kindness over strict justice, the same constant paradoxes, and the same erratic shifts between tenderness and violence that we already suffer today.

We begin by examining the mappings of the POS of corresponding tokens (see Appendix C.1 for the full percentage and count matrices). We find that En POS tags of content words are mostly stable in translations to Fr and Ru (sums of the values on the main diagonals account for 78 and 77% of the total number of word pairs respectively). Notable exceptions are the negative particle not, which is in a one-to-many alignment in French with ne and pas, certain types of auxiliaries analyzed as verbs in both Ru and Fr, and proper nouns, which often get mapped to Fr nouns (cf. § 9 and discussion in [Samardžic et al., 2010] ).

metadata.title: Fine-Grained Analysis of Cross-Linguistic Syntactic Divergences

text: We begin by analyzing the part-of-speech (POS) mappings of corresponding tokens (see Appendix C.1 for the full percentage and count matrices). We find that English POS tags for content words remain largely stable in translations into French and Russian: the sums of the values along the main diagonals account for 78% and 77% of the total word pairs, respectively. Notable exceptions include the negative particle *not*, which exhibits a one-to-many alignment with French *ne* and *pas*; certain types of auxiliaries that are analyzed as verbs in both Russian and French; and proper nouns, which are frequently mapped to French common nouns (cf. § 9 and the discussion in Samardžić et al., 2010).

They talked on; she, in her gently persistent way, returning always to the need of thorough grounding in education and to the advantages of Latin as part of the foundation for any career. She drew her ideal of the successful man, and it was largely in her father’s image, with a few unmistakable lines and touches of color from the image of Mr. Butler. He listened eagerly, with receptive ears, lying on his back and looking up and joying in each movement of her lips as she talked. But his brain was not receptive. There was nothing alluring in the pictures she drew, and he was aware of a dull pain of disappointment and of a sharper ache of love for her. In all she said there was no mention of his writing, and the manuscripts he had brought to read lay neglected on the ground.

The conversation continued; she, with her soft but steady determination, kept returning to the necessity of a rigorous academic foundation and the value of Latin as a cornerstone for any profession. She described her vision of a truly accomplished man, a figure modeled largely after her father, though colored by certain unmistakable traits belonging to Mr. Butler. He lay on his back and listened with rapt attention, gazing upward and finding a quiet joy in the very movement of her lips as she spoke. Yet, his mind remained unpersuaded. There was nothing enticing in the life she envisioned, and he felt a heavy sense of disappointment coupled with a sharp, piercing love for her. Throughout her talk, she made no mention of his own work, and the manuscripts he had hoped to read to her sat forgotten on the grass.

Such a perfect, delicate, and necessary finish did the figure give to the dark pile of hills that it seemed to be the only obvious justification of their outline. Without it, there was the dome without the lantern; with it the architectural demands of the mass were satisfied. The scene was strangely homogeneous, in that the vale, the upland, the barrow, and the figure above it amounted only to unity. Looking at this or that member of the group was not observing a complete thing, but a fraction of a thing.

So perfect, so subtle, and so essential was the figure’s effect on the dark cluster of hills that it seemed the only plain reason for their shape. Without it, there was a dome lacking its lantern; with it, the whole architectural need of the mass was fulfilled. The scene was remarkably unified, for the valley, the upland, the barrow, and the figure standing above it together made only a single whole. To look at one part or another of the grouping was not to see something complete, but merely a fragment of one.

Boldwood remained silent after that, and the noise from indoors was again just audible, until, a few minutes later, light wheels could be distinguished coming down the hill. They drew nearer, and ceased at the gate. Boldwood hastened back to the door, and opened it; and the light shone upon Bathsheba coming up the path.

The words slipped out half under his breath, yet every person lingering on the porch caught them clear as a bell. Elias Thorne made no reply after that, and the faint, muffled din of chatter from inside the house drifted back to their ears, until a few minutes had passed and the soft rattle of light carriage wheels rose above it, winding down the oak-dusted slope above the farm. The clatter grew steadily louder, then cut off sharp right at the white picket gate. Elias hurried back to the front door and tugged it open; warm golden lamplight spilled out across the gravel walk, gilding Elowen Voss as she climbed the path toward them.

“The government of France were greatly enraged at the escape of their victim and spared no pains to detect and punish his deliverer. The plot of Felix was quickly discovered, and De Lacey and Agatha were thrown into prison. The news reached Felix and roused him from his dream of pleasure. His blind and aged father and his gentle sister lay in a noisome dungeon while he enjoyed the free air and the society of her whom he loved. This idea was torture to him. He quickly arranged with the Turk that if the latter should find a favourable opportunity for escape before Felix could return to Italy, Safie should remain as a boarder at a convent at Leghorn; and then, quitting the lovely Arabian, he hastened to Paris and delivered himself up to the vengeance of the law, hoping to free De Lacey and Agatha by this proceeding.

The ruling council of Veyra seethed with unbridled fury when their high-profile political prisoner slipped their grasp, and poured every resource at their disposal into hunting down the person who’d orchestrated his breakout. Elias’s role in the plot was uncovered within days, and his blind, elderly father Thibault and soft-spoken younger sister Elodie were seized and thrown into a squalid, vermin-ridden city jail. Word of the arrests found Elias while he was still drifting in the giddy haze of his new romance, and the shock ripped him violently out of his sunlit reverie. The thought that his infirm father and gentle sister were rotting in a reeking, lightless cell while he wandered free in the southern countryside, doted on by the woman he adored, felt like a hot knife twisted steadily between his ribs. He struck a hasty deal with Kadir, the freed Levantine prisoner: if the man found a safe chance to flee the region before Elias could return from his journey, Lira, Kadir’s daughter and Elias’s beloved, would take up temporary residence as a boarder in the coastal convent at San Luca until he could send for her. Leaving the dark-eyed young woman he loved behind, he raced straight to the Veyraussian capital, turning himself over to the courts without resistance, hoping his voluntary surrender would convince the authorities to release his father and sister from their captivity.

With Bathsheba a hastened act was a rash act; but, as does not always happen, time gained was prudence insured. It must be added, however, that time was very seldom gained. At this period the single opinion in the parish on herself and her doings that she valued as sounder than her own was Gabriel Oak’s. And the outspoken honesty of his character was such that on any subject, even that of her love for, or marriage with, another man, the same disinterestedness of opinion might be calculated on, and be had for the asking. Thoroughly convinced of the impossibility of his own suit, a high resolve constrained him not to injure that of another. This is a lover’s most stoical virtue, as the lack of it is a lover’s most venial sin. Knowing he would reply truly she asked the question, painful as she must have known the subject would be. Such is the selfishness of some charming women. Perhaps it was some excuse for her thus torturing honesty to her own advantage, that she had absolutely no other sound judgment within easy reach.

For Bathsheba, any choice made in a rush was always a reckless one; and unusually, whenever she gave herself time to deliberate, she was guaranteed to act sensibly. The catch, though, was that she almost never carved out that extra time for herself. Right then, the only person in the whole parish whose take on her and her choices she trusted more than her own judgment was Gabriel Oak. His character was so unflinchingly, bluntly honest that she could count on him to give a fully unbiased take no matter the topic, even if it was about her having feelings for another man, or marrying someone else—all she had to do was ask. He was fully convinced there was no chance she’d ever return his own romantic feelings, so he held himself to a strict, noble code not to sabotage any other suitor’s shot with her. That’s the most stoic, unselfish virtue a person in love can have, just as failing to live up to that standard is the most forgivable fault a lover can commit. She knew he’d give her a truthful answer, so she asked him the question anyway, even though she had to have realized how much that topic would hurt him. That’s the kind of casual selfishness some utterly captivating women carry with them. To be fair, there was a small excuse for her leaning on his honesty for her own gain even when it pained him: she truly didn’t have anyone else whose good sense she could easily access to weigh in on the matter.

Or like harmonious thunder rolling through the courts of heaven:
Beneath them sit the aged men, wise guardians of the poor.
Then cherish pity, lest you turn an angel from your door.

"But I suppose all sorts of people could write an Irish pamphlet. Nobody but you could write 'The Silent Places.' Oh, _why_ don't you finish that great beautiful thing, and leave all this world of reality and newspapers, all these Crude, Vulgar, Quarrelsome, Jarring things to other people? You have the magic gift, you might be a poet, you can take us out of all these horrid things that are, away to Beautyland, and you are just content to be a critic and a disputer. It's your surroundings. It's your sordid realities. It's that Practicality at your elbow. You ought never to see a newspaper. You ought never to have an American come within ten miles of you. You ought to live on bowls of milk drunk in valleys of asphodel."

“But I daresay any number of people could contrive an Irish pamphlet. Only you could have written ‘The Still Places.’ Oh, why don’t you bring that great, lovely thing to its end, and leave all this world of fact and journals, all these coarse, noisy, contentious, discordant affairs, to other people? You have the enchanted faculty; you might be a poet. You can carry us away from all these dreadful realities to Beauty-land, and instead you are content to be merely a critic and a quarreller. It is your company about you. It is your wretched realities. It is that Practicality always at your elbow. You ought never to catch sight of a newspaper. You ought never to let an American come within ten miles of you. You ought to live on dishes of milk sipped in valleys of asphodel.”

“You, who call Frankenstein your friend, seem to have a knowledge of my crimes and his misfortunes. But in the detail which he gave you of them he could not sum up the hours and months of misery which I endured wasting in impotent passions. For while I destroyed his hopes, I did not satisfy my own desires. They were for ever ardent and craving; still I desired love and fellowship, and I was still spurned. Was there no injustice in this? Am I to be thought the only criminal, when all humankind sinned against me? Why do you not hate Felix, who drove his friend from his door with contumely? Why do you not execrate the rustic who sought to destroy the saviour of his child? Nay, these are virtuous and immaculate beings! I, the miserable and the abandoned, am an abortion, to be spurned at, and kicked, and trampled on. Even now my blood boils at the recollection of this injustice.

You, who claim kinship with Victor, appear acquainted with my transgressions and his sorrows. Yet in the account he rendered you, he could not reckon the seasons and ages of torment I passed, consumed by futile rage. For though I shattered his aspirations, I never quenched my own longings. They remained perpetually burning and ravenous; still I yearned for tenderness and communion, and still I was rejected. Was there no wrong in this? Must I alone bear the name of malefactor, when all of mankind trespassed against me? Why do you not revile Felix, who thrust his companion from his threshold with scorn? Why do you not curse the peasant who strove to murder the rescuer of his child? Ah, but these are spotless and righteous souls! I, the wretch and the outcast, am a monstrosity, to be derided, and beaten, and ground into the dust. Even now my veins surge with fire at the memory of this inequity.

text: And yet this was only forty-two miles from London. Surely it brought things within the suburban range. If Matching's Easy were in America, commuters would live there. But in supposing that, Mr. Direck displayed his ignorance of a fact of the greatest importance to all who would understand England. There is a gap in the suburbs of London. The suburbs of London stretch west and south and even west by north, but to the north-eastward there are no suburbs; instead there is Essex. Essex is not a suburban county; it is a characteristic and individualised county which wins the heart. Between dear Essex and the centre of things lie two great barriers, the East End of London and Epping Forest. Before a train could get to any villadom with a cargo of season-ticket holders it would have to circle about this rescued woodland and travel for twenty unprofitable miles, and so once you are away from the main Great Eastern lines Essex still lives in the peace of the eighteenth century, and London, the modern Babylon, is, like the stars, just a light in the nocturnal sky. In Matching's Easy, as Mr. Britling presently explained to Mr. Direck, there are half-a-dozen old people who have never set eyes on London in their lives--and do not want to.

And yet this was only forty-two miles from London. Surely that ought to place it within the suburban orbit. If Matching’s Easy were in America, commuters would be living there. But in making that assumption, Mr. Direck betrayed a lack of knowledge of a matter of the first importance to anyone who wishes to understand England. There is a gap in the suburbs of London. The suburbs spread west and south, and even west by north, but to the north-east there are no suburbs at all; there is Essex instead. Essex is no suburban county; it is a county of its own, distinct and lovable, and it conquers the heart. Between dear Essex and the very centre of affairs lie two immense barriers, the East End of London and Epping Forest. Before a train could carry a load of season-ticket holders to any district of villas, it would have to swing around this saved woodland and cover twenty useless miles; and so, once one is beyond the main Great Eastern lines, Essex still lives in the quiet of the eighteenth century, while London, the modern Babylon, is, like the stars, only a light in the night sky. In Matching’s Easy, as Mr. Britling soon explained to Mr. Direck, there are half a dozen old people who have never looked upon London in their lives—and do not wish to.

To this spot, the man bearing the appearance of a countryman, hastened unobserved; and after a moment’s survey of the place, he began to descend.

The stone steps the young woman had nodded toward sat on the Kentish riverbank, on the same side of the old stone bridge as Saint Thomas’s Church, forming a rickety public landing from the Thames. The man, who carried the rough, sun-browned look of a farmer from the outlying Weald, hurried to the spot without drawing any eyes; after a quick, sweeping glance at the empty riverfront around him, he began to pick his way down the slippery, algae-slick treads.

Entity and Relation Summarization. To make our system more user-friendly and interesting, we add analytic functionalities for the most frequent entity and relation summarization. For example, in Figure 4 , if scientists are interested in finding the most frequent diseases, they can search "entity type = DISEASEORSYNDROME" in our analytic interface and see the top entities such as tumor and breast cancer. Similarly, if scientists are interested in finding the most frequent chemicaldisease pairs with a treatment relation, they can search "pattern = DISEASEORSYNDROME treat with CHEMICAL" in our analytic interface and see the top entity pairs such as HCC&sorafenib. More interestingly, if researchers are interested in finding the most frequent diseases that can be treated by a specific chemical (e.g., nivolumab), they can search "entity = nivolumab & pattern = DISEASE-ORSYNDROME treat with CHEMICAL" in our analytic interface and see the most frequent diseases, such as melanoma and NSCLC, that can be treated with nivolumab. With these analytic functionalities, EVIDENCEMINER can help scientists uncover important research issues, leading to more effective research and more in-depth quantitative analysis. metadata.title: EVIDENCEMINER: Textual Evidence Discovery for Life Sciences

text: Entity and Relation Summarization. To make our system more user-friendly and engaging, we incorporate analytic functions for summarizing the most frequent entities and relations. For instance, in Figure 4, if scientists want to identify the most frequent diseases, they can enter "entity type = DISEASEORSYNDROME" into our analytic interface and view the top entities, such as tumor and breast cancer. Likewise, if scientists wish to find the most frequent chemical-disease pairs associated with a treatment relation, they can search for "pattern = DISEASEORSYNDROME treat with CHEMICAL" in our analytic interface and inspect the top entity pairs, such as HCC&sorafenib. More interestingly, if researchers want to determine the most frequent diseases that can be treated by a specific chemical (e.g., nivolumab), they can search "entity = nivolumab & pattern = DISEASE-ORSYNDROME treat with CHEMICAL" in our analytic interface and obtain the most frequent diseases, such as melanoma and NSCLC, that can be treated with nivolumab. Through these analytic functions, EVIDENCEMINER can assist scientists in identifying important research questions, thereby enabling more effective research and more thorough quantitative analysis. (2015) and JNC, these task settings do not use fulltext articles but only lead (6.6% of words in full articles, Gigaword) and lead three sentences (25.9% of words in full articles, JNC) as source documents for abstractive summarization. Hence, we hypothesize that the source documents under these task settings contain insufficient information for generating headlines. In other words, headline generation models might be faced with supervision data where headlines cannot be generated from source documents and learned to be untruthful, i.e., producing pieces of information that are not mentioned in source documents.

metadata.title: Improving Truthfulness of Headline Generation
metadata.section: Background of the datasets and settings

text: Table 2 provides a summary of the datasets and task settings. As shown in the rows for Rush et al. (2015) and JNC, these settings do not employ full-text articles; instead, they use only the lead section (6.6% of the words in the full articles in Gigaword) and the first three sentences (25.9% of the words in the full articles in JNC) as source documents for abstractive summarization. We therefore hypothesize that, under these task settings, the source documents contain insufficient information for headline generation. In other words, headline generation models may be trained on supervision data in which headlines cannot be produced from the source documents and may consequently learn to be untruthful, that is, to generate information not mentioned in the source documents.

Clarence was twenty-two now, and was my head executive, my right hand. He was a darling; he was equal to anything; there wasn't anything he couldn't turn his hand to. Of late I had been training him for journalism, for the time seemed about right for a start in the newspaper line; nothing big, but just a small weekly for experimental circulation in my civilization-nurseries. He took to it like a duck; there was an editor concealed in him, sure. Already he had doubled himself in one way; he talked sixth century and wrote nineteenth. His journalistic style was climbing, steadily; it was already up to the back settlement Alabama mark, and couldn't be told from the editorial output of that region either by matter or flavor.

Clarence was now twenty-two and served as my chief lieutenant, my indispensable right hand. He was a marvel, capable of rising to any occasion; there was simply no task he couldn't master. Lately, I had been mentoring him in the field of journalism, as the time felt right to launch a newspaper—nothing major, just a modest weekly for trial distribution in my various outposts of progress. He embraced the work instinctively; a true editor was hidden within him. He had already developed a dual nature: he spoke the local sixth-century dialect but wrote in the nineteenth-century style. His journalistic prose was steadily improving; it had already reached the standard of a rural Alabama paper, matching that region's editorial output perfectly in both content and tone.

A sharp prod from the officer’s staff against his back undid him further, and tears welled up unbidden. Under this double weight of fear, he could barely whisper his faltering reply, each word catching in his throat. At this, a man clad in a pale waistcoat pronounced him a simpleton—a remark delivered with such brisk authority that it was plainly meant to hearten the lad and set him wholly at peace.

My cosmopolitan friend offered an elaborate farewell and departed, certain he’d spotted someone he knew through the swirl of smoke and the hum of chattering crowds. He was torn by feelings as strange and inexplicable as those of his youth. The same sensation possessed him now that had come over him in childhood whenever some disagreeable confession had to be made to his parents. He knew he could not yet endure an encounter with her, could not bear the burden of telling her, of being watched while he did so. Suddenly he veered to the left. He went off as though he had not noticed her, across the lawn toward the small summer-house on the rise that looked down upon the main road. She called after him, but he made no reply....

It was Mr. Britling's habit, a habit he had set himself to acquire after much irritating experience, to disregard Aunt Wilshire. She was not, strictly speaking, his aunt; she was one of those distant cousins we find already woven into our lives when we attain to years of responsibility. She had been a presence in his father's household when Mr. Britling was a boy. Then she had been called "Jane," or "Cousin Jane," or "Your cousin Wilshire." It had been a kindly freak of Mr. Britling's to promote her to Aunty rank.

Ignoring Aunt Wilshire was a deliberate habit Mr. Britling had cultivated after years of exasperating encounters with her, a routine he adhered to reliably. She was not, strictly speaking, his aunt at all; she was one of those distant cousins who seem to have already been woven into the fabric of our lives by the time we reach an age of adult responsibility. She had been a steady presence in his father’s household when Mr. Britling was a boy, back when she went simply by “Jane,” “Cousin Jane,” or occasionally “your cousin Wilshire.” Elevating her to the formal rank of Aunt had been a gentle, playful whim of Mr. Britling’s, later on.

I don’t care for close ways.”

“Even if they were feeling ashamed, I still don’t see why they couldn’t have held the whole thing right here,” said the broad, ample-framed woman, whose stiff stays creaked like well-worn shoes every time she stooped or turned. “It’s a fine thing to gather all the neighbors together and raise a good, lively ruckus now and again; a wedding’s just as fitting an occasion for it as any seasonal holiday, if you ask me. I’ve never had any patience for stuffy, close-mouthed ways of doing things.”
