The existing neural network-based models have shown great success in recognizing implicit discourse relations. It mainly includes 1) Basic neural networks (Braud and Denis, 2015; Zhang et al., 2015; can learn the dense vector representations of discourse arguments, which can capture the semantic information to some extent. Further studies exploit different attention or memory mechanisms to capture the critical information of argument pairs. 2) Complex neural models (Chen et al., 2016; Lei et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2018) utilize gated relevance networks or neural tensor networks to capture the deeper interactions between two discourse arguments. 3) Joint learning architectures (Qin et al., 2017; Bai and Zhao, 2018; Xu et al., 2019) exploit implicit connective cues, different granularity of text, or topic-level relevant information to improve the discourse relation prediction. However, these approaches still have the following drawbacks: 1) do not make full use of the annotated discourse relation signal to explore the argumentrelation features; 2) neglect the extra information in the low-dimensional continuous embedding space, i.e., the direction or structure information of the vectors.

metadata.title: TransS-Driven Joint Learning Architecture for Implicit Discourse Relation Recognition

text: Existing neural network-based models have achieved substantial success in recognizing implicit discourse relations. These models mainly include: 1) basic neural networks (Braud and Denis, 2015; Zhang et al., 2015), which can learn dense vector representations of discourse arguments and thereby capture semantic information to some extent; subsequent studies further employ various attention or memory mechanisms to extract the key information from argument pairs. 2) Complex neural models (Chen et al., 2016; Lei et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2018), which use gated relevance networks or neural tensor networks to model the deeper interactions between two discourse arguments. 3) Joint learning architectures (Qin et al., 2017; Bai and Zhao, 2018; Xu et al., 2019), which exploit implicit connective cues, different levels of textual granularity, or topic-level relevant information to improve discourse relation prediction. However, these approaches still have the following drawbacks: 1) they do not fully exploit the annotated discourse relation signal to investigate argument-relation features; 2) they overlook the additional information contained in the low-dimensional continuous embedding space, namely the directional or structural information of the vectors.

To tend her by day and by night;
But my rose turned away with jealousy,
And her thorns were my only delight.

text:
I walked out to my lovely flowering bush,  
To care for her morning and night;  
But she turned aside, stung by envy’s push,  
And her prickles were all my delight.

Mr. Bumble was fairly taken by surprise, and fairly beaten. He had a decided propensity for bullying: derived no inconsiderable pleasure from the exercise of petty cruelty; and, consequently, was (it is needless to say) a coward. This is by no means a disparagement to his character; for many official personages, who are held in high respect and admiration, are the victims of similar infirmities. The remark is made, indeed, rather in his favour than otherwise, and with a view of impressing the reader with a just sense of his qualifications for office.

Mr. Bumble found himself utterly startled and thoroughly outmatched. He possessed a marked inclination for tyranny, taking no small delight in the practice of casual cruelty, and was therefore—needless to state—a craven at heart. This is far from a slight upon his nature, for many esteemed and admired public figures suffer from comparable frailties. The observation is offered, in truth, more to his credit than not, aiming to furnish the reader with a proper appreciation of his fitness for authority.

It’s steadier than anything else in the world.”

He wanted to give the brat sixpence, and he had only threepence and a shilling, and he didn't know what to do and his brain couldn't think. It would be a shocking thing to give her a shilling, and he couldn't somehow give just coppers for so important a thing as Hugh's death. Then all this problem vanished and he handed the child the shilling. She stared at him, inquiring, incredulous. "Is there a reply, Sir, please?"

He had the ridiculous notion that this should be a sixpenny telegram. The matter plagued him. He wanted to hand the little wretch sixpence, and yet all he possessed was a threepenny bit and a shilling; he could not see what was to be done, and his mind refused to work. It would be a dreadful thing to give her a shilling, and somehow he could not offer mere coppers for so grave a business as Rupert’s death. Then, all at once, the difficulty dropped away, and he put the shilling into the child’s hand. She looked up at him, questioning, disbelieving. “Is there an answer, sir, please?”

The first is the BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002 ) score since it is still the standard metric in MT. We use BLEU-2 to match the lower median of sentence lengths we observe across the English and the foreign data (see Fig 1) . BLEU matches whole words rather than word pieces, which prevents us from assigning partial credit to subword matches, which could be especially relevant for foreign target languages with rich morphology. We therefore use three additional metrics that operate on the level of word pieces: CharacTER (Wang et al., 2016) , ChrF (Popovic, 2016) and ChrF++ (Popovic, 2017) . CharacTER is a measure derived from TER (Translation Edit Rate), where edit rate is calculated on character level, whereas shift rate is measured on the word level. It calculates the minimum number of character edits required to adjust a hypothesis, until the reference is matched, normalized by the length of the hypothesis sentence. For easier comparison, we report 1.0 − characT ER scores. ChrF is a simple F-measure reflecting precision and recall of the matching character n-grams. ChrF++ adds word unigrams and bi-grams to the standard ChrF for a higher human correlation score. We experiment with different combinations of character n-grams (n = 3, 5 as suggested in Popovic (2016)) and word n-grams (n = 0, 1, 2 as suggested in Popovic (2017) ).

metadata.title: PuzzLing Machines: A Challenge on Learning From Small Data

text: We first consider BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002), as it remains the standard metric in MT. Specifically, we use BLEU-2 to align with the lower median sentence length observed across the English and foreign-language datasets (see Fig. 1). Since BLEU operates on whole words rather than word pieces, it does not permit partial credit for subword matches, a limitation that may be particularly relevant for foreign target languages with rich morphology. We therefore additionally report three metrics defined over word pieces: CharacTER (Wang et al., 2016), ChrF (Popovic, 2016), and ChrF++ (Popovic, 2017). CharacTER is derived from TER (Translation Edit Rate), with edit rate computed at the character level and shift rate at the word level. It measures the minimum number of character-level edits needed to transform a hypothesis into the reference, normalized by the hypothesis length. For ease of comparison, we report 1.0 − CharacTER scores. ChrF is a straightforward F-measure based on matching character n-grams and reflects precision and recall. ChrF++ extends standard ChrF by incorporating word unigrams and bigrams, yielding higher correlation with human judgments. We evaluate different combinations of character n-grams (n = 3, 5, as recommended by Popovic, 2016) and word n-grams (n = 0, 1, 2, as recommended by Popovic, 2017).

I made about three passes in the air, and then there was an awful crash and that old tower leaped into the sky in chunks, along with a vast volcanic fountain of fire that turned night to noonday, and showed a thousand acres of human beings groveling on the ground in a general collapse of consternation. Well, it rained mortar and masonry the rest of the week. This was the report; but probably the facts would have modified it.

I banked three times overhead, cutting through the cool night air, before a thunderous crash split the dark. The weathered old tower blew apart in jagged chunks, hurled skyward alongside a roiling volcanic geyser of flame that turned midnight as bright as full noon, illuminating a thousand acres of huddled, crumpled people all undone by overwhelming, paralyzing terror. Mortar and chipped masonry rained down on the surrounding land for the rest of the week, or so the account claims; the actual facts of the incident would almost certainly have tempered those outlandish details.

And here was the man Spencer, organizing all knowledge for him, reducing everything to unity, elaborating ultimate realities, and presenting to his startled gaze a universe so concrete of realization that it was like the model of a ship such as sailors make and put into glass bottles. There was no caprice, no chance. All was law. It was in obedience to law that the bird flew, and it was in obedience to the same law that fermenting slime had writhed and squirmed and put out legs and wings and become a bird.

Spencer stood as the architect of all understanding, weaving every fragment of knowledge into a singular, cohesive system. He defined the fundamental nature of reality, revealing a cosmos so tangible and structured that it resembled those intricate model ships that sailors carefully assemble inside glass bottles. There was no room for randomness or caprice; everything was governed by law. It was these immutable rules that allowed a bird to take flight, and the very same principles that had once driven primordial ooze to churn and evolve, eventually sprouting the limbs and feathers required to transform into a creature of the air.

And while the countryside of England changed steadily from its lax pacific amenity to the likeness of a rather slovenly armed camp, while long-fixed boundaries shifted and dissolved and a great irreparable wasting of the world's resources gathered way, Mr. Britling did his duty as a special constable, gave his eldest son to the Territorials, entertained Belgians, petted his soldiers in the barn, helped Teddy to his commission, contributed to war charities, sold out securities at a loss and subscribed to the War Loan, and thought, thought endlessly about the war.

All the while, the rolling English countryside shifted slowly from its soft, unhurried, peaceful charm into the rough, half-disorganized semblance of a slatternly military encampment; long-standing property lines blurred and dissolved, and a vast, irreversible drain on the world’s finite resources gained unstopping momentum. Mr. Hale fulfilled his duties as a volunteer reserve constable, sent his eldest son to enlist in the County Yeomanry, hosted Belgian refugee families in his unused guest wing, fussed over the infantrymen billeted in his old hay barn, helped his godson Toby secure an officer’s commission, donated generously to war relief charities, sold off chunks of his securities portfolio at a steep loss to subscribe to the War Loan, and turned the grim, unspooling realities of the conflict over and over in his mind, never able to set the thought of it aside for long.

_Our boys_, he wrote, _have died, fighting one against the other. They have been fighting upon an issue so obscure that your German press is still busy discussing what it was. For us it was that Belgium was invaded and France in danger of destruction. Nothing else could have brought the English into the field against you. But why you invaded Belgium and France and whether that might have been averted we do not know to this day. And still this war goes on and still more boys die, and these men who do not fight, these men in the newspaper offices and in the ministries plan campaigns and strokes and counter-strokes that belong to no conceivable plan at all. Except that now for them there is something more terrible than war. And that is the day of reckoning with their own people._

“Our boys,” he wrote, “have died, turned against one another in battle. They have fought over a cause so murky that your own German press is still debating what it ever was. For us, the issue was unambiguous: Belgium had been invaded, and France teetered on the brink of total destruction. Nothing else could have roused the English to take up arms against you. But why you chose to invade Belgium and France, and whether that incursion could ever have been averted, we do not know to this day. Still the war drags on, still more boys perish, while the men who never face combat—those in newspaper offices and government ministries—devise campaigns, offensives and countermoves that follow no conceivable, coherent strategy at all. Except for one truth, now: there is something more terrifying to them than war itself. And that is the day they must face reckoning with their own people.”

When she had disappeared Charley, with misgiving in his eyes, slowly came from the stable door, and going to another point in the bank he looked over. Eustacia was leaning against it on the outside, her face covered with her hands, and her head pressing the dewy heather which bearded the bank’s outer side. She appeared to be utterly indifferent to the circumstance that her bonnet, hair, and garments were becoming wet and disarranged by the moisture of her cold, harsh pillow. Clearly something was wrong.

Once she had vanished entirely from view, Charley, his eyes heavy with unease, moved slowly away from the stable doorway, then picked a different spot along the bank to peer over its edge. Eustacia was leaning against the bank’s outer face, her face buried in her hands, her head pressed into the dewy heather that bristled along the slope’s outer edge. She seemed completely unconcerned that her bonnet, hair, and clothing were growing damp and rumpled from the moisture seeping out of that cold, scratchy makeshift pillow. It was obvious something was deeply amiss.

From that time, in all weathers, she waited there two hours. As the clock struck two, she was there, and at four she turned resignedly away. When it was not too wet or inclement for her child to be with her, they went together; at other times she was alone; but, she never missed a single day.

After that gray, unremarkable Tuesday, no matter how brutal the weather grew, Elara kept her two-hour vigil at the splintered oak fence marking the edge of the depot lot. When the town clock struck two, she was already leaning against its weathered slats, and when it chimed four, she turned for home with a quiet, resigned slump to her shoulders. On days when the rain stayed light and the wind did not bite hard enough to risk her young daughter Mia falling ill, the girl came with her, kicking at loose gravel along the path as they went; on harsher days she made the trip alone, but she never once missed a single visit.

Results: We report automatic metrics averaged across three random initializations for all learned models, and use bootstrap tests (Berg-Kirkpatrick et al., 2012) for statistical significance. Table 2 presents the results. While reranking using C old appears to help the generation model, it still substantially underperforms all other models, across all metrics. Although this model is trained on considerably more data, it does not have access to C old during training and uses fewer inputs and consequently has less context than the edit model. Reranking slightly deteriorates the edit model's performance with respect to SARI; however, it provides statistically significant improvements on most other metrics. Although two of the baselines achieve slightly higher BLEU-4 scores than our best model, these differences are not statistically significant, and our model is better at editing comments, as shown by the results on exact match, SARI, and GLEU. In particular, our edit models beat all other models with wide, statistically significant, margins on SARI, which explicitly measures performance on edit operations. Furthermore, merely copying C old , yields a relatively high BLEU-4 score of 46.218. The return type substitution and return type substitution w/ null handling baselines produce predictions that are identical to C old for 74.73% and 65.76% of the test examples, respectively, while it is only 9.33% for the reranked edit model. In other words, the baselines attain high scores on automatic metrics and even beat our model on BLEU-4, without actually performing edits on the majority of examples. This further underlines the shortcomings of some of these metrics and the importance of conducting human evaluation for this task.

metadata.title: Learning to Update Natural Language Comments Based on Code Changes

text: Results: We report automatic evaluation metrics averaged over three random random initializations for all learned models, and assess statistical significance using bootstrap tests (Berg-Kirkpatrick et al., 2012). The results are shown in Table 2. Although reranking with \(C_{\text{old}}\) seems to benefit the generation model, it still lags far behind all other models on every metric. Despite being trained on substantially more data, this model does not have access to \(C_{\text{old}}\) at training time and relies on fewer inputs, and therefore has less contextual information than the edit model. Reranking causes a slight drop in the edit model’s SARI score, but yields statistically significant gains on most of the remaining metrics. While two baselines obtain marginally higher BLEU-4 scores than our best model, these differences are not statistically significant, and our model is superior at comment editing, as reflected by exact match, SARI, and GLEU. In particular, our edit models outperform all other systems by wide and statistically significant margins on SARI, which directly evaluates edit operations. Moreover, simply copying \(C_{\text{old}}\) already gives a relatively strong BLEU-4 score of 46.218. The return type substitution and return type substitution w/ null handling baselines produce outputs identical to \(C_{\text{old}}\) for 74.73% and 65.76% of the test examples, respectively, whereas this occurs for only 9.33% of the reranked edit model’s predictions. In other words, these baselines achieve high automatic scores and even surpass our model on BLEU-4 without actually editing the majority of examples. This further highlights the limitations of these metrics and the need for human evaluation on this task.

“And consider, ma’am,” said Oliver, as the tears forced themselves into his eyes, despite of his efforts to the contrary. “Oh! consider how young and good she is, and what pleasure and comfort she gives to all about her. I am sure—certain—quite certain—that, for your sake, who are so good yourself; and for her own; and for the sake of all she makes so happy; she will not die. Heaven will never let her die so young.”

“Pray think on it, madam,” said Elias, hot tears stinging the corners of his eyes no matter how hard he blinked them back. “Oh! only think how gentle and young she is, how she brings such light and ease to every soul about her. I know it—swear it, fully swear it—that for your sake, who are so good and kind yourself; and for her own; and for the sake of all she fills with such quiet happiness; she will not die. The heavens will never let her pass from this world so early in her days.”

Yon wandering rill that marks the hill,
And glances o’er the brae, Sir,
Slides by a bower, where mony a flower
Sheds fragrance on the day, Sir;
There Damon lay, with Sylvia gay,
To love they thought no crime, Sir,
The wild birds sang, the echoes rang,
While Damon’s heart beat time, Sir.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Yon winding rill that carves the hill,
And skims across the brae, Sir,
Glides past a bower where scores of flowers
Breathe soft scent through the day, Sir;
There Damon reclined, with Sylvia fair,
Their sweet love they held no crime, Sir,
Wild warblers trilled, bright echoes swelled,
As Damon’s pulse kept time, Sir.

My country, my beloved country! who but a native can tell the delight I took in again beholding thy streams, thy mountains, and, more than all, thy lovely lake!

I suspect, my dear companion, that I grow wearisome by lingering upon these opening scenes; yet they were days of relative peace, and I recall them with joy. My homeland, my adored home! Who but a native can know the rapture of beholding again your rivers, your peaks, and, above all, your exquisite lake!

The mouth seemed formed less to speak than to quiver, less to quiver than to kiss. Some might have added, less to kiss than to curl. Viewed sideways, the closing-line of her lips formed, with almost geometric precision, the curve so well known in the arts of design as the cima-recta, or ogee. The sight of such a flexible bend as that on grim Egdon was quite an apparition. It was felt at once that the mouth did not come over from Sleswig with a band of Saxon pirates whose lips met like the two halves of a muffin. One had fancied that such lip-curves were mostly lurking underground in the South as fragments of forgotten marbles. So fine were the lines of her lips that, though full, each corner of her mouth was as clearly cut as the point of a spear. This keenness of corner was only blunted when she was given over to sudden fits of gloom, one of the phases of the night-side of sentiment which she knew too well for her years.

That mouth looked shaped not so much for speaking as for trembling, not so much for trembling as for kissing. Some would have added that it was made less for kissing than to curl softly at the corners. Seen in profile, the seam where her lips pressed together traced, with nearly mathematical exactness, the curve familiar to decorative artists as the cima-recta, or ogee. To catch sight of such a soft, supple curve on harsh, unforgiving Egdon Heath was little short of a ghostly apparition. You knew immediately that this mouth had not crossed the sea from Sleswig alongside a band of Saxon raiders, whose lips fit together as plain and unshapely as the two halves of a cut muffin. Most would have assumed such delicate lip curves only existed half-buried in southern soil, broken fragments of long-forgotten marble statues. The lines of her lips were so exquisitely shaped that, even at their fullest, each corner of her mouth was chiseled as sharply as the tip of a spear. That sharp definition at the corners softened only when she was seized by sudden bouts of melancholy, one facet of the darker, more brooding side of feeling that she knew far too intimately for someone so young.

Behind the luminous streams of this _aurora militaris_, she could see the hue of Troy’s sword arm, spread in a scarlet haze over the space covered by its motions, like a twanged harpstring, and behind all Troy himself, mostly facing her; sometimes, to show the rear cuts, half turned away, his eye nevertheless always keenly measuring her breadth and outline, and his lips tightly closed in sustained effort. Next, his movements lapsed slower, and she could see them individually. The hissing of the sword had ceased, and he stopped entirely.

Through the shimmering trails of this military dawn-light, she glimpsed the tint of Troy's blade-hand, diffused in a crimson blur across the arc of its swings, much like a plucked harp string vibrating, and beyond it all Troy himself, mainly turned toward her; occasionally, to demonstrate the backhand strokes, half pivoted away, yet his gaze perpetually sharp, gauging her form and silhouette, his mouth pressed firm with steady exertion. Then his motions decelerated, and she could discern each one distinctly. The sword’s whisper faded, and he came to a full halt.

“But the upshot of it all—of my thinking and reading and loving—is that I am going to move to Grub Street. I shall leave masterpieces alone and do hack-work—jokes, paragraphs, feature articles, humorous verse, and society verse—all the rot for which there seems so much demand. Then there are the newspaper syndicates, and the newspaper short-story syndicates, and the syndicates for the Sunday supplements. I can go ahead and hammer out the stuff they want, and earn the equivalent of a good salary by it. There are free-lances, you know, who earn as much as four or five hundred a month. I don’t care to become as they; but I’ll earn a good living, and have plenty of time to myself, which I wouldn’t have in any position.”

The end result of all my reflections, my studies, and my passions is this: I am bound for Grub Street. I’ll abandon any thought of masterpieces and turn to hack-work—jokes, squibs, feature pieces, light verse, society rhymes—all the fluff that seems to be in such endless demand. Then there are the newspaper syndicates, the short-story syndicates, and the syndicates that feed the Sunday supplements. I can churn out exactly the stuff they want and pull in the equivalent of a decent salary. There are free-lances, as you know, who make four or five hundred a month. I have no wish to become one of them; but I’ll earn a comfortable living and keep ample time for myself, which no fixed position would ever allow me.

It was in the latter end of September that I again quitted my native country. My journey had been my own suggestion, and Elizabeth therefore acquiesced, but she was filled with disquiet at the idea of my suffering, away from her, the inroads of misery and grief. It had been her care which provided me a companion in Clerval—and yet a man is blind to a thousand minute circumstances which call forth a woman’s sedulous attention. She longed to bid me hasten my return; a thousand conflicting emotions rendered her mute as she bade me a tearful, silent farewell.

It was late in September when I once more left my homeland. The voyage had been my own idea, so Elizabeth had consented, but she was deeply troubled by the thought of me enduring sorrow and anguish far from her side. She had thoughtfully arranged for Clerval to accompany me—yet a man overlooks countless small details that a woman’s careful eye would catch. She wished to urge me to come back quickly; a host of warring feelings kept her silent as she gave me a tearful, wordless goodbye.

text: _I want to tell you that all the stress and passion of this war has not made us here in Matching's Easy forget our friend your son. He was one of us, he had our affection, he had friends here who are still his friends. We found him honourable and companionable, and we share something of your loss. I have got together for you a few snapshots I chance to possess in which you will see him in the sunshine, and which will enable you perhaps to picture a little more definitely than you would otherwise do the life he led here. There is one particularly that I have marked. Our family is lunching out-of-doors, and you will see that next to your son is a youngster, a year or so his junior, who is touching glasses with him. I have put a cross over his head. He is my eldest son, he was very dear to me, and he too has been, killed in this war. They are, you see, smiling very pleasantly at each other._

I want you to know that, amid all the strain and fervour of this war, we here in Matching’s Easy have not forgotten your son, who was our friend. He belonged among us, held our affection, and still has friends in this place who remain loyal to his memory. We found him upright and good company, and we feel a portion of your grief as our own. I have gathered for you a few snapshots I happen to have, in which you will see him in sunlight, and which may help you to picture a little more clearly the life he led here. One in particular I have marked. Our family is dining out of doors, and you will notice beside your son a young man, perhaps a year younger, touching glasses with him. I have placed a cross above his head. He is my eldest boy, he was very precious to me, and he too has been killed in this war. You see them there, smiling so warmly at one another.

I make no claim to know—this life  
Of missteps, blindness, endless strife,  
Where nothing stands, all things just seem,  
And we are shadows of the dream.

The trees beneath which she sat were singularly battered, rude, and wild, and for a few minutes Mrs. Yeobright dismissed thoughts of her own storm-broken and exhausted state to contemplate theirs. Not a bough in the nine trees which composed the group but was splintered, lopped, and distorted by the fierce weather that there held them at its mercy whenever it prevailed. Some were blasted and split as if by lightning, black stains as from fire marking their sides, while the ground at their feet was strewn with dead fir-needles and heaps of cones blown down in the gales of past years. The place was called the Devil’s Bellows, and it was only necessary to come there on a March or November night to discover the forcible reasons for that name. On the present heated afternoon, when no perceptible wind was blowing, the trees kept up a perpetual moan which one could hardly believe to be caused by the air.

The trees under which she rested were singularly rough, battered, and savage, and for a few moments Mrs. Ashdown forgot her own storm-worn weariness to regard theirs. Not a branch in the little company of nine but had been splintered, lopped, or twisted awry by the violence of the weather, which, whenever it rose, held the spot in its cruel grasp. Some were scorched and riven as though by lightning, with dark stains running down their trunks like fire-marks, while the earth below was littered with dead pine-needles and drifts of cones flung down by the gales of former years. The place was known as the Devil’s Bellowing, and one had only to visit it on a March or November night to understand the strength of the name. Yet on the present sultry afternoon, when no breeze was perceptible, the trees maintained a continual lament that could scarcely be thought the mere voice of the air.

aims to revise the text by adding missing facts and deleting unsupported facts. Table 1 gives an example of the task. As far as we know, no previous work did address the problem. In a text-to-text generation, given a text, the system automatically creates another text, where the new text can be a text in another language (machine translation), a summary of the original text (summarization), or a text in better form (text editing). In a table-to-text generation, given a table containing facts in triples, the system automatically composes a text, which describes the facts. The former is a text-to-text problem, and the latter a table-to-text problem. In comparison, fact-based text editing can be viewed as a 'text&table-to-text' problem.

metadata.title: Fact-based Text Editing

text: The objective is to revise a text by incorporating missing facts and removing unsupported ones. Table 1 provides an illustrative example of this task. To the best of our knowledge, no prior work has specifically addressed this problem. In text-to-text generation, a system receives an input text and automatically produces a new text, which may be a translation into another language (machine translation), a condensed version of the original (summarization), or an improved rendition (text editing). In table-to-text generation, the system is given a table of facts represented as triples and automatically composes a descriptive text from those facts. The former constitutes a text-to-text problem, while the latter is a table-to-text problem. By contrast, fact-based text editing can be conceptualized as a “text & table-to-text” problem.

But turning back 't was slow.

Who came to give her thanks,
But she lay deep in sleep.
How brief to sail across the sea
And see her likeness, warm with life—
Yet turning home, how long.

But do I desire unreasonably much in wanting what is called life—music, poetry, passion, war, and all the beating and pulsing that are going on in the great arteries of the world? “He doesn’t take the blessing of winning her for granted,” Eustacia whispered, “and in that regard, he is a good man. Many women would go to great lengths for a husband like him. But am I craving something unreasonably excessive when I long for what is called true life—music, poetry, burning passion, the fire of grand struggle, all the thrumming, surging pulse that beats in the great arteries of the wider world? That was the exact shape of my youthful dream; I never got to hold it, yet I once thought I saw the path to it in my Clym.”

To Boldwood women had been remote phenomena rather than necessary complements—comets of such uncertain aspect, movement, and permanence, that whether their orbits were as geometrical, unchangeable, and as subject to laws as his own, or as absolutely erratic as they superficially appeared, he had not deemed it his duty to consider.

To him, women were distant curiosities rather than essential partners—celestial bodies of such vague form, motion, and endurance that he had never felt compelled to wonder if their paths were governed by the same rigid principles as his own, or if they were as truly chaotic as they appeared on the surface.

In order to design a neural network that is efficient to train and that exploits syntactic information while producing high-quality translations, we base our model on the Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017) and upgrade its encoder with parent-scaled self-attention (PASCAL) heads at layer l s . PASCAL heads enforce contextualization from the syntactic dependencies of each source token, and, in practice, we replace standard selfattention heads with PASCAL ones in the first layer as its inputs are word embeddings that lack any contextual information. Our PASCAL sub-layer has the same number H of attention heads as other layers.

metadata.title: Enhancing Machine Translation with Dependency-Aware Self-Attention

text: To develop a neural network that trains efficiently, leverages syntactic information, and yields high-quality translations, we adopt the Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017) and augment its encoder at layer \(l_s\) with parent-scaled self-attention (PASCAL) heads. PASCAL heads drive contextualization based on the syntactic dependencies of each source token; concretely, we substitute standard self-attention heads with PASCAL heads in the first layer, since its inputs are word embeddings devoid of contextual information. This PASCAL sub-layer retains the same number \(H\) of attention heads as the other layers.

In front of Mr. Direck the little stout man was being alert. Teddy was supporting the attack near the middle of the field, crying "Centre!" while Mr. Britling, very round and resolute, was bouncing straight towards the threatened goal. But Mrs. Teddy, running as swiftly as her sister, was between Teddy and the ball. Whack! the little short man's stick had clashed with Cecily's. Confused things happened with sticks and feet, and the little short man appeared to be trying to cut down Cecily as one cuts down a tree, she tried to pass the ball to her centre forward--too late, and then Mrs. Teddy had intercepted it, and was flickering back towards Mr. Britling's goal in a rush in which Mr. Direck perceived it was his duty to join.

Running hard to lend help to Mr. Raeburn came the youngest Britling boy, a delightful contrast. He was like a puffball propping and assisting a conger eel. Before Mr. Direck, the little stout man was full of alertness. Teddy was backing the attack near the middle of the field, shouting “Centre!” while Mr. Britling, very round and determined, bounced straight toward the threatened goal. But Mrs. Teddy, running as fast as her sister, stood between Teddy and the ball. Whack! the little short man’s stick struck Cecily’s. A tangle of sticks and feet followed, and the little short man seemed to be trying to bring Cecily down as one fells a tree. She attempted to pass the ball to her centre forward—too late—and then Mrs. Teddy had intercepted it, and was darting back toward Mr. Britling’s goal in a rush that Mr. Direck saw it was his duty to join.

“Food, however, became scarce, and I often spent the whole day searching in vain for a few acorns to assuage the pangs of hunger. When I found this, I resolved to quit the place that I had hitherto inhabited, to seek for one where the few wants I experienced would be more easily satisfied. In this emigration I exceedingly lamented the loss of the fire which I had obtained through accident and knew not how to reproduce it. I gave several hours to the serious consideration of this difficulty, but I was obliged to relinquish all attempt to supply it, and wrapping myself up in my cloak, I struck across the wood towards the setting sun. I passed three days in these rambles and at length discovered the open country. A great fall of snow had taken place the night before, and the fields were of one uniform white; the appearance was disconsolate, and I found my feet chilled by the cold damp substance that covered the ground.

Food grew scarce, all the same, and I would often wander for entire days searching fruitlessly for a handful of acorns to dull the sharp, twisting pangs of hunger. Once I accepted this unyielding state of things, I made up my mind to leave the spot I had inhabited up until that point, to search for somewhere my few simple needs might be met with less struggle. As I prepared to set off, I grieved deeply over the loss of the fire I had stumbled upon by chance, for I had no idea how to make a new one for myself. I spent hours turning the problem over carefully in my mind, but I was eventually forced to abandon any hope of replacing it. Pulling my cloak tight around my shoulders, I struck out through the woods, heading in the direction of the sinking sun. I wandered for three days before I finally came upon open, unfenced countryside. A heavy snowstorm had blown through the night before, painting every stretch of field the same stark, unbroken white. The sight was dreary and desolate, and I felt my feet growing numb, stung by the cold, damp crust that blanketed the ground.

Rendered in a manner desperate, by her state and by the beckoning of their conductor, he drew over his neck the arm that shook upon his shoulder, lifted her a little, and hurried her into the room. He sat her down just within the door, and held her, clinging to him.

In a gesture born of urgency, compelled by her condition and the conductor’s summoning, he pulled the trembling arm from his shoulder around his neck, raised her slightly, and hastened her inside. He settled her just past the threshold, steadying her as she clung to him.

“I expected this reception,” said the dæmon. “All men hate the wretched; how, then, must I be hated, who am miserable beyond all living things! Yet you, my creator, detest and spurn me, thy creature, to whom thou art bound by ties only dissoluble by the annihilation of one of us. You purpose to kill me. How dare you sport thus with life? Do your duty towards me, and I will do mine towards you and the rest of mankind. If you will comply with my conditions, I will leave them and you at peace; but if you refuse, I will glut the maw of death, until it be satiated with the blood of your remaining friends.”

“I anticipated this kind of welcome,” the creature said. “Society has always detested the miserable; imagine, then, the scorn I must face, being the most wretched of all living things. Even you, my maker, abhor and reject your own creation, though we are bound by a link that can only be broken by the death of one of us. You intend to take my life, but how can you treat the gift of existence so lightly? Fulfill your responsibilities to me, and I will honor my obligations to you and the rest of humanity. If you agree to my terms, I will leave you and everyone else in peace; however, if you refuse, I will feed the hunger of death until it is gorged on the blood of every friend you have left.”

“Well, then, there 's photographing,” said Captain Abel. “It doos seem amazing, as the minister said: you set down, and square yourself, and slick your hair, and stare stiddy into a funnel, and a man ducks his head under a covering, and pop! there you be, as natural as life,--if not more so. And when Uncle Capen was a young man, there wasn't nothing but portraits and minnytures, and these black-paper-and-scissors portraits,--what do they call 'em? Yes, sir, all that come in under his observation.”

“Well, then, there’s photography,” said Captain Silas. “It does seem astonishing, as the minister said: you sit down, and square yourself up, and smooth your hair, and stare steady into a little funnel, and a man ducks his head under a cloth, and pop! there you are, as natural as life—if not more so. And when Uncle Hiram was a young man, there was nothing but portraits and miniatures, and those black-paper-and-scissors likenesses—what do they call them? Yes, sir, all that came within his observation.”

For there can be no denying that Mr. Britling was, in a sense, distinguished. The hero and subject of this novel was at its very beginning a distinguished man. He was in the _Who's Who_ of two continents. In the last few years he had grown with some rapidity into a writer recognised and welcomed by the more cultivated sections of the American public, and even known to a select circle of British readers. To his American discoverers he had first appeared as an essayist, a serious essayist who wrote about aesthetics and Oriental thought and national character and poets and painting. He had come through America some years ago as one of those Kahn scholars, those promising writers and intelligent men endowed by Auguste Kahn of Paris, who go about the world nowadays in comfort and consideration as the travelling guests of that original philanthropist--to acquire the international spirit. Previously he had been a critic of art and literature and a writer of thoughtful third leaders in the London _Times_. He had begun with a Pembroke fellowship and a prize poem. He had returned from his world tour to his reflective yet original corner of _The Times_ and to the production of books about national relationships and social psychology, that had brought him rapidly into prominence.

There is no question that Mr. Britling possessed a certain distinction. At the outset of this story, the man who stands at its center was already a figure of note. His name appeared in the _Who's Who_ of two continents. Over the preceding few years, he had swiftly emerged as an author recognized and embraced by the more discerning American readership, and even known among a small, selective audience in Britain. To those who first discovered him across the Atlantic, he had presented himself as a serious essayist, one who explored aesthetics, Eastern philosophy, national identity, poets, and the visual arts. Years before, he had traveled through America as a Kahn fellow—one of those gifted writers and thinkers funded by the Parisian philanthropist Auguste Kahn, who sends such promising individuals around the globe in comfort and esteem to cultivate an international outlook. Earlier still, he had served as a critic of art and literature and composed reflective op-ed pieces for the London _Times_. His career had begun with a fellowship at Pembroke and a prize-winning poem. After returning from his global journey, he settled back into his contemplative yet distinctive niche at _The Times_ and began producing books on national relations and social psychology, which quickly propelled him into the limelight.

He crossed on the ferry to San Francisco and made his way to an employment office. “Any kind of work, no trade,” he told the agent; and was interrupted by a new-comer, dressed rather foppishly, as some workingmen dress who have instincts for finer things. The agent shook his head despondently.

He took the ferry across to San Francisco and headed straight for a hiring hall. “Anything at all—I’ve got no skilled trade,” he said to the clerk, just as another man walked in, turned out with a touch of dandyism, the way certain laborers dress when they’ve an inborn taste for something finer. The clerk gave a dispirited shake of his head.

“Why, yes. You do corporation work. You have no working-class nor criminal practice. You don’t depend upon wife-beaters and pickpockets for your income. You get your livelihood from the masters of society, and whoever feeds a man is that man’s master. Yes, you are a henchman. You are interested in advancing the interests of the aggregations of capital you serve.”

“Oh, of course. Your practice is entirely corporate. You don’t take on working-class clients or criminal cases, so you never rely on wife-beaters or small-time pickpockets for your income. Your entire livelihood comes straight from the ruling masters of society, and everyone knows the hand that feeds a man is the hand that commands him. That makes you a hired henchman, plain and simple. Your only priority is advancing the interests of the massive capital conglomerates you work for.”

A black and white striped cat . for relevance, informativeness and how humanlike the generating is using a 3-point Likert-like scale. Judges were required to pass a qualification test, and a regime of spam detection was imposed. 8 Overall judge preferences for relevance, informativeness and human-likeness, presented as raw numbers and a percentage of the total, are shown in Table 7 . A strong preference can be observed for DialoGPT over PersonalityChat. Table 7 also suggests that the "vanilla" DialoGPT medium model may already be close to human response quality. Unexpectedly, we found that judges may prefer the MMI variant over human responses, probably because of many of the true human responses are erratic or idiosyncratic, or are tied to internet memes that happened to be unfamiliar to the judges. 9 (See Section 4.2 for the conditions underlying this effect.) Further details, including a test of significance and the human evaluation template used, are provided in the Appendix.

metadata.title: DIALOGPT : Large-Scale Generative Pre-training for Conversational Response Generation

text: We evaluate relevance, informativeness, and human-likeness using a 3-point Likert-style scale. All judges completed a qualification test, and a spam detection protocol was enforced. Table 7 reports the aggregated judge preferences for relevance, informativeness, and human-likeness, both as raw counts and as percentages. The results reveal a clear preference for DialoGPT over PersonalityChat. Moreover, Table 7 indicates that the standard DialoGPT medium model may already approach human-level response quality. Surprisingly, we observe that judges sometimes favor the MMI variant over genuine human responses, likely because many actual human replies are erratic, idiosyncratic, or reference internet memes unfamiliar to the judges. (Section 4.2 discusses the conditions behind this effect.) The Appendix supplies additional details, including a significance test and the human evaluation template.

An old man came into the remoter light of the fire from the direction of the homestead. He was the same who had overtaken the reddleman on the road that afternoon. He looked wistfully to the top of the bank at the woman who stood there, and his teeth, which were quite unimpaired, showed like parian from his parted lips.

An aged man emerged from the farther glow of the fire, coming up from the direction of the farmhouse. It was he who had met the reddleman on the road that afternoon. He raised his eyes with a wistful air to the crest of the bank where the woman was standing, and the teeth revealed between his parted lips were sound and white as parian.

“In the name of the assembled companions in misfortune,” said a gentleman of courtly appearance and address, coming forward, “I have the honour of giving you welcome to La Force, and of condoling with you on the calamity that has brought you among us. May it soon terminate happily! It would be an impertinence elsewhere, but it is not so here, to ask your name and condition?”

“In the name of this company of fellow sufferers,” said a gentleman of polished bearing and manner, stepping a little in advance, “I have the honour to bid you welcome to La Force, and to offer you my sympathy in the misfortune that has conducted you hither. May it end speedily and well! It might elsewhere seem an impertinence, but it is not so here, to inquire your name and station?”

Yes, we were sold at auction, like swine. In a big town and an active market we should have brought a good price; but this place was utterly stagnant and so we sold at a figure which makes me ashamed, every time I think of it. The King of England brought seven dollars, and his prime minister nine; whereas the king was easily worth twelve dollars and I as easily worth fifteen. But that is the way things always go; if you force a sale on a dull market, I don't care what the property is, you are going to make a poor business of it, and you can make up your mind to it. In another part of the hill an altogether different scene began to force itself upon the eye towards midday. A circular tent, of exceptional newness and size, was in course of erection here. As the day drew on, the flocks began to change hands, lightening the shepherd’s responsibilities; and they turned their attention to this tent and inquired of a man at work there, whose soul seemed concentrated on tying a bothering knot in no time, what was going on.

Elsewhere upon the slope, as the sun climbed toward noon, a wholly separate spectacle commenced to impress itself upon the sight. A round marquee, remarkable for its freshness and uncommon breadth, was being raised upon that spot. With the advancing hours, the sheep passed into other care, easing the herder’s burden; so the men shifted their gaze toward this pavilion and asked a fellow labouring there—whose spirit appeared wholly bent on subduing a vexing tangle without delay—what affair was afoot.

When Noah saw that the intelligence he communicated perfectly paralysed Mr. Bumble, he imparted additional effect thereunto, by bewailing his dreadful wounds ten times louder than before; and when he observed a gentleman in a white waistcoat crossing the yard, he was more tragic in his lamentations than ever: rightly conceiving it highly expedient to attract the notice, and rouse the indignation, of the gentleman aforesaid.

When Noah realized that the news he had delivered had utterly stunned Mr. Bumble, he heightened the impact by lamenting his terrible injuries with tenfold the volume; and upon spotting a gentleman in a white waistcoat crossing the yard, he made his cries more theatrical than ever, correctly judging it essential to capture the attention and stir the outrage of that very gentleman.

“Yet I cannot ask you to renounce your country and friends to fulfil this task; and now that you are returning to England, you will have little chance of meeting with him. But the consideration of these points, and the well balancing of what you may esteem your duties, I leave to you; my judgment and ideas are already disturbed by the near approach of death. I dare not ask you to do what I think right, for I may still be misled by passion.

“Yet I cannot require you to forsake your native land and your friends in order to accomplish this charge; and now that you are about to return to England, you will have little opportunity of encountering him. But the weighing of these matters, and the careful adjustment of what you may judge to be your duties, I must leave to yourself; my own reason and sentiments are already unsettled by the close approach of death. I dare not entreat you to do what I believe to be right, for even now I may still be deceived by feeling.

Mr. Chitling was older in years than the Dodger: having perhaps numbered eighteen winters; but there was a degree of deference in his deportment towards that young gentleman which seemed to indicate that he felt himself conscious of a slight inferiority in point of genius and professional aquirements. He had small twinkling eyes, and a pock-marked face; wore a fur cap, a dark corduroy jacket, greasy fustian trousers, and an apron. His wardrobe was, in truth, rather out of repair; but he excused himself to the company by stating that his “time” was only out an hour before; and that, in consequence of having worn the regimentals for six weeks past, he had not been able to bestow any attention on his private clothes. Mr. Chitling added, with strong marks of irritation, that the new way of fumigating clothes up yonder was infernal unconstitutional, for it burnt holes in them, and there was no remedy against the county. The same remark he considered to apply to the regulation mode of cutting the hair: which he held to be decidedly unlawful. Mr. Chitling wound up his observations by stating that he had not touched a drop of anything for forty-two moral long hard-working days; and that he “wished he might be busted if he warn’t as dry as a lime-basket.”

Mr. Chitling was older than the Dodger, perhaps by several years; he may have been about eighteen. Yet there was something in the way he carried himself toward that young gentleman that suggested he was aware of a slight inferiority in matters of wit and professional skill. He had small, restless eyes and a face pitted with marks; he wore a fur cap, a dark corduroy jacket, greasy fustian trousers, and an apron. His clothes, in truth, were in a poor state; but he made his excuse to the company by saying that his “time” had only run out an hour earlier, and that, after wearing the regimentals for the past six weeks, he had not had a chance to give any attention to his own wardrobe. Mr. Chitling went on, with evident annoyance, to declare that the new method of fumigating clothes up there was infernally unconstitutional, because it burned holes in them, and there was no redress against the county. He said the same complaint applied to the approved way of cutting the hair, which he considered flatly unlawful. He ended by saying that he had not taken a drop of anything for forty-two long, hard, moral days of labor, and that he “wished he might be busted if he warn’t as dry as a lime-basket.”

And also, he wanted to see the old country from which his maternal grandmother had sprung. Wasn't there even now in his bedroom in New York a water-colour of Market Saffron church, where the dear old lady had been confirmed? And generally he wanted to see Europe. As an interesting side show to the excursion he hoped, in his capacity of the rather underworked and rather over-salaried secretary of the Massachusetts Society for the Study of Contemporary Thought, to discuss certain agreeable possibilities with Mr. Britling, who lived at Matching's Easy.

On top of everything else, he’d long been curious to visit the old country his maternal grandmother had hailed from. Didn’t he still keep a watercolor of Market Saffron church propped on a shelf in his New York bedroom, the very church where that dear old woman had been confirmed as a young girl? More broadly, he was just eager to explore Europe at large, full stop. As a low-stakes, enjoyable side piece of the excursion, he hoped to connect with Mr. Britling, who lived in Matching’s Easy, to talk through a few promising potential opportunities in his capacity as secretary of the Massachusetts Society for the Study of Contemporary Thought— a position that left him drastically underworked and noticeably overpaid, if anyone bothered to ask.

“And so,” he concluded, in a swift résumé, “no state composed of the slave-types can endure. The old law of development still holds. In the struggle for existence, as I have shown, the strong and the progeny of the strong tend to survive, while the weak and the progeny of the weak are crushed and tend to perish. The result is that the strong and the progeny of the strong survive, and, so long as the struggle obtains, the strength of each generation increases. That is development. But you slaves—it is too bad to be slaves, I grant—but you slaves dream of a society where the law of development will be annulled, where no weaklings and inefficients will perish, where every inefficient will have as much as he wants to eat as many times a day as he desires, and where all will marry and have progeny—the weak as well as the strong. What will be the result? No longer will the strength and life-value of each generation increase. On the contrary, it will diminish. There is the Nemesis of your slave philosophy. Your society of slaves—of, by, and for, slaves—must inevitably weaken and go to pieces as the life which composes it weakens and goes to pieces.

“And so,” he concluded, in a sharp, rapid summary, “no state built of people with a slave’s mindset can ever endure. The ancient law of development still holds unshaken. In the struggle for existence, as I have demonstrated, the strong and their descendants are disposed to survive, while the weak and their children are crushed and fated to perish. The outcome is that the strong and their line persist, and as long as that struggle remains in place, each generation grows more capable than the last. That is the very essence of development. But you slaves— I will grant that it is a miserable thing to be a slave— but you cling to a fantasy of a society where the law of development is struck down, where no weak, unfit person ever perishes, where every incompetent person gets all the food they crave whenever they want it, and where everyone may marry and have children—the weak just as freely as the strong. What will come of this? The strength and inherent vitality of each generation will no longer rise. On the contrary, it will dwindle. That is the nemesis of your slave philosophy. Your society of slaves, by slaves, and for slaves, will inevitably weaken and crumble just as the lives that form it weaken and are snuffed out.”

He reached the race-course at two o’clock, and remained either there or in the town till nine. But Fanny’s image, as it had appeared to him in the sombre shadows of that Saturday evening, returned to his mind, backed up by Bathsheba’s reproaches. He vowed he would not bet, and he kept his vow, for on leaving the town at nine o’clock in the evening he had diminished his cash only to the extent of a few shillings.

He reached the race-course at two o’clock and stayed there, or in the town, until nine. Yet Fanny’s image, as it had come before him in the dim shadows of that Saturday evening, kept returning to his thoughts, reinforced by Bathsheba’s reproaches. He swore that he would not bet, and he kept his word; for when he left the town at nine that night, his money was reduced by only a few shillings.

“The war with the English nation, as a nation, is at an end. The nation has retired from the field and the war. Before it can be persuaded to return, war will have ceased. This campaign is the only one that is going to be fought. It will be brief --the briefest in history. Also the most destructive to life, considered from the standpoint of proportion of casualties to numbers engaged. We are done with the nation; henceforth we deal only with the knights. English knights can be killed, but they cannot be conquered. We know what is before us. While one of these men remains alive, our task is not finished, the war is not ended. We will kill them all.” [Loud and long continued applause.]

Our war against the English as a unified nation is at an end. The entire populace has withdrawn from the battlefield and stepped away from the conflict. Long before they could ever be persuaded to return to fighting, all hostilities will have ceased entirely. This is the only remaining campaign we will wage. It will be brief—the shortest in recorded history, in fact. It will also be the most devastating to human life, when measured by the ratio of casualties to the total number of troops engaged. We have no further business with the English nation at large; from now on, we contend only with their knights. English knights can be killed, but they can never be conquered. We know full well what lies before us. As long as even one of these men remains alive, our task is unfinished, and the war is not over. We will kill every last one of them. [Loud, long-sustained applause erupted.]

About two o’clock the mist cleared away, and we beheld, stretched out in every direction, vast and irregular plains of ice, which seemed to have no end. Some of my comrades groaned, and my own mind began to grow watchful with anxious thoughts, when a strange sight suddenly attracted our attention and diverted our solicitude from our own situation. We perceived a low carriage, fixed on a sledge and drawn by dogs, pass on towards the north, at the distance of half a mile; a being which had the shape of a man, but apparently of gigantic stature, sat in the sledge and guided the dogs. We watched the rapid progress of the traveller with our telescopes until he was lost among the distant inequalities of the ice.

Around two in the afternoon, the fog finally lifted, revealing an endless expanse of jagged ice fields stretching toward the horizon in every direction. Some of my crewmates groaned in despair, and I felt a growing sense of unease myself, until a bizarre sight suddenly caught our attention and pulled our focus away from our own predicament. About half a mile off, we spotted a low-slung sled pulled by a team of dogs heading north; a figure that appeared human, though of immense, towering proportions, sat in the vehicle and guided the pack. We tracked the traveler’s swift progress through our telescopes until he eventually vanished behind the uneven ridges of the distant frozen landscape.

The traveller fared slowly on his way, who fared towards Paris from England in the autumn of the year one thousand seven hundred and ninety-two. More than enough of bad roads, bad equipages, and bad horses, he would have encountered to delay him, though the fallen and unfortunate King of France had been upon his throne in all his glory; but, the changed times were fraught with other obstacles than these. Every town-gate and village taxing-house had its band of citizen-patriots, with their national muskets in a most explosive state of readiness, who stopped all comers and goers, cross-questioned them, inspected their papers, looked for their names in lists of their own, turned them back, or sent them on, or stopped them and laid them in hold, as their capricious judgment or fancy deemed best for the dawning Republic One and Indivisible, of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death.

The traveller made his way slowly, he who journeyed toward Paris from England in the autumn of the year seventeen hundred and ninety-two. He would have found delay enough in bad roads, bad conveyances, and bad horses, even had the fallen and unhappy King of France still sat upon his throne in all his splendour; but the altered times presented hindrances of another sort besides these. At every town gate and village tollhouse stood their bands of citizen-patriots, national muskets in a most ready and explosive condition, stopping all who came and went, questioning them closely, examining their papers, searching for their names in lists of their own, sending them back, or onward, or into custody, as their whim or caprice dictated, in the service of the dawning Republic One and Indivisible, of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death.

We had adventures all along. One day we ran into a procession. And such a procession! All the riffraff of the kingdom seemed to be comprehended in it; and all drunk at that. In the van was a cart with a coffin in it, and on the coffin sat a comely young girl of about eighteen suckling a baby, which she squeezed to her breast in a passion of love every little while, and every little while wiped from its face the tears which her eyes rained down upon it; and always the foolish little thing smiled up at her, happy and content, kneading her breast with its dimpled fat hand, which she patted and fondled right over her breaking heart.

“‘I have done— Put by the lute. Song and singing soon are over As the airy shades that hover In among the purple clover. I have done— Put by the lute. Once I sang as early thrushes Sing among the dewy bushes; Now I’m mute. I am like a weary linnet, For my throat has no song in it; I have had my singing minute. I have done. Put by the lute.’”

“I have finished—set the lute aside. Song and singing pass away quickly, like the airy shadows that drift among the purple clover. I have finished—set the lute aside. Once I sang like the early thrushes singing in the dewy bushes; now I am silent. I am like an exhausted linnet, for there is no song left in my throat; my moment for singing has come and gone. I have finished. Set the lute aside.”

“You old flea-headed woodchuck-chaser,” I said to him—“you moon-baying, rabbit-pointing, egg-stealing old beagle, can’t you see that I don’t want to leave you? Can’t you see that we’re both Pups in the Wood and the missis is the cruel uncle after you with the dish towel and me with the flea liniment and a pink bow to tie on my tail. Why not cut that all out and be pards forever more?”

You scruffy, woodchuck-hunting old mutt," I said to him. "You moon-howling, rabbit-tracking, egg-thieving beagle, don't you realize I have no desire to leave you? Can't you see we are both like those pups lost in the forest, while the mistress plays the part of the wicked uncle, chasing you with a kitchen towel and coming for me with flea ointment and a pink ribbon for my tail? Why don't we just give all that up and be partners for good?

But the most radical change of all, and the one that pleased her most, was the change in his speech. Not only did he speak more correctly, but he spoke more easily, and there were many new words in his vocabulary. When he grew excited or enthusiastic, however, he dropped back into the old slurring and the dropping of final consonants. Also, there was an awkward hesitancy, at times, as he essayed the new words he had learned. On the other hand, along with his ease of expression, he displayed a lightness and facetiousness of thought that delighted her. It was his old spirit of humor and badinage that had made him a favorite in his own class, but which he had hitherto been unable to use in her presence through lack of words and training. He was just beginning to orientate himself and to feel that he was not wholly an intruder. But he was very tentative, fastidiously so, letting Ruth set the pace of sprightliness and fancy, keeping up with her but never daring to go beyond her.

But the deepest alteration of all, and the one that gave her the keenest pleasure, was the change in his speech. He not only spoke more correctly, but with greater ease, and his vocabulary had grown rich with many unfamiliar words. Yet when excitement or ardor took hold of him, he slipped back into the old drawl and the soft swallowing of final sounds. There was, too, at times an uneasy hesitation as he tried out the new words he had acquired. On the other hand, with this new fluency there came a quickness and playful wit in his thinking that delighted her. It was the same old turn for humor and banter that had once made him a favorite among his own companions, though until now he had been unable to bring it forth in her presence for want of words and training. He was only just beginning to find his bearings and to feel that he was not altogether a stranger. But he proceeded with great caution, almost with punctilious care, letting Clara determine the tempo of gayety and invention, matching her pace, yet never venturing to pass beyond it.

Out from his tent rode great Sir Sagramor, an imposing tower of iron, stately and rigid, his huge spear standing upright in its socket and grasped in his strong hand, his grand horse's face and breast cased in steel, his body clothed in rich trappings that almost dragged the ground--oh, a most noble picture. A great shout went up, of welcome and admiration.

Out from his pavilion rode the mighty Sir Almeric, a lofty pillar of steel, grave and unbending, with his long lance set upright in its rest and held fast in his powerful hand; his great horse, too, had its face and chest armored in steel, while its body was hung with rich trappings that nearly swept the earth—truly a splendid sight. A loud acclaim arose then, full of greeting and wonder.

The sun rose presently and sent its unobstructed splendors over the land, and we saw a prodigious host moving slowly toward us, with the steady drift and aligned front of a wave of the sea. Nearer and nearer it came, and more and more sublimely imposing became its aspect; yes, all England was there, apparently. Soon we could see the innumerable banners fluttering, and then the sun struck the sea of armor and set it all aflash. Yes, it was a fine sight; I hadn't ever seen anything to beat it.

Dawn broke soon, pouring its unhindered brilliance across the earth, and we beheld an immense multitude advancing upon us with the measured surge and even crest of an ocean swell. Closer and closer it drew, and ever more awesomely magnificent its appearance grew; truly, the whole of England seemed assembled before us. Presently we discerned the countless pennons tossing, and then the sunlight smote that metal tide and kindled it into a glittering blaze. Indeed, it was a splendid spectacle; I had never witnessed its equal.

“You made mention of the young lady as a golden-haired doll. The young lady is Miss Manette. If you had been a fellow of any sensitiveness or delicacy of feeling in that kind of way, Sydney, I might have been a little resentful of your employing such a designation; but you are not. You want that sense altogether; therefore I am no more annoyed when I think of the expression, than I should be annoyed by a man’s opinion of a picture of mine, who had no eye for pictures: or of a piece of music of mine, who had no ear for music.”

“You referred to the girl as a mere porcelain figurine. That girl is Miss Beaumont. If you were a man of any grace or subtle discernment in such matters, Elias, I might have taken umbrage at your choice of words; but you are not. You are utterly wanting in that faculty; I am therefore no more troubled by your speech than I would be by a blind man’s critique of my canvases, or a deaf man’s estimation of my sonatas.”

Oliver thought the old gentleman must be a decided miser to live in such a dirty place, with so many watches; but, thinking that perhaps his fondness for the Dodger and the other boys, cost him a good deal of money, he only cast a deferential look at the Jew, and asked if he might get up.

Oliver figured the old gentleman had to be a thorough miser, dwelling in such a grimy den amid all those timepieces; yet, supposing his affection for the Dodger and the other lads might drain his purse considerably, he merely gave the Jew a respectful glance and inquired whether he might rise.

Despairing cries float ceaselessly toward me,
The call of my nearest lover, putting forth, alarm’d, uncertain,
The sea I am quickly to sail, come tell me,
Come tell me where I am speeding, tell me my destination.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
A wail drifts in, unbroken, toward my ear—  
the voice of the one I love, pitched close, half-frightened, unsure.  
The tide I’m bound to ride, tell me now—  
tell me what shore I’m racing toward, name the port I’m after.

It may have been observed that there is no regular path for getting out of love as there is for getting in. Some people look upon marriage as a short cut that way, but it has been known to fail. Separation, which was the means that chance offered to Gabriel Oak by Bathsheba’s disappearance, though effectual with people of certain humours, is apt to idealize the removed object with others—notably those whose affection, placid and regular as it may be, flows deep and long. Oak belonged to the even-tempered order of humanity, and felt the secret fusion of himself in Bathsheba to be burning with a finer flame now that she was gone—that was all.

It is often observed that while there is a clear road for falling into love, no such defined path exists for getting out of it. Some regard marriage as a shortcut to that destination, yet it is a cure known to fail. Separation, the remedy chance offered Gabriel Oak upon Bathsheba’s disappearance, might be effective for certain personalities, but it often serves to idealize the absent person for others—specifically those whose love, however quiet and steady, runs deep. Oak belonged to this even-tempered class of men, and he found the secret union of his soul with Bathsheba’s to be burning with a more refined intensity now that she was gone.

The young adventurer was of pleasing presence, and moved serenely and watchfully. By daylight he was a salesman in a piano store. He wore his tie drawn through a topaz ring instead of fastened with a stick pin; and once he had written to the editor of a magazine that “Junie’s Love Test” by Miss Libbey, had been the book that had most influenced his life.

The young wanderer had an agreeable look about him, and he carried himself with a quiet, observant grace. During business hours he sold pianos in a downtown shop. He threaded his necktie through a topaz ring rather than securing it with a pin, and he had once sent a letter to a magazine editor confessing that Miss Libbey’s “Junie’s Love Test” was the book that had most shaped his life.

“Be happy, my dear Victor,” replied Elizabeth; “there is, I hope, nothing to distress you; and be assured that if a lively joy is not painted in my face, my heart is contented. Something whispers to me not to depend too much on the prospect that is opened before us, but I will not listen to such a sinister voice. Observe how fast we move along and how the clouds, which sometimes obscure and sometimes rise above the dome of Mont Blanc, render this scene of beauty still more interesting. Look also at the innumerable fish that are swimming in the clear waters, where we can distinguish every pebble that lies at the bottom. What a divine day! How happy and serene all nature appears!”

“Try to be at peace, my dearest Victor,” Elizabeth said. “I trust there is nothing left to trouble your mind. Please believe that even if my face doesn't radiate a visible joy, I am truly content in my heart. A small voice inside warns me not to place too much faith in the future we've envisioned, yet I refuse to listen to such dark premonitions. Notice how quickly we are traveling and how the clouds, drifting around the summit of Mont Blanc, make this beautiful landscape even more captivating. Watch the countless fish darting through the transparent water; it is so clear we can see every single stone on the lakebed. What a glorious day this is! The whole natural world seems so joyful and calm.”

Bathsheba continued walking. The horse was seen descending the hill, and the wearisome series had to be repeated in reverse order: Whitepits, Springmead, Cappel’s Piece, The Flats, Middle Field, Sheeplands, Sixteen Acres. She hoped Tall had had presence of mind enough to give the mare up to Gabriel, and return himself on foot. The rider neared them. It was Tall.

Bathsheba kept walking. The horse was now visible as it came down the hill, and the tedious procession had to be gone through again in reverse: Whitepits, Springmead, Cappel’s Piece, The Flats, Middle Field, Sheeplands, Sixteen Acres. She hoped Tall had been sensible enough to leave the mare with Gabriel and come back on foot himself. The rider drew closer. It was Tall.

Exceedingly red-eyed and grim, as if he had been up all night at a party which had taken anything but a convivial turn, Jerry Cruncher worried his breakfast rather than ate it, growling over it like any four-footed inmate of a menagerie. Towards nine o’clock he smoothed his ruffled aspect, and, presenting as respectable and business-like an exterior as he could overlay his natural self with, issued forth to the occupation of the day.

Jerry Cruncher, his eyes bloodshot and his face severe as though he'd spent the night at a gathering that had soured into something far from festive, merely picked at his breakfast instead of eating it, muttering over the food like some caged beast in a zoo. As nine o'clock drew near, he composed his disheveled demeanor, and, cloaking his true nature beneath the most decent and industrious front he could muster, stepped out to take up the day's work.

“And then I went into Griffin’s to hae my boots hobbed, and then I went to Riggs’s batty-cake shop, and asked ’em for a penneth of the cheapest and nicest stales, that were all but blue-mouldy, but not quite. And whilst I was chawing ’em down I walked on and seed a clock with a face as big as a baking trendle—”

And while I was chawing them down I walked on and saw a clock with a face as big as a baking-trindle—”
