It contains three language pairs: German→English, Romanian→English and English→French (Och and Ney, 2000a; Mihalcea and Pedersen, 2003) . We learn a joint byte pair encoding (BPE) for the source and the target language with 40k merge operation (Sennrich et al., 2016) . To convert from alignments between word pieces to alignments between words, we align a source word to a target word if an alignment link exists between any of its word pieces.

metadata.title: End-to-End Neural Word Alignment Outperforms GIZA++

text: We adopt the same experimental setup 5 as described by Zenkel et al. (2019) and used by Garg et al. (2019). It includes three language pairs: German→English, Romanian→English, and English→French (Och and Ney, 2000a; Mihalcea and Pedersen, 2003). We learn a joint byte pair encoding (BPE) model for the source and target languages using 40k merge operations (Sennrich et al., 2016). To transform alignments between word pieces into alignments between words, we align a source word with a target word whenever an alignment link exists between any of their word pieces.

This company of pilgrims resembled Chaucer's in this: that it had in it a sample of about all the upper occupations and professions the country could show, and a corresponding variety of costume. There were young men and old men, young women and old women, lively folk and grave folk. They rode upon mules and horses, and there was not a side-saddle in the party; for this specialty was to remain unknown in England for nine hundred years yet.

The assembly of travelers mirrored Chaucer’s pilgrims in one distinct way: it comprised a cross-section of nearly every prominent trade and profession the nation possessed, reflected in a diverse array of garments. The group featured a mix of the aged and the young, men and women alike, some brimming with energy while others remained stoic. They journeyed atop horses and mules, yet not a single side-saddle was among them, for that specific convenience would not be introduced to England for another nine hundred years.

Furthermore, we propose a method called Top+Clique (TC), which selects the top-N sentences and the semantically central non-top-N sentences to build the pseudo references. TC adopts .456 .417 .304 .415 .423 .311 SLRG .461 .423 .306 .419 .423 .310 SCI .409 .364 .261 .393 .383 .280 SCG .383 .344 .245 .373 .365 .265 Position-aware graphs SPSI . 478 .437 .319 .429 .435 .321 SPSG .472 .432 .313 .427 .432 .318 TC .490 .449 .329 .450 .454 .336 the following steps: (i) Label top-N sentences from each document as salient. (ii) With the remaining (non-top-N ) sentences, build a graph such that only "highly similar" sentences have an edge between them. (iii) Obtain the cliques from the graph and select the semantically central sentence (i.e. the sentence with highest average similarity with other sentences in the clique) from each clique as potentially salient sentences. (iv) For each potentially salient sentence, label it as salient if it is not highly similar to any top-N sentences. Based on preliminary experiments on 20 topics, we let N = 10 and the threshold value be 0.75 for "highly similar". Table 4 presents the graph-based methods' performance. Except for SC G , all other graph-based methods outperform baselines in Table 1 . Positionagnostic graph-based methods perform worse not only than the the position-aware ones, but even than the best method in Table 2 , which simply uses the full source documents as pseudo references. In addition, we find that the position-aware graph-based sentence extraction methods perform worse than simply extracting top sentences (Table 3 ). These findings indicate that the position bias remains the most effective heuristic in selecting salient information from news articles; when position information is unavailable (e.g. sentences in source documents are randomly shuffled), it might be better to use all sentences rather than selecting a subset of sentences from the source to build pseudo references.

text: Leaving her instructions with Gabriel and Maryann, that they were to see everything carefully locked up for the night, she went out of the house just at the close of a timely thunder-shower, which had refined the air, and daintily bathed the coat of the land, though all beneath was dry as ever. Freshness was exhaled in an essence from the varied contours of bank and hollow, as if the earth breathed maiden breath; and the pleased birds were hymning to the scene. Before her, among the clouds, there was a contrast in the shape of lairs of fierce light which showed themselves in the neighbourhood of a hidden sun, lingering on to the farthest north-west corner of the heavens that this midsummer season allowed.

After charging Gabriel and Maryann with the task of securing the house for the night, she stepped outdoors just as a brief thunderstorm concluded. The rain had cleansed the atmosphere and lightly dampened the earth's surface, though the ground underneath remained entirely dry. A crisp vitality radiated from every ridge and valley, as if the soil itself were exhaling a pure, sweet breath, and the joyful birds offered a chorus to the landscape. In front of her, the sky displayed a striking contrast: patches of violent light flickered around a veiled sun, which drifted toward the furthest northwestern edge of the horizon that the midsummer solstice would permit.

Since the young man's departure he had sent two postcards of greeting directly to the "Familie Britling," and one letter through the friendly intervention of Mr. Britling's American publisher. Once also he sent a message through a friend in Norway. The postcards simply recorded stages in the passage of a distraught pacifist across Holland to his enrolment. The letter by way of America came two months later. He had been converted into a combatant with extreme rapidity. He had been trained for three weeks, had spent a fortnight in hospital with a severe cold, and had then gone to Belgium as a transport driver--his father had been a horse-dealer and he was familiar with horses. "If anything happens to me," he wrote, "please send my violin at least very carefully to my mother." It was characteristic that he reported himself as very comfortably quartered in Courtrai with "very nice people." The niceness involved restraints. "Only never," he added, "do we talk about the war. It is better not to do so." He mentioned the violin also in the later communication through Norway. Therein he lamented the lost fleshpots of Courtrai. He had been in Posen, and now he was in the Carpathians, up to his knees in snow and "very uncomfortable...."

After the young man left, he mailed two quick greeting postcards directly addressed to the “Familie Britling,” and one letter passed along by Mr. Britling’s friendly American publisher. He also sent a short update once through a mutual acquaintance based in Norway. The postcards did little more than mark each leg of the frazzled pacifist’s journey across Holland as he made his way to enlist. The letter routed through America arrived two months after his departure. He’d been transitioned into a combat role extremely quickly. He’d completed three weeks of training, spent a full fortnight in the hospital with a severe cold, then been deployed to Belgium as a transport driver—his father had worked as a horse dealer, so he knew his way around horses very well. “If anything happens to me,” he wrote, “please at least send my violin to my mother, packed as carefully as you possibly can.” It was perfectly in keeping with his character that he reported staying in very comfortable lodgings in Courtrai with “really nice people.” That niceness came with unspoken constraints, though. “The one thing we never do,” he added, “is talk about the war. It’s better not to bring it up.” He mentioned the violin again in the later communication sent via Norway. In that note, he mourned the easy, pleasant comforts he’d left behind in Courtrai. He’d been stationed in Posen after that, and now he was in the Carpathians, wading through snow up to his knees and “very uncomfortable….”

He had been a lad of whom something was expected. Beyond this all had been chaos. That he would be successful in an original way, or that he would go to the dogs in an original way, seemed equally probable. The only absolute certainty about him was that he would not stand still in the circumstances amid which he was born.

He was a young man from whom much was anticipated, yet outside of that singular promise, everything remained a muddle. It appeared just as likely that he would achieve success through some unconventional path as it was that he would ruin himself in an equally singular way. The one undeniable truth was that he could never stay stagnant within the station of his birth.

Oliver felt such fear come over him when he recognised the place, that, for the instant, he forgot the agony of his wound, and thought only of flight. Flight! He could scarcely stand: and if he were in full possession of all the best powers of his slight and youthful frame, whither could he fly? He pushed against the garden-gate; it was unlocked, and swung open on its hinges. He tottered across the lawn; climbed the steps; knocked faintly at the door; and, his whole strength failing him, sunk down against one of the pillars of the little portico.

Oliver was seized with such dread when he recognized the place that, for a moment, he forgot the pain of his wound and thought only of escape. Escape! He could scarcely keep his feet: and even if he had been in full possession of all the best strength of his slight, youthful frame, where could he have fled? He pressed against the garden gate; it was unfastened and swung open on its hinges. He staggered across the lawn, mounted the steps, knocked faintly at the door, and, his strength wholly giving way, sank down against one of the pillars of the little portico.

Why did I not die? More miserable than man ever was before, why did I not sink into forgetfulness and rest? Death snatches away many blooming children, the only hopes of their doting parents; how many brides and youthful lovers have been one day in the bloom of health and hope, and the next a prey for worms and the decay of the tomb! Of what materials was I made that I could thus resist so many shocks, which, like the turning of the wheel, continually renewed the torture?

Why haven’t I died? More wretched than any person who has ever walked this earth, why didn’t I slip into total forgetfulness and finally rest? Death snatches countless thriving children from the world, the only hope their doting parents live for; how many brides and young lovers are bursting with health and bright possibility one day, only to become fodder for worms and the slow rot of the grave the next? What even am I made of, that I can withstand so many blows, each one spinning back around like a turning wheel to heap fresh torture on me again and again?

Results Figure 2 shows surprise and uncertainty reduction measures and human suspense judgements for an example story (text and further examples in Appendix C). We performed model selection using the correlations on the development set, which are given in Table 2 . We experimented with all the measures introduced in Section 3.1, computing sets of alternative sentences either us- ing generated continuations (Gen) or continuations sampled from the corpus (Cor), except for S Ely , which can be computed without alternatives. We compared the LSTM and GRU variants (see Section 4) and experimented with rollouts of up to three sentences. We tried L1 and L2 distance for the Ely measures, but only report L1, which always performed better.

metadata.title: Modelling Suspense in Short Stories as Uncertainty Reduction over Neural Representation

text: Results Figure 2 presents the surprise and uncertainty-reduction measures, along with human suspense judgments, for an example story (the text and additional examples are provided in Appendix C). We conducted model selection on the basis of correlations in the development set, reported in Table 2. We tested all measures introduced in Section 3.1, computing sets of alternative sentences either by using generated continuations (Gen) or continuations sampled from the corpus (Cor), except for S_Ely, which can be computed without alternatives. We compared the LSTM and GRU variants (see Section 4) and experimented with rollouts of up to three sentences. For the Ely measures, we tried both L1 and L2 distance, but report only L1, which consistently performed better.

“I fancy I heard something outside the shed,” said Christian. “I wish troubles would come in the daytime, for then a man could show his courage, and hardly beg for mercy of the most broomstick old woman he should see, if he was a brave man, and able to run out of her sight!”

“I could’ve sworn I heard a faint stir just outside the lean-to,” said Tobin. “I wish all troubles showed up in the daylight, when a man can stand firm and show his mettle, and would never have to beg mercy even of the scruffiest broom-brandishing old crone he stumbled across, so long as he was bold enough, and quick enough to dart out of her line of sight.”

A consciousness of a vast impassivity in all which lay around him took possession even of Yeobright in his wild walk towards Alderworth. He had once before felt in his own person this overpowering of the fervid by the inanimate; but then it had tended to enervate a passion far sweeter than that which at present pervaded him. It was once when he stood parting from Eustacia in the moist still levels beyond the hills.

A sense of the profound neutrality in the surrounding world gripped Julian during his frantic journey toward Blackwood. He had experienced this crushing of human heat by the lifeless material of the earth once before, though in that earlier instance, the sensation had merely dampened a sentiment far gentler than the one now consuming him. It had occurred as he stood taking leave of Elara within the damp, hushed lowlands that lay beyond the crest of the hills.

He had debated between the Berkeley Free Library and the Oakland Free Library, and decided upon the latter because Ruth lived in Oakland. Who could tell?—a library was a most likely place for her, and he might see her there. He did not know the way of libraries, and he wandered through endless rows of fiction, till the delicate-featured French-looking girl who seemed in charge, told him that the reference department was upstairs. He did not know enough to ask the man at the desk, and began his adventures in the philosophy alcove. He had heard of book philosophy, but had not imagined there had been so much written about it. The high, bulging shelves of heavy tomes humbled him and at the same time stimulated him. Here was work for the vigor of his brain. He found books on trigonometry in the mathematics section, and ran the pages, and stared at the meaningless formulas and figures. He could read English, but he saw there an alien speech. Norman and Arthur knew that speech. He had heard them talking it. And they were her brothers. He left the alcove in despair. From every side the books seemed to press upon him and crush him.

He had weighed the merits of the Berkeley and Oakland public libraries, ultimately choosing the latter because it was Ruth’s town. It seemed a plausible place to encounter her, and the hope of a chance meeting drew him there. Unfamiliar with the layout of such buildings, he drifted aimlessly through the fiction stacks until a slender, sharp-featured woman—who reminded him of a French girl—informed him that the reference materials were located on the upper floor. Too inexperienced to seek guidance from the desk clerk, he started his journey in the philosophy section. While he had heard of the subject, he never imagined so many words had been devoted to it. The massive, crowded shelves filled him with a sense of inadequacy, yet they also sparked a fierce intellectual curiosity. Here was a task that would test his mental strength. In the math department, he leafed through trigonometry textbooks, gazing at equations that looked like a foreign code. He could read his own language, but this was a different dialect entirely—one that Norman and Arthur mastered with ease. They were her brothers, and they belonged here. He fled the alcove in a fit of hopelessness, feeling the weight of the surrounding volumes pressing in on him from every direction.

The Britling child hated Buster Brown. He regarded Buster Brown as a totally unnecessary infant. He detested the way he wore his hair and the peculiar cut of his knickerbockers and--him. He thought Buster Brown the one drop of paraffin in the otherwise delicious feast of the Sunday Supplement. But he was a diplomatic child.

The Thorne boy loathed Sonny Skyler. He considered the perky comic-strip character an entirely unnecessary little upstart. He despised the blunt, too-neat cut of his hair, the stiff, old-fashioned cut of his knickerbockers, and—him, full stop. He thought Sonny Skyler was the single drop of turpentine spoiling the otherwise lovely spread of the Sunday funnies section. But he was a tactful, shrewd child.

He uttered these words in a feeble voice, being very faint and weak; but they were overheard at once. The curtain at the bed’s head was hastily drawn back, and a motherly old lady, very neatly and precisely dressed, rose as she undrew it, from an arm-chair close by, in which she had been sitting at needle-work.

He got the words out in a thin, reedy voice, so drained and lightheaded he could barely push the sounds past his lips, but someone caught every syllable immediately. The curtain strung across the head of the bed was yanked back in a rush, and a gentle, motherly older woman, dressed with neat, precise care, rose as she pulled it to the side – she’d been settled in the armchair right beside the bed, working on a sewing project.

Never was ingenuity exercised so sorely as the traveller here exercised hers. Every conceivable aid, method, stratagem, mechanism, by which these last desperate eight hundred yards could be overpassed by a human being unperceived, was revolved in her busy brain, and dismissed as impracticable. She thought of sticks, wheels, crawling—she even thought of rolling. But the exertion demanded by either of these latter two was greater than to walk erect. The faculty of contrivance was worn out. Hopelessness had come at last.

No wanderer had ever strained their cleverness as thin as Elowen did that day. Every plausible trick, tool, ruse, or makeshift she could imagine for slipping across those final, desperate eight hundred yards without being spotted cycled through her racing mind, only to be discarded as unworkable. She considered propping herself on scavenged branches, rigging a low rolling frame, creeping on hands and knees—she even entertained the thought of tucking into a tight ball and rolling the whole stretch. But both crawling and rolling would demand far more physical exertion than simply striding upright, conspicuous as that would be. Every last spark of resourcefulness had burned down to ash. Hopelessness settled over her at last, cold and unyielding.

As for Bathsheba, she was not deceived into the belief that Farmer Boldwood had walked by on business or in idleness. She collected the probabilities of the case, and concluded that she was herself responsible for Boldwood’s appearance there. It troubled her much to see what a great flame a little wildfire was likely to kindle. Bathsheba was no schemer for marriage, nor was she deliberately a trifler with the affections of men, and a censor’s experience on seeing an actual flirt after observing her would have been a feeling of surprise that Bathsheba could be so different from such a one, and yet so like what a flirt is supposed to be.

Bathsheba, for her part, was under no illusion that Farmer Boldwood had passed by on some errand or merely out of idle wandering. She weighed the likelihoods and understood that his presence there was her own doing. It disturbed her deeply to realize how great a blaze a small spark might ignite. Bathsheba was not one to scheme for a husband, nor did she consciously play with men’s hearts, and any seasoned critic who watched a true coquette at work and then observed her would have been astonished to find Bathsheba so unlike that figure, yet so closely resembling what a flirt is imagined to be.

Master Bates backed this advice with sundry moral admonitions of his own: which, being exhausted, he and his friend Mr. Dawkins launched into a glowing description of the numerous pleasures incidental to the life they led, interspersed with a variety of hints to Oliver that the best thing he could do, would be to secure Fagin’s favour without more delay, by the means which they themselves had employed to gain it.

Young Bates supported these suggestions with several moralizing lectures of his own. Once he had finished, he and his companion, Mr. Dawkins, began a vivid account of the many thrills inherent to their way of life. They peppered this description with various hints to Oliver that his smartest move would be to win Fagin’s approval without further hesitation, using the same methods they had employed to earn it themselves.

The door was flung back till it kicked the wall and trembled from top to bottom with the blow. Mr. Oak appeared in the entry with a steaming face, hay-bands wound about his ankles to keep out the snow, a leather strap round his waist outside the smock-frock, and looking altogether an epitome of the world’s health and vigour. Four lambs hung in various embarrassing attitudes over his shoulders, and the dog George, whom Gabriel had contrived to fetch from Norcombe, stalked solemnly behind.

The door slammed open, shuddering from frame to base as it struck the wall. Mr. Oak stood in the doorway, his face flushed with heat, twists of hay bound around his ankles to fend off the snow, a leather belt cinched over his smock, the very picture of robust vitality. Four lambs dangled across his shoulders in a tangle of awkward poses, and the dog George, whom Gabriel had managed to bring from Norcombe, paced gravely at his heels.

We entered the gloomy prison chamber and beheld Justine sitting on some straw at the farther end; her hands were manacled, and her head rested on her knees. She rose on seeing us enter, and when we were left alone with her, she threw herself at the feet of Elizabeth, weeping bitterly. My cousin wept also.

We stepped into the shadowed cell and found Celeste at its far edge, seated upon a heap of straw; iron cuffs bound her wrists, and her brow lay heavy upon her knees. At the sight of our entrance she stood, and the moment the guards withdrew and left us three alone, she cast herself at Helena’s feet in a storm of tears. My cousin wept with her.

He stopped as if in doubt whether this waiting vehicle could have anything to do with the flight of his wife or not. The sight of Yeobright at once banished Wildeve’s sober feelings, who saw him again as the deadly rival from whom Eustacia was to be kept at all hazards. Hence Wildeve did not speak, in the hope that Clym would pass by without particular inquiry.

Silas hesitated, struck by the suspicion that this stationary coach might be the very instrument of his wife’s disappearance. The appearance of Silas instantly dispelled Julian’s calm, as he once again perceived the man as the mortal adversary from whom Elara must be sequestered by any means. Julian, therefore, maintained a guarded silence, praying that Silas would pass along the road without turning his attention toward the shadows.

“God! We are animals! Brute-beasts!” Martin muttered aloud, as he watched the progress of the fight. It was to him, with his splendid power of vision, like gazing into a kinetoscope. He was both onlooker and participant. His long months of culture and refinement shuddered at the sight; then the present was blotted out of his consciousness and the ghosts of the past possessed him, and he was Martin Eden, just returned from sea and fighting Cheese-Face on the Eighth Street Bridge. He suffered and toiled and sweated and bled, and exulted when his naked knuckles smashed home.

"Good God, we are nothing but beasts! Savage animals!" Martin whispered under his breath while tracking the brawl's every move. To his keen, perceptive eyes, the violence unfolded like a flickering kinetoscope. He found himself trapped between being a witness and a combatant. His recent months of intellectual growth and social polish recoiled from the spectacle, but then the present moment dissolved. The shadows of his history took hold of him, and he was once again the Martin Eden of old, back from a long voyage and trading blows with Cheese-Face on the Eighth Street Bridge. He felt the agony, the labor, the sweat, and the gore, experiencing a primal triumph each time his bare knuckles struck home.

It was with this extraordinary procession trooping at his and Maria’s heels into a confectioner’s in quest of the biggest candy-cane ever made, that he encountered Ruth and her mother. Mrs. Morse was shocked. Even Ruth was hurt, for she had some regard for appearances, and her lover, cheek by jowl with Maria, at the head of that army of Portuguese ragamuffins, was not a pretty sight. But it was not that which hurt so much as what she took to be his lack of pride and self-respect. Further, and keenest of all, she read into the incident the impossibility of his living down his working-class origin. There was stigma enough in the fact of it, but shamelessly to flaunt it in the face of the world—her world—was going too far. Though her engagement to Martin had been kept secret, their long intimacy had not been unproductive of gossip; and in the shop, glancing covertly at her lover and his following, had been several of her acquaintances. She lacked the easy largeness of Martin and could not rise superior to her environment. She had been hurt to the quick, and her sensitive nature was quivering with the shame of it. So it was, when Martin arrived later in the day, that he kept her present in his breast-pocket, deferring the giving of it to a more propitious occasion. Ruth in tears—passionate, angry tears—was a revelation to him. The spectacle of her suffering convinced him that he had been a brute, yet in the soul of him he could not see how nor why. It never entered his head to be ashamed of those he knew, and to take the Silvas out to a Christmas treat could in no way, so it seemed to him, show lack of consideration for Ruth. On the other hand, he did see Ruth’s point of view, after she had explained it; and he looked upon it as a feminine weakness, such as afflicted all women and the best of women.

He was at the head of this bizarre, chattering procession, with Maria trotting right beside him, as the whole group filed into a confectioner’s shop in search of the largest candy cane ever made, when he ran straight into Ruth and her mother. Mrs. Morse was visibly stunned. Even Ruth felt wounded: she cared a great deal about keeping up proper appearances, and seeing her lover pressed shoulder to shoulder with Maria, leading that ragtag army of scruffy Portuguese street kids, was far from a flattering sight. But what hurt far more than the spectacle itself was what she took as his complete lack of pride and self-respect. Worse, sharpest of all her grievances, she read the entire incident as proof that he would never outrun the stain of his working-class origins. The mere fact of those roots carried enough stigma on its own, but to shamelessly flaunt them in full view of polite society—her society—was crossing a line she could not forgive. Though her engagement to Martin had been kept private, their long, close familiarity had already sparked no small amount of gossip; and as she stood in the shop, stealing covert glances at her lover and his rowdy following, she spotted several of her own acquaintances watching the scene unfold. She did not possess Martin’s easy, unselfconscious generosity of spirit, and could not rise above the unspoken judgments of her social circle. The shame cut her to the quick, her sensitive nature thrumming with the humiliation of the moment. That was why, when Martin stopped by her home later that same day, he tucked the gift he’d brought for her deep in his breast pocket, putting off giving it to her until a more favorable moment arose. The sight of Ruth in tears—hot, passionate, angry tears—was a total revelation to him. Her obvious suffering convinced him he had behaved like a thoughtless brute, even if deep in his soul he could not grasp exactly what he had done wrong, or why it mattered so much. It never once crossed his mind to feel ashamed of the people he cared for, and treating the Silva children to a special Christmas outing seemed to him in no way a sign of disregard for Ruth. All the same, once Ruth laid out her perspective for him, he did understand where she was coming from; he wrote the whole reaction off as a silly feminine foible, the kind that afflicted every woman, even the very best of them.

In a few minutes after, I heard the creaking of my door, as if some one endeavoured to open it softly. I trembled from head to foot; I felt a presentiment of who it was and wished to rouse one of the peasants who dwelt in a cottage not far from mine; but I was overcome by the sensation of helplessness, so often felt in frightful dreams, when you in vain endeavour to fly from an impending danger, and was rooted to the spot.

A couple of minutes later, I caught the faint creak of my door, as though someone was trying to push it open as quietly as possible. I shook uncontrollably from head to toe; I knew in my bones who it was, and I wanted to call out to rouse one of the peasants living in the small cottage just a short distance from my home. But I was swallowed up by that same helpless, heavy feeling you get in terrifying dreams, when you fight with everything you have to run from a looming danger and can’t make your body cooperate no matter how hard you try. I was rooted straight to the spot.

Sing on, sweet thrush, upon the leafless bough,
Sing on, sweet bird, I listen to thy strain,
See aged Winter, ’mid his surly reign,
At thy blythe carol, clears his furrowed brow.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Warble, kind thrush, upon the naked bough,
Warble, dear bird; I harken to your strain;
See ancient Winter, in his sullen reign,
At your bright song, unbend his wrinkled brow.

The rise of encoder-decoder models (Cho et al., 2014; Sutskever et al., 2014) as well as the attention (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Vaswani et al., 2017) and copy mechanisms (Gu et al., 2016; Gulcehre et al., 2016) has dramatically changed the landscape, and now one can perform the task relatively easily with an encoder-decoder model such as Transformer provided that a sufficient amount of data is available. For example, Li et al. (2018) introduce a deep reinforcement learning framework for paraphrasing, consisting of a generator and an evaluator. Yin et al. (2019) formalize the problem of text edit as learning and utilization of edit representations and propose an encoder-decoder model for the task. Zhao et al. (2018) integrate paraphrasing rules with the Transformer model for text simplification. Zhao et al. (2019) proposes a method for English grammar correction using a Transformer and copy mechanism.

metadata.title: Fact-based Text Editing

text: The advent of encoder-decoder architectures (Cho et al., 2014; Sutskever et al., 2014), together with attention mechanisms (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Vaswani et al., 2017) and copy mechanisms (Gu et al., 2016; Gulcehre et al., 2016), has substantially reshaped the field, such that the task can now be addressed relatively straightforwardly with an encoder-decoder model such as Transformer, assuming sufficient training data are available. For instance, Li et al. (2018) propose a deep reinforcement learning framework for paraphrasing that comprises a generator and an evaluator. Yin et al. (2019) cast text editing as the learning and use of edit representations and present an encoder-decoder model for this purpose. Zhao et al. (2018) combine paraphrasing rules with the Transformer model for text simplification. Zhao et al. (2019) introduce a method for English grammar correction based on a Transformer and a copy mechanism.

But Martin went no further into the matter. He led the conversation off into other channels. They had lunch in the restaurant, where he ordered wine and expensive delicacies and afterward he danced with her and with no one but her, till she was tired. He was a good dancer, and she whirled around and around with him in a heaven of delight, her head against his shoulder, wishing that it could last forever. Later in the afternoon they strayed off among the trees, where, in the good old fashion, she sat down while he sprawled on his back, his head in her lap. He lay and dozed, while she fondled his hair, looked down on his closed eyes, and loved him without reserve. Looking up suddenly, he read the tender advertisement in her face. Her eyes fluttered down, then they opened and looked into his with soft defiance.

Julian allowed the subject to drift away, steering their talk into lighter currents. They dined in the hotel’s hall, where he indulged in vintage wines and rare delicacies; afterward, he claimed her for every dance, ignoring the room until her strength began to fail. He moved with an effortless grace, and she spun in a dizzying paradise, her forehead resting against his jacket, wishing the world might stop spinning right there. Later, they wandered deep into the grove where, in the timeless way of lovers, she sat upon the moss and he stretched out, his head heavy in her lap. He dozed fitfully while she ran her fingers through his hair, watching his quiet face and loving him with a total, unshielded heart. When he opened his eyes suddenly, he saw the naked devotion written in her gaze. Her lashes brushed her cheeks for a second, then her eyes met his again with a gentle, stubborn pride.

Haggard Saint Antoine had had only one exultant week, in which to soften his modicum of hard and bitter bread to such extent as he could, with the relish of fraternal embraces and congratulations, when Madame Defarge sat at her counter, as usual, presiding over the customers. Madame Defarge wore no rose in her head, for the great brotherhood of Spies had become, even in one short week, extremely chary of trusting themselves to the saint’s mercies. The lamps across his streets had a portentously elastic swing with them.

Haggard Saint Antoine had known only a single triumphant week to temper his meager, bitter crust with the savor of brotherly embraces and shared rejoicing, when Madame Defarge was once more at her counter, presiding over her patrons as she always did. She wore no rose in her hair, for in that brief span the vast fraternity of Spies had grown exceedingly wary of entrusting themselves to the saint’s tender mercies. The streetlamps above his avenues swayed with a dreadfully elastic motion.

It was at this time, at the lowest ebb, that the smooth-running editorial machine broke down. A cog must have slipped or an oil-cup run dry, for the postman brought him one morning a short, thin envelope. Martin glanced at the upper left-hand corner and read the name and address of the _Transcontinental Monthly_. His heart gave a great leap, and he suddenly felt faint, the sinking feeling accompanied by a strange trembling of the knees. He staggered into his room and sat down on the bed, the envelope still unopened, and in that moment came understanding to him how people suddenly fall dead upon receipt of extraordinarily good news.

It was then, at the very bottom of the trough, that the smooth-working editorial machine finally failed. Some gear must have slipped, or an oil cup must have gone dry, for one morning the postman handed him a short, narrow envelope. Martin looked at the upper left corner and saw the name and address of the _Transcontinental Monthly_. His heart leaped violently, and at once he felt faint, that dropping sensation accompanied by an odd trembling in his knees. He made his way unsteadily to his room and sat down on the bed, the envelope still unopened, and in that instant he understood how people can suddenly die on receiving news that is extraordinarily good.

Of course, it would have been best, all round, for Merlin to waive etiquette and quit and call it half a day, since he would never be able to start that water, for he was a true magician of the time; which is to say, the big miracles, the ones that gave him his reputation, always had the luck to be performed when nobody but Merlin was present; he couldn't start this well with all this crowd around to see; a crowd was as bad for a magician's miracle in that day as it was for a spiritualist's miracle in mine; there was sure to be some skeptic on hand to turn up the gas at the crucial moment and spoil everything. But I did not want Merlin to retire from the job until I was ready to take hold of it effectively myself; and I could not do that until I got my things from Camelot, and that would take two or three days.

It would have been far more sensible, certainly, for Valerius to set aside his dignity and abandon the field at once, for he was never going to coax a drop from that dry earth. He was a quintessential sorcerer of his era; which is to say, those legendary feats that secured his fame were invariably performed when no one but Valerius was there to witness them. He could hardly command the spring to flow before such a vast assembly; a throng of spectators was as fatal to a medieval wonder as a skeptic is to a modern séance. There is always some doubter present, ready to strike a match at the critical moment and expose the fraud. Yet, I had no wish for Valerius to retire until I was prepared to seize the stage with proper effect myself. That remained impossible until my apparatus arrived from the Citadel, a journey that would require at least two or three days.

Those dreadful walls of Newgate, which have hidden so much misery and such unspeakable anguish, not only from the eyes, but, too often, and too long, from the thoughts, of men, never held so dread a spectacle as that. The few who lingered as they passed, and wondered what the man was doing who was to be hanged tomorrow, would have slept but ill that night, if they could have seen him.

The grim walls of Newgate, which had long concealed so much suffering and silent agony from the eyes and, quite often, the consciences of the world, had never contained a sight as horrifying as this. The few people who paused as they walked by, curious about the man scheduled for execution the following morning, would have found little rest that night had they actually been able to see him.

“Show myself a man of spirit?... Ah, well! let me take the name of drunkard humbly—let me be a man of contrite knees—let it be! I know that I always do say ‘Please God’ afore I do anything, from my getting up to my going down of the same, and I be willing to take as much disgrace as there is in that holy act. Hah, yes!... But not a man of spirit? Have I ever allowed the toe of pride to be lifted against my hinder parts without groaning manfully that I question the right to do so? I inquire that query boldly?”

“Prove myself a man of mettle?... Ah, very well! I will accept the title of drunkard meekly—let me be a man bowed on penitent knees—so be it! I am aware that I utter ‘God willing’ before every deed, from my rising to my resting, and I am ready to bear whatever shame such a sacred habit may carry. Hah, indeed!... Yet not a man of spirit? Have I ever permitted pride’s boot to be aimed at my backside without stoutly groaning that I dispute the privilege to do so? I put that question boldly?”

“But it is very strange--now, at least, is it not very strange”--said Defarge, rather pleading with his wife to induce her to admit it, “that, after all our sympathy for Monsieur her father, and herself, her husband’s name should be proscribed under your hand at this moment, by the side of that infernal dog’s who has just left us?”

“Yet isn’t it passing odd—at this hour, above all, isn’t it passing odd,” said Defarge, almost coaxing his wife to concede the point, “that after all our pity for Monsieur her father, and for her too, the name of her husband should lie condemned by your own script just now, alongside that fiendish cur’s who only this moment departed?”

In the morning a regiment of cavalry had left the town, and a sergeant and his party had been beating up for recruits through the four streets. As the end of the day drew on, and he found himself not hired, Gabriel almost wished that he had joined them, and gone off to serve his country. Weary of standing in the market-place, and not much minding the kind of work he turned his hand to, he decided to offer himself in some other capacity than that of bailiff.

By morning a troop of horse had ridden out of the town, and a sergeant with his men had gone searching the four streets for recruits. Toward evening, as the day wore down, Gabriel, still without a place, came near to wishing he had gone with them and taken the King’s shilling. Tired of lingering in the market square, and caring little what sort of labor he might set himself to, he resolved to present himself in some other role than that of a bailiff.

At 6 o’clock the waiter brought her dinner and carried away the typewritten bill of fare. When Sarah ate she set aside, with a sigh, the dish of dandelions with its crowning ovarious accompaniment. As this dark mass had been transformed from a bright and love-indorsed flower to be an ignominious vegetable, so had her summer hopes wilted and perished. Love may, as Shakespeare said, feed on itself: but Sarah could not bring herself to eat the dandelions that had graced, as ornaments, the first spiritual banquet of her heart’s true affection.

As the clock struck six, the waiter delivered her evening meal and retrieved the typed menu. Sarah began to eat, but with a heavy sigh, she pushed aside the plate of dandelion greens and its crowning garnish of egg. Just as these once-vibrant blossoms, symbols of romance, had been reduced to a lowly vegetable, the hopes she had nurtured all summer had similarly withered and died. Though poets claim that love can sustain itself, Sarah could not bring herself to consume the very flowers that had decorated the first sacred feast of her heart’s true devotion.

Charley wiped his own eyes, which were rather blurred with moisture, and stepped closer to the casement. “Mr. Venn is asking Christian Cantle to sing,” he replied, “and Christian is moving about in his chair as if he were much frightened at the question, and his father has struck up a stave instead of him.”

Charley brushed a hand across his damp lashes, the pane before him swimming faintly, and drew nearer to the window. “It’s Mr. Venn,” he said, “putting the same request to Christian Cantle, and Christian is shifting in his seat like a man half-scared out of his wits, while his father has broken into a song in his place.”

On a night in mid-July, one thousand seven hundred and eighty-nine, Mr. Lorry came in late, from Tellson’s, and sat himself down by Lucie and her husband in the dark window. It was a hot, wild night, and they were all three reminded of the old Sunday night when they had looked at the lightning from the same place.

On a night in mid-July, seventeen hundred and eighty-nine, Mr. Lorry came home late from Tellson’s and seated himself with Lucie and her husband by the darkened window. It was a hot, restless night, and the three of them were all reminded of that old Sunday evening when they had watched the lightning from the same spot.

Venn was loth to depart, for all on earth that interested him lay under this roof. As nobody in the house had any more sleep that night, except the two who slept for ever, there was no reason why he should not remain. So he retired into the niche of the fireplace where he had used to sit, and there he continued, watching the steam from the double row of banknotes as they waved backwards and forwards in the draught of the chimney till their flaccidity was changed to dry crispness throughout. Then the woman came and unpinned them, and, folding them together, carried the handful upstairs. Presently the doctor appeared from above with the look of a man who could do no more, and, pulling on his gloves, went out of the house, the trotting of his horse soon dying away upon the road.

Venn was reluctant to go, for everything on earth that stirred his interest was under that roof. Since no one in the house would sleep again that night, save the two who slept forever, there was no reason he should not stay. So he withdrew to the niche in the fireplace where he had once sat, and remained there, watching the steam rise from the double row of banknotes as they swayed to and fro in the chimney draft, until their limp softness had given way entirely to a dry, crisp stiffness. Then the woman came to unpin them, and, gathering them together, carried the little stack upstairs. Soon after, the doctor came down from above with the air of a man who had done all he could, and, drawing on his gloves, left the house, the quick sound of his horse’s trotting soon fading along the road.

The girl lay still, as if in a faint. Rudolf looked around the room excitedly for a barrel. People must be rolled upon a barrel who—no, no; that was for drowned persons. He began to fan her with his hat. That was successful, for he struck her nose with the brim of his derby and she opened her eyes. And then the young man saw that hers, indeed, was the one missing face from his heart’s gallery of intimate portraits. The frank, grey eyes, the little nose, turning pertly outward; the chestnut hair, curling like the tendrils of a pea vine, seemed the right end and reward of all his wonderful adventures. But the face was wofully thin and pale.

The girl lay motionless, as though in a swoon. Emil cast an eager glance around the room for a barrel. People who had—no, no; that was for those drawn from water. He began to waft her with his hat. This proved effective, for he tapped her nose with the edge of his derby and she opened her eyes. Then the young man perceived that it was indeed her face, the one absent from the gallery of his heart’s most secret portraits. The open grey eyes, the small nose with its saucy outward turn; the chestnut hair, curling like the tendrils of a pea vine, seemed the proper conclusion and reward of all his marvelous adventures. Yet the face was terribly thin and pale.

The imagination of the observer clung by preference to that vanished, solitary figure, as to something more interesting, more important, more likely to have a history worth knowing than these newcomers, and unconsciously regarded them as intruders. But they remained, and established themselves; and the lonely person who hitherto had been queen of the solitude did not at present seem likely to return.

The only clear meaning in this sky-backed pantomime of shadows was that the woman had no connection with the figures that had replaced her, was carefully keeping out of their way, and had come there for some purpose other than theirs. The observer’s imagination clung by preference to that vanished, solitary form, as though it were something more interesting, more important, more likely to have a history worth knowing than these newcomers, and it unconsciously treated them as intruders. But they stayed, and settled in; and the lonely figure who had until then reigned over the solitude did not seem, for the moment, likely to return.

Mrs. Sowerberry, who had a good deal of taste in the undertaking way, was much struck by the novelty of this idea; but, as it would have been compromising her dignity to have said so, under existing circumstances, she merely inquired, with much sharpness, why such an obvious suggestion had not presented itself to her husband’s mind before? Mr. Sowerberry rightly construed this, as an acquiescence in his proposition; it was speedily determined, therefore, that Oliver should be at once initiated into the mysteries of the trade; and, with this view, that he should accompany his master on the very next occasion of his services being required.

Mrs. Sowerberry, who had a sharp, practiced knack for all the ins and outs of the undertaking trade, was genuinely impressed by how novel and clever this idea was. But admitting that outright in the moment would have undermined her carefully maintained dignity, so she gave no outward sign of her approval. Instead, she snapped the question sharply, as if chiding him for a careless oversight: why had such an obvious, common-sense suggestion never crossed her husband’s mind any earlier? Mr. Sowerberry correctly read this sharp retort as her full acquiescence to his proposal. They settled the matter quickly: Oliver would be immediately initiated into all the closely guarded nuances of the trade, and to that end, he would accompany his master on the very next job that required his employer’s services.

He brought his great discovery to Ruth, sharing with her all his joy and wonder in it. But she did not seem to be so enthusiastic over it. She tacitly accepted it and, in a way, seemed aware of it from her own studies. It did not stir her deeply, as it did him, and he would have been surprised had he not reasoned it out that it was not new and fresh to her as it was to him. Arthur and Norman, he found, believed in evolution and had read Spencer, though it did not seem to have made any vital impression upon them, while the young fellow with the glasses and the mop of hair, Will Olney, sneered disagreeably at Spencer and repeated the epigram, “There is no god but the Unknowable, and Herbert Spencer is his prophet.”

He presented his monumental revelation to Ruth, attempting to convey the full measure of his awe and delight. However, she did not share his fervor. She accepted his words with a quiet reserve, appearing to have already encountered such concepts during her own schooling. The ideas failed to move her as intensely as they had moved him; he might have been surprised by this had he not reasoned that the material was simply familiar to her, while to him it was a startling new light. He found that Arthur and Norman also accepted evolution and had read Spencer, yet the work seemed to have made no significant impact on them. Meanwhile, Will Olney—the bespectacled youth with the unruly hair—dismissed Spencer with a caustic sneer, repeating the mocking refrain: “There is no god but the Unknowable, and Herbert Spencer is his prophet.”

There was some amiable sparring between the worthy man and Mr. Britling about bringing Mr. Direck to church on Sunday morning. "He's terribly Lax," said Mr. Dimple to Mr. Direck, smiling radiantly. "Terribly Lax. But then nowadays Everybody _is_ so Lax. And he's very Good to my Coal Club; I don't know what we should do without him. So I just admonish him. And if he doesn't go to church, well, anyhow he doesn't go anywhere else. He may be a poor churchman, but anyhow he's not a dissenter...."

There was some friendly banter between the good man and Mr. Britling over bringing Mr. Direck to church on Sunday morning. “He’s terribly lax,” Mr. Dimple said to Mr. Direck, beaming. “Terribly lax. But then these days everybody is so lax. And he’s very good to my Coal Club; I don’t know what we should do without him. So I just keep after him a little. And if he doesn’t go to church, well, at any rate he doesn’t go anywhere else. He may be a poor churchman, but at least he isn’t a dissenter....”

It sounded good! In this atmosphere of telephones and lightning communication with distant regions, I was breathing the breath of life again after long suffocation. I realized, then, what a creepy, dull, inanimate horror this land had been to me all these years, and how I had been in such a stifled condition of mind as to have grown used to it almost beyond the power to notice it.

It was a welcome sound! Amidst the ring of telephones and the rush of instant communication with the world, I was finally drawing breath after a long period of choking. I realized then what a ghostly, stagnant, and lifeless nightmare this land had been to me, and how my mind had become so suppressed that I had grown used to the gloom almost beyond the point of noticing it.

Something being expected of him, he had not been at home many days before a great curiosity as to why he stayed on so long began to arise in the heath. The natural term of a holiday had passed, yet he still remained. On the Sunday morning following the week of Thomasin’s marriage a discussion on this subject was in progress at a hair-cutting before Fairway’s house. Here the local barbering was always done at this hour on this day, to be followed by the great Sunday wash of the inhabitants at noon, which in its turn was followed by the great Sunday dressing an hour later. On Egdon Heath Sunday proper did not begin till dinner-time, and even then it was a somewhat battered specimen of the day.

Because something was evidently expected of him, he had not been home many days before a strong curiosity began to spread through the heath about why he was staying so long. The normal length of a holiday had already gone by, yet he still remained. On the Sunday morning after the week of Thomasin’s wedding, the matter was being discussed at a haircutting in front of Fairway’s house. At that hour and on that day, the local barbering was always carried out here, to be followed at noon by the great Sunday washing of the inhabitants, and then an hour later by the great Sunday dressing. On Egdon Heath, Sunday in the full sense did not begin until dinner-time, and even then it was only a rather battered version of the day.

If Sydney Carton ever shone anywhere, he certainly never shone in the house of Doctor Manette. He had been there often, during a whole year, and had always been the same moody and morose lounger there. When he cared to talk, he talked well; but, the cloud of caring for nothing, which overshadowed him with such a fatal darkness, was very rarely pierced by the light within him.

Sydney Carton never once stood out in the Manette household, if he ever stood out anywhere. Over the course of an entire year, he had visited repeatedly, yet he remained the same sullen, brooding fixture. On the rare occasions he felt like speaking, his words carried weight; but the inner light that might have broken through was almost always smothered by the fatal shadow of his indifference to everything.

An important feature of the dependency analysis in UD is that content words are considered the principal components of dependency relations. Within this framework, function words are generally dependents of the content word they relate to most closely. The primacy of content words brings out cross-linguistic parallels that would be harder to detect with other annotation frameworks since function words are highly variable across languages. Importantly, dependency paths between content words do not generally contain function words. As a result, by comparing paths across languages, differences in the surface realization are often masked, and argument structure and linkage differences emphasized.

metadata.title: Fine-Grained Analysis of Cross-Linguistic Syntactic Divergences

text: A core design property of Universal Dependencies (UD) dependency annotation schemas is the designation of content words as the head units of all dependency relations. Under this framework, function words are uniformly marked as dependents of the content word with which they share the closest syntactic and semantic association. This prioritization of content words surfaces cross-linguistic structural parallels that are far less detectable in alternative annotation schemes, as function word inventories and syntactic behavior exhibit extreme typological variability across languages. Critically, dependency paths connecting pairs of content words almost never include intervening function word nodes as a result of this design choice. Accordingly, cross-linguistic comparison of these content-word dependency paths naturally obscures variation in surface morphosyntactic realization, while highlighting divergent properties of argument structure and inter-constituent linkage across languages.

Monks cast a look of hate, which, even then, he could not dissemble, at the astonished boy, and sat down near the door. Mr. Brownlow, who had papers in his hand, walked to a table near which Rose and Oliver were seated.

Finally, when nine o’clock arrived and they had begun to assume the night's business was over, Mr. Losberne and Mr. Grimwig entered the room. They were followed by Mr. Brownlow and a man whose appearance made Oliver nearly cry out in shock. They informed him that this was his brother—the same individual he had encountered in the market town and later seen peering through his bedroom window with Fagin. Monks directed a look of pure, undisguised malice at the stunned boy before taking a seat near the door. Mr. Brownlow, clutching a handful of documents, walked over to a table where Rose and Oliver were sitting.

He could see to read, now, and he was unaware of the pain of the action. It was the _White Mouse_ that was offering him forty dollars, and the story was “The Whirlpool,” another of his early horror stories. He read the letter through again and again. The editor told him plainly that he had not handled the idea properly, but that it was the idea they were buying because it was original. If they could cut the story down one-third, they would take it and send him forty dollars on receipt of his answer.

The light had grown sufficient for Arthur to make out the script, and the strain of it no longer registered against his exhaustion. *The Silver Shroud* was proposing thirty-five dollars for "The Maelstrom," a piece from his first forays into the gothic. He traced the sentences once more, then again. The correspondent admitted frankly that the prose lacked polish, yet the core premise was striking enough to warrant a sale. If he would permit them to excise a third of the text, the check would be mailed as soon as his confirmation reached their desk.

“There she died,” said Monks, “after a lingering illness; and, on her death-bed, she bequeathed these secrets to me, together with her unquenchable and deadly hatred of all whom they involved—though she need not have left me that, for I had inherited it long before. She would not believe that the girl had destroyed herself, and the child too, but was filled with the impression that a male child had been born, and was alive. I swore to her, if ever it crossed my path, to hunt it down; never to let it rest; to pursue it with the bitterest and most unrelenting animosity; to vent upon it the hatred that I deeply felt, and to spit upon the empty vaunt of that insulting will by dragging it, if I could, to the very gallows-foot. She was right. He came in my way at last. I began well; and, but for babbling drabs, I would have finished as I began!”

“That’s where she died,” Monks said, “wasting away after a long, drawn-out illness. On her deathbed, she passed all those secrets down to me, along with her ravenous, poisonous hatred for everyone tangled up in them—though she didn’t even need to leave that part to me. I’d inherited that same hatred deep in my bones years before she was even dying. She refused to believe the girl had killed herself and taken the baby with her, not for a second. She was completely convinced a baby boy had been born, and that he was still alive out there somewhere. I swore to her if that boy ever crossed my path, I’d hunt him down without mercy. I’d never let him rest, hound him with the most bitter, unforgiving malice I possessed. I’d pour every drop of my deep-seated hatred out on him, and tear down that empty, arrogant brag in that offensive will by dragging him straight to the gallows steps if I could manage it. She was right. He finally turned up in my life. I got off to a perfect start, too, and if it wasn’t for those chattering, loose-lipped whores running their mouths, I’d have finished the job just as clean as I started it!”

He was kept busy disposing of his heap of manuscripts. He was overwhelmed by requests from editors. It had been discovered that he was a stylist, with meat under his style. _The Northern Review_, after publishing “The Cradle of Beauty,” had written him for half a dozen similar essays, which would have been supplied out of the heap, had not _Burton’s Magazine_, in a speculative mood, offered him five hundred dollars each for five essays. He wrote back that he would supply the demand, but at a thousand dollars an essay. He remembered that all these manuscripts had been refused by the very magazines that were now clamoring for them. And their refusals had been cold-blooded, automatic, stereotyped. They had made him sweat, and now he intended to make them sweat. _Burton’s Magazine_ paid his price for five essays, and the remaining four, at the same rate, were snapped up by _Mackintosh’s Monthly, The Northern Review_ being too poor to stand the pace. Thus went out to the world “The High Priests of Mystery,” “The Wonder-Dreamers,” “The Yardstick of the Ego,” “Philosophy of Illusion,” “God and Clod,” “Art and Biology,” “Critics and Test-tubes,” “Star-dust,” and “The Dignity of Usury,”—to raise storms and rumblings and mutterings that were many a day in dying down.

He was kept fully occupied with the task of selling off his mountain of manuscripts. Editors were now inundating him with requests, having finally recognized that he was a true stylist whose work possessed genuine substance. Following the publication of “The Cradle of Beauty,” *The Northern Review* petitioned him for six more essays. He might have obliged them from his existing stock had *Burton’s Magazine* not stepped in with a speculative offer of five hundred dollars per piece for five essays. He responded by setting his price at a thousand dollars each. He could not forget that these same magazines had once rejected these very manuscripts with a mechanical, cold-blooded indifference. They had once made him suffer through their neglect, and now he intended to make them pay. *Burton’s* met his price for five, and the remaining four were snatched up at the same rate by *Mackintosh’s Monthly*, leaving the cash-strapped *Northern Review* unable to compete. In this manner, “The High Priests of Mystery,” “The Wonder-Dreamers,” “The Yardstick of the Ego,” “Philosophy of Illusion,” “God and Clod,” “Art and Biology,” “Critics and Test-tubes,” “Star-dust,” and “The Dignity of Usury” were released to the public, sparking a series of intellectual storms and lingering controversies that would resonate for a long time to come.

Soapy had confidence in himself from the lowest button of his vest upward. He was shaven, and his coat was decent and his neat black, ready-tied four-in-hand had been presented to him by a lady missionary on Thanksgiving Day. If he could reach a table in the restaurant unsuspected success would be his. The portion of him that would show above the table would raise no doubt in the waiter’s mind. A roasted mallard duck, thought Soapy, would be about the thing—with a bottle of Chablis, and then Camembert, a demi-tasse and a cigar. One dollar for the cigar would be enough. The total would not be so high as to call forth any supreme manifestation of revenge from the café management; and yet the meat would leave him filled and happy for the journey to his winter refuge.

Soapy had unshakable confidence in every part of himself from the lowest button of his vest upward. He was clean-shaven, his coat was presentable, and his crisp pre-tied black four-in-hand tie had been a gift from a lady missionary on Thanksgiving Day. If he could make his way to a table in the restaurant without raising suspicion, his gambit would be a success. Every inch of him visible above the table would give the waitstaff no cause for doubt. A roasted mallard duck, Soapy mused, would be just the thing—paired with a bottle of Chablis, followed by Camembert cheese, a demi-tasse, and a cigar. One dollar would be more than enough for the cigar. The total bill would not be steep enough to provoke any excessive retaliation from the café management, yet the rich meat would leave him sated and happy for the journey to his winter refuge.

At least half a dozen more were severally drawn forth from the same box, and surveyed with equal pleasure; besides rings, brooches, bracelets, and other articles of jewellery, of such magnificent materials, and costly workmanship, that Oliver had no idea, even of their names.

Muttering these and similar thoughts to himself, the Jew returned the watch to its secure hiding spot. He then pulled out at least six more from the same case, examining each with the same delight, along with rings, brooches, bracelets, and other pieces of jewelry so rich in material and exquisite in craft that Oliver couldn't even guess what they were called.

Translating what is being said is arguably the most important aspect of machine translation, and has been the main focus of all its efforts so far. However, how something is said also has an impact on how the final translation is perceived. have pointed out that demographic aspects of language do play a role in translation, and could help in personalization. As Vanmassenhove et al. (2018) have shown, gendered inflections like "Sono stanco/a" (Italian I am tired) are an important aspect of correct translations.

metadata.title: "You Sound Just Like Your Father" Commercial Machine Translation Systems Include Stylistic Biases

text: While the central objective of machine translation has long been to accurately convey what is being said, the manner in which it is expressed also shapes the perception of the resulting translation. Prior work has highlighted that demographic properties of language are relevant to translation and can facilitate personalization. For instance, Vanmassenhove et al. (2018) demonstrated that correctly handling gendered inflections—such as the Italian “Sono stanco/a” (“I am tired”)—constitutes a crucial dimension of translation quality.

This dataset contains about 1.2 million essays with over 83 million words written by approximately 174,000 learners with a wide range of CEFR levels (A1-C2) (language proficiency level). We used the student essays as input and the CEFR level assigned by a grader as output to train the AES model. Due to the imbalanced distribution of levels as shown in To train the GED model, we use the First Certificate in English dataset (FCE). This dataset contains 1,224 essays written by English learners who took the First Certificate in English (FCE) exam. These essays have been manually tagged based on 77 error types (Yannakoudakis et al., 2011) . We used 30,953 sentences from FCE for training, 2,720 for testing, and 2,222 for development. text: Then the court begged my pardon, and hoped I would not fail to tell his lordship it was in no wise the court's fault that this high-handed thing had happened. I said I would make it all right, and so took my leave. Took it just in time, too; he was starting to ask me why I didn't fetch out these facts the moment I was arrested. I said I would if I had thought of it--which was true --but that I was so battered by that man that all my wit was knocked out of me--and so forth and so on, and got myself away, still mumbling. I didn't wait for breakfast. No grass grew under my feet. I was soon at the slave quarters. Empty--everybody gone! That is, everybody except one body--the slave-master's. It lay there all battered to pulp; and all about were the evidences of a terrific fight. There was a rude board coffin on a cart at the door, and workmen, assisted by the police, were thinning a road through the gaping crowd in order that they might bring it in.

Then the court apologized to me and said they hoped I would be sure to tell his lordship that this high-handed business was in no way the court’s fault. I answered that I would set it right, and so took my leave. I did so just in time, too; he was beginning to ask why I had not brought out these facts the instant I was arrested. I told him I would have done so if I had thought of it—which was true—but that I had been so battered by that man that all sense had been knocked out of me, and the like, and so on, and on, and I got myself away, still muttering. I did not stay for breakfast. No grass grew under my feet. I was soon at the slave quarters. Empty—everybody gone! That is, everyone except one body—the slave-master’s. It lay there beaten into a pulp, and all around were the signs of a desperate struggle. At the door stood a rough board coffin on a cart, and workmen, with the police helping them, were clearing a passage through the staring crowd so they could bring it inside.

“I have not touched you,” said Troy, quietly. “It was mere sleight of hand. The sword passed behind you. Now you are not afraid, are you? Because if you are I can’t perform. I give my word that I will not only not hurt you, but not once touch you.”

“I never made contact with you,” Troy said in a calm voice. “It was only a trick of the hand. The blade went right past your back. You’re not frightened now, are you? For if you are, I won’t be able to go on. I promise I’ll neither harm you nor so much as brush against you.”

“O, sir, at another time you shall know my name, and who my mother was, and who my father, and how I never knew their hard, hard history. But I cannot tell you at this time, and I cannot tell you here. All that I may tell you, here and now, is, that I pray to you to touch me and to bless me. Kiss me, kiss me! O my dear, my dear!”

“Oh, sir, someday I’ll share my name with you, and tell you about my mother and my father, and how I was never told the harsh, bitter story of their lives. But I can’t speak of it now, and I can’t speak of it in this place. All I can say, here and now, is that I beg you to lay your hands on me and give me your blessing. Kiss me, kiss me! Oh, my darling, my darling!”

“Several changes of day and night passed, and the orb of night had greatly lessened, when I began to distinguish my sensations from each other. I gradually saw plainly the clear stream that supplied me with drink and the trees that shaded me with their foliage. I was delighted when I first discovered that a pleasant sound, which often saluted my ears, proceeded from the throats of the little winged animals who had often intercepted the light from my eyes. I began also to observe, with greater accuracy, the forms that surrounded me and to perceive the boundaries of the radiant roof of light which canopied me. Sometimes I tried to imitate the pleasant songs of the birds but was unable. Sometimes I wished to express my sensations in my own mode, but the uncouth and inarticulate sounds which broke from me frightened me into silence again.

Days and nights cycled by a handful of times, and the moon had shrunk to a thin sliver by the time I started telling my different sensations apart. Slowly, I could make out crisp details: the bright, running stream that gave me water to drink, and the trees that draped their leaves over me for shade. A thrill of joy shot through me when I first realized the soft, pretty sounds that kept drifting to my ears came from the small winged creatures that had so often darted between my eyes and the sun, cutting off the light. I also started noticing the shapes around me more clearly, and could pick out the edges of the glowing, bright ceiling of sky that arched overhead. Every now and then I tried to copy the birds’ sweet songs, but I couldn’t get the notes right. Other times I wanted to put the things I was feeling into my own kind of expression, but the rough, garbled noises that burst out of my mouth scared me so badly I went quiet all over again.

It was not much that Ruth wanted to say—merely that Norman had been going to take her to a lecture that night, but that he had a headache, and she was so disappointed, and she had the tickets, and that if he had no other engagement, would he be good enough to take her?

Ruth had only a small thing to ask—just that Norman had planned to escort her to a lecture that evening, but he’d come down with a headache, and she felt so let down, and she still held the tickets, and if he happened to be free, might he be kind enough to accompany her instead?

They were happy days, those of Oliver’s recovery. Everything was so quiet, and neat, and orderly; everybody so kind and gentle; that after the noise and turbulence in the midst of which he had always lived, it seemed like Heaven itself. He was no sooner strong enough to put his clothes on, properly, than Mr. Brownlow caused a complete new suit, and a new cap, and a new pair of shoes, to be provided for him. As Oliver was told that he might do what he liked with the old clothes, he gave them to a servant who had been very kind to him, and asked her to sell them to a Jew, and keep the money for herself. This she very readily did; and, as Oliver looked out of the parlour window, and saw the Jew roll them up in his bag and walk away, he felt quite delighted to think that they were safely gone, and that there was now no possible danger of his ever being able to wear them again. They were sad rags, to tell the truth; and Oliver had never had a new suit before.

The days of Oliver’s recovery were unreservedly happy. Every part of the house was quiet, neat, and perfectly ordered; everyone around him was so kind and gentle that, after a lifetime spent amid constant noise and turmoil, the place felt like nothing short of heaven. As soon as he was strong enough to dress himself properly, Mr. Brownlow arranged for a full new suit, a new cap, and a new pair of shoes to be made for him. When Oliver was told he could do whatever he liked with his old clothes, he gave them to a servant who had been particularly good to him, and asked her to sell them to the Jewish peddler who frequented the area and keep the money for herself. She did so gladly, and as Oliver watched from the parlor window while the peddler rolled the clothes into his bag and walked off, he felt thoroughly delighted to know they were gone for good, and there was no possible chance he would ever have to wear them again. To tell the truth, those clothes were little more than tattered rags, and Oliver had never owned a new suit in his entire life.

Mr. Britling temporised by saying he would "make inquiries," and put Mr. Van der Pant off for two days. Then he decided to go up to London with him and "make inquiries on the spot." Mr. Van der Pant did not discover his family, but Mr. Britling discovered the profound truth of a comment of Herr Heinrich's which he had hitherto considered utterly trivial, but which had nevertheless stuck in his memory. "The English," Herr Heinrich had said, "do not understanding indexing. It is the root of all good organisation."

Mr. Britling stalled for time, promising to "investigate the matter," and thus managed to delay Mr. Van der Pant for two days. Eventually, he resolved to travel to London with him to conduct his search firsthand. While the trip failed to reunite Mr. Van der Pant with his family, it led Mr. Britling to recognize the deep validity of a remark by Herr Heinrich—one he had once dismissed as insignificant, yet which had remained lodged in his mind. "The English," Herr Heinrich had observed, "lack a grasp of indexing, which is the very foundation of all efficient organization."

The initial annotations extracted by the RBS were manually corrected and extended by a biology expert using the BRAT interface (Stenetorp et al., 2012) . We define three relation types: TRADE-OFF, ARGUMENT-MODIFIER and NOT-A-TRADE-OFF. The latter denotes phrases that are related to a trigger word, but not by a TRADE-OFF relation. These syntactically similar relations provide useful training signal as negative samples. Negative samples are important because possible trigger words can be contiguous, e.g., the phrase 'negative correlation' denotes a TRADE-OFF relation, whereas 'correlation' by itself does not. As a result, the annotation of training examples is harder, and lexical and syntactic patterns that correctly signify the relation are sparse (Peng et al., 2017) . For simplicity's sake, with some abuse of terminology, we refer to all such relations collectively as trade-offs.

metadata.title: In Layman's Terms: Semi-Open Relation Extraction from Scientific Texts

text: Initial annotations extracted by the RBS were manually corrected and expanded by a biology domain expert using the BRAT annotation interface (Stenetorp et al., 2012). We formally define three distinct relation types for this task: TRADE-OFF, ARGUMENT-MODIFIER, and NOT-A-TRADE-OFF. The last of these categories describes phrases associated with a trigger word, but not connected via a TRADE-OFF relation. These syntactically similar relation instances serve as useful negative samples that provide valuable training signal. Negative samples are critically important because candidate trigger words can appear in contiguous multi-word sequences: for example, the phrase “negative correlation” denotes a TRADE-OFF relation, whereas the standalone term “correlation” does not. This property increases the difficulty of training example annotation, and lexical and syntactic patterns that reliably signify the target relation are relatively sparse (Peng et al., 2017). For the sake of simplicity, with minor abuse of terminology, we refer to all three of these relation types collectively as trade-offs.
