“I’d never have credited it, not for a moment, if we hadn’t confirmed it with our own eyes, so to speak,” Joseph Poorgrass hiccupped, “that every cup, every last one of the finest knives and forks, and every empty bottle stands exactly where it began, untouched and unthieved.”

Then, to business. I tested the electric signals from the gatling platform to the cave, and made sure that they were all right; I tested and retested those which commanded the fences--these were signals whereby I could break and renew the electric current in each fence independently of the others at will. I placed the brook-connection under the guard and authority of three of my best boys, who would alternate in two-hour watches all night and promptly obey my signal, if I should have occasion to give it --three revolver-shots in quick succession. Sentry-duty was discarded for the night, and the corral left empty of life; I ordered that quiet be maintained in the cave, and the electric lights turned down to a glimmer.

Then it was down to business. I checked the electric signals running from the gatling platform to the cave and satisfied myself that they were in working order; I checked and rechecked those controlling the fences—signals by which I could interrupt and restore the electric current in each fence separately, whenever I chose. I put the brook connection under the supervision of three of my best boys, who were to take turns on two-hour watches throughout the night and, if necessary, obey my signal at once—three revolver shots in rapid succession. Sentry duty was suspended for the night, and the corral was left deserted; I gave orders that silence be kept in the cave and that the electric lights be dimmed to a faint glow.

A trading-boat, with a sail of the softened colour of a dead leaf, then glided into his view, floated by him, and died away. As its silent track in the water disappeared, the prayer that had broken up out of his heart for a merciful consideration of all his poor blindnesses and errors, ended in the words, “I am the resurrection and the life.”

A trading boat, its sail the muted color of a dead leaf, then drifted into view, passed him, and vanished. As the quiet wake it left upon the water faded, the prayer that had risen from his heart for a merciful judgment of all his poor blindnesses and mistakes ended with the words, “I am the resurrection and the life.”

I had started a teacher-factory and a lot of Sunday-schools the first thing; as a result, I now had an admirable system of graded schools in full blast in those places, and also a complete variety of Protestant congregations all in a prosperous and growing condition. Everybody could be any kind of a Christian he wanted to; there was perfect freedom in that matter. But I confined public religious teaching to the churches and the Sunday-schools, permitting nothing of it in my other educational buildings. I could have given my own sect the preference and made everybody a Presbyterian without any trouble, but that would have been to affront a law of human nature: spiritual wants and instincts are as various in the human family as are physical appetites, complexions, and features, and a man is only at his best, morally, when he is equipped with the religious garment whose color and shape and size most nicely accommodate themselves to the spiritual complexion, angularities, and stature of the individual who wears it; and, besides, I was afraid of a united Church; it makes a mighty power, the mightiest conceivable, and then when it by and by gets into selfish hands, as it is always bound to do, it means death to human liberty and paralysis to human thought.

I had begun by setting up a teacher-factory and a good many Sunday-schools; as a result, I now had a splendid system of graded schools running at full speed in those places, along with a full range of Protestant congregations, all flourishing and steadily increasing. Anyone was free to be whatever kind of Christian he chose; there was complete liberty in that regard. But I limited public religious instruction to the churches and the Sunday-schools, allowing none of it in my other schools. I could easily have favored my own denomination and made everybody a Presbyterian without any difficulty, but that would have violated a law of human nature: spiritual needs and instincts differ among people just as much as physical appetites, complexions, and features do, and a man is at his moral best only when he is fitted with the religious garment whose color, shape, and size suit as closely as possible the spiritual complexion, odd angles, and stature of the person wearing it; besides, I was wary of a united Church. It is a tremendous power, the greatest imaginable, and once it falls into selfish hands, as it inevitably does, it spells the death of human liberty and the crippling of human thought.

The poor boy was in such a state that he could hardly answer me. It was pitiful to see a creature so terrified, so unnerved, so demoralized. But he promised everything; and on my side he made me promise over and over again that I would remain his friend, and never turn against him or cast any enchantments upon him. Then he worked his way out, staying himself with his hand along the wall, like a sick person.

The poor boy was so shaken he could barely get a word out in reply to me. It was gut-wrenching to look at a person so frozen with terror, so frayed of nerve, so thoroughly demoralized. He promised to do anything I asked of him, but first he made me vow over and over that I would remain his friend, never turn against him, never cast any enchantments to harm him. After that he picked his way out of the room, steadying himself with one hand along the wall every step of the way, as if he were gravely ill.

“The father of Safie had been the cause of their ruin. He was a Turkish merchant and had inhabited Paris for many years, when, for some reason which I could not learn, he became obnoxious to the government. He was seized and cast into prison the very day that Safie arrived from Constantinople to join him. He was tried and condemned to death. The injustice of his sentence was very flagrant; all Paris was indignant; and it was judged that his religion and wealth rather than the crime alleged against him had been the cause of his condemnation.

Lira’s father had been the root of her family’s downfall. A Persian trader who had resided in Amsterdam for nearly two decades, he fell out of favor with the city’s ruling council for reasons I never managed to unearth. He was arrested and locked in a mildewed jail cell the very same afternoon Lira arrived from Isfahan to reunite with him. His trial was rushed, and the verdict handed down was a death sentence. The unfairness of the ruling was impossibly flagrant; the entire city seethed with indignation, and it was widely agreed that his faith and sizable fortune, not any crime he was alleged to have committed, were the real drivers of his condemnation.

The strong tide, so swift, so deep, and certain, was like a congenial friend, in the morning stillness. He walked by the stream, far from the houses, and in the light and warmth of the sun fell asleep on the bank. When he awoke and was afoot again, he lingered there yet a little longer, watching an eddy that turned and turned purposeless, until the stream absorbed it, and carried it on to the sea.--“Like me.”

The powerful current, so rapid, so deep, and so sure, seemed in the morning hush like a kindly companion. He wandered beside the water, far from the dwellings, and in the sun’s light and warmth he fell asleep upon the bank. When he woke and rose again, he stayed there still a while longer, gazing at a whirlpool that spun and spun without purpose, until the stream took it into itself and bore it onward to the sea.—“Like me.”

The pyBART library provides a Python converter from English UD trees to BART. pyBART subsumes the enhancements of the EUD Java implementation provided in Stanford Core-NLP, 6 and extends them as described in §4. While py-BART's default performs all enhancements, it can be configured to follow a more selective behavior. pyBART has two modes: (1) a converter from CoNLLU-formatted UD trees to CoNLLUformatted BART structures; 7 and (2) a spaCy (Honnibal and Montani, 2017) pipeline component. 8 After registering pyBART as a spaCy pipeline, tokens on the analyzed document will have a . .parent list field, containing the list of parents of the token in the BART structure. Each item is a dictionary specifying-in addition to the parent-token id and dependency label-also the extra information described in §2.2. See Figure 2 for an illustration of the API.

metadata.title: pyBART: Evidence-based Syntactic Transformations for IE

text: The pyBART library offers a Python-based converter from English UD trees to BART. pyBART incorporates the enhancements of the EUD Java implementation available in Stanford CoreNLP, 6 and further extends them as described in §4. Although pyBART performs all enhancements by default, it can also be configured to behave more selectively. pyBART operates in two modes: (1) a converter from CoNLLU-formatted UD trees to CoNLLU-formatted BART structures; 7 and (2) a spaCy (Honnibal and Montani, 2017) pipeline component. 8 Once pyBART is registered as a spaCy pipeline, tokens in the processed document receive a .parent list field containing the list of parents for that token in the BART structure. Each entry is a dictionary that specifies, in addition to the parent-token identifier and dependency label, the extra information described in §2.2. See Figure 2 for an illustration of the API.

Last, we conduct a correlation study between the translation difficulties as measured by XMI and the linguistic and data-dependent properties of each translation task, following the approaches of and . Table 2 lists Pearson's and Spearman's correlation coefficients for data-dependent metrics, where bold values indicate statistically significant results (p < 0.05) after Bonferroni correction (p < 0.0029). Interestingly, the only features that significantly correlate with our metric are related to the type-to-token ratio (TTR) for the source language and the distance between source and target TTRs. This implies that a potential explanation for the differences in translation difficulty lies in lexical variation. For full correlation results, refer to App. D.

metadata.title: It's Easier to Translate out of English than into it: Measuring Neural Translation Difficulty by Cross-Mutual Information
metadata.section: Correlations with linguistic and data features

text: Finally, we conduct a correlation analysis examining the relationship between translation difficulty as measured by XMI and the linguistic and data-dependent properties of each translation task, following the approaches of  and . Table 2 reports Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients for all data-dependent metrics, with bolded values indicating statistically significant results (p < 0.05) after Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons (adjusted threshold p < 0.0029). Notably, the only features that exhibit a significant correlation with our difficulty metric are source language type-to-token ratio (TTR) and the pairwise distance between source and target language TTR values. This finding implies that lexical variation offers a potential explanatory mechanism for observed cross-task differences in translation difficulty. Full correlation results are provided in Appendix D.

Love in a hut, with water and a crust,
Is--Love, forgive us!--cinders, ashes, dust;
Love in a palace is perhaps at last
More grievous torment than a hermit's fast:--
That is a doubtful tale from faery land,
Hard for the non-elect to understand.
Had Lycius liv'd to hand his story down,
He might have given the moral a fresh frown,
Or clench'd it quite: but too short was their bliss
To breed distrust and hate, that make the soft voice hiss.
Besides, there, nightly, with terrific glare
Love, jealous grown of so complete a pair,
Hover'd and buzz'd his wings, with fearful roar,
Above the lintel of their chamber door,
And down the passage cast a glow upon the floor.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
Affection in a shack, on crumbs and rain,
Turns—pardon, Love!—to embers, soot, and pain;
A royal hall's embrace may prove at length
A sharper ache than solitude's spare strength:—
A whispered myth from some enchanted sphere,
Too strange for common hearts to ever hear.
Had Lycius survived to pass his legend on,
He might have bent the lesson toward a frown,
Or sealed it shut: but joy's brief, fragile thread
Prevents the spite and doubt that make soft tones shed.
And there, each dusk, with dreadful, burning stare,
Love, envious of a bond so deeply rare,
Beat wings that roared and whirred in jealous fright
Above the threshold of their room at night,
And flung a lurid gleam along the passage light.

“Well, since it has come to this,” said Mrs. Yeobright sadly, “I will own to you, reddleman, that I thought of going. I should be much happier if we were reconciled. The marriage is unalterable, my life may be cut short, and I should wish to die in peace. He is my only son; and since sons are made of such stuff I am not sorry I have no other. As for Thomasin, I never expected much from her; and she has not disappointed me. But I forgave her long ago; and I forgive him now. I’ll go.”

“Well, since matters have reached this pass,” Mrs. Yeobright said mournfully, “I will confess to you, reddleman, that I had considered going. I would be far easier in my heart if we made peace. The marriage cannot be undone, my days may be few, and I long to die in charity. He is my only son; and seeing what sons are made of, I am not grieved that I have no other. As for Thomasin, I never looked for much from her, and she has not failed my expectation. But I pardoned her long ago, and I pardon him now. I shall go.”

He drew out his flute and began to play “Jockey to the Fair” in the style of a man who had never known a moment’s sorrow. Oak could pipe with Arcadian sweetness, and the sound of the well-known notes cheered his own heart as well as those of the loungers. He played on with spirit, and in half an hour had earned in pence what was a small fortune to a destitute man.

He produced his flute and struck up “Jockey to the Fair” with the easy grace of someone untouched by grief. Oak played with a pastoral sweetness that lifted not only the spirits of the idlers nearby but his own as well. He continued with lively feeling, and within half an hour had collected in pennies what amounted to a modest treasure for a man with nothing.

He was a fairly well-educated man for one of middle class—exceptionally well educated for a common soldier. He spoke fluently and unceasingly. He could in this way be one thing and seem another: for instance, he could speak of love and think of dinner; call on the husband to look at the wife; be eager to pay and intend to owe.

For a man of middle-class background, he was reasonably well educated—exceptionally learned, even, for a common rank-and-file soldier. He spoke smoothly, fluently, and almost without pause. This gift of gab let him embody one thing while appearing to be another: he could gush about the tender thrills of love while his thoughts drifted to his next meal, drop by a husband’s home for a supposed visit when his real target was the man’s wife, or act impatient to settle a tab when he fully intended to leave the debt unpaid.

His house stood recessed from the road, and the stables, which are to a farm what a fireplace is to a room, were behind, their lower portions being lost amid bushes of laurel. Inside the blue door, open half-way down, were to be seen at this time the backs and tails of half-a-dozen warm and contented horses standing in their stalls; and as thus viewed, they presented alternations of roan and bay, in shapes like a Moorish arch, the tail being a streak down the midst of each. Over these, and lost to the eye gazing in from the outer light, the mouths of the same animals could be heard busily sustaining the above-named warmth and plumpness by quantities of oats and hay. The restless and shadowy figure of a colt wandered about a loose-box at the end, whilst the steady grind of all the eaters was occasionally diversified by the rattle of a rope or the stamp of a foot.

His house stood back from the road, and the stables, which are to a farm what a fireplace is to a room, lay behind it, their lower parts hidden among laurel bushes. Through the blue door, opened halfway, one could then see the backs and tails of half a dozen warm, contented horses standing in their stalls; viewed in this way, they showed alternate patches of roan and bay, shaped like a Moorish arch, with the tail forming a streak down the center of each. Above them, and beyond the sight of anyone looking in from the outer light, the animals’ mouths could be heard busily maintaining that warmth and plumpness with oats and hay in generous measure. A restless, shadowy colt moved about a loose box at the far end, while the steady grinding of the feeders was at intervals broken by the rattle of a rope or the stamping of a hoof.

[Pop] } Figure 6 : Epigraph structure for the AMR in Fig. 2 2.3 Encoding: No Surprises When configuring a tree from a graph, the epigraph is used to control where triples occur in the tree. If at each step the layout markers in the epigraph allow the configuration process to navigate a tree with no surprises (that is, when the source or target of each triple is the current node on a node-context stack), then it will produce the same tree that was decoded to get the graph. 6 Otherwise, such as when a graph is modified or constructed without an epigraph, the algorithm will switch to another procedure that repeatedly passes over the list of remaining triples and configures those whose source or target are already in the tree under construction. If no triples are inserted in a pass, the remaining triples are discarded and a warning is logged that the graph is disconnected. The semantic model is used to properly configure inverted branches as necessary.

text: [Pop] } Figure 6: Epigraph structure for the AMR in Fig. 2. 2.3 Encoding: No Surprises When constructing a tree from a graph, the epigraph specifies where triples are realized in the tree. If, at every step, the layout markers in the epigraph permit the configuration process to traverse a tree without surprises—that is, when the source or target of each triple corresponds to the current node on the node-context stack—then the resulting tree will be identical to the one that was decoded to produce the graph. 6 Otherwise, for example when a graph has been altered or created without an epigraph, the algorithm falls back to an alternative procedure that repeatedly scans the list of remaining triples and configures those whose source or target is already present in the tree under construction. If a pass inserts no triples, the remaining ones are discarded and a warning is issued indicating that the graph is disconnected. The semantic model is used to correctly configure inverted branches whenever required.

He wandered over miles and miles of ground, and still came back to the old place. Morning and noon had passed, and the day was on the wane, and still he rambled to and fro, and up and down, and round and round, and still lingered about the same spot. At last he got away, and shaped his course for Hatfield.

He roamed across vast stretches of land, yet always found himself returning to that familiar spot. Morning turned to noon, the day began to fade, and still he drifted back and forth, circling and retracing his steps, unable to leave that one place behind. Finally he broke free and set his path toward Hatfield.

They had some cold meat for dinner, and sat so long after it, while Mr. Sikes indulged himself with three or four pipes, that Oliver began to feel quite certain they were not going any further. Being much tired with the walk, and getting up so early, he dozed a little at first; then, quite overpowered by fatigue and the fumes of the tobacco, fell asleep.

Their midday meal was plain cold cured meat, and they lingered at the scuffed wooden table for so long afterward, while Mr. Hale worked his way through three or four chipped clay pipes, that Luka grew thoroughly convinced they had no intention of setting out again that day. Worn ragged from the miles they’d tramped and the hour he’d risen before dawn, he drifted in fitful half-sleep at first; then, fully overpowered by bone-deep fatigue and the thick, acrid curl of tobacco smoke hanging heavy in the stuffy cottage air, he slipped completely into unconsciousness.

Both the trenches were frightfully smashed about, and in the first one there were little knots and groups of dead. We got to work at once shying the sandbags over from the old front of the trench to the parados. Our guns had never stopped all the time; they were now plastering the third line trenches. And almost at once the German shells began dropping into us. Of course they had the range to an inch. One didn't have any time to feel and think; one just set oneself with all one's energy to turn the trench over....

Not every German fled. A handful stayed with their machine guns until they were run through with bayonets. Both trenches had been battered to pieces, and in the forward one lay scattered clusters of dead. We set to immediately, heaving sandbags from the trench’s old front over to its rear wall. Our artillery had never let up the whole while; now it was hammering the third-line trenches. And almost at once German shells began falling among us. Naturally, they had the range down to the inch. There was no time to feel or reflect; you just threw every ounce of yourself into reversing the trench.

There was about an average of two dogs to one man; and these sat in expectant attitudes till a spent bone was flung to them, and then they went for it by brigades and divisions, with a rush, and there ensued a fight which filled the prospect with a tumultuous chaos of plunging heads and bodies and flashing tails, and the storm of howlings and barkings deafened all speech for the time; but that was no matter, for the dog-fight was always a bigger interest anyway; the men rose, sometimes, to observe it the better and bet on it, and the ladies and the musicians stretched themselves out over their balusters with the same object; and all broke into delighted ejaculations from time to time. This challenge has been tackled by exploiting huge amounts of hand-annotated data in a supervised fashion (Raganato et al., 2017b; Bevilacqua and Navigli, 2019; Vial et al., 2019; Bevilacqua and Navigli, 2020) or, alternatively, by harnessing structured information (Agirre et al., 2014; Moro et al., 2014; , such as that available within existing lexical knowledge bases (LKBs) like WordNet (Fellbaum, 1998) . Despite achieving better overall results, supervised systems require tremendous efforts in order to produce data for several languages (Navigli, 2018; Pasini, 2020) , whereas knowledge-based approaches can easily be applied in multilingual environments due to the wide array of languages covered by LKBs like Ba-belNet 1 (Navigli and Ponzetto, 2012) , or the Open Multilingual WordNet (Bond and Foster, 2013) . Moreover, it is widely acknowledged that the performance of a knowledge-based WSD system is strongly correlated with the structure of the LKB employed (Boyd-Graber et al., 2006; Lemnitzer et al., 2008; Navigli and Lapata, 2010; Ponzetto and Navigli, 2010) . In fact, the knowledge available within LKBs reflects the fact that words can be linked via two types of semantic relations: paradigmatic relations -i.e. the most frequently encountered relations in LKBs -concern the substitution of lexical units, and determine to which level in a hierarchy a language unit belongs by semantic analogy with units similar to it; conversely, syntagmatic relations concern the positioning of such units, by linking elements belonging to the same hierarchical level (e.g., words), which appear in the same context (e.g., a sentence). As a case in point, a paradigmatic relation exists, independently of a given context, between the words farm n and workplace n (where a farm is a type of workplace), whereas a syntagmatic relation is entertained between the words work v and farm n , e.g., in the sentence 'her husband works in a farm as a labourer.'

metadata.title: Personalized PageRank with Syntagmatic Information for Multilingual Word Sense Disambiguation

text: This challenge has been addressed either by leveraging large volumes of manually annotated data in a supervised manner (Raganato et al., 2017b; Bevilacqua and Navigli, 2019; Vial et al., 2019; Bevilacqua and Navigli, 2020) or by drawing on structured information (Agirre et al., 2014; Moro et al., 2014), such as that contained in existing lexical knowledge bases (LKBs) like WordNet (Fellbaum, 1998). Although supervised systems generally attain superior overall performance, they demand substantial effort to generate training data across multiple languages (Navigli, 2018; Pasini, 2020), whereas knowledge-based methods can be readily deployed in multilingual settings thanks to the broad language coverage of LKBs such as BabelNet (Navigli and Ponzetto, 2012) or the Open Multilingual WordNet (Bond and Foster, 2013). Furthermore, it is well established that the performance of a knowledge-based word sense disambiguation system is closely tied to the structure of the underlying LKB (Boyd-Graber et al., 2006; Lemnitzer et al., 2008; Navigli and Lapata, 2010; Ponzetto and Navigli, 2010). Indeed, the knowledge encoded in LKBs reflects the fact that words can be connected through two kinds of semantic relations: paradigmatic relations—the type most commonly found in LKBs—pertain to the substitution of lexical units and determine the hierarchical level a language unit occupies through semantic analogy with similar units; syntagmatic relations, by contrast, concern the arrangement of such units, linking elements that belong to the same hierarchical level (e.g., words) and co-occur in the same context (e.g., a sentence). For instance, a paradigmatic relation holds, independently of any specific context, between the words farm (noun) and workplace (noun) (since a farm is a kind of workplace), whereas a syntagmatic relation obtains between work (verb) and farm (noun), as in the sentence “her husband works in a farm as a labourer.”

Data We evaluate NAR-MT training on both the WMT16 En-Ro (around 610k sentence pairs) and the WMT14 En-De (around 4.5M sentence pairs) parallel corpora along with the associated WMT Models WMT16 WMT14 En→Ro Ro→En En→De De→En NAT-FT (Gu et al., 2017) 27 We report all results on test sets. We used the Romanian portion of the News Crawl 2015 corpus and the English portion of the Europarl v7/v8 corpus 2 as monolingual text for our En-Ro experiments, which are both about 4 times larger than the original paired data. We used the News Crawl 2007/2008 corpora for German and English monolingual text 2 in our En-De experiments, and downsampled them to ∼3 million sentences per language. The data statistics are summarized in Table 1 . The monolingual data are processed following Lee et al. (2018) , which are tokenized and segmented into subword units (Sennrich et al., 2015b) . The vocabulary is shared between source and target languages and has ∼40k units. We use BLEU to evaluate the translation quality 3 .

What if her interest in him were not so entirely that of a relative as it had formerly been? To Yeobright any possibility of this sort was a serious matter; and he almost felt troubled at the thought of it. Every pulse of loverlike feeling which had not been stilled during Eustacia’s lifetime had gone into the grave with her. His passion for her had occurred too far on in his manhood to leave fuel enough on hand for another fire of that sort, as may happen with more boyish loves. Even supposing him capable of loving again, that love would be a plant of slow and laboured growth, and in the end only small and sickly, like an autumn-hatched bird.

What if her care for him were no longer merely that of a kinswoman, as it had once been? To Yeobright any hint of such a shift was a grave affair, and the notion almost unsettled him. Every pulse of romantic feeling that had not been extinguished while Eustacia lived had been buried with her. His love for her had struck too late in his manhood to leave behind enough tinder for another blaze of that kind, as can occur with younger, more boyish affections. Even if he were capable of loving again, that love would be a slow and toilsome growth, stunted and sickly in the end, like a bird hatched in autumn.

“Oh, it is true enough. I may as well be hung for a sheep as a lamb (an old country saying, not of much account, but it will do for a rough soldier), and so I will speak my mind, regardless of your pleasure, and without hoping or intending to get your pardon. Why, Miss Everdene, it is in this manner that your good looks may do more harm than good in the world.” The sergeant looked down the mead in critical abstraction. “Probably some one man on an average falls in love with each ordinary woman. She can marry him: he is content, and leads a useful life. Such women as you a hundred men always covet—your eyes will bewitch scores on scores into an unavailing fancy for you—you can only marry one of that many. Out of these say twenty will endeavour to drown the bitterness of despised love in drink; twenty more will mope away their lives without a wish or attempt to make a mark in the world, because they have no ambition apart from their attachment to you; twenty more—the susceptible person myself possibly among them—will be always draggling after you, getting where they may just see you, doing desperate things. Men are such constant fools! The rest may try to get over their passion with more or less success. But all these men will be saddened. And not only those ninety-nine men, but the ninety-nine women they might have married are saddened with them. There’s my tale. That’s why I say that a woman so charming as yourself, Miss Everdene, is hardly a blessing to her race.”

“Oh, it’s quite true enough. I may as well be hanged for a sheep as for a lamb—an old country saying, not worth much, but it suits a rough soldier—and so I’ll say what I think, without regard to whether it pleases you, and without expecting or meaning to win your pardon. Why, Miss Everdene, it is in this way that your beauty may do more harm than good in the world.” The sergeant gazed down the meadow in reflective disapproval. “As a rule, perhaps one man falls in love with every ordinary woman. She can marry him; he is satisfied, and goes on to lead a useful life. But women such as you are desired by a hundred men at once—your eyes will cast scores and scores into hopeless admiration for you—you can marry only one of them. Of those, let us say twenty will try to drown the pain of rejected love in drink; twenty more will waste their lives in idleness, with no desire and no effort to make any mark in the world, because they have no ambition beyond their devotion to you; twenty more—the impressionable sort, myself perhaps among them—will be forever trailing after you, going wherever they can merely catch sight of you, doing desperate things. Men are such hopeless fools! The rest may manage, with greater or less success, to get over their passion. But all these men will be made unhappy. And not only those ninety-nine men, but the ninety-nine women they might have married are made unhappy too. There’s my story. That is why I say that a woman as lovely as you, Miss Everdene, is scarcely a blessing to her kind.”

“I will,” she answered triumphantly. “When you went out this evening I thought that as baby was asleep I would see where you were going to so mysteriously without telling me. So I ran out and followed behind you. You stopped at the place where the road forks, looked round at the bonfires, and then said, ‘Damn it, I’ll go!’ And you went quickly up the left-hand road. Then I stood and watched you.”

“I did,” she declared with a victorious lilt. “After you slipped away tonight, I reckoned that with the little one slumbering I’d discover where you were heading so secretively, without a word to me. So I darted out and trailed behind you. You paused at the fork in the lane, glanced back at the blaze of the bonfires, and then muttered, ‘To hell with it, I’m going!’ And you hurried off along the rightward path. Then I stopped and kept my eyes on you.”

Mr. Noah Claypole: receiving a free pardon from the Crown in consequence of being admitted approver against Fagin: and considering his profession not altogether as safe a one as he could wish: was, for some little time, at a loss for the means of a livelihood, not burdened with too much work. After some consideration, he went into business as an informer, in which calling he realises a genteel subsistence. His plan is, to walk out once a week during church time attended by Charlotte in respectable attire. The lady faints away at the doors of charitable publicans, and the gentleman being accommodated with three-penny worth of brandy to restore her, lays an information next day, and pockets half the penalty. Sometimes Mr. Claypole faints himself, but the result is the same.

Having secured a full pardon for testifying against Fagin, and deciding his former criminal life was far too hazardous, Mr. Noah Claypole spent some time searching for an easy way to make ends meet. Eventually, he established himself as a professional informant, a trade that allows him to live quite comfortably. His routine involves walking out once a week during Sunday services with Charlotte, who dresses quite respectably for the occasion. She makes a show of fainting at the doorstep of a kind-hearted innkeeper, and when Noah is sold a small glass of brandy to help her recover, he reports the violation the next day and keeps half the fine. On occasion, Mr. Claypole performs the fainting spell himself, but the profit remains identical.

We have been praised. But all along, Essex has done well. I can't reckon to get back yet, but there are such things as leave for eight-and-forty hours or so in England....

In any case, your son is safe and unhurt, even if he is currently struggling with bitterness and sorrow. We felt a great sense of relief that night. Rumors are circulating that we will soon be granted some time off and a chance to refit. Our casualties were quite high, though we have received official praise. Through it all, the Essex has performed well. I can’t count on returning home permanently just yet, but there is always the possibility of a forty-eight-hour pass back in England.

The afternoon drew on apace, and, looking to the right towards the sea as he walked beside the horse, Poorgrass saw strange clouds and scrolls of mist rolling over the long ridges which girt the landscape in that quarter. They came in yet greater volumes, and indolently crept across the intervening valleys, and around the withered papery flags of the moor and river brinks. Then their dank spongy forms closed in upon the sky. It was a sudden overgrowth of atmospheric fungi which had their roots in the neighbouring sea, and by the time that horse, man, and corpse entered Yalbury Great Wood, these silent workings of an invisible hand had reached them, and they were completely enveloped, this being the first arrival of the autumn fogs, and the first fog of the series.

The afternoon waned swiftly, and as Poorgrass trudged beside the horse, he glanced seaward to the right. There he saw queer clouds and trailing ribbons of mist unfurling over the long ridges that hemmed the land in that direction. They swelled into thicker volumes, drifting lazily across the hollows between and curling about the dry, paper-like reeds along the moor and riverbanks. Soon their clammy, porous masses sealed off the sky. It was a swift efflorescence of vaporous growths rooted in the nearby sea, and by the time horse, man, and corpse reached Yalbury Great Wood, these mute machinations of an unseen agency had overtaken them, wrapping them entirely—the first arrival of the autumn fogs, and the first fog of the season.

metadata.title: Few-shot Slot Tagging with Collapsed Dependency Transfer and Label-enhanced Task-adaptive Projection Network

text: Our improvements in the 5-shot setting are relatively narrow. This is because NER domains differ in genre and vocabulary. As a result, compared with SNIPS, knowledge transfer is more difficult, but it is more beneficial to rely on domain-specific support examples. This trend becomes even more pronounced as the number of shots increases. In the 5-shot setting, the strongest baseline, WPZ, benefits more from additional shots because it relies only on the support set for prediction. By contrast, the gains from more shots are weaker for our model because it incorporates more prior knowledge.

In my eyes, the finest part was the domestic rhythm of that small apartment—the eager, flowing conversations once the day’s work was done; the snug suppers and bright, simple breakfasts; the sharing of dreams, each one braided into the other’s or else too slight to matter alone; the steady give-and-take of encouragement; and—forgive my guilelessness—midnight bites of cheese sandwiches and stuffed olives.

What then became of me? I know not; I lost sensation, and chains and darkness were the only objects that pressed upon me. Sometimes, indeed, I dreamt that I wandered in flowery meadows and pleasant vales with the friends of my youth, but I awoke and found myself in a dungeon. Melancholy followed, but by degrees I gained a clear conception of my miseries and situation and was then released from my prison. For they had called me mad, and during many months, as I understood, a solitary cell had been my habitation.

What then became of me? I cannot say; I lost all feeling, and nothing but chains and darkness seemed to bear upon me. At times, indeed, I dreamed that I roamed through flowery meadows and pleasant valleys with the friends of my youth, yet I awoke to find myself in a dungeon. A melancholy mood came over me, but little by little I recovered a clear sense of my sufferings and my condition, and was then released from my prison. They had, it seems, declared me mad, and for many months, as I afterward learned, a solitary cell had been my dwelling.

Text-to-speech (TTS) We also support end-toend text-to-speech (E2E-TTS), which can be applied after ST outputs a translation. The E2E-TTS model consists of the feature generation network converting an input text to acoustic features (e.g., log-mel filterbank coefficients) and the vocoder network converting the features to a waveform. Tacotron 2 (Shen et al., 2018) , Transformer-TTS , FastSpeech (Ren et al., 2019) , and their variants such as a multi-speaker model are supported as the feature generation network. WaveNet (van den Oord et al., 2016) and Parallel WaveGAN are available as the vocoder network. See Hayashi et al. (2020) for more details.

metadata.title: ESPnet-ST: All-in-One Speech Translation Toolkit

text: Our framework incorporates end-to-end text-to-speech (E2E-TTS) capabilities, which can be applied to the translations produced by the ST system. The E2E-TTS architecture comprises a feature generation network that maps input text to acoustic representations—such as log-mel filterbank coefficients—and a vocoder network that synthesizes these features into a final waveform. For the feature generation stage, we support Tacotron 2 (Shen et al., 2018), Transformer-TTS, FastSpeech (Ren et al., 2019), and their respective extensions, including multi-speaker models. Available vocoder options include WaveNet (van den Oord et al., 2016) and Parallel WaveGAN. For a more exhaustive discussion, we refer readers to Hayashi et al. (2020).

On golden dishes and in baskets bright
Of wreathed silver: sumptuous they stand
In the retired quiet of the night,
Filling the chilly room with perfume light.--
"And now, my love, my seraph fair, awake!

Or I shall drowse beside thee, so my soul doth ache."

text:
These dainties heaped he with a glowing hand  
On golden platters and in baskets bright  
Of twisting silver: lavishly they stand  
Within the hush and shelter of the night,  
Suffusing the cold room with fragrance light.--  
“And now, my love, my seraph, waking fair!  
Thou art my heaven, and I am thine eremite:  
Lift up thine eyes, for gentle St. Agnes’ prayer,  
Or I shall sink in sleep beside thee, sore my soul with care.”

I can see it with half an eye, and there’s one ingredient in it that shuts it out of the magazines. It’s guts, and magazines have no use for that particular commodity. What they want is wish-wash and slush, and God knows they get it, but not from you.”

“That’s more like it,” came the softened answer. “You make an attempt at writing, but the attempt comes to nothing. I honor and admire your failure. I know the sort of thing you write; I can recognize it with one glance, and there is one element in it that bars it from the magazines. It has nerve, and magazines have no appetite for that particular thing. What they prefer is drivel and sentimentality, and heaven knows they get plenty of that—but not from you.”

It was late that night and after an eternity of resolutions and doubts and indecisions that Mrs. Britling went to her husband. He was sitting close up to the fire with his chin upon his hands, waiting for her; he felt that she would come to him, and he was thinking meanwhile of Hugh with a slow unprogressive movement of the mind. He showed by a movement that he heard her enter the room, but he did not turn to look at her. He shrank a little from her approach.

It was deep into the night when Mrs. Britling finally went to her husband, after what had felt like an eternity of weighing resolutions, nursing doubts, and wrestling with indecision. He was huddled close to the fire, chin resting on his hands, waiting for her; he had known she would come, and in the interim his thoughts had moved slowly, circling nowhere, fixed entirely on Hugh. A small, subtle shift of his posture signaled he’d heard her enter the room, but he did not turn to look at her. He shrank back just slightly as she approached.

After the jolt of the food panic and a brief, financial scare, the vast inertia of everyday life in England asserted itself. When the public went to the banks for the new paper money, the banks tendered gold--apologetically. The supply of the new notes was very insufficient, and there was plenty of gold. After the first impression that a universal catastrophe had happened there was an effect as if nothing had happened.

Once the initial shock of the food crisis and a fleeting monetary dread had passed, the immense, sluggish routine of daily English life reasserted its hold. When people approached the banks for the freshly issued paper currency, the banks—almost apologetically—offered gold instead. The new notes were in woefully short supply, while gold remained abundant. Following that first sensation of an all-encompassing disaster, an eerie calm settled, as though nothing had occurred at all.

“Well,” he confessed, “I was a bit shivery at first; but I got used to it. I used to feel sorry for that poor girl, though. That made me forget to be afraid. She was such a beauty, in spirit as well as in appearance, and she was only slightly touched; yet she was doomed to lie there, living the life of a primitive savage and rotting slowly away. Leprosy is far more terrible than you can imagine it.”

“Well,” he admitted, “I felt a chill of fear at first, but I grew accustomed to it. Still, my heart ached for that poor girl, and pity drove out the dread. She was lovely in soul as much as in face, and her mind was only lightly clouded; yet she was condemned to languish there, living like some primitive creature while her body decayed by inches. Leprosy is more dreadful than you could ever conceive.”

We first filter out dialogs that have inappropriate content using keyword matching. We then select 3,608 ten-turn dialogs on movies, because movie dialogs are more coherent and diverse compared to other topics according to both real users and Amazon selected experts. We observe that dialogs with more than eight turns are more meaningful and semantically versatile, while dialogs more than 10 turns exceed the max length limit of the BERT model (512 tokens). So we select dialogs that have ten turns. Our approach could support longer conversations by adopting a memory footprint efficient algorithm for self-attention to support sequences with thousands of tokens . We leave this to future work. We aim to evaluate user experience for each dialog from the same chatbot of the same length. This is significantly more challenging than identifying which chatbot provides a better user experience on average since our problem setup requires us to capture more subtle difference in user experience.

metadata.title: Beyond User Self-Reported Likert Scale Ratings: A Comparison Model for Automatic Dialog Evaluation

text: We first remove dialogs containing inappropriate content via keyword matching. We then choose 3,608 ten-turn dialogs centered on movies, since both real users and Amazon-selected experts indicate that movie dialogs are more coherent and diverse than dialogs on other topics. We observe that dialogs with more than eight turns tend to be more meaningful and semantically versatile, whereas dialogs longer than 10 turns exceed the BERT model’s maximum length constraint of 512 tokens. We therefore retain dialogs with exactly ten turns. In principle, our method could be extended to longer conversations by incorporating a memory-efficient self-attention algorithm capable of handling sequences with thousands of tokens; we leave this direction for future work. Our goal is to assess user experience for each dialog from the same chatbot at the same length. This is substantially more challenging than determining which chatbot yields a better average user experience, because our setting requires capturing more subtle differences in user experience.

When he was nineteen, a year before his apprenticeship came to an end,
he quarrelled with his master, left him, and continued his training in
London as a student at St. Thomas's Hospital and Guy's. Gradually,
however, during the months that followed, though he was an industrious
and able medical student, Keats came to realize that poetry was his true
vocation; and as soon as he was of age, in spite of the opposition of
his guardian, he decided to abandon the medical profession and devote
his life to literature.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
At nineteen, one year before his apprenticeship was done,  
he fell out with his master, left him, and went on  
to London, there to study on at St. Thomas’s Hospital and Guy’s.  
Yet slowly, in the months that followed, although a diligent  
and capable student of medicine, Keats came to see  
that poetry was his truest calling; and, once he was of age,  
despite his guardian’s resistance, he chose to leave the medical art  
and give his whole life to letters.

Mr. Jarvis Lorry and Miss Manette, emerging from the wine-shop thus, joined Monsieur Defarge in the doorway to which he had directed his own company just before. It opened from a stinking little black courtyard, and was the general public entrance to a great pile of houses, inhabited by a great number of people. In the gloomy tile-paved entry to the gloomy tile-paved staircase, Monsieur Defarge bent down on one knee to the child of his old master, and put her hand to his lips. It was a gentle action, but not at all gently done; a very remarkable transformation had come over him in a few seconds. He had no good-humour in his face, nor any openness of aspect left, but had become a secret, angry, dangerous man.

Mr. Jarvis Lorry and Miss Manette stepped out of the wine-shop and joined Monsieur Defarge at the entrance where he had just gathered his own group. The doorway led from a foul little dark courtyard and served as the common entry to a massive block of buildings, packed with countless tenants. In the dim, tile-floored vestibule that gave onto the dim, tile-floored stairway, Monsieur Defarge dropped to one knee before his former master’s daughter and pressed her hand to his lips. The gesture was tender, yet the execution was anything but; in a matter of moments, a striking change had swept over him. All trace of geniality and openness had vanished from his face, leaving behind a guarded, wrathful, menacing figure.

It was a cloudy, muggy, and very dark night, and the rays from Troy’s lantern spread into the two old yews with a strange illuminating power, flickering, as it seemed, up to the black ceiling of cloud above. He felt a large drop of rain upon the back of his hand, and presently one came and entered one of the holes of the lantern, whereupon the candle sputtered and went out. Troy was weary and it being now not far from midnight, and the rain threatening to increase, he resolved to leave the finishing touches of his labour until the day should break. He groped along the wall and over the graves in the dark till he found himself round at the north side. Here he entered the porch, and, reclining upon the bench within, fell asleep.

It was a cloudy, close, and very dark night, and the glow from Troy’s lantern spread through the two ancient yews with a strange, almost magical effect, seeming to flicker upward to the black canopy of cloud above. He felt a large raindrop strike the back of his hand, and presently another found its way into one of the lantern’s holes, whereupon the candle spluttered and went out. Troy was tired, and as it was now not far from midnight, with the rain threatening to grow heavier, he decided to leave the final touches of his work until daylight returned. He groped his way along the wall and over the graves in the darkness until he found himself on the north side. There he went into the porch and, leaning back on the bench inside, fell asleep.

Dataset. We conduct experiments on Spider (Yu et al., 2018) and Spider UTran dataset. Spider is a large-scale, human annotated, cross-domain textto-SQL benchmark. Spider UTran is our modified dataset to evaluate robustness, created by injecting the untranslatable questions into Spider. We obtained 5,330 additional untranslatable questions (4,733 for training and 597 for development) from the original Spider dataset. To ensure the quality of our synthetic dataset, we hired SQL experts from Upwork 9 to annotate the auto-generated untranslatable examples in the dev set. We conduct our evaluation by following the database split setting, as illustrated in Table 1 . The split follows the original dataset hence there is no test set of Spider UTran (the test set of Spider is not publicly accessible).

metadata.title: PHOTON: A Robust Cross-Domain Text-to-SQL System

text: Dataset. We conduct experiments on Spider (Yu et al., 2018) and the Spider UTran dataset. Spider is a large-scale, human-annotated, cross-domain text-to-SQL benchmark. Spider UTran is our modified dataset for evaluating robustness, created by injecting untranslatable questions into Spider. From the original Spider dataset, we obtained 5,330 additional untranslatable questions (4,733 for training and 597 for development). To ensure the quality of our synthetic dataset, we hired SQL experts from Upwork 9 to annotate the automatically generated untranslatable examples in the development set. We evaluate following the database split setting, as shown in Table 1. This split follows the original dataset; therefore, Spider UTran has no test set (the Spider test set is not publicly accessible).

Center embedding, the ability to embed a phrase in the middle of another phrase of the same type, is a hallmark feature of natural language syntax. Center-embedding creates NESTED SYNTACTIC DEPENDENCIES, which could pose a challenge for some language models. To succeed in generating expectations about how sentences will continue in the context of multiple center embedding, a model must maintain a representation not only of what words appear in the preceding context but also of the order of those words, and must predict that upcoming words occur in the appropriate order. In this test suite we use verb transitivity and subjectverb plausibility to test model capabilities in this respect. For example, A below is a correct centerembedding, but B is not:

No eye had marked his approach, yet his presence was now an established fact. Sterling moved within a singular aura of solemnity that cast a shadow over all in his vicinity; and the conversation, which Elara’s arrival had merely tempered, was now utterly extinguished.

Nothing could be more complete than the alteration that had taken place in my feelings since the night of the appearance of the dæmon. I had before regarded my promise with a gloomy despair as a thing that, with whatever consequences, must be fulfilled; but I now felt as if a film had been taken from before my eyes and that I for the first time saw clearly. The idea of renewing my labours did not for one instant occur to me; the threat I had heard weighed on my thoughts, but I did not reflect that a voluntary act of mine could avert it. I had resolved in my own mind that to create another like the fiend I had first made would be an act of the basest and most atrocious selfishness, and I banished from my mind every thought that could lead to a different conclusion.

The transformation of my inner state since the night the creature appeared was absolute. I had previously regarded my promise with a sense of hopeless resignation, believing it a burden that must be endured regardless of the cost; now, however, it felt as though a shroud had been lifted from my sight, granting me a sudden, piercing clarity. Not for a single moment did I consider returning to my work. Though the monster’s threats haunted my thoughts, I refused to believe that any voluntary action of mine should be taken to avert his wrath. I had resolved that to fashion another being like the first would be an act of the most profound and wicked selfishness, and I cast out every impulse that might have swayed me from this conviction.

Wherever he happened to be—at the Press Club, at the Redwood Club, at pink teas and literary gatherings—always were remembered “The Ring of Bells” and “The Peri and the Pearl” when they were first published. And always was Martin’s maddening and unuttered demand: Why didn’t you feed me then? It was work performed. “The Ring of Bells” and “The Peri and the Pearl” are not changed one iota. They were just as artistic, just as worth while, then as now. But you are not feeding me for their sake, nor for the sake of anything else I have written. You’re feeding me because it is the style of feeding just now, because the whole mob is crazy with the idea of feeding Martin Eden.

So it unfolded. In every place he found himself—at the Journalists’ Hall, among the cedar-paneled rooms of the Elmwood, at rose-scented receptions and literary soirées—the talk invariably circled back to “The Chime of Voices” and “The Sylph and the Sapphire” when those works first saw print. And always, Martin’s furious and silent cry rose within him: Why did you not nourish me then? The labor was already done. “The Chime of Voices” and “The Sylph and the Sapphire” remain untouched in any syllable. They were as finely wrought, as full of merit, in that earlier hour as they are today. Yet you do not sustain me for their excellence, nor for the sake of any other page I have penned. You sustain me because that is the current mode of sustenance, because the entire multitude has grown wild with the notion of sustaining Martin Eden.

Now, before all the work-folk here assembled, can you swear to your words as the shepherd asks ye?”

“Cain Ball, you are no mere child at the breast any longer,” Joseph intoned, his voice as grave and hollow as the moment required. “You understand what it means to swear an oath. It is a fearsome covenant, mark you—one you speak aloud and bind with your very life’s seal, and the prophet Matthew warns that on whomever it falls, it will crush him utterly to dust. Now, before all the laborers gathered here, can you swear to your testimony as the shepherd demands?”
