“They are rather deathly. Now I’ll be more interesting, and let you see some loose play—giving all the cuts and points, infantry and cavalry, quicker than lightning, and as promiscuously—with just enough rule to regulate instinct and yet not to fetter it. You are my antagonist, with this difference from real warfare, that I shall miss you every time by one hair’s breadth, or perhaps two. Mind you don’t flinch, whatever you do.”

“They are somewhat deadly. But now I shall be more diverting, and let you witness a freer display—dealing out cuts and thrusts, infantry and cavalry, faster than lightning, and just as indiscriminately, with only so much order as is needed to guide instinct without shackling it. You are my adversary, though with this difference from actual war: I shall miss you every time by a hair’s breadth, or perhaps two. See that you do not flinch, whatever happens.”

The persistent torrent from the gurgoyle’s jaws directed all its vengeance into the grave. The rich tawny mould was stirred into motion, and boiled like chocolate. The water accumulated and washed deeper down, and the roar of the pool thus formed spread into the night as the head and chief among other noises of the kind created by the deluging rain. The flowers so carefully planted by Fanny’s repentant lover began to move and writhe in their bed. The winter-violets turned slowly upside down, and became a mere mat of mud. Soon the snowdrop and other bulbs danced in the boiling mass like ingredients in a cauldron. Plants of the tufted species were loosened, rose to the surface, and floated off.

The unrelenting downpour spewing from the gargoyle’s mouth poured all its unbridled force straight down into the open grave. The deep, warm brown loam churned and roiled, bubbling thick as melted chocolate. Water kept piling up, carving deeper into the earth as it settled, and the low, thunderous roar of the growing pool cut through the dark, louder than any other sound the drenching storm had stirred up across the grounds. The blooms Fanny’s regretful former lover had tended so gently started to twist and thrash in their planting bed. The winter violets flipped slowly root-over-bloom, dissolving into nothing but a sodden, muck-covered mat. Before long, snowdrops and other bulb flowers bounced and tossed in the churning mire like bits of seasoning tossed into a bubbling cauldron. Clumping, tufted ground cover plants were torn free from the soil, bobbed up to the top of the pool, and drifted away on the murky water.

“Another circumstance strengthened and confirmed these feelings. Soon after my arrival in the hovel I discovered some papers in the pocket of the dress which I had taken from your laboratory. At first I had neglected them, but now that I was able to decipher the characters in which they were written, I began to study them with diligence. It was your journal of the four months that preceded my creation. You minutely described in these papers every step you took in the progress of your work; this history was mingled with accounts of domestic occurrences. You doubtless recollect these papers. Here they are. Everything is related in them which bears reference to my accursed origin; the whole detail of that series of disgusting circumstances which produced it is set in view; the minutest description of my odious and loathsome person is given, in language which painted your own horrors and rendered mine indelible. I sickened as I read. ‘Hateful day when I received life!’ I exclaimed in agony. ‘Accursed creator! Why did you form a monster so hideous that even _you_ turned from me in disgust? God, in pity, made man beautiful and alluring, after his own image; but my form is a filthy type of yours, more horrid even from the very resemblance. Satan had his companions, fellow devils, to admire and encourage him, but I am solitary and abhorred.’

“Another event deepened and confirmed these emotions. Not long after I reached the hut, I found some papers in the pocket of the garment I had taken from your laboratory. At first I paid them no attention; but once I could read the characters in which they were written, I set myself to examine them carefully. They proved to be your journal for the four months before my creation. In these pages you recorded in the smallest detail each stage of your work; mixed with this were notes of domestic events. You surely remember these papers. They are here. In them is everything that concerns my cursed beginning; the entire account of that revolting chain of circumstances that brought it about is laid bare; and the most exact description of my vile, detestable form is given in language that reflected your own terror and made mine unchangeable. I grew faint as I read. ‘Miserable day when I was given life!’ I cried in torment. ‘Accursed maker! Why did you fashion a monster so monstrous that even you recoiled from me in horror? God, in mercy, made man fair and attractive, in his own likeness; but my shape is a foul copy of yours, made more dreadful by the very resemblance. Satan had his companions, his fellow demons, to admire him and cheer him on, but I am alone and loathed.’

Mr. Bumble’s conduct on being left to himself, was rather inexplicable. He opened the closet, counted the teaspoons, weighed the sugar-tongs, closely inspected a silver milk-pot to ascertain that it was of the genuine metal, and, having satisfied his curiosity on these points, put on his cocked hat corner-wise, and danced with much gravity four distinct times round the table.

Mr. Mudge’s demeanor, when at last he was left to his own devices, was quite remarkable. He pried open the buffet, took a census of the teaspoons, appraised the weight of the sugar-grippers, and peered intently at a silver cream-pot to ensure it was of the genuine sort; then, having pacified his avarice, he set his three-cornered hat at a tilt and pranced with profound solemnity exactly four times around the mahogany.

Among the shifting, sonorous, pulsing crowd glimpses could be had of Jerry’s high hat, battered by the winds and rains of many years; of his nose like a carrot, battered by the frolicsome, athletic progeny of millionaires and by contumacious fares; of his brass-buttoned green coat, admired in the vicinity of McGary’s. It was plain that Jerry had usurped the functions of his cab, and was carrying a “load.” Indeed, the figure may be extended and he be likened to a bread-waggon if we admit the testimony of a youthful spectator, who was heard to remark “Jerry has got a bun.”

Amid the surging, clamoring, thrumming throng, one could catch fleeting glimpses of Ollie’s tall top hat, scuffed and dented by years of gale winds and driving rain; of his carrot-shaped nose, bashed by the rowdy, sportive offspring of millionaire magnates and by snappish, unruly fares; of his brass-buttoned forest green coat, widely admired by folks who frequented the blocks around O’Malley’s Taphouse. It was plain to see that Ollie had usurped his cab’s less dignified purpose, and was toting a very obvious “load.” Indeed, the figure can be extended to liken him to a baked goods delivery wagon, if we credit the testimony of a young bystander, who was heard to remark, “Ollie’s got a proper bun on.”

By my authority as executive I threw Merlin into prison--the same cell I had occupied myself. Then I gave public notice by herald and trumpet that I should be busy with affairs of state for a fortnight, but about the end of that time I would take a moment's leisure and blow up Merlin's stone tower by fires from heaven; in the meantime, whoso listened to evil reports about me, let him beware. Furthermore, I would perform but this one miracle at this time, and no more; if it failed to satisfy and any murmured, I would turn the murmurers into horses, and make them useful. Quiet ensued.

Exercising my sovereign prerogative, I consigned Merlin to the very dungeon that had once held me. Then I proclaimed far and wide, by clarion and crier, that for a fortnight I should be absorbed in matters of governance; thereafter, I might snatch a brief hour to shatter Merlin’s stone spire with celestial flame. Meanwhile, let any who gave ear to slanders against me take heed. Moreover, this single marvel would I work for now, and none other; should it prove insufficient and any dare mutter, those malcontents I would transform to draught horses and set them to labor. A hush descended.

_Politically the British Empire is a clumsy collection of strange accidents. It is a thing as little to be proud of as the outline of a flint or the shape of a potato. For the mass of English people India and Egypt and all that side of our system mean less than nothing; our trade is something they do not understand, our imperial wealth something they do not share. Britain has been a group of four democracies caught in the net of a vast yet casual imperialism; the common man here is in a state of political perplexity from the cradle to the grave. None the less there is a great people here even as there is a great people in Russia, a people with a soul and character of its own, a people of unconquerable kindliness and with a peculiar genius, which still struggle towards will and expression. We have been beginning that same great experiment that France and America and Switzerland and China are making, the experiment of democracy. It is the newest form of human association, and we are still but half awake to its needs and necessary conditions. For it is idle to pretend that the little city democracies of ancient times were comparable to the great essays in practical republicanism that mankind is making to-day. This age of the democratic republics that dawn is a new age. It has not yet lasted for a century, not for a paltry hundred years.... All new things are weak things; a rat can kill a man-child with ease; the greater the destiny, the weaker the immediate self-protection may be. And to me it seems that your complete and perfect imperialism, ruled by Germans for Germans, is in its scope and outlook a more antiquated and smaller and less noble thing than these sprawling emergent giant democracies of the West that struggle so confusedly against it...._

Politically regarded, the British Empire is an ungainly patchwork of haphazard occurrences, no more a cause for pride than the contour of a chipped stone or the silhouette of a turnip. To the ordinary English multitude, India and Egypt and the whole apparatus of that dominion signify less than a cipher; our commerce is a riddle they cannot read, our imperial profits a store they never touch. Britain has been four democracies tangled in the mesh of an immense yet careless imperialism; the common man here drifts in political bewilderment from birth to burial. Yet there exists a great people here, just as a great people dwells in Russia, a people with its own soul and temper, a people of indomitable generosity and a strange genius, still groping toward purpose and utterance. We have set out upon that same bold experiment which France and America and Switzerland and China pursue, the experiment of democracy. It is the newest mode of human fellowship, and we remain only half roused to its demands and its prerequisites. For it is vain to pretend that the miniature city democracies of antiquity were akin to the vast ventures in practical republicanism that humanity attempts today. This era of the democratic republics whose dawn is breaking is a fresh era. It has not yet endured a century, not a meagre hundred years…. All new things are frail things; a rodent can slay a human infant with ease; the grander the destiny, the feebler the immediate self-defence may prove. And to my mind your finished and flawless imperialism, governed by Germans for Germans, is in its reach and vision a more obsolete and narrower and baser thing than these sprawling, emergent giant democracies of the West that struggle so chaotically against it….

All the while that Mr. Britling had been indulging in these imaginative slaughterings and spending the tears and hate that had gathered in his heart, his reason had been sitting apart and above the storm, like the sun waiting above thunder, like a wise nurse watching and patient above the wild passions of a child. And all the time his reason had been maintaining silently and firmly, without shouting, without speech, that the men who had made this hour were indeed not devils, were no more devils than Mr. Britling was a devil, but sinful men of like nature with himself, hard, stupid, caught in the same web of circumstance. "Kill them in your passion if you will," said reason, "but understand. This thing was done neither by devils nor fools, but by a conspiracy of foolish motives, by the weak acquiescences of the clever, by a crime that was no man's crime but the natural necessary outcome of the ineffectiveness, the blind motives and muddleheadedness of all mankind."

All the time Mr. Britling had been lost in these mental massacres, pouring out the grief and fury that had pooled inside him, his reason had hovered detached above the turmoil—like the sun poised beyond a storm, like a calm nurse watching over a child's wild tantrums with patient stillness. And through it all, that reason had been holding its ground, quietly and unwaveringly, without outcry or declaration, insisting that the men who had brought about this moment were not fiends, no more fiends than Mr. Britling himself was a fiend, but flawed human beings of the same kind as he—stubborn, obtuse, tangled in the same nets of circumstance. "Rage against them, kill them in your heart if you must," reason seemed to say, "but see clearly. This was not the work of demons or fools, but of foolish impulses conspiring together, of clever people yielding weakly, of a crime that belonged to no single person but sprang inevitably from the fecklessness, the blind drives, the muddled thinking of all humanity."

Parent ignoring Due to the lack of parallel corpora with gold-standard parses, we rely on noisy annotations from an external parser. However, the performance of syntactic parsers drops abruptly when evaluated on out-of-domain data (Dredze et al., 2007) . To prevent our model from overfitting to noisy dependencies, we introduce a regularization technique for the PASCAL sub-layer: parent ignoring. In a similar vein as dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014) , we disregard information during the training phase. Here, we ignore the position of the parent of a given token by randomly setting each row of D p to 1 ∈ R T with some probability q.

metadata.title: Enhancing Machine Translation with Dependency-Aware Self-Attention

text: Because parallel corpora with gold-standard syntactic annotations are unavailable, we instead depend on noisy parse outputs produced by an external parser. Yet, as Dredze et al. (2007) show, parser accuracy degrades sharply when applied to out-of-domain text. To mitigate the risk of the model overfitting to unreliable dependency signals, we propose a regularization strategy for the PASCAL sub-layer termed parent ignoring. Analogous to dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014), this method discards information during training: specifically, we suppress the parent position of a token by randomly replacing each row of \(D^p\) with the uniform vector \(\mathbf{1} \in \mathbb{R}^T\) with probability \(q\).

Speech translation (ST), where converting speech signals in a language to text in another language, is a key technique to break the language barrier for human communication. Traditional ST systems involve cascading automatic speech recognition (ASR), text normalization (e.g., punctuation insertion, case restoration), and machine translation (MT) modules; we call this Cascade-ST (Ney, 1999; Casacuberta et al., 2008; Kumar et al., 2014) . Recently, sequence-to-sequence (S2S) models have become the method of choice in implementing both the ASR and MT modules (c.f. (Chan et al., 2016; Bahdanau et al., 2015) ). This convergence of models has opened up the possibility of designing end-to-end speech translation (E2E-ST) systems, where a single S2S directly maps speech in a source language to its translation in the target language (Bérard et al., 2016; .

metadata.title: ESPnet-ST: All-in-One Speech Translation Toolkit

text: Speech translation (ST)—the process of converting spoken language signals into written text in a different language—serves as a crucial technology for overcoming linguistic barriers in human communication. Conventional ST pipelines consist of cascaded components: automatic speech recognition (ASR), text normalization (which includes tasks such as punctuation insertion and case restoration), and machine translation (MT); we refer to this architecture as Cascade-ST (Ney, 1999; Casacuberta et al., 2008; Kumar et al., 2014). In recent years, sequence-to-sequence (S2S) models have emerged as the preferred approach for implementing both ASR and MT modules (see, e.g., Chan et al., 2016; Bahdanau et al., 2015). This methodological convergence has enabled the development of end-to-end speech translation (E2E-ST) systems, in which a single S2S model directly maps source-language speech to its target-language translation (Bérard et al., 2016).

Martin’s face, flushed at first, paled as he read on. It was perfect art. Form triumphed over substance, if triumph it could be called where the last conceivable atom of substance had found expression in so perfect construction as to make Martin’s head swim with delight, to put passionate tears into his eyes, and to send chills creeping up and down his back. It was a long poem of six or seven hundred lines, and it was a fantastic, amazing, unearthly thing. It was terrific, impossible; and yet there it was, scrawled in black ink across the sheets of paper. It dealt with man and his soul-gropings in their ultimate terms, plumbing the abysses of space for the testimony of remotest suns and rainbow spectrums. It was a mad orgy of imagination, wassailing in the skull of a dying man who half sobbed under his breath and was quick with the wild flutter of fading heart-beats. The poem swung in majestic rhythm to the cool tumult of interstellar conflict, to the onset of starry hosts, to the impact of cold suns and the flaming up of nebulae in the darkened void; and through it all, unceasing and faint, like a silver shuttle, ran the frail, piping voice of man, a querulous chirp amid the screaming of planets and the crash of systems.

Alden’s face, flushed at first, went white as he read on. It was flawless art. Form had conquered substance, if conquest it could be called where the last imaginable particle of substance had found utterance in so exquisite a structure as to leave Alden’s head whirling with delight, to bring tears of passion to his eyes, and to send a shiver creeping over his spine. It was a long poem of six or seven hundred lines, and it was a strange, astonishing, otherworldly thing. It was dreadful, impossible; and yet there it lay, scratched in black ink across the pages of paper. It treated of man and the gropings of his soul in their final terms, sounding the depths of space for the witness of the farthest suns and the colors of rainbow spectra. It was a wild revel of imagination, carousing in the mind of a dying man who murmured half under his breath and was alive with the feverish flutter of failing heartbeats. The poem moved in grand cadence to the cold uproar of interstellar strife, to the advance of starry legions, to the collision of icy suns and the blazing into life of nebulae in the darkened waste; and through it all, continual and faint, like a silver thread, ran the fragile, piping voice of man, a petulant note amid the shrieking of planets and the shattering of worlds.

“The child,” replied Monks, “when her father died in a strange place, in a strange name, without a letter, book, or scrap of paper that yielded the faintest clue by which his friends or relatives could be traced—the child was taken by some wretched cottagers, who reared it as their own.”

“The girl,” Monks answered, “after her father died in an unfamiliar place under an assumed name, leaving no letters, books, or even a shred of paper that might have revealed his true identity—she was taken in by some impoverished locals, who raised her as if she were their own.”

“You graceless girl! How can you have the cruel heart to repeat what they say? Unfeeling thing that you are.... But _I’ll_ see if you or anybody else in the village, or town either, dare do such a thing!” She started off, pacing from fireplace to door, and back again.

“You wretched child! What malice of spirit permits you to give breath to such base rumors? You are a cold and pitiless thing.... But I shall see to it that neither you nor any soul in this parish, nor the borough beyond, dares to utter another word of it!” She broke into a restless stride, pacing a frantic path from the hearth to the threshold and back again.

But, though the Doctor tried hard, and never ceased trying, to get Charles Darnay set at liberty, or at least to get him brought to trial, the public current of the time set too strong and fast for him. The new era began; the king was tried, doomed, and beheaded; the Republic of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death, declared for victory or death against the world in arms; the black flag waved night and day from the great towers of Notre Dame; three hundred thousand men, summoned to rise against the tyrants of the earth, rose from all the varying soils of France, as if the dragon’s teeth had been sown broadcast, and had yielded fruit equally on hill and plain, on rock, in gravel, and alluvial mud, under the bright sky of the South and under the clouds of the North, in fell and forest, in the vineyards and the olive-grounds and among the cropped grass and the stubble of the corn, along the fruitful banks of the broad rivers, and in the sand of the sea-shore. What private solicitude could rear itself against the deluge of the Year One of Liberty--the deluge rising from below, not falling from above, and with the windows of Heaven shut, not opened!

Even as the Doctor poured every ounce of his effort into securing Charles Darnay’s release, or at the very least getting him a formal court hearing, and never once let up in that pursuit, the roaring tide of public sentiment in that era moved far too quickly and powerfully for him to push against. A new age had dawned: the king was put on trial, condemned to death, and publicly beheaded. The Republic, whose rallying cry was Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death, had declared that it would accept nothing short of total victory or utter destruction in its fight against the armed monarchies of the rest of the world. A pitch-black flag flew around the clock from the towering spires of Notre Dame Cathedral. Three hundred thousand men, called to rise up against the tyrants ruling every corner of the globe, had sprung up across every inch of French soil, as if the mythic dragon’s teeth had been scattered far and wide, bearing fruit just as readily on craggy hilltops and flat lowlands, in bare rock, gravel beds, and soft alluvial silt; under the blazing southern sun and the mist-shrouded northern clouds; across windswept moors and dense old forests, through sun-warmed vineyards, gnarled olive groves, pastures of clipped grass and golden corn stubble, along the lush banks of wide, winding rivers, and in the fine sand of the coastline. What small, personal worry could possibly hold its ground against the overwhelming flood of the first year of Liberty – a flood that swelled upward from the ground below rather than pouring down from the sky, with the windows of Heaven sealed shut rather than thrown open to unleash the waters?

"That perhaps is the only way in which wars of this kind will ever be brought to an end. By women insisting on killing the kind of people who make them. Rooting them out. By a campaign of pursuit and assassination that will go on for years and years after the war itself is over.... Murder is such a little gentle punishment for the crime of war.... It would be hardly more than a reproach for what has happened. Falling like snow. Death after death. Flake by flake. This prince. That statesman. The count who writes so fiercely for war.... That is what I am going to do. If Teddy is really dead.... We women were ready enough a year or so ago to starve and die for the Vote, and that was quite a little thing in comparison with this business.... Don't you see what I mean? It's so plain and sensible, Cissie. Whenever a man sits and thinks whether he will make a war or not, then he will think too of women, women with daggers, bombs; of a vengeance that will never tire nor rest; of consecrated patient women ready to start out upon a pilgrimage that will only end with his death.... I wouldn't hurt these war makers. No. In spite of the poison gas. In spite of trench feet and the men who have been made blind and the wounded who have lain for days, dying slowly in the wet. Women ought not to hurt. But I would kill. Like killing dangerous vermin. It would go on year by year. Balkan kings, German princes, chancellors, they would have schemed for so much--and come to just a rattle in the throat.... And if presently other kings and emperors began to prance about and review armies, they too would go....

Perhaps that’s the only way such wars will ever truly stop. By women resolving to eliminate the sort of men who start them. Hunting them down. Through a campaign of tracking and killing that stretches on for years and years beyond the war’s official end.... Murder is such a small, tender penalty for the offense of war.... It would be scarcely more than a quiet rebuke for everything that’s occurred. Drifting down like snow. One death after another. Flake by flake. This prince. That statesman. The count whose editorials roar so fiercely for conflict.... That’s what I mean to do. If Teddy really is gone.... We women were perfectly willing a year or so ago to starve and die for the Vote, and that was a trifling matter next to this undertaking.... Don’t you grasp what I’m saying? It’s so straightforward and logical, Cissie. Whenever a man sits weighing whether to launch a war, he’ll also weigh the thought of women, women with knives, with bombs; of a retribution that never wearies or pauses; of dedicated, patient women prepared to set out on a pilgrimage that ends only with his death.... I wouldn’t wound these war makers. No. Despite the poison gas. Despite trench foot and the men blinded and the wounded who’ve lain for days, slowly dying in the mud. Women should not wound. But I would kill. Like exterminating dangerous vermin. It would continue year after year. Balkan kings, German princes, chancellors, they would have plotted for so much—and come to nothing but a rasp in the throat.... And if soon other kings and emperors started to strut about and inspect armies, they too would go.

Right pleasant people in a sunny land! Yet here pressing relentlessly upon his mind were the murders of Visé, the massacres of Dinant, the massacres of Louvain, murder red-handed and horrible upon an inoffensive people, foully invaded, foully treated; murder done with a sickening cant of righteousness and racial pretension....

Truly delightful people in a land filled with light! Yet his mind was haunted by the persistent shadow of the Visé murders and the massacres at Dinant and Louvain. It was a bloody, horrific violence inflicted on a blameless population, unjustly invaded and brutally handled—slaughter carried out with a sickening veneer of moral justification and racial arrogance.

1 It builds a chain of representations that anticipates what will come next in a story, allowing us to infer measures of suspense. For a given sentence, we use GPT as our word encoder (word enc in Figure 1 ) which turns each word in a sentence into a word embedding w i . Then, we use an RNN (sent enc) to turn the word embeddings of the sentences into a sentence embedding γ i . Each sentence is represented by the hidden state of its last word, which is then fed into a second RNN (story enc) that computes a story embedding. The overall story representation is the hidden state of its last sentence. Crucially, this model also gives us e t , a contextualised representation of the current sentence at point t in the story, to compute surprise and uncertainty reduction. Model training includes a generative loss gen to improve the quality of the sentences generated by the model. We concatenate the word representations w j for all word embeddings in the latest sentence with the latest story embedding e max(t) . This is run through affine ELU layers to produce enriched word embedding representations, analogous to the Deep Fusion model (Gülçehre et al., 2015), with story state instead of a translation model. The related Cold Fusion approach (Sriram et al., 2018) proved inferior.

metadata.title: Modelling Suspense in Short Stories as Uncertainty Reduction over Neural Representation

text: It constructs a sequence of representations that predicts what will happen next in a story, enabling us to derive measures of suspense. For each sentence, we use GPT as our word encoder (word enc in Figure 1), which maps every word in a sentence to a word embedding w_i. We then apply an RNN (sent enc) to transform the word embeddings of the sentence into a sentence embedding γ_i. Each sentence is represented by the hidden state of its final word, which is then passed to a second RNN (story enc) that computes a story embedding. The overall story representation is the hidden state of its final sentence. Importantly, this model also provides us with e_t, a contextualized representation of the current sentence at point t in the story, which is used to compute surprise and uncertainty reduction. Model training also includes a generative loss gen to improve the quality of the sentences produced by the model. We concatenate the word representations w_j for all word embeddings in the most recent sentence with the latest story embedding e_max(t). This is then passed through affine ELU layers to yield enriched word-embedding representations, in a manner analogous to the Deep Fusion model (Gülçehre et al., 2015), but using story state rather than a translation model. The related Cold Fusion approach (Sriram et al., 2018) performed worse.

I closed not my eyes that night. My internal being was in a state of insurrection and turmoil; I felt that order would thence arise, but I had no power to produce it. By degrees, after the morning’s dawn, sleep came. I awoke, and my yesternight’s thoughts were as a dream. There only remained a resolution to return to my ancient studies and to devote myself to a science for which I believed myself to possess a natural talent. On the same day I paid M. Waldman a visit. His manners in private were even more mild and attractive than in public, for there was a certain dignity in his mien during his lecture which in his own house was replaced by the greatest affability and kindness. I gave him pretty nearly the same account of my former pursuits as I had given to his fellow professor. He heard with attention the little narration concerning my studies and smiled at the names of Cornelius Agrippa and Paracelsus, but without the contempt that M. Krempe had exhibited. He said that “These were men to whose indefatigable zeal modern philosophers were indebted for most of the foundations of their knowledge. They had left to us, as an easier task, to give new names and arrange in connected classifications the facts which they in a great degree had been the instruments of bringing to light. The labours of men of genius, however erroneously directed, scarcely ever fail in ultimately turning to the solid advantage of mankind.” I listened to his statement, which was delivered without any presumption or affectation, and then added that his lecture had removed my prejudices against modern chemists; I expressed myself in measured terms, with the modesty and deference due from a youth to his instructor, without letting escape (inexperience in life would have made me ashamed) any of the enthusiasm which stimulated my intended labours. I requested his advice concerning the books I ought to procure.

I didn’t sleep a wink all that night. My whole mind was roiling with chaos and unrest, even as I sensed some kind of clarity would eventually settle out of it—yet I had no power to force that order to come. Slowly, once dawn had broken across the sky, I finally drifted off. When I woke, all the frantic, tangled thoughts I’d had the night before felt like a faded, half-remembered dream. All that remained was a firm resolution to return to my long-held course of study, and devote myself fully to the science I’d always believed I had a natural talent for.

I went to visit Professor Waldman that very same day. He was far gentler and more charming in private than he was in public; the quiet, stately dignity he carried during his lectures softened entirely in his own home, replaced by open, warm affability and kindness. I told him almost the exact same account of my past pursuits that I’d shared with his fellow professor. He listened closely as I walked him through my earlier studies, and smiled when I mentioned the names of Cornelius Agrippa and Paracelsus, with none of the sneering contempt Professor Krempe had shown at those very same names.

“These were men whose relentless, unflagging zeal laid most of the foundation for the knowledge modern scientists build their work on,” he said. “They did the hard work of bringing so many key facts to light; our far simpler task is to give those facts new, standardized names and sort them into connected, logical systems. Even the work of brilliant men, no matter how misdirected it might seem at the time, almost always ends up serving the lasting good of humanity in the end.”

I listened to his words, which he delivered without any trace of arrogance or performative pretension, then told him his lecture had erased all the prejudices I’d held against modern chemists. I spoke in careful, measured terms, with the modesty and deference a young student owes their instructor, careful not to let any of the fiery enthusiasm spurring my planned work slip out; my inexperience with the world would have left me mortified to show that level of unguarded eagerness. I ended by asking him for advice on which books I ought to purchase for my studies.

About two hundred yards off, in the flat, we built a pen of scantlings, about four feet high, and laid planks on it, and so made a platform. We covered it with swell tapestries borrowed for the occasion, and topped it off with the abbot's own throne. When you are going to do a miracle for an ignorant race, you want to get in every detail that will count; you want to make all the properties impressive to the public eye; you want to make matters comfortable for your head guest; then you can turn yourself loose and play your effects for all they are worth. I know the value of these things, for I know human nature. You can't throw too much style into a miracle. It costs trouble, and work, and sometimes money; but it pays in the end. Well, we brought the wires to the ground at the chapel, and then brought them under the ground to the platform, and hid the batteries there. We put a rope fence a hundred feet square around the platform to keep off the common multitude, and that finished the work. My idea was, doors open at 10:30, performance to begin at 11:25 sharp. I wished I could charge admission, but of course that wouldn't answer. I instructed my boys to be in the chapel as early as 10, before anybody was around, and be ready to man the pumps at the proper time, and make the fur fly. Then we went home to supper.

About two hundred yards distant, upon the level ground, we constructed an enclosure of slender timbers, rising some four feet, and laid planks across it to form a stage. This we draped with sumptuous tapestries borrowed for the purpose, and crowned it with the abbot’s own throne. When one means to perform a miracle before an unlettered people, every detail that tells must be attended to; the properties must strike the public gaze, and the chief guest must be seated in comfort; only then can you give yourself free rein and work your effects for all they are worth. I understand such things, for I understand human nature. You cannot invest a miracle with too much ceremony. It costs exertion, labour, and sometimes coin; but it repays itself in the end. We ran the wires down to the chapel wall, then carried them underground to the platform, and concealed the batteries there. Around the stage we set a rope barrier a hundred feet square, to hold back the common crowd, and with that the preparations were complete. My design was to open the doors at half past ten, the performance to begin precisely at twenty-five minutes past eleven. I wished I might charge admission, but naturally that would not serve. I directed my lads to be in the chapel by ten, before any spectators gathered, ready to work the pumps at the appointed moment and make the fur fly. Then we went home to supper.

“Lack-a-day, sir, they be not of that breed! Sir Launcelot will give battle to dragons, and will abide by them, and will assail them again, and yet again, and still again, until he do conquer and destroy them; and so likewise will Sir Pellinore and Sir Aglovale and Sir Carados, and mayhap others, but there be none else that will venture it, let the idle say what the idle will. And, la, as to yonder base rufflers, think ye they have not their fill, but yet desire more?”

“Alas, sir, they are not of that kind! Sir Launcelot will do battle with dragons, and stand his ground against them, and attack them again and yet again, and still again, until he has conquered and destroyed them; and so too will Sir Pellinore and Sir Aglovale and Sir Carados, and perhaps others besides, but there are none else who will dare it, no matter what idle men may say. And indeed, as for those low ruffians yonder, do you think they have not had enough already, but still crave more?”

At four o’clock there was a gentle knock at the door. It was from Charley, who had been sent by Captain Vye to inquire if anything had been heard of Eustacia. The girl who admitted him looked in his face as if she did not know what answer to return, and showed him in to where Venn was seated, saying to the reddleman, “Will you tell him, please?”

At four o’clock there came a soft tap at the door. It was from Ned, who had been sent by Captain Waye to learn whether any news had reached them of Selina. The maid who admitted him looked up into his face as though at a loss for what reply to make, and ushered him in to where Durnford was seated, saying to the younger man, “Will you tell him, please?”

Mr. Bumble grasped the undertaker by the arm, and led him into the building. Mr. Sowerberry was closeted with the board for five minutes; and it was arranged that Oliver should go to him that evening “upon liking”—a phrase which means, in the case of a parish apprentice, that if the master find, upon a short trial, that he can get enough work out of a boy without putting too much food into him, he shall have him for a term of years, to do what he likes with.

Mr. Bumble took the undertaker by the arm and guided him inside. Mr. Sowerberry conferred privately with the board for five minutes, and it was settled that Oliver would be sent to him that very evening "on approval"—a term which, for a parish apprentice, signifies that if the master discovers, after a brief trial, that he can extract sufficient labor from a boy without feeding him too generously, he may keep him for a number of years, to treat as he pleases.

But Martin restored the blonde to Jimmy, and the three of them, with half a dozen friends, watched the revolving couples and laughed and joked with one another. Everybody was glad to see Martin back. No book of his been published; he carried no fictitious value in their eyes. They liked him for himself. He felt like a prince returned from excile, and his lonely heart burgeoned in the geniality in which it bathed. He made a mad day of it, and was at his best. Also, he had money in his pockets, and, as in the old days when he returned from sea with a pay-day, he made the money fly.

But Martin gave the blonde back to Jimmy, and the three of them, with half a dozen friends, watched the whirling couples and laughed and bantered among themselves. Everyone was pleased to see Martin again. No book of his had yet been published; in their eyes he held no fictional worth. They liked him for his own sake. He felt like a prince come home from exile, and his lonely heart opened and blossomed in the warmth that enfolded him. He made a wild day of it and was at his very best. And he had money in his pockets too, and, as in the old days when he came back from sea with his wages, he let the money go freely.

“I don’t know, except that there are many things other people care for which I don’t; and that’s partly why I think I ought to do this. For one thing, my body does not require much of me. I cannot enjoy delicacies; good things are wasted upon me. Well, I ought to turn that defect to advantage, and by being able to do without what other people require I can spend what such things cost upon anybody else.”

“I can’t say for certain, other than that there are so many things other people hold dear that I don’t care for at all; that’s part of why I feel I ought to go through with this. For one thing, I have very few physical needs. Fancy delicacies hold no pleasure for me; all those fine, costly indulgences would be wasted on someone who can’t appreciate them. Well, I ought to turn that quirk of mine to good use: since I can get by perfectly well without all the things other people rely on, I can put the money those things would cost toward anyone who actually needs it.”

“The question was perfectly fair,” he returned—and there was something incongruous in the serious earnestness with which he applied himself to an argument on a valentine. “You know it is always expected that privy inquiries will be made: that’s where the—fun lies.” If the word “fun” had been “torture,” it could not have been uttered with a more constrained and restless countenance than was Boldwood’s then.

“That’s a perfectly reasonable question,” he returned—and there was a strange dissonance to the grave, unyielding earnestness he brought to debating the merits of a handwritten valentine. “You know it’s always taken for granted that quiet, private inquiries will be made: that’s where the—fun of it lies.” If he had swapped the word “fun” for “torture,” it could not have been spoken with a more taut, fidgety countenance than the one Hale wore in that exact instant.

“Why should I not die if I wish?” she said tremulously. “I have made a bad bargain with life, and I am weary of it—weary. And now you have hindered my escape. O, why did you, Charley! What makes death painful except the thought of others’ grief?—and that is absent in my case, for not a sigh would follow me!”

“What right have you to bar my exit?” she cried, her voice trembling with exhaustion. “I have brokered a ruinous debt with existence, and I am tired—so very tired of the struggle. Why did you intervene, Julian? What is the sting of the grave, if not the sorrow of those we leave? Yet I am spared even that, for not a single breath of mourning would follow me into the dark.”

"Our officers are beastly good chaps, nearly all of them. That's where the perplexity of the whole thing comes in. If only they weren't such good chaps! If only they were like the Prussian officers to their men, then we'd just take on a revolution as well as the war, and make everything tidy at once. But they are decent, they are charming.... Only they do not think hard, and they do not understand that doing a job properly means doing it as directly and thought-outly as you possibly can. They won't worry about things. If their tempers were worse perhaps their work might be better. They won't use maps or timetables or books of reference. When we move to a new place they pick up what they can about it by hearsay; not one of our lot has the gumption to possess a contoured map or a Michelin guide. They have hearsay minds. They are fussy and petty and wasteful--and, in the way of getting things done, pretentious. By their code they're paragons of honour. Courage--they're all right about that; no end of it; honesty, truthfulness, and so on--high. They have a kind of horsey standard of smartness and pluck, too, that isn't bad, and they have a fine horror of whiskers and being unbuttoned. But the mistake they make is to class thinking with whiskers, as a sort of fussy sidegrowth. Instead of classing it with unbuttonedupness. They hate economy. And preparation....

Nearly every last one of our officers is a total stand-up guy. That’s exactly what makes this whole situation so baffling. If only they weren’t such decent blokes! If they treated their men the way Prussian officers treat theirs, we could stage a revolution right alongside fighting the war, and sort every last thing out all in one go. But they’re kind, they’re endlessly charming… they just don’t put any real work into thinking things through, and they don’t grasp that doing a job properly means tackling it as intentionally and deliberately as you possibly can. They refuse to fuss over the important details. If they had worse tempers, their work might actually be far stronger. They won’t bother with maps, timetables, or reference books. When we move to a new posting, they only pick up whatever information they can secondhand, from gossip and casual chatter; not a single one of them has the good sense to carry a topographical map or a Michelin guide. They operate entirely on hearsay. They’re nitpicky, small-minded, and wasteful—and when it comes to actually getting tasks completed, they’re full of empty posturing. By their own internal code, they’re perfect paragons of honor. Courage? They’ve got more than enough of that, no argument there. Honesty, trustworthiness, all those values? They rank incredibly high on all of them. They also have that sort of equestrian-adjacent standard of sharpness and pluck that’s not half bad, and they’re properly horrified by unkempt facial hair and walking around with their uniforms half-unbuttoned. But the massive mistake they make is lumping critical thinking in with scraggly beards, writing it off as some fussy, unnecessary side thing, instead of grouping it with being slovenly and unbuttoned, something that should be avoided at all costs. They loathe being economical. And they hate advance planning even more.

When the morning came at last, I was in a bad enough plight: seedy, drowsy, fagged, from want of sleep; weary from thrashing around, famished from long fasting; pining for a bath, and to get rid of the animals; and crippled with rheumatism. And how had it fared with the nobly born, the titled aristocrat, the Demoiselle Alisande la Carteloise? Why, she was as fresh as a squirrel; she had slept like the dead; and as for a bath, probably neither she nor any other noble in the land had ever had one, and so she was not missing it. Measured by modern standards, they were merely modified savages, those people. This noble lady showed no impatience to get to breakfast--and that smacks of the savage, too. On their journeys those Britons were used to long fasts, and knew how to bear them; and also how to freight up against probable fasts before starting, after the style of the Indian and the anaconda. As like as not, Sandy was loaded for a three-day stretch.

By the time dawn finally broke, I was a total wreck: ragged, groggy, and drained from a lack of sleep; sore from tossing and turning, starving from the long fast, and dying for a bath to rid myself of the pests, all while being stiff with rheumatism. And how was the high-born lady, the titled aristocrat Demoiselle Alisande la Carteloise, getting along? Well, she was as lively as a squirrel; she had slept like the dead. As for a bath, it is likely that neither she nor any other noble in the country had ever had one, so she didn't feel its absence. By modern standards, those people were essentially just slightly refined savages. This noblewoman showed no eagerness for breakfast—which is another primitive trait. During their travels, these Britons were used to long periods without food and knew how to endure them; they also knew how to gorge themselves in preparation for a fast, much like a native hunter or an anaconda. In all probability, Sandy had fueled up for a three-day trek.

But for me, he never would have got out. Morgan le Fay hated him with her whole heart, and she never would have softened toward him. And yet his crime was committed more in thoughtlessness than deliberate depravity. He had said she had red hair. Well, she had; but that was no way to speak of it. When red-headed people are above a certain social grade their hair is auburn.

To eliminate unfair comparisons caused by additional information in label names, we propose the L-WPZ+CDT by enhancing the WarmProtoZero (WPZ) model with label name representation same to L-TapNet and incorporating it into the proposed CRF framework. It combines label name embedding and prototype as each label representation. Its improvements over WPZ mainly come from label semantics, collapsed dependency transfer and pair-wise embedding. L-TapNet+CDT outperforms L-WPZ+CDT by 4.79 F1 scores demonstrating the effectiveness of embedding projection. When compared with TapNet+CDT, L-TapNet+CDT achieves an improvement of 4.54 F-score on average, which shows that considering label semantics and prototype helps improve emission score calculation. Table 3 shows the results of 5-shots experiments, which verify the proposed model's generalization ability in more shots situations. The results are consistent with 1-shot setting in general trending.

metadata.title: Few-shot Slot Tagging with Collapsed Dependency Transfer and Label-enhanced Task-adaptive Projection Network

text: To prevent unfair comparisons arising from supplementary information in label names, we introduce L-WPZ+CDT by augmenting the WarmProtoZero (WPZ) model with label name representations identical to those in L-TapNet and integrating it within the proposed CRF framework. This approach fuses label name embeddings with prototypes to form each label representation. Its gains over WPZ primarily stem from label semantics, collapsed dependency transfer, and pair-wise embedding. L-TapNet+CDT surpasses L-WPZ+CDT by 4.79 F1 points, underscoring the benefit of embedding projection. Relative to TapNet+CDT, L-TapNet+CDT yields an average improvement of 4.54 F-score, indicating that jointly leveraging label semantics and prototypes enhances emission score computation. Table 3 reports 5-shot experimental outcomes, confirming the model’s generalization capacity under increased shot conditions. These findings align with the overall trends observed in the 1-shot setting.

At the very instant when he brought the loop over his head previous to slipping it beneath his arm-pits, and when the old gentleman before-mentioned (who had clung so tight to the railing of the bridge as to resist the force of the crowd, and retain his position) earnestly warned those about him that the man was about to lower himself down—at that very instant the murderer, looking behind him on the roof, threw his arms above his head, and uttered a yell of terror.

Right at the moment he’d slipped the loop over his head, just before drawing it down to fit beneath his armpits, and the aforementioned elderly gentleman—who’d clung so firmly to the bridge railing he’d fended off the press of the crowd and kept his place—was urgently warning everyone nearby that the man was about to lower himself over the side, that very same instant the murderer glanced behind him from the roof, flung both arms above his head, and let out a raw yell of terror.

Troy entered Weatherbury churchyard about ten o’clock and went immediately to the corner where he had marked the vacant grave early in the morning. It was on the obscure side of the tower, screened to a great extent from the view of passers along the road—a spot which until lately had been abandoned to heaps of stones and bushes of alder, but now it was cleared and made orderly for interments, by reason of the rapid filling of the ground elsewhere.

Troy stepped into Weatherbury churchyard at roughly ten o’clock, and made straight for the corner where he’d marked out the empty grave plot first thing that morning. It sat on the unobtrusive side of the tower, almost entirely hidden from the view of people passing along the adjacent road. Until just a short while before, that spot had been left forgotten, heaped with piles of loose stone and thick alder bushes, but it had recently been cleared and straightened out to accommodate new burials, as the rest of the cemetery grounds were filling up at a rapid clip.

A gigantic beauty of a stallion, fresh and responsive to my caresses,
Head high in the forehead, wide between the ears,
Limbs glossy and supple, tail dusting the ground,
Eyes full of sparkling wickedness, ears finely cut, flexibly moving.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
A towering beauty of a stallion, quick to answer my touch,  
His head lifted high, broad between the ears,  
His limbs polished and yielding, his tail brushing the dust,  
His eyes bright with a glint of mischief, his ears sharp-cut, softly alive.

Any one of these partners would have disinherited his son on the question of rebuilding Tellson’s. In this respect the House was much on a par with the Country; which did very often disinherit its sons for suggesting improvements in laws and customs that had long been highly objectionable, but were only the more respectable.

Each of these partners would have disinherited his son over the mere suggestion of modernizing Tellson’s. In this respect, the firm was much like the country, which often cast out its own citizens for recommending improvements to laws and customs that were clearly harmful, yet were considered all the more venerable for their very antiquity.

“Mealy. I know a friend who has a beef-faced boy; a fine boy, they call him; with a round head, and red cheeks, and glaring eyes; a horrid boy; with a body and limbs that appear to be swelling out of the seams of his blue clothes; with the voice of a pilot, and the appetite of a wolf. I know him! The wretch!”

Dusty. I am familiar with a companion who harbors a mutton-jowled youth; a 'grand boy,' as he is called; with a spherical cranium, crimsoned jowls, and staring orbs; a detestable creature; with a torso and limbs that appear to be bursting from the seams of his indigo attire; possessing the roar of a boatswain and the voracity of a beast. I recognize him! The miscreant!

On the ground the groups of villagers were still occupied in doing all they could to keep down the conflagration, which was not much. They were all tinged orange, and backed up by shadows of varying pattern. Round the corner of the largest stack, out of the direct rays of the fire, stood a pony, bearing a young woman on its back. By her side was another woman, on foot. These two seemed to keep at a distance from the fire, that the horse might not become restive.

The villagers on the ground still labored to quell the blaze, though their efforts availed little. All of them were stained orange, with shadows of shifting shapes pressing behind them. Around the edge of the largest stack, just beyond the fire’s direct glare, a pony stood carrying a young woman on its back. Beside her, on foot, was another woman. They kept well back from the flames, careful not to unsettle the horse.

“Let her see all she wants of him,” was Mr. Morse’s advice. “The more she knows him, the less she’ll love him, I wager. And give her plenty of contrast. Make a point of having young people at the house. Young women and young men, all sorts of young men, clever men, men who have done something or who are doing things, men of her own class, gentlemen. She can gauge him by them. They will show him up for what he is. And after all, he is a mere boy of twenty-one. Ruth is no more than a child. It is calf love with the pair of them, and they will grow out of it.”

“Let her spend as much time with that boy as she pleases,” was Mr. Hale’s blunt counsel. “The more she learns who he really is, the faster that silly infatuation will fizzle, I’d put good money on it. And make sure she’s got plenty of points of comparison, too. Go out of your way to host young folks at the house—girls her age, and all manner of young men: sharp, driven men who’ve already built something of themselves, or are well on their way to doing so, men of her own social station, proper gentlemen. She’ll hold him up next to them, and they’ll lay bare exactly how unfit he is. He’s barely twenty-one, after all, still little more than a boy himself. Lila’s hardly more than a child either. This is nothing but puppy love on both their parts, and they’ll grow out of it soon enough.”

They fished with a spade, at first. Presently the honoured parent appeared to be adjusting some instrument like a great corkscrew. Whatever tools they worked with, they worked hard, until the awful striking of the church clock so terrified Young Jerry, that he made off, with his hair as stiff as his father’s.

They began by fishing with a spade. After a while, the respected father seemed to be setting some contrivance in order, a thing like a monstrous corkscrew. Whatever tools they used, they used them laboriously, and kept at it with all their might, until the dreadful clang of the church clock so alarmed Little Ned that he fled away, his hair standing as bristly as his father’s.

Martin seemed suddenly to wake up. He opened the kit bag and oiled his wheel, putting graphite on the chain and adjusting the bearings. Joe was halfway down to the saloon when Martin passed by, bending low over the handle-bars, his legs driving the ninety-six gear with rhythmic strength, his face set for seventy miles of road and grade and dust. He slept in Oakland that night, and on Sunday covered the seventy miles back. And on Monday morning, weary, he began the new week’s work, but he had kept sober.

It was like Martin snapped out of a daze all at once. He popped open his gear bag, lubed his bike wheel, rubbed graphite onto the chain, and tweaked the bearings until they sat just right. Joe was already halfway to the bar when Martin zoomed past him, hunched low over the handlebars, legs pumping the 96-gear with steady, powerful rhythm, his jaw set for the full seventy miles of rough pavement, steep grades, and swirling dust ahead. He crashed for the night in Oakland, then turned around and covered the same seventy-mile trip back on Sunday. Come Monday morning, he showed up to start the new workweek exhausted, but he had stayed completely sober the whole time.

“It is of no importance,” said Troy, lightly. “Well, I shall be down in the fields with you some time this week; but I have a few matters to attend to first. So good-day to you. We shall, of course, keep on just as friendly terms as usual. I’m not a proud man: nobody is ever able to say that of Sergeant Troy. However, what is must be, and here’s half-a-crown to drink my health, men.”

“It hardly matters at all,” said Hale, flicking a fleck of road dust from his uniform cuff with a lazy, dismissive grin. “I’ll be out joining you lads in the lower fields one day this week, mind – but there’s a handful of loose ends I need to tie up before I can get away. So I bid you good day for now. We’ll keep on as friendly terms as we always have, obviously. I’ve never been one for putting on airs: no soul alive could ever claim Corporal Hale thinks too highly of himself. But what’s fated is fated, eh? Here’s half a crown for you lot to buy a round and toast my good health, lads.”

It was on a dreary night of November that I beheld the accomplishment of my toils. With an anxiety that almost amounted to agony, I collected the instruments of life around me, that I might infuse a spark of being into the lifeless thing that lay at my feet. It was already one in the morning; the rain pattered dismally against the panes, and my candle was nearly burnt out, when, by the glimmer of the half-extinguished light, I saw the dull yellow eye of the creature open; it breathed hard, and a convulsive motion agitated its limbs.

It was a grim, dreary November night when I finally stood witness to the fruit of all my grueling toil. I was twisted up with an anxiety so sharp it edged close to outright agony as I arranged my life-giving instruments around me, ready to breathe a spark of being into the lifeless form stretched out on the floor at my feet. It was already one in the morning; rain pattered miserably against the windowpanes, and my candle was guttering down to its final dregs, when in the faint, wavering glow of the half-extinguished light, I saw the creature’s murky yellow eye flutter open. It pulled in a harsh, ragged breath, and a sharp convulsive tremor shook through all of its limbs.

Yeobright did not interrupt the preparations, and went home again. The next morning, when Thomasin withdrew the curtains of her bedroom window, there stood the Maypole in the middle of the green, its top cutting into the sky. It had sprung up in the night, or rather early morning, like Jack’s bean-stalk. She opened the casement to get a better view of the garlands and posies that adorned it. The sweet perfume of the flowers had already spread into the surrounding air, which, being free from every taint, conducted to her lips a full measure of the fragrance received from the spire of blossom in its midst. At the top of the pole were crossed hoops decked with small flowers; beneath these came a milk-white zone of Maybloom; then a zone of bluebells, then of cowslips, then of lilacs, then of ragged-robins, daffodils, and so on, till the lowest stage was reached. Thomasin noticed all these, and was delighted that the May revel was to be so near.

Clym did not pause the bustle, and returned to his dwelling. At dawn, when Tamsin drew back the linen from her chamber casement, the Maypole already pierced the common’s heart, its crown needling the heavens. It had risen in the darkness—or earliest morning—like a stalk from a fairy tale. She pushed the lattice wide to study the wreaths and nosegays that clothed it. The honeyed breath of the blossoms had already seeped into the unstained air, which bore to her mouth an undiluted draught of the scent cascading from that floral steeple. At the summit, crossed hoops wore a fretwork of tiny blooms; below them hung a creamy band of hawthorn; then a girdle of harebells, then cowslips, then lilac, then ragged-robin, narcissus, and onward, until the lowest tier was met. Tamsin marked each ring, and rejoiced that the Maying would unfold so close at hand.

The young lady—for youth had revealed its presence in her buoyant bound up the bank—walked along the top instead of descending inside, and came to the corner where the fire was burning. One reason for the permanence of the blaze was now manifest: the fuel consisted of hard pieces of wood, cleft and sawn—the knotty boles of old thorn trees which grew in twos and threes about the hillsides. A yet unconsumed pile of these lay in the inner angle of the bank; and from this corner the upturned face of a little boy greeted her eyes. He was dilatorily throwing up a piece of wood into the fire every now and then, a business which seemed to have engaged him a considerable part of the evening, for his face was somewhat weary.

The young woman’s vitality was evident in the spirited way she ascended the slope. Rather than stepping down into the hollow, she kept to the ridge until she reached the spot where the fire glowed. The reason the flames burned so steadily was now clear: the fuel was made of dense, split logs—the gnarled trunks of old thorn trees that grew in small clusters across the hills. A stack of this wood sat in the crook of the bank, and from beside it, a young boy looked up at her. He was slowly feeding the fire, tossing in a fresh piece every few minutes with a listless air that suggested he had been at this work for hours, for his face looked quite worn.

But his chief trouble was that he did not know any editors or writers. And not merely did he not know any writers, but he did not know anybody who had ever attempted to write. There was nobody to tell him, to hint to him, to give him the least word of advice. He began to doubt that editors were real men. They seemed cogs in a machine. That was what it was, a machine. He poured his soul into stories, articles, and poems, and intrusted them to the machine. He folded them just so, put the proper stamps inside the long envelope along with the manuscript, sealed the envelope, put more stamps outside, and dropped it into the mail-box. It travelled across the continent, and after a certain lapse of time the postman returned him the manuscript in another long envelope, on the outside of which were the stamps he had enclosed. There was no human editor at the other end, but a mere cunning arrangement of cogs that changed the manuscript from one envelope to another and stuck on the stamps. It was like the slot machines wherein one dropped pennies, and, with a metallic whirl of machinery had delivered to him a stick of chewing-gum or a tablet of chocolate. It depended upon which slot one dropped the penny in, whether he got chocolate or gum. And so with the editorial machine. One slot brought checks and the other brought rejection slips. So far he had found only the latter slot.

His greatest difficulty, however, was that he knew no editors and no writers. In fact, he knew no one at all who had ever tried to write. There was no one to instruct him, to suggest a thing, to offer even the faintest piece of advice. He began to wonder whether editors were even real people. They seemed more like parts of a mechanism. And that was what it was: a mechanism. He poured his heart into stories, articles, and poems, and committed them to the machine. He folded them exactly right, enclosed the proper stamps inside the long envelope with the manuscript, sealed it, added more stamps on the outside, and dropped it into the mailbox. It crossed the continent, and after a certain time the mailman brought the manuscript back in yet another long envelope, with the very stamps he had enclosed pasted on the outside. There had been no human editor at the far end, only a clever contrivance of gears that transferred the manuscript from one envelope to another and affixed the stamps. It was like those slot machines into which one dropped pennies and, with a clatter of metal machinery, received either a stick of chewing gum or a tablet of chocolate. Which one came out depended on which slot the penny went into. And so it was with the editorial machine. One slot produced checks, the other rejection slips. Up to now, he had discovered only the latter slot.

Her gaze rested for a moment on the muscular neck, heavy corded, almost bull-like, bronzed by the sun, spilling over with rugged health and strength. And though he sat there, blushing and humble, again she felt drawn to him. She was surprised by a wanton thought that rushed into her mind. It seemed to her that if she could lay her two hands upon that neck that all its strength and vigor would flow out to her. She was shocked by this thought. It seemed to reveal to her an undreamed depravity in her nature. Besides, strength to her was a gross and brutish thing. Her ideal of masculine beauty had always been slender gracefulness. Yet the thought still persisted. It bewildered her that she should desire to place her hands on that sunburned neck. In truth, she was far from robust, and the need of her body and mind was for strength. But she did not know it. She knew only that no man had ever affected her before as this one had, who shocked her from moment to moment with his awful grammar.

Her eyes lingered briefly on the powerful neck, thickly sinewed and nearly bovine, darkened by the sun and brimming with robust vitality. Even as he remained there, flushed and meek, she felt herself drawn toward him once more. A reckless notion darted into her mind and startled her. She imagined that if she could just press both hands against that neck, all its vigor and force would pour into her. The thought appalled her. It seemed to uncover a hidden depravity she had never suspected in herself. To her, strength had always seemed coarse and animalistic. Her notion of male beauty had ever favored lean elegance. Still, the thought clung stubbornly. It perplexed her that she should long to set her hands on that sun-darkened neck. In fact, she was anything but sturdy, and what her body and spirit craved was strength. But she was unaware of it. She only knew that no man had ever stirred her as this one did, who continually jolted her with his dreadful grammar.

I let it go at that. Then I read in a low voice, to as many as could crowd their shaven heads within hearing distance, part of the account of the miracle of the restoration of the well, and was accompanied by astonished and reverent ejaculations all through: “Ah-h-h!” “How true!” “Amazing, amazing!” “These be the very haps as they happened, in marvelous exactness!” And might they take this strange thing in their hands, and feel of it and examine it?--they would be very careful. Yes. So they took it, handling it as cautiously and devoutly as if it had been some holy thing come from some supernatural region; and gently felt of its texture, caressed its pleasant smooth surface with lingering touch, and scanned the mysterious characters with fascinated eyes. These grouped bent heads, these charmed faces, these speaking eyes --how beautiful to me! For was not this my darling, and was not all this mute wonder and interest and homage a most eloquent tribute and unforced compliment to it? I knew, then, how a mother feels when women, whether strangers or friends, take her new baby, and close themselves about it with one eager impulse, and bend their heads over it in a tranced adoration that makes all the rest of the universe vanish out of their consciousness and be as if it were not, for that time. I knew how she feels, and that there is no other satisfied ambition, whether of king, conqueror, or poet, that ever reaches half-way to that serene far summit or yields half so divine a contentment.

I didn’t push the matter further. Then I read aloud softly, pitched to carry only to the cluster of people who’d pressed in close enough to fit their shaved heads within earshot, a section of the account of the well’s miraculous restoration. The entire reading was punctuated by shocked, reverent exclamations: “Ohhhh!” “That’s exactly how it went!” “Incredible, just incredible!” “These are the actual events, word for word, perfectly spot-on!”

When they asked if they could hold this strange, precious object in their own hands to feel and examine it—promising they would be as careful as could be—I said yes. They passed it between them, handling it as cautiously and devoutly as if it were a sacred relic sent down from some otherworldly realm. They ran their fingers lightly over its texture, lingered to stroke its warm, smooth surface, and stared at the mysterious printed characters with rapt, fascinated eyes.

All those huddled, bowed heads, those entranced faces, those bright, expressive eyes—they struck me as so deeply beautiful. Wasn’t this little volume my most cherished darling, after all? And wasn’t all that quiet awe, eager curiosity, and unprompted respect the most eloquent, sincere tribute and unforced compliment it could ever receive?

In that moment, I understood exactly how a mother feels when other women, be they strangers or friends, crowd around to hold her newborn baby, leaning in all at once out of pure, urgent affection, their heads bowed over the infant in a rapt, adoring haze that wipes the entire rest of the universe from their consciousness, like nothing else exists for those few seconds. I knew that quiet, soaring joy, and I knew no other fulfilled ambition—whether that of a king, a conqueror, or a celebrated poet—ever comes even halfway to that serene, distant high, or delivers half as much divine, unshakable contentment.

A sacred thing was this book her lodger had made, a fetich of friendship. It softened the blow of his having been a laundryman, and though she could not understand a line of it, she knew that every line of it was great. She was a simple, practical, hard-working woman, but she possessed faith in large endowment.

He wasn’t boasting or trying to impress. All he wanted was to bring her joy, to make her proud, to prove her years of belief in him had been worth it. She placed the book in the front room, resting it on the family Bible. This volume her boarder had created was something hallowed, a token of friendship. It eased the sting of his past as a laundry worker, and even though she couldn’t grasp a single sentence, she understood that every sentence was important. She was a plain, pragmatic, industrious woman, yet she carried an abundant gift of faith.

And if Martin wants to be one of them, I miss my guess. But what’s all that got to do with Herbert Spencer anyway? Martin’s just discovered Spencer, and he’s wild over him. Why? Because Spencer is taking him somewhere. Spencer couldn’t take me anywhere, nor you. We haven’t got anywhere to go. You’ll get married some day, and I’ll have nothing to do but keep track of the lawyers and business agents who will take care of the money my father’s going to leave me.”

“Smart enough, I’ll give you that,” came the sharp retort, “but it’s perfectly fair, too. The only people who actually know their Latin are pharmacists, barristers, and classics department lecturers. And if Elias wants to end up as any of those, I’ve badly misjudged him. But what on earth does any of that have to do with Herbert Spencer, anyway? Elias just stumbled across Spencer’s work a little while back, and he’s utterly wild for it. Why? Because Spencer gives him a path to follow, somewhere to go. Spencer would never lead me anywhere, and he wouldn’t do a thing for you either. We don’t have anyplace we need to reach in the first place. You’ll get married and settle down eventually, and all I’ll ever have to occupy myself with is checking in on the solicitors and financial managers who handle the fortune my father’s set to leave me.”

Our approach is inspired by the deliberation process when a human expert is deducing a semantic graph from a sentence. The output graph starts from an empty graph and spans incrementally in a node-by-node manner. At any time step of this process, we are distilling the information for the next expansion. We call it expansion because the new node, as an abstract concept of some specific text fragments in the input sentence, is derived to complete some missing elements in the current semantic graph. Specifically, given the input sentence and the current partially constructed graph, we are answering two critical questions: which part of the input sequence to abstract, and where in the output graph to construct the new concept. For instance, Figure 1 (a) and (b) show two possible choices for the next expansion.
