text: About two hours after this occurrence we heard the ground sea, and before night the ice broke and freed our ship. We, however, lay to until the morning, fearing to encounter in the dark those large loose masses which float about after the breaking up of the ice. I profited of this time to rest for a few hours.

Some two hours later, the surge of the ground sea became audible, and before nightfall the ice fractured, setting our vessel free. We remained hove-to until morning, however, fearing the dark and those massive, drifting floes that wander after the pack breaks up. I seized this time to find a few hours of rest.

Once, it entered his mind that there was a deliberate rebuff in all this. He caught her spirit of antagonism and strove to divine the message that her hands pronounced upon the keys. Then he dismissed the thought as unworthy and impossible, and yielded himself more freely to the music. The old delightful condition began to be induced. His feet were no longer clay, and his flesh became spirit; before his eyes and behind his eyes shone a great glory; and then the scene before him vanished and he was away, rocking over the world that was to him a very dear world. The known and the unknown were commingled in the dream-pageant that thronged his vision. He entered strange ports of sun-washed lands, and trod market-places among barbaric peoples that no man had ever seen. The scent of the spice islands was in his nostrils as he had known it on warm, breathless nights at sea, or he beat up against the southeast trades through long tropic days, sinking palm-tufted coral islets in the turquoise sea behind and lifting palm-tufted coral islets in the turquoise sea ahead. Swift as thought the pictures came and went. One instant he was astride a broncho and flying through the fairy-colored Painted Desert country; the next instant he was gazing down through shimmering heat into the whited sepulchre of Death Valley, or pulling an oar on a freezing ocean where great ice islands towered and glistened in the sun. He lay on a coral beach where the cocoanuts grew down to the mellow-sounding surf. The hulk of an ancient wreck burned with blue fires, in the light of which danced the _hula_ dancers to the barbaric love-calls of the singers, who chanted to tinkling _ukuleles_ and rumbling tom-toms. It was a sensuous, tropic night. In the background a volcano crater was silhouetted against the stars. Overhead drifted a pale crescent moon, and the Southern Cross burned low in the sky.

For a moment it crossed his mind that there was intent in the slight. He had felt the strain of her hostility, and he tried to read the meaning her fingers were uttering on the keyboard. Then he put the notion away as absurd and impossible, and surrendered himself the more wholly to the strain of the music. The old enchantment began again to steal over him. His feet were no longer of clay, and his body seemed turned to spirit; before his eyes and behind them there shone a vast radiance; and then the room dissolved, and he was gone, drifting upon the world that was to him so dear. The familiar and the strange mingled in the dream-procession that crowded his sight. He came into far harbors of sun-drenched coasts, and wandered through bazars among savage peoples no man had ever beheld. The odor of the spice islands filled his nostrils as it had on hot, breathless nights at sea, or he drove into the southeast trades through long tropical days, leaving behind him palm-clad coral islets in the blue-green sea and raising palm-clad coral islets in the blue-green sea before him. The visions flashed by with the speed of thought. In one instant he was on a mustang, riding hard through the radiant country of the Painted Desert; in the next he peered through wavering heat into the chalk-white tomb of Death Valley, or rowed across a frozen ocean where towering ice islands shone and sparkled in the sun. He stretched on a coral strand where the coco palms leaned down to the soft, murmuring surf. The hulk of some ancient wreck flamed with sapphire fire, and in that glow the hula girls moved to the wild love-songs of the chanters, who sang to the bright plink of ukuleles and the deep boom of tom-toms. It was a lush, tropical night. Behind them a volcano’s crater stood black against the stars. Above, a slender moon drifted pale through the heavens, and the Southern Cross burned faintly low in the sky.

We follow the setting of the vocabularies described in Zhu et al. (2019) . In En2ZhSum, we surround each target sentence with tags "<t>" and "</t>". If there is no special explanation, the limit on the number of translation candidate m in our models is set to 10. All the parameters are initialized via Xavier initialization method (Glorot and Bengio, 2010) . We train our models using configuration transformer base (Vaswani et al., 2017) , which contains a 6-layer encoder and a 6-layer decoder with 512-dimensional hidden representations. Each mini-batch contains a set of document-summary pairs with roughly 3,072 source and 3,072 target tokens. We apply Adam optimizer (Kingma and Ba, 2015) with β 1 = 0.9, β 2 = 0.998, and = 10 −9 . For evaluation, we use beam search with a beam size 4 and length penalty 0.6. All our methods are trained and tested on a single NVIDIA TITAN XP.

metadata.title: Attend, Translate and Summarize: An Efficient Method for Neural Cross-Lingual Summarization

text: We adopt the vocabulary configuration described in Zhu et al. (2019). In En2ZhSum, each target sentence is enclosed within the tags "<t>" and "</t>". Unless otherwise specified, the maximum number of translation candidates m in our models is set to 10. All parameters are initialized using the Xavier initialization scheme (Glorot and Bengio, 2010). We train our models with the Transformer base configuration (Vaswani et al., 2017), which consists of a 6-layer encoder and a 6-layer decoder with 512-dimensional hidden states. Each mini-batch comprises a set of document–summary pairs containing approximately 3,072 source tokens and 3,072 target tokens. We use the Adam optimizer (Kingma and Ba, 2015) with β₁ = 0.9, β₂ = 0.998, and ε = 10⁻⁹. For evaluation, we employ beam search with a beam size of 4 and a length penalty of 0.6. All our methods are trained and tested on a single NVIDIA TITAN XP.

“I stand aside, messieurs, by my heap of stones, to see the soldiers and their prisoner pass (for it is a solitary road, that, where any spectacle is well worth looking at), and at first, as they approach, I see no more than that they are six soldiers with a tall man bound, and that they are almost black to my sight--except on the side of the sun going to bed, where they have a red edge, messieurs. Also, I see that their long shadows are on the hollow ridge on the opposite side of the road, and are on the hill above it, and are like the shadows of giants. Also, I see that they are covered with dust, and that the dust moves with them as they come, tramp, tramp! But when they advance quite near to me, I recognise the tall man, and he recognises me. Ah, but he would be well content to precipitate himself over the hill-side once again, as on the evening when he and I first encountered, close to the same spot!”

I step aside, gentlemen, beside my pile of stones, to watch the soldiers and their captive go by (for this is a lonely road, where any sight is worth observing), and at first, as they draw near, I see only that they are six soldiers escorting a tall man in restraints, and they appear nearly black to my eyes—except along the edge where the sun is setting, where they glow with a crimson rim, gentlemen. I also notice their stretched shadows falling across the sunken ridge on the far side of the road, and climbing the slope beyond it, resembling the shadows of giants. I notice too that they are coated in dust, and the dust travels with them as they advance, tramp, tramp! But when they come quite close to me, I know the tall man, and he knows me. Ah, how gladly he would fling himself down the hillside once more, just as on that evening when he and I first met, near this very place!

We go on with studying bilingual word embedding by quantitative analysis of the new word pairs, which are detected by searching bilingual words that are neighbors in the word embedding space, and evaluate them using the ground-truth bilingual dictionary. In particular, we split the Muse dictionary of Chinese-to-English into standard training set and test set as in BLI (Artetxe et al., 2018a) . The training set is used for the dictionary-based systems, including our AT/Biview AT, UNMT+SWET, and Muse, which is a BLI toolkit. The test set is used to evaluate these systems by computing the precision of discovered translation words given the source words in the test set. The neighborhood is computed by CSLS distance (Conneau et al., 2018) . Table 3 shows the precision, where precision@k indicates the accuracy of top-k predicted candidate. Muse induces new word pairs through either the supervised way or the unsupervised way. MuseSupervised is better than MuseUnsupervised since it is supervised by the ground-truth bilingual dictionary. Our AT/Bi-view AT surpasses MuseSupervised by a large margin. UNMT+SWET/UWET also obtains good performance through the word embedding transformation. Bi-view AT significantly surpasses UNMT+SWET/UWET in precision@5 and precision@10, while is worse than them in precision@1. This indicates that Bi-view AT can produce better n-best translation words that are beneficial for NMT beam decoding to find better translations.

metadata.title: Bilingual Dictionary Based Neural Machine Translation without Using Parallel Sentences

text: We continue our investigation of bilingual word embeddings by quantitatively analyzing newly identified word pairs—those discovered by searching for bilingual words that are neighbors in the embedding space—and evaluating them against a ground-truth bilingual dictionary. Specifically, we partition the Muse Chinese–English dictionary into a standard training set and test set following the BLI setup (Artetxe et al., 2018a). The training set is employed for dictionary-based systems, including our AT and Bi-view AT, UNMT+SWET, and Muse, which is a BLI toolkit. The test set is used to assess these systems by measuring the precision of translation words retrieved for the source words in the test set. Neighborhoods are determined using the CSLS distance (Conneau et al., 2018). Table 3 reports the precision, where precision@k denotes the accuracy of the top-k predicted candidates. Muse induces new word pairs in either a supervised or an unsupervised manner. MuseSupervised outperforms MuseUnsupervised because it is guided by the ground-truth bilingual dictionary. Our AT and Bi-view AT exceed MuseSupervised by a substantial margin. UNMT+SWET/UWET also achieves strong performance via word embedding transformation. Bi-view AT significantly surpasses UNMT+SWET/UWET in precision@5 and precision@10, though it is slightly weaker in precision@1. This suggests that Bi-view AT generates higher-quality n-best translation word lists, which are advantageous for NMT beam search in identifying better translations.

When he had reclined for some considerable time, gloomily pondering, he discerned above the ferns a drawn bonnet of white silk approaching from the left, and Yeobright knew directly that it covered the head of her he loved. His heart awoke from its apathy to a warm excitement, and, jumping to his feet, he said aloud, “I knew she was sure to come.”

After he had rested there for a long while, brooding in a dark mood, he caught sight of a white silk bonnet rising above the ferns to his left. Yeobright knew instantly that it was the woman he loved. His heart shook off its lethargy, replaced by a sudden, glowing thrill; he leaped to his feet and exclaimed, “I was certain she would come.”

Mr. Van der Pant had had shots at various Zeppelins. When the Zeppelins came to Antwerp everybody turned out on the roofs and shot at them. He was contemptuous of Zeppelins. He made derisive gestures to express his opinion of them. They could do nothing unless they came low, and if they came low you could hit them. One which ventured down had been riddled; it had had to drop all its bombs--luckily they fell in an open field--in order to make its lame escape. It was all nonsense to say, as the English papers did, that they took part in the final bombardment. Not a Zeppelin.... So he talked, and the Britling family listened and understood as much as they could, and replied and questioned in Anglo-French. Here was a man who but a few days ago had been steering his bicycle in the streets of Antwerp to avoid shell craters, pools of blood, and the torn-off arms and shoulder-blades of women. He had seen houses flaring, set afire by incendiary bombs, and once at a corner he had been knocked off his bicycle by the pouff of a bursting shell.... Not only were these things in the same world with us, they were sitting at our table.

Mr. Van der Pant had frequently taken aim at the Zeppelins. Whenever the airships loomed over Antwerp, the residents would gather on their rooftops to fire at them. He held the vessels in complete contempt, using mocking gestures to convey his disdain for their presence. They were powerless unless they flew low, he insisted, and if they did, they became easy targets. He described one that had descended only to be riddled with gunfire; it had been forced to jettison its entire payload—which fortunately struck an empty field—simply to make a crippled escape. He dismissed the reports in the English press as utter nonsense; not a single Zeppelin had participated in the final bombardment. 

As he recounted these events, the Britlings listened, following as best they could and offering questions in a fractured Anglo-French. Sitting before them was a man who, just days prior, had been cycling through the streets of Antwerp, steering around shell craters, pools of blood, and the severed limbs of women. He had watched houses go up in flames from incendiary strikes and had once been blown off his bicycle by the concussive blast of a shell. These nightmares were not merely part of the same world; they were present, sharing a meal at their very table.

Bathsheba remained firm in this opinion till Monday, when two circumstances conjoined to shake it. The first was a short paragraph in the local newspaper, which, beyond making by a methodizing pen formidable presumptive evidence of Troy’s death by drowning, contained the important testimony of a young Mr. Barker, M.D., of Budmouth, who spoke to being an eyewitness of the accident, in a letter to the editor. In this he stated that he was passing over the cliff on the remoter side of the cove just as the sun was setting. At that time he saw a bather carried along in the current outside the mouth of the cove, and guessed in an instant that there was but a poor chance for him unless he should be possessed of unusual muscular powers. He drifted behind a projection of the coast, and Mr. Barker followed along the shore in the same direction. But by the time that he could reach an elevation sufficiently great to command a view of the sea beyond, dusk had set in, and nothing further was to be seen.

Bathsheba held unwaveringly to her conviction until Monday, when two coinciding circumstances shattered her certainty. The first was a short item in the local newspaper, which, set down in the precise, methodical prose of a careful writer, laid out formidable, highly credible evidence that Troy had drowned, alongside the critical testimony of young Dr. Barker of Budmouth, who wrote to the editor to state he had witnessed the accident firsthand. In his account, he noted he had been traversing the cliffs on the cove’s far side just as the sun was sinking below the horizon. At that moment, he spotted a bather caught in the current just outside the cove’s mouth, and knew instantly the man had precious little chance of survival unless he possessed uncommon physical strength. The swimmer drifted behind a rocky outcrop of the coastline, and Barker followed along the shore in the same direction. But by the time he managed to reach a vantage point high enough to see the stretch of sea beyond the rock, dusk had settled, and no further sign of the man could be found.

He didn't know what it was. The tranquillity which I now enjoyed did not endure. Memory brought madness with it, and when I thought of what had passed, a real insanity possessed me; sometimes I was furious and burnt with rage, sometimes low and despondent. I neither spoke nor looked at anyone, but sat motionless, bewildered by the multitude of miseries that overcame me.

The brief peace I had found soon withered away. My memories ushered in a state of frenzy, and whenever I reflected on what had transpired, a true delirium seized my mind. I swung between fits of searing rage and depths of hollow despair. I became silent and unseeing, sitting in a frozen stupor, paralyzed by the overwhelming weight of the tragedies that had befallen me.

Sunday he had intended to devote to studying for the high school examination, but the pearl-diving article lured him away, and he spent the day in the white-hot fever of re-creating the beauty and romance that burned in him. The fact that the _Examiner_ of that morning had failed to publish his treasure-hunting article did not dash his spirits. He was at too great a height for that, and having been deaf to a twice-repeated summons, he went without the heavy Sunday dinner with which Mr. Higginbotham invariably graced his table. To Mr. Higginbotham such a dinner was advertisement of his worldly achievement and prosperity, and he honored it by delivering platitudinous sermonettes upon American institutions and the opportunity said institutions gave to any hard-working man to rise—the rise, in his case, which he pointed out unfailingly, being from a grocer’s clerk to the ownership of Higginbotham’s Cash Store.

Sunday was meant for cramming for the high school exam, but the article on pearl diving seduced him, and he surrendered the hours to the white-hot thrill of reshaping the beauty and romance blazing inside him. That morning’s _Examiner_ hadn’t printed his treasure-hunting piece, yet his mood stayed aloft, untouched. Ignoring two calls to the table, he skipped the hefty Sunday dinner Mr. Higginbotham always laid on as a trophy of his worldly success. To Mr. Higginbotham, that meal proclaimed his prosperity, and he sanctified it with trite little sermons on American institutions and the chances they offered any industrious man to climb—his own climb, as he never failed to note, having lifted him from grocery clerk to proprietor of Higginbotham’s Cash Store.

I must look in at Tellson’s; so I will go there at once and come back presently. Then, we will take him a ride into the country, and dine there, and all will be well.”

“I think,” he whispered to Mrs. Baines, after a troubled pause of thought, “I think it would be wiser not to address him at present, nor in any way stir him. I must step into Harwood’s; so I shall go there immediately and return in a little while. Then we will carry him out for a drive into the country, dine there, and everything will be right.”

Miss Corner was drawn into the sunset party. She talked to Mr. Carmine and displayed, Mr. Direck thought, great originality of mind. She said "The City of the Sun" was like the cities the boys sometimes made on the playroom floor. She said it was the dearest little city, and gave some amusing particulars. She described the painted walls that made the tour of the Civitas Solis a liberal education. She asked Mr. Carmine, who was an authority on Oriental literature, why there were no Indian nor Chinese Utopias.

Miss Corner was drawn into the sunset party. She spoke with Mr. Carmine and, Mr. Direck thought, showed a remarkable originality of mind. She said that “The City of the Sun” was like the cities the boys sometimes built on the playroom floor. She called it the dearest little city and went on to give some entertaining details. She described the painted walls, which made the circuit of the Civitas Solis a liberal education. Then she asked Mr. Carmine, who was an authority on Oriental literature, why there were no Indian or Chinese Utopias.

They sat round a little table under the cedar trees amusing themselves with these experiments, and after that Cecily and Mr. Britling and the two small boys entertained themselves by drawing pigs with their eyes shut, and then Mr. Britling and Teddy played hard at Badminton until it was time for tea. And Cecily sat by Mr. Direck and took an interest in his accident, and he told her about summer holidays in the Adirondacks and how he loved to travel. She said she would love to travel. He said that so soon as he was better he would go on to Paris and then into Germany. He was extraordinarily curious about this Germany and its tremendous militarism. He'd far rather see it than Italy, which was, he thought, just all art and ancient history. His turn was for modern problems. Though of course he didn't intend to leave out Italy while he was at it. And then their talk was scattered, and there was great excitement because Herr Heinrich had lost his squirrel.

The group clustered around a small table beneath the cedar boughs, passing the time with their little experiments. Once that wrapped up, Cecily, Mr. Britling, and the two young boys amused themselves by sketching pigs with their eyes closed, before Mr. Britling and Teddy launched into a fierce game of badminton that lasted right up until tea was due to be served. Cecily sat beside Mr. Direck in the meantime, asking after the details of his injury, and he told her of summer holidays spent in the Adirondacks, and how much he loved to wander the world. She admitted she ached to travel too. He explained that as soon as he recovered fully, he planned to head first to Paris, then on into Germany. He was intensely curious about the country, and its pervasive, overwhelming militarism; he’d far rather explore that than Italy, which he dismissed as little more than a landscape of art and ancient history. His interest lay squarely in modern issues, though he did not intend to skip Italy entirely on his trip, of course. Their conversation was soon broken off, though, as a flurry of excitement swept the gathering: Herr Heinrich’s pet squirrel had gone missing.

Defarge refreshed himself with a draught of wine--but, he took less than was given to the stranger, as being himself a man to whom it was no rarity--and stood waiting until the countryman had made his breakfast. He looked at no one present, and no one now looked at him; not even Madame Defarge, who had taken up her knitting, and was at work.

Laurent roused himself with a swallow of rough red wine—he took far less than he’d served the unknown traveler, for it was no scarce luxury to a man who lived above the tavern’s cellar—and stood waiting while the hill country farmer finished his morning meal. He cast no glance at any person present, and not a soul in the taproom paid him any mind now; not even his wife Elodie, who had picked up her knitting and was working stitches in steady, unhurried motions.

Thus it had come to pass, that Tellson’s was the triumphant perfection of inconvenience. After bursting open a door of idiotic obstinacy with a weak rattle in its throat, you fell into Tellson’s down two steps, and came to your senses in a miserable little shop, with two little counters, where the oldest of men made your cheque shake as if the wind rustled it, while they examined the signature by the dingiest of windows, which were always under a shower-bath of mud from Fleet-street, and which were made the dingier by their own iron bars proper, and the heavy shadow of Temple Bar. If your business necessitated your seeing “the House,” you were put into a species of Condemned Hold at the back, where you meditated on a misspent life, until the House came with its hands in its pockets, and you could hardly blink at it in the dismal twilight. Your money came out of, or went into, wormy old wooden drawers, particles of which flew up your nose and down your throat when they were opened and shut. Your bank-notes had a musty odour, as if they were fast decomposing into rags again. Your plate was stowed away among the neighbouring cesspools, and evil communications corrupted its good polish in a day or two. Your deeds got into extemporised strong-rooms made of kitchens and sculleries, and fretted all the fat out of their parchments into the banking-house air. Your lighter boxes of family papers went up-stairs into a Barmecide room, that always had a great dining-table in it and never had a dinner, and where, even in the year one thousand seven hundred and eighty, the first letters written to you by your old love, or by your little children, were but newly released from the horror of being ogled through the windows, by the heads exposed on Temple Bar with an insensate brutality and ferocity worthy of Abyssinia or Ashantee.

So it was that Tellson’s had reached a state of absolute, triumphant inconvenience. Once you had wrenched open a door that resisted with a stubborn, rattling gasp, you tumbled down two steps into the bank, finding yourself in a cramped and wretched shop. At two small counters, ancient clerks caused your check to flutter as if caught in a draft while they scrutinized your signature by the filthiest windows imaginable. These panes were constantly pelted by the mud of Fleet Street and further obscured by thick iron bars and the oppressive shadow of Temple Bar. Should your affairs require an audience with "the House," you were confined to a sort of death-row cell in the rear; there, you might ponder a life of regret until the management arrived, hands in pockets, barely discernible in the murky twilight. Your cash was pulled from or deposited into decaying wooden drawers, which released clouds of fine rot into your lungs every time they were handled. The banknotes emitted a stale, damp scent, as though they were rapidly reverting to the shredded rags from which they were born. Any silver you left for safekeeping was stored near the local sewers, where the foul vapors stripped away its luster within forty-eight hours. Your legal deeds were shoved into makeshift vaults that had once been kitchens, where the humid air seemed to draw the very oil from the parchment. Lighter crates of family records were carried to an upstairs room—a hollow dining hall containing a massive table that never saw a feast. There, even in 1780, the tenderest letters from lost loves or small children were kept in the grim shadow of the severed heads displayed on Temple Bar, a display of mindless ferocity that would have seemed barbaric in the most savage of lands.

paring to ground truth. Three types of error are referred to Missing (Mi), Redundant (Re) and Wrong (Wr) in Table 10 . For NLG, semantic errors can be either missing or generating wrong slot values in the given semantics (Wen et al., 2015) . Our model makes fewer mistakes in all these error sources comparing to the baseline Decoupled. We believe this is because the clustering property learned in the latent space provides better feature representations at a global scale, eventually benefiting NLU and NLG.

metadata.title: A Generative Model for Joint Natural Language Understanding and Generation

text: Table 10 categorizes errors relative to the ground truth into three types: Missing (Mi), Redundant (Re), and Wrong (Wr). In NLG, semantic errors typically involve either omitting slot values or generating incorrect ones for a given semantics (Wen et al., 2015). Compared with the Decoupled baseline, our model commits fewer errors across all these categories. We attribute this improvement to the clustering structure acquired in the latent space, which yields more globally coherent feature representations and consequently enhances both NLU and NLG performance.

At this point of Mr. Bumble’s discourse, Oliver, just hearing enough to know that some allusion was being made to his mother, recommenced kicking, with a violence that rendered every other sound inaudible. Sowerberry returned at this juncture. Oliver’s offence having been explained to him, with such exaggerations as the ladies thought best calculated to rouse his ire, he unlocked the cellar-door in a twinkling, and dragged his rebellious apprentice out, by the collar.

At this point in Mr. Bumble’s speech, Oliver, catching only enough to understand that some reference was being made to his mother, began kicking again, with such fury that all other sounds were drowned out. At that moment Sowerberry came back. Once Oliver’s misconduct had been explained to him, with all the embellishments the ladies considered most likely to stir his anger, he unlocked the cellar door in an instant, and hauled his mutinous apprentice out by the collar.

One thing troubled me along at first--the immense interest which people took in me. Apparently the whole nation wanted a look at me. It soon transpired that the eclipse had scared the British world almost to death; that while it lasted the whole country, from one end to the other, was in a pitiable state of panic, and the churches, hermitages, and monkeries overflowed with praying and weeping poor creatures who thought the end of the world was come. Then had followed the news that the producer of this awful event was a stranger, a mighty magician at Arthur's court; that he could have blown out the sun like a candle, and was just going to do it when his mercy was purchased, and he then dissolved his enchantments, and was now recognized and honored as the man who had by his unaided might saved the globe from destruction and its peoples from extinction. Now if you consider that everybody believed that, and not only believed it, but never even dreamed of doubting it, you will easily understand that there was not a person in all Britain that would not have walked fifty miles to get a sight of me. Of course I was all the talk--all other subjects were dropped; even the king became suddenly a person of minor interest and notoriety. Within twenty-four hours the delegations began to arrive, and from that time onward for a fortnight they kept coming. The village was crowded, and all the countryside. I had to go out a dozen times a day and show myself to these reverent and awe-stricken multitudes. It came to be a great burden, as to time and trouble, but of course it was at the same time compensatingly agreeable to be so celebrated and such a center of homage. It turned Brer Merlin green with envy and spite, which was a great satisfaction to me. But there was one thing I couldn't understand--nobody had asked for an autograph. I spoke to Clarence about it. By George! I had to explain to him what it was. Then he said nobody in the country could read or write but a few dozen priests. Land! think of that.

Initially, one thing bothered me: the overwhelming public fascination I inspired. It seemed as though every soul in the kingdom was desperate for a glimpse of me. Word quickly spread that the eclipse had terrified the populace nearly to the point of collapse. While the darkness lasted, the entire country had been gripped by a pathetic hysteria; every chapel, cell, and monastery was packed with sobbing wretches convinced that Judgment Day had arrived. Then came the reports that the architect of this cataclysm was a newcomer, a sorcerer of immense power at King Arthur’s court. Rumor had it that he could have extinguished the sun as easily as a candle flame, and was on the verge of doing so until his compassion was won. He had supposedly broken his own spell and was now hailed as the savior who, single-handedly, had rescued the earth from ruin and humanity from death. 

Given that everyone accepted this tale as absolute truth, never even entertaining a doubt, it is easy to see why anyone in Britain would have trekked fifty miles just to see me. Naturally, I was the sole topic of conversation, and all other matters were forgotten; even the King found himself eclipsed in notoriety. Within a day, envoys started appearing, and they continued to pour in for two weeks. The village and the surrounding fields were swamped. I was forced to step outside several times a day to present myself to these worshipful, terrified crowds. While it became a tedious drain on my time, there was an undeniable pleasure in being the object of such reverence. Best of all, it turned old Merlin sick with jealousy, which gave me immense satisfaction. Yet, one detail baffled me: no one had requested an autograph. I mentioned this to Clarence, and good grief, I actually had to explain the concept to him. He informed me that, aside from a handful of clerics, not a single person in the land could read or write. Good heavens, imagine that.

“As there is ample time I will tell you, though you know well enough. I mean that it is extraordinary that you should be alone in my absence. Tell me, now, where is he who was with you on the afternoon of the thirty-first of August? Under the bed? Up the chimney?”

“Since the hour permits, I shall be explicit, though you surely grasp my intent. It strikes me as singularly odd that you should be found quite so solitary during my time away. Pray, inform me: where is that gentleman who shared your company on the final afternoon of August? Is he concealed beneath the divan? Or perhaps wedged within the flue?”

Came a beautiful fall day, warm and languid, palpitant with the hush of the changing season, a California Indian summer day, with hazy sun and wandering wisps of breeze that did not stir the slumber of the air. Filmy purple mists, that were not vapors but fabrics woven of color, hid in the recesses of the hills. San Francisco lay like a blur of smoke upon her heights. The intervening bay was a dull sheen of molten metal, whereon sailing craft lay motionless or drifted with the lazy tide. Far Tamalpais, barely seen in the silver haze, bulked hugely by the Golden Gate, the latter a pale gold pathway under the westering sun. Beyond, the Pacific, dim and vast, was raising on its sky-line tumbled cloud-masses that swept landward, giving warning of the first blustering breath of winter.

A magnificent autumn day arrived, warm and slow-moving, charged with the quiet intensity of the shifting season. It was a true California Indian summer, marked by a misty sun and stray currents of air that left the stillness of the atmosphere undisturbed. Ethereal purple hazes, less like vapor and more like tapestries of pure pigment, tucked themselves into the hollows of the hills. San Francisco appeared as a faint smudge of smoke upon its ridges. The bay stretched out between them with the flat luster of liquid metal, its surface dotted with sailboats that sat frozen or wandered with the sluggish tide. Distant Tamalpais, shrouded in a silver glow, loomed beside the Golden Gate, which lay like a shimmering amber road under the sinking sun. Farther out, the vast and shadowy Pacific churned up great heaps of clouds on the horizon, sending them shoreward to signal the first cold gusts of the coming winter.

A reserved and sombre expression had returned to Boldwood’s face again, but it poorly cloaked his feelings when he pronounced Bathsheba’s name; and his feverish anxiety continued to show its existence by a galloping motion of his fingers upon the side of his thigh as he went down the stairs.

Boldwood’s face had settled back into its usual guarded, melancholy cast, yet the mask slipped the moment he spoke Bathsheba’s name, betraying him utterly. As he descended the stairs, his restless agitation betrayed itself still, his fingers drumming a rapid, uneven beat against his thigh.

Then he went to a locked closet, and took from a locked drawer therein a small circular case the size of a pillbox, and was about to put it into his pocket. But he lingered to open the cover and take a momentary glance inside. It contained a woman’s finger-ring, set all the way round with small diamonds, and from its appearance had evidently been recently purchased. Boldwood’s eyes dwelt upon its many sparkles a long time, though that its material aspect concerned him little was plain from his manner and mien, which were those of a mind following out the presumed thread of that jewel’s future history.

Then he crossed to a fastened cupboard, and from a fastened drawer within it took a little round case, no larger than a pillbox, and was on the point of slipping it into his pocket. Yet he paused, detaining it just long enough to lift the lid and cast a fleeting look within. There lay a woman’s ring, encircled all around with small diamonds, and from its look it had plainly been but lately bought. His eyes rested for a long while upon its many glances, though that the thing itself had little hold upon his thoughts was evident from his bearing and countenance, which were those of a man whose mind was tracing, as he supposed, the future history of that jewel.

The cloth was laid for supper; the table was covered with bread and butter, plates and glasses; a porter-pot and a wine-bottle. At the upper end of the table, Mr. Noah Claypole lolled negligently in an easy-chair, with his legs thrown over one of the arms: an open clasp-knife in one hand, and a mass of buttered bread in the other. Close beside him stood Charlotte, opening oysters from a barrel: which Mr. Claypole condescended to swallow, with remarkable avidity. A more than ordinary redness in the region of the young gentleman’s nose, and a kind of fixed wink in his right eye, denoted that he was in a slight degree intoxicated; these symptoms were confirmed by the intense relish with which he took his oysters, for which nothing but a strong appreciation of their cooling properties, in cases of internal fever, could have sufficiently accounted.

The supper cloth was spread ready for the meal, the table loaded with bread and butter, plates and glasses, a stout porter pot and a bottle of wine. At the head of the table, Mr. Noah Claypole slouched lazily in an easy chair, his legs slung carelessly over one armrest: an open pocket knife in one hand, a thick chunk of buttered bread in the other. Standing just beside him was Charlotte, prying oysters open from a barrel: morsels Mr. Claypole deigned to swallow with sharp, greedy enthusiasm. An unusual bright redness across the young gentleman’s nose, and a sort of fixed, half-winking twitch in his right eye, marked that he was slightly intoxicated; these symptoms were confirmed by the fierce relish with which he ate his oysters, a preference that could only be adequately explained by a strong awareness of their cooling qualities, perfect for soothing the internal fever brought on by drink.

Extracting alignments with attention-based models works well when used in combination with greedy translation inference (Li et al., 2019) . However, the alignment task involves predicting an alignment between a sentence and an observed translation, which requires forced decoding. When a token in the target sentence is unexpected given the preceding target prefix, attention activations computed during forced decoding are not reliable because they do not explicitly condition on the target word being aligned. Zenkel et al. (2019) introduce a method called attention optimization, which searches for attention activations that maximize the probability of the output sequence by directly optimizing the attention activations A in the alignment layer using gradient descent for the given sentence pair (x, y) to maximize the probability of each observed target token y i while keeping all other parameters of the neural network M fixed:

metadata.title: End-to-End Neural Word Alignment Outperforms GIZA++

text: He realized how much he slept, and how much he desired to sleep. Of old, he had hated sleep. It had robbed him of precious moments of living. Four hours of sleep in the twenty-four had meant being robbed of four hours of life. How he had grudged sleep! Now it was life he grudged. Life was not good; its taste in his mouth was without tang, and bitter. This was his peril. Life that did not yearn toward life was in fair way toward ceasing. Some remote instinct for preservation stirred in him, and he knew he must get away. He glanced about the room, and the thought of packing was burdensome. Perhaps it would be better to leave that to the last. In the meantime he might be getting an outfit.

It hit him just how much time he spent asleep these days, and how badly he craved even more of it. Once, he’d loathed sleep, seen it as a thief snatching precious hours he could have spent fully alive. Four hours of rest out of every twenty-four had felt like four full hours of his life ripped away; he’d begrudged every single second he spent unconscious. Now, though, it was waking life he resented. Nothing about being alive felt good anymore; the very taste of existence on his tongue was flat, no spark to it at all, just a sharp, lingering bitterness. That was the danger he was in. When a life had no drive to keep going, it was already halfway to fading out entirely. Some faint, distant survival instinct tugged at the back of his mind, and he knew he had to get out of there. He scanned the room around him, and the thought of packing up all his things felt so heavy and draining he could barely stomach it. Maybe it made more sense to leave that task for the very end. In the meanwhile, he could go pick up the travel outfit and supplies he’d need first.

“You shall know directly. Write on; there are but a few words more.” He dictated again. “‘I am thankful that the time has come, when I can prove them. That I do so is no subject for regret or grief.’” As he said these words with his eyes fixed on the writer, his hand slowly and softly moved down close to the writer’s face.

“You’ll learn what this is about right away. Keep writing; there are only a few more words left.” He began dictating again. “‘I am grateful the moment has finally arrived when I can prove them true. That I do so is no cause for regret or sorrow.’” His eyes remained locked on the writer as he spoke the words, and his hand moved slowly, gently downward until it hovered just beside the writer’s face.

“I know you cannot. But he stole in in my absence and robbed me. Why didn’t he win you away before, when nobody would have been grieved?—when nobody would have been set tale-bearing. Now the people sneer at me—the very hills and sky seem to laugh at me till I blush shamefully for my folly. I have lost my respect, my good name, my standing—lost it, never to get it again. Go and marry your man—go on!”

“I understand you can’t. But he crept in while I was gone and plundered me. Why didn’t he take you earlier, when no one would have mourned?—when no one would have whispered stories. Now the folk mock me—the very hills and heavens appear to jeer at me until I flush with disgrace at my own foolishness. I’ve forfeited my honor, my reputation, my place—lost it, and I’ll never reclaim it. Go and wed your man—go ahead!”

Bathsheba was far from dreaming that the dark and silent shape upon which she had so carelessly thrown a seed was a hotbed of tropic intensity. Had she known Boldwood’s moods, her blame would have been fearful, and the stain upon her heart ineradicable. Moreover, had she known her present power for good or evil over this man, she would have trembled at her responsibility. Luckily for her present, unluckily for her future tranquillity, her understanding had not yet told her what Boldwood was. Nobody knew entirely; for though it was possible to form guesses concerning his wild capabilities from old floodmarks faintly visible, he had never been seen at the high tides which caused them.

Bathsheba had no idea that the dark, silent figure into which she had so carelessly cast a seed contained a furnace of tropical intensity. Had she understood Boldwood’s moods, her self-reproach would have been dreadful, and the stain upon her conscience impossible to erase. And had she realized the power she now held, for good or for ill, over this man, she would have shuddered at the weight of such responsibility. Fortunately for her at that moment, though disastrously for her later peace, she had not yet grasped what Boldwood truly was. No one understood him completely; although one could infer something of his wild possibilities from the old floodmarks still faintly visible, he had never been seen in the high tides that had made them.

“Well, no. A philosopher once said in my hearing that the old builders, who worked when art was a living thing, had no respect for the work of builders who went before them, but pulled down and altered as they thought fit; and why shouldn’t we? ‘Creation and preservation don’t do well together,’ says he, ‘and a million of antiquarians can’t invent a style.’ My mind exactly. I am for making this place more modern, that we may be cheerful whilst we can.”

“Well, no. I once heard a philosopher remark that the ancient builders, those who labored when art was still alive, held no reverence for their predecessors’ work—they tore down and reshaped as they saw fit. Why shouldn’t we do the same? ‘Creation and preservation are ill-matched companions,’ he said, ‘and a million antiquarians could never devise a new style.’ That echoes my own thoughts exactly. I believe in modernizing this place, so we might enjoy some cheer while we still can.”

“I have saved you from being ill-used once, and I will again, and I do now,” continued the girl aloud; “for those who would have fetched you, if I had not, would have been far more rough than me. I have promised for your being quiet and silent; if you are not, you will only do harm to yourself and me too, and perhaps be my death. See here! I have borne all this for you already, as true as God sees me show it.”

“I got you out of being mistreated once, I’ll do it again, and I’m doing it right now,” the girl said loudly. “If I hadn’t come for you, the people who were supposed to fetch you would’ve been way rougher than I am. I already gave my word you’d stay calm and keep quiet. If you don’t, you’ll only hurt both yourself and me, and you might even be the reason I end up dead. Look here! I’ve already put up with all of this for you, and God as my witness, every bit of what I’m saying is true.”

Martin had enjoyed the fight, with a recrudescence of the old fighting thrills. But they quickly died away, and he was oppressed by a great sadness. He felt very old—centuries older than those careless, care-free young companions of his others days. He had travelled far, too far to go back. Their mode of life, which had once been his, was now distasteful to him. He was disappointed in it all. He had developed into an alien. As the steam beer had tasted raw, so their companionship seemed raw to him. He was too far removed. Too many thousands of opened books yawned between them and him. He had exiled himself. He had travelled in the vast realm of intellect until he could no longer return home. On the other hand, he was human, and his gregarious need for companionship remained unsatisfied. He had found no new home. As the gang could not understand him, as his own family could not understand him, as the bourgeoisie could not understand him, so this girl beside him, whom he honored high, could not understand him nor the honor he paid her. His sadness was not untouched with bitterness as he thought it over.

Martin relished the conflict, feeling a brief revival of his former combative spirit. However, the sensation faded rapidly, leaving him burdened by a profound melancholy. He felt incredibly old—eons older than the reckless, happy-go-lucky friends of his youth. He had moved too far along his path to ever retreat. Their lifestyle, which he once embraced, now seemed repulsive to him. He felt let down by everything; he had become a total outsider. Just as the local beer tasted harsh, their presence felt unrefined. The distance between them was immense, an abyss created by the countless books he had read. He was an exile of his own making, having ventured so far into the intellectual wilderness that his old home was unreachable. Yet, being human, his natural desire for community remained starving. He hadn't found a new sanctuary. His old friends, his family, and the social elite all failed to understand him, and even the girl at his side—the one he placed on a pedestal—could not grasp his true self or the depth of his respect for her. A touch of bitterness colored his sorrow as he dwelled on his isolation.

It is scarcely necessary to state that Gabriel could not avoid noting the farmer’s bearing to-night towards their entertainer. Yet there was nothing exceptional in his actions beyond what appertained to his time of performing them. It was when the rest were all looking away that Boldwood observed her; when they regarded her he turned aside; when they thanked or praised he was silent; when they were inattentive he murmured his thanks. The meaning lay in the difference between actions, none of which had any meaning of itself; and the necessity of being jealous, which lovers are troubled with, did not lead Oak to underestimate these signs.

Gabriel could hardly fail to mark, without need of saying so, the farmer’s conduct that evening toward their hostess. Still, there was nothing singular in his gestures save the moment he chose for them. While the others averted their gaze, Boldwood watched her; when they looked her way, he glanced off; if they offered thanks or compliments, he kept quiet; if their attention strayed, he whispered his gratitude. The import resided in the contrast among acts that, taken singly, signified nothing; and the jealous watchfulness that afflicts lovers did not prevent Oak from reading these tokens truly.

Gabriel’s farm had been stocked by a dealer—on the strength of Oak’s promising look and character—who was receiving a percentage from the farmer till such time as the advance should be cleared off. Oak found that the value of stock, plant, and implements which were really his own would be about sufficient to pay his debts, leaving himself a free man with the clothes he stood up in, and nothing more.

Elias Thorne’s upland farm had been fully stocked by a regional supply merchant—who’d agreed to the arrangement solely on the weight of Thorne’s earnest bearing and solid local reputation—who collected a fixed percentage of the farm’s monthly earnings until the full sum of the initial advance was repaid. When Thorne tallied all his holdings, he discovered the total worth of livestock, crop stores, and tools that rightfully belonged to him was barely enough to settle all his outstanding debts, leaving him unburdened by obligations but owning nothing beyond the scuffed work clothes he had on his back.

No holiday soldiers--youthful, yet veterans,
Worn, swart, handsome, strong, of the stock of homestead and workshop,
Harden’d of many a long campaign and sweaty march,
Inured on many a hard-fought bloody field.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
No festive soldiers—young, and yet already veterans,  
Rugged, dark-skinned, handsome, strong, drawn from homestead and workshop,  
Seared by many a long campaign and sweat-soaked march,  
Tempered on many a hard-won, bloodied field.

"The old system of life was organisation. That is where Germany is still the most ancient of European states. It's a reversion to a tribal cult. It's atavistic.... To organise or discipline, or mould characters or press authority, is to assume that you have reached finality in your general philosophy. It implies an assured end. Heinrich has his assured end, his philological professorship or thereabouts as a part of the Germanic machine. And that too has its assured end in German national assertion. Here, we have none of those convictions. We know we haven't finality, and so we are open and apologetic and receptive, rather than wilful.... You see all organisation, with its implication of finality, is death. We feel that. The Germans don't. What you organise you kill. Organised morals or organised religion or organised thought are dead morals and dead religion and dead thought. Yet some organisation you must have. Organisation is like killing cattle. If you do not kill some the herd is just waste. But you musn't kill all or you kill the herd. The unkilled cattle are the herd, the continuation; the unorganised side of life is the real life. The reality of life is adventure, not performance. What isn't adventure isn't life. What can be ruled about can be machined. But priests and schoolmasters and bureaucrats get hold of life and try to make it _all_ rules, _all_ etiquette and regulation and correctitude.... And parents and the love of parents make for the same thing. It is all very well to experiment for oneself, but when one sees these dear things of one's own, so young and inexperienced and so capable of every sort of gallant foolishness, walking along the narrow plank, going down into dark jungles, ah! then it makes one want to wrap them in laws and foresight and fence them about with 'Verboten' boards in all the conceivable aspects...."

The old way of life was built on structure. That’s why Germany remains the most archaic of European nations—a throwback to tribal worship, a kind of atavism. To organise, to discipline, to shape character or impose authority, is to act as if you’ve arrived at a final truth in your overall philosophy. It presupposes a fixed destination. Heinrich has his fixed destination, his professorship in philology or something like it, slotted into the Germanic machinery. And that, in turn, has its fixed destination in German national self-assertion. Over here, we hold no such certainties. We know we haven’t reached finality, so we stay open, apologetic, receptive, instead of willful. You see, all organisation, because it implies finality, is death. We sense that. The Germans don’t. Whatever you organise, you kill. Organised morals, organised religion, organised thought—these are dead morals, dead religion, dead thought. And yet some organisation is necessary. Organisation is like slaughtering cattle. If you don’t kill some, the herd simply goes to waste. But you mustn’t kill them all, or you destroy the herd itself. The unkilled cattle are the herd, the ongoing life; the unorganised side of existence is the real life. The essence of life is adventure, not achievement. What isn’t adventure isn’t life. What can be governed can be mechanised. But priests and schoolmasters and bureaucrats seize hold of life and try to turn it entirely into rules, entirely into etiquette and regulation and correctness. And parents, and parental love, push toward the same end. It’s all very well to experiment on one’s own, but when you watch those dear creatures of your own, so young and untried and so ready for every kind of splendid folly, stepping along the narrow plank, descending into dark jungles—ah, then you long to swaddle them in laws and foresight and fence them round with “Verboten” signs on every conceivable side.

His right hand was lifted to his mouth; lost entirely in his own thoughts as he gnawed at his overgrown, blackened nails, he revealed between his mostly toothless gums a handful of sharp, jagged fangs that belonged far more properly to a stray dog or a scavenging rat.

A tremendous roar arose from the throat of Saint Antoine, and a forest of naked arms struggled in the air like shrivelled branches of trees in a winter wind: all the fingers convulsively clutching at every weapon or semblance of a weapon that was thrown up from the depths below, no matter how far off.

A tremendous roar rose from the throat of Saint Antoine, and a forest of bare arms fought upward in the air like withered branches of trees in a winter wind: all the fingers convulsively clutching at every weapon, or at any object that might pass for one, flung up from the depths below, no matter how distant.

The first remedy is to replace the hard symbolic comparison of natural language "labels" with a soft comparison of texts' meaning, using semantic vector space representations. Recently, a number of MT evaluation methods appeared focusing on semantic comparison of reference and system translations (Shimanaka et al., 2018; Clark et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019) . While these correlate better than n-gram overlap metrics with human assessments, they do not address inherent limitations stemming from the need for reference translations, namely: (1) references are expensive to obtain; (2) they assume a single correct solution and bias the evaluation, both automatic and human (Dreyer and Marcu, 2012; Fomicheva and Specia, 2016) , and (3) limitation of MT evaluation to language pairs with available parallel data.

metadata.title: On the Limitations of Cross-lingual Encoders as Exposed by Reference-Free Machine Translation Evaluation

text: The first approach involves substituting rigid symbolic matching of natural language "labels" with a flexible semantic comparison of textual meaning grounded in vector space representations. In recent years, several machine translation evaluation techniques have emerged that center on the semantic comparison of reference and system outputs (Shimanaka et al., 2018; Clark et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019). Although these methods correlate more strongly with human judgments than n-gram overlap metrics, they do not overcome fundamental constraints arising from the reliance on reference translations, specifically: (1) references are costly to produce; (2) they presuppose a single valid rendering and introduce bias into both automatic and human evaluation (Dreyer and Marcu, 2012; Fomicheva and Specia, 2016); and (3) they restrict MT evaluation to language pairs for which parallel data are available.

His eloquence is forcible and touching; nor can I hear him, when he relates a pathetic incident or endeavours to move the passions of pity or love, without tears. What a glorious creature must he have been in the days of his prosperity, when he is thus noble and godlike in ruin! He seems to feel his own worth and the greatness of his fall.

We don’t always limit our conversations to his personal history and the misfortunes he’s endured. On every subject within general literature, he showcases a staggering breadth of knowledge and a sharp, perceptive grasp of nuance. He speaks with such compelling, heartfelt eloquence that I never manage to hold back tears when he recounts a devastating story, or tries to rouse feelings of pity or tenderness in those listening. If he carries himself with this much dignity and almost divine grace even in ruin, I can only fathom how extraordinary he must have been in the height of his success. He seems fully conscious of both his own inherent worth, and the sheer magnitude of how far he has fallen.

Yet there was nothing to anchor the idea; the wagon, though headed toward Weatherbury, could easily pass through and beyond it, and the woman they spoke of sounded like the owner of some property. She was dancing to wondrous music, and her partner was the man in silver armour who had accompanied her through the previous fantastic changes, the visor of his helmet being closed. The mazes of the dance were ecstatic. Soft whispering came into her ear from under the radiant helmet, and she felt like a woman in Paradise. Suddenly these two wheeled out from the mass of dancers, dived into one of the pools of the heath, and came out somewhere into an iridescent hollow, arched with rainbows. “It must be here,” said the voice by her side, and blushingly looking up she saw him removing his casque to kiss her. At that moment there was a cracking noise, and his figure fell into fragments like a pack of cards.

Out of the shifting pageant, a quieter vision slowly took shape: the heath emerged in shadowy outline behind the brilliant foreground. She was dancing to unearthly music, partnered by the silver-armored man who had moved beside her through all the earlier fantastic transformations, his helmet’s visor shut. The dance’s winding steps were rapturous. A soft whisper reached her ear from beneath the gleaming helmet, and she felt herself a woman in Paradise. Suddenly the two of them spun away from the crowd of dancers, plunged into one of the heath’s dark pools, and surfaced in a shimmering hollow vaulted with rainbows. “It must be here,” said the voice at her side, and when she looked up, blushing, she saw him lifting his helmet to kiss her. In that instant came a sharp crack, and his figure shattered like a pack of cards.

Why, man, I could offend you by asking you to eat something.”

Though Mr. Britling's anxiety about his son was dispelled, his mind remained curiously apprehensive throughout July. He had a feeling that things were not going well with the world, a feeling he tried in vain to dispel by various distractions. Perhaps some subtler subconscious analysis of the situation was working out probabilities that his conscious self would not face. And when presently he bicycled off to Mrs. Harrowdean for flattery, amusement, and comfort generally, he found her by no means the exalting confirmation of everything he wished to believe about himself and the universe, that had been her delightful rôle in the early stages of their romantic friendship. She maintained her hostility to Edith; she seemed bent on making things impossible. And yet there were one or two phases of the old sustaining intimacies.

Even though Mr. Britling no longer felt anxious about his son, a strange, unshakable sense of foreboding stayed with him throughout the whole of July. He carried a quiet certainty that the world was going awry, a feeling he tried and failed to shake off with all manner of trivial diversions. It may well have been that some subtle, subconscious breakdown of the circumstances around him was calculating risks his conscious mind refused to acknowledge. When he eventually bicycled over to Mrs. Harrowdean’s seeking flattery, lighthearted fun, and general comfort, however, he found she was far from the uplifting, approving presence who had validated every one of his beliefs about himself and the wider world back in the giddy early days of their romantic friendship. She still clung to her open hostility toward Edith, and seemed set on making their entire dynamic unworkable. Even so, faint traces of their old, sustaining intimacy still surfaced between them on occasion.

“He’ll be all right after a time,” she assured him, while she wondered what the trouble was that Martin was in. “But you’d best get a job first an’ steady down. Bernard does like to see a man at honest work. That stuff in the newspapers broke ’m all up. I never saw ’m so mad before.”

“He’ll be all right after a while,” she told him, though she was wondering what kind of trouble Martin had got himself into. “But you’d better get a job first and settle down. Bernard does like to see a man at honest work. That stuff in the newspapers upset him badly. I never saw him so angry before.”

In parallel, individual attention heads of Transformer models have also received much focus. Clark et al. (2019) showed that individual heads recognize standard Part of Speech (POS) and Dependency (DEP) relationships (e.g., Objects of the Preposition (POBJ) and Determinants (DET)) with high fidelity. Vig and Belinkov (2019) also explored the dependency relations across heads and discovered that initial layers typically encode positional relations, middle layers capture the most dependency relations, and later layers look for unique patterns and structures. These insights are exposed interactively through EXBERT.

metadata.title: EXBERT: A Visual Analysis Tool to Explore Learned Representations in Transformer Models

text: Concurrently, the functional properties of individual attention heads in Transformer architectures have garnered significant targeted research attention. Clark et al. (2019) demonstrated that standalone heads can identify standard part-of-speech (POS) tags and dependency (DEP) relations, including prepositional objects (POBJ) and determiners (DET), with high fidelity to human-annotated labels. Vig and Belinkov (2019) further explored cross-head dependency encoding patterns across model depth, finding that early layers predominantly represent positional relational information, middle layers capture the vast majority of dependency relations, and upper layers specialize in detecting unique linguistic patterns and structural properties. These empirical insights are made available for interactive exploration through the EXBERT tool.

In this work, we comparatively evaluate a number of reference-free MT evaluation metrics that build on the most recent developments in multilingual representation learning, namely cross-lingual contextualized embeddings (Devlin et al., 2019) and cross-lingual sentence encoders (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). We investigate two types of crosslingual reference-free metrics: (1) Soft token-level alignment metrics find the optimal soft alignment between source sentence and system translation using Word Mover's Distance (WMD) (Kusner et al., 2015) . Zhao et al. (2019) recently demonstrated that WMD operating on BERT representations (Devlin et al., 2019) substantially outperforms baseline MT evaluation metrics in the reference-based setting. In this work, we investigate whether WMD can yield comparable success in the reference-free (i.e., cross-lingual) setup; (2) Sentence-level similarity metrics measure the similarity between sentence representations of the source sentence and system translation using cosine similarity.

metadata.title: On the Limitations of Cross-lingual Encoders as Exposed by Reference-Free Machine Translation Evaluation

text: In the present study, we conduct a comparative evaluation of multiple reference-free machine translation (MT) evaluation metrics rooted in cutting-edge advances in multilingual representation learning, specifically cross-lingual contextualized embeddings (Devlin et al., 2019) and cross-lingual sentence encoders (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). We examine two distinct categories of cross-lingual reference-free metrics: (1) Soft token-level alignment metrics, which compute the optimal soft alignment between a source sentence and its corresponding system-generated translation via Word Mover’s Distance (WMD) (Kusner et al., 2015). Recent work by Zhao et al. (2019) demonstrates that WMD applied to BERT-derived representations (Devlin et al., 2019) substantially outperforms standard baseline MT evaluation metrics in reference-based evaluation settings. In this work, we investigate whether WMD can achieve comparable performance in the reference-free (i.e., cross-lingual) evaluation framework; (2) Sentence-level similarity metrics, which quantify the semantic similarity between the sentence-level representations of the source input and system translation using cosine similarity.

This was enough to re-establish him in his original opinion. He knew now that he was right, and that Troy was wrong. Every voice in nature was unanimous in bespeaking change. But two distinct translations attached to these dumb expressions. Apparently there was to be a thunder-storm, and afterwards a cold continuous rain. The creeping things seemed to know all about the later rain, but little of the interpolated thunder-storm; whilst the sheep knew all about the thunder-storm and nothing of the later rain.

That was all it took to snap him back to the convictions he’d held from the start. He knew now he was right, and Troy was wrong. Every small signal from the natural world aligned to confirm a shift was on its way. But these wordless, quiet cues carried two very separate interpretations. It was clear a thunderstorm would roll through first, followed by an unbroken stretch of cold, steady rain. The small crawling creatures seemed to sense every detail of the coming rain, but had no idea the thunderstorm would strike first; the sheep, by contrast, knew full well the thunderstorm was imminent, but had no notion of the lingering cold rain that would come after it.

(a) the labels appear in the header, followed by an annotating section; tagging suggestions are shown as underlined spans at the bottom of the page. A user may hover over a tagging suggestion or select a span in order to apply a label to a substring. Fig. 4 illustrates how for the sentence "Tahawwur Hussain Rana who was born in Pakistan but is a Canadian citizen" our framework can capture both a relation label between NEs and the subsequent natural language explanation. First, the user is tasked to find the NEs in the sentence. After labeling at least two non-consecutive spans of text as NEs, the user may check off the boxes that appear above the labeled NEs. Once two boxes have been checked off, the labels in the blue header are replaced with the labels for relations. The clickorder of the checked boxes is displayed and is considered the order of the relation. Also, we display a recommend label to the user in the header section with a circle (Fig. 2a) . After clicking on a label, a pop-up appears asking the user to indicate semantic and syntactic reasons as to why the labeling decision is true. Since the natural language explanations are assumed to be made up of predefined predicates, as the user types we incrementally provide predicates to aid the construction of an explanation (Fig. 2b) . In this way, we nudge users towards writing explanations the semantic parser is able to break down, allowing our framework to extract a useful logical form from the explanation.

metadata.title: LEAN-LIFE: A Label-Efficient Annotation Framework Towards Learning from Explanation

text: The interface presents labels in the header, followed by an annotation area; tagging suggestions appear as underlined spans near the bottom of the page. A user can hover over a suggestion or select a span to assign a label to a substring. As shown in Fig. 4, for the sentence “Tahawwur Hussain Rana who was born in Pakistan but is a Canadian citizen,” our framework is able to capture both a relation label linking named entities (NEs) and the accompanying natural language explanation. The user first identifies the NEs in the sentence. After marking at least two non-consecutive text spans as NEs, the user may tick the checkboxes that emerge above the labeled NEs. Once two boxes are ticked, the header labels are replaced with relation labels. The sequence in which the boxes are checked is recorded and treated as the relation order. In addition, a recommended label is shown in the header with a circular indicator (Fig. 2a). Upon selecting a label, a pop-up prompts the user to supply semantic and syntactic justifications for the labeling decision. Because the natural language explanations are expected to consist of predefined predicates, we offer incremental predicate suggestions as the user types, facilitating explanation construction (Fig. 2b). This design nudges users to produce explanations that the semantic parser can decompose, thereby enabling our framework to derive a meaningful logical form from the explanation.

More formally, we consider a TM as a set of K sentence pairs {(s k , t k ) ∶ k = 1, . . . , K} where s k and t k are mutual translations. A TM must be conveniently stored so as to allow fast access to the pair (s k , t k ) that shows the highest similarity between s k and any given new sentence. Many methods to compute sentence similarity have been explored, mainly falling into two broad categories: lexical matches (i.e. fuzzy match) and distributional semantics. The former relies on the number of overlaps between the sentences taken into account. The latter counts on the generalisation power of neural networks when building vector representations. Next, we describe the similarity measures employed in this work.

metadata.title: Boosting Neural Machine Translation with Similar Translations

text: Formally, we define a translation memory (TM) as a collection of $K$ bilingual pairs $\{(s_k, t_k)\}_{k=1}^K$, where $s_k$ and $t_k$ represent mutual translations. To ensure computational efficiency, the TM must be structured for rapid retrieval of the pair $(s_k, t_k)$ that maximizes the similarity between $s_k$ and a given query sentence. Existing methodologies for measuring sentence similarity typically bifurcate into lexical matching—often referred to as fuzzy matching—and distributional semantics. While the former focuses on the extent of discrete overlap between sentences, the latter exploits the generalization capacity of neural networks to generate latent vector representations. We proceed by detailing the specific similarity measures adopted in this research.

Almost for the first time in his life, Troy, as he stood by this dismantled grave, wished himself another man. It is seldom that a person with much animal spirit does not feel that the fact of his life being his own is the one qualification which singles it out as a more hopeful life than that of others who may actually resemble him in every particular. Troy had felt, in his transient way, hundreds of times, that he could not envy other people their condition, because the possession of that condition would have necessitated a different personality, when he desired no other than his own. He had not minded the peculiarities of his birth, the vicissitudes of his life, the meteor-like uncertainty of all that related to him, because these appertained to the hero of his story, without whom there would have been no story at all for him; and it seemed to be only in the nature of things that matters would right themselves at some proper date and wind up well. This very morning the illusion completed its disappearance, and, as it were, all of a sudden, Troy hated himself. The suddenness was probably more apparent than real. A coral reef which just comes short of the ocean surface is no more to the horizon than if it had never been begun, and the mere finishing stroke is what often appears to create an event which has long been potentially an accomplished thing.

Perhaps for the first time since his youth, Julian, as he lingered beside the disturbed earth of the grave, felt a profound longing to be any man but himself. It is a rare occurrence for one possessed of such vital energy not to feel that the simple fact of his own identity is the very quality that renders his life more promising than the lives of those who might otherwise mirror him. Julian had realized, in his shallow fashion, countless times before, that he could never truly envy the station of another; to possess their world would require a different spirit, and he had never wished for any spirit but his own. He had been indifferent to the scandals of his birth, the turbulence of his career, and the flickering instability of his future, for these were merely the attributes of the central figure in a drama that would be nothing without him; it seemed only natural that the plot would eventually resolve itself into a graceful end. On this particular morning, however, the deception vanished, and Julian was seized by a sudden, sharp hatred for his own being. The immediacy of this change was more illusion than fact. A bank of coral that stops just short of the tide is no more visible to the sailor than if it did not exist, and the final, crowning growth is often mistaken for the creation of a thing that has, in truth, been building toward completion for a long time.

It is a struggle,” said Rose, “but one I am proud to make; it is a pang, but one my heart shall bear.”

It is a conflict,” she said, “but one I meet with pride; it is a grief, but one my spirit shall endure.”

She leaped to the conviction that she was being branded a recipient of shameful favors, fearing that her old suitor’s name was being invoked as a direct assault upon her honor.

And as he to the court-yard pass'd along,
Each third step did he pause, and listen'd oft
If he could hear his lady's matin-song,
Or the light whisper of her footstep soft;
And as he thus over his passion hung,
He heard a laugh full musical aloft;
When, looking up, he saw her features bright
Smile through an in-door lattice, all delight.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
And as he went across the court-yard’s space,  
At every third slow step he paused to heed  
If he might catch his lady’s matin-lay,  
Or hear the gentle whisper of her tread;  
And, while he thus delayed beneath love’s chase,  
A laugh came sounding sweetly overhead;  
Then, looking up, he saw her radiant face  
Smile through the inward lattice, glad and bright.

“Yes, he has,” said Clym in a neutral tone. “Well, I wish with all my heart that I could say, marry him. But I cannot forget what my mother thought on that matter, and it goes rather against me not to respect her opinion. There is too much reason why we should do the little we can to respect it now.”

“That’s right, he has,” Clym said in a flat, uninflected tone. “I wish with all my heart I could tell you to marry him, truly. But I cannot shake the memory of my mother’s feelings on this matter, and it goes against my conscience to dismiss her judgment. We have more than enough cause now to do whatever small things we can to respect her wishes.”

She passed with her candle into an inner room, where, among other utensils, were two large brown pans, containing together perhaps a hundredweight of liquid honey, the produce of the bees during the foregoing summer. On a shelf over the pans was a smooth and solid yellow mass of a hemispherical form, consisting of beeswax from the same take of honey. Susan took down the lump, and cutting off several thin slices, heaped them in an iron ladle, with which she returned to the living-room, and placed the vessel in the hot ashes of the fireplace. As soon as the wax had softened to the plasticity of dough she kneaded the pieces together. And now her face became more intent. She began moulding the wax; and it was evident from her manner of manipulation that she was endeavouring to give it some preconceived form. The form was human.

Candle in hand, she stepped into a back room where, amid other household items, stood two broad brown basins holding close to a hundred pounds of liquid honey, the bees’ yield from the summer just past. On a shelf above them rested a firm, yellow hemisphere of beeswax drawn from that same harvest. Susan lifted the block down, sliced off several thin pieces, and piled them into an iron ladle. Carrying it back to the living-room, she set the ladle among the hot embers of the hearth. Once the wax softened to the pliancy of dough, she worked the fragments together. Her expression now sharpened with concentration. She began to shape the wax, and the way her hands moved made it clear she was striving to model a form she already held in mind. The form was a human one.
