“Well, someone definitely did—and listen up, neighbors,” Gabriel, who was normally one of the quietest, gentlest men you’d ever meet, rose to the occasion with all the brisk, unyielding fire of a soldier jumping into action. “This right here is my fist.” He set his knuckles down, only slightly smaller than an average loaf of bread, exactly dead center of the maltster’s small table, rapping it twice against the worn wood as if to make sure every pair of eyes in the room got the full picture of exactly what that fist could do before he kept speaking. “Now—the first man in this parish I hear badmouthing our mistress, well” —he lifted the fist and slammed it back down the way Thor might have tested the weight of his hammer—“he’s gonna taste and feel every bit of it, and that’s a promise. So Ikey’s horn-rimmed, spectacled nose and his slender, studiously stooped frame were familiar sights around the Blue Light, and both his counsel and his attention were eagerly sought.

metadata.title: Multi-Hypothesis Machine Translation Evaluation

text: Despite all the mentioned advantages, multilingual NMT remains a challenging task since the language diversity and model capacity limitations lead to inferior performance against individual models that are sufficiently trained. So recent efforts in multilingual NMT mainly focus on enlarging the model capacity, either by introducing multiple Encoders and Decoders to handle different languages (Firat et al., 2016; Zoph and Knight, 2016) , or enhancing the attention mechanism with language-specific signals (Blackwood et al., 2018) . On the other hand, there have been some efforts to model the specificity of different languages. Johnson et al. (2017) and Ha et al. (2016) tackle this by simply adding some pre-designed tokens at the beginning of the source/target sequence, but we argue that such signals are not strong enough to learn enough language-specific information to transform the continuous representation of each language into the shared semantic space based on our observations.

metadata.title: Language-aware Interlingua for Multilingual Neural Machine Translation

text: Despite these advantages, multilingual NMT remains a difficult task because language diversity and limitations in model capacity often result in worse performance than that of sufficiently trained individual models. Accordingly, recent work in multilingual NMT has primarily concentrated on increasing model capacity, either by introducing multiple encoders and decoders to manage different languages (Firat et al., 2016; Zoph and Knight, 2016), or by augmenting the attention mechanism with language-specific signals (Blackwood et al., 2018). By contrast, some efforts have focused on capturing the specificity of individual languages. Johnson et al. (2017) and Ha et al. (2016) address this by simply adding manually designed tokens to the beginning of the source or target sequence, but based on our observations, we argue that such signals are not sufficiently strong to learn enough language-specific information to transform each language’s continuous representation into the shared semantic space.

Although models for predicting keyphrases have been extensively evaluated on their ability to reproduce author's keywords, it still remains unclear whether they can be usefully applied in information retrieval. One reason for this lack of evidence may have been their relatively low performance discouraging attempts at using them for indexing (Liu et al., 2010; Hasan and Ng, 2014 ). Yet, recently proposed models not only achieve much better performance, but also display a property that may have a significant impact on retrieval effectiveness: the capacity to generate keyphrases that do not appear in the source text. These absent keyphrases do not just highlight the topics that are most relevant, but provide some form of semantic expansion by adding new content (e.g. synonyms, semantically related terms) to the index (Greulich, 2011) . The goal of this paper is two-fold: to gather empirical evidence as to whether current keyphrase generation models are good enough to improve scientific document retrieval, and to gain further insights into the performance of these models from an extrinsic perspective. Our contributions are listed as follows:

metadata.title: Keyphrase Generation for Scientific Document Retrieval

text: About half a dozen men came forward; and, one being selected by the magistrate, he deposed that he had been out fishing the night before with his son and brother-in-law, Daniel Nugent, when, about ten o’clock, they observed a strong northerly blast rising, and they accordingly put in for port. It was a very dark night, as the moon had not yet risen; they did not land at the harbour, but, as they had been accustomed, at a creek about two miles below. He walked on first, carrying a part of the fishing tackle, and his companions followed him at some distance. As he was proceeding along the sands, he struck his foot against something and fell at his length on the ground. His companions came up to assist him, and by the light of their lantern they found that he had fallen on the body of a man, who was to all appearance dead. Their first supposition was that it was the corpse of some person who had been drowned and was thrown on shore by the waves, but on examination they found that the clothes were not wet and even that the body was not then cold. They instantly carried it to the cottage of an old woman near the spot and endeavoured, but in vain, to restore it to life. It appeared to be a handsome young man, about five and twenty years of age. He had apparently been strangled, for there was no sign of any violence except the black mark of fingers on his neck.

About half a dozen men stepped forward, and when one was chosen by the magistrate, he stated under oath that he had been out fishing the previous night with his son and his brother-in-law, Daniel Nugent, when, about ten o’clock, they noticed a hard northerly wind beginning to rise, and so turned in for shore. It was a very dark night, the moon not yet having risen; they did not go in at the harbour, but, as they were accustomed to do, at a creek some two miles below it. He went on ahead, carrying part of the fishing gear, while the others followed at some distance behind. As he was making his way along the sands, his foot struck against something and he fell full length to the ground. His companions hurried up to help him, and by the light of their lantern they discovered that he had fallen upon the body of a man, who seemed to all appearances dead. At first they supposed it to be the corpse of someone drowned and washed ashore by the waves, but on closer inspection they found that the clothing was not wet, and that the body was not even cold. They immediately carried it to the cottage of an old woman nearby and tried, though without success, to bring it back to life. It seemed to be that of a handsome young man, about twenty-five years old. He had evidently been strangled, for there was no mark of injury save the black imprint of fingers around his neck.

“I wish I had known that you would be here alone,” she said seriously, “and that we were going to have such an idle, pleasant time as this. Pleasure not known beforehand is half wasted; to anticipate it is to double it. I have not thought once today of having you all to myself this afternoon, and the actual moment of a thing is so soon gone.”

“Had I realized you’d be quite unattended,” she said with a solemn air, “and that we’d share such a leisurely, delightful hour—well, joy unlooked-for is half squandered; to foresee it is to redouble its sweetness. All day long I never once imagined I’d possess you entirely this afternoon, and the living instant of any delight vanishes so swiftly.”

Troy stood at the entrance to the booth, where a gipsy-woman was frying pancakes over a little fire of sticks and selling them at a penny a-piece, and looked over the heads of the people within. He could see nothing of Pennyways, but he soon discerned Bathsheba through an opening into the reserved space at the further end. Troy thereupon retreated, went round the tent into the darkness, and listened. He could hear Bathsheba’s voice immediately inside the canvas; she was conversing with a man. A warmth overspread his face: surely she was not so unprincipled as to flirt in a fair! He wondered if, then, she reckoned upon his death as an absolute certainty. To get at the root of the matter, Troy took a penknife from his pocket and softly made two little cuts crosswise in the cloth, which, by folding back the corners left a hole the size of a wafer. Close to this he placed his face, withdrawing it again in a movement of surprise; for his eye had been within twelve inches of the top of Bathsheba’s head. It was too near to be convenient. He made another hole a little to one side and lower down, in a shaded place beside her chair, from which it was easy and safe to survey her by looking horizontally.

Rowan paused at the edge of the stall, where a Romani woman was toasting flatcakes above a small blaze of twigs and vending them for a copper each, and peered across the crowd gathered inside. He caught no glimpse of Merrick, but soon spotted Seraphine through a gap leading to the cordoned-off section at the far end. Rowan then withdrew, circled the pavilion into the shadows, and strained his ears. He could hear Seraphine’s tones just beyond the canvas; she was speaking with a man. A flush crept over his cheeks: surely she was not so shameless as to dally at a carnival! He wondered whether she now counted his dying as a settled fact. To uncover the truth, Rowan drew a small blade from his coat and quietly incised two tiny crossing slits in the fabric, which, when the corners were folded back, yielded an opening no larger than a coin. He brought his face near this peephole, then jerked it away in a start; for his gaze had rested scarcely a foot from the crown of Seraphine’s head. It was far too close for comfort. He cut a second aperture slightly to one side and farther down, in a dim nook beside her seat, from which he could easily and safely observe her by looking straight ahead.

Translation vs. Prediction. Is it beneficial to predict new ratings for the words in TargetMT rather than using them as final lexicon entries straight away? For each TargetGold lexicon (cf. Table 2 ), we intersect its word material with that in TargetMT and TargetPred. Then, we compute the correlation between TargetPred and TargetMT with the gold standard. This analysis was done on the respective train sets because using TargetMT rather than TargetPred is only an option for entries known at training time. Table 6 depicts the results of this comparison averaged over all gold lexicons. As hypothesized, the TargetPred lexicons agree, on average, more with human judgment than the TargetMT lexicons, suggesting that the word emotion model acts as a value-adding post-processor, partly mitigating rating inconsistencies introduced by mere translation of the lexicons. The observation holds for each individual emotion variable with particularly large benefits for Arousal, where the postprocessed TargetPred lexicons are on average 14%-points better compared to the translation-only TargetMT lexicons. This seems to indicate that lexical Arousal is less consistent between translational equivalents compared to other emotional meaning components like Valence and Sadness, which appear to be more robust against translation.

metadata.title: Learning and Evaluating Emotion Lexicons for 91 Languages

text: Translation versus Prediction. Is it advantageous to predict new ratings for the words in TargetMT rather than adopting them directly as final lexicon entries? For each TargetGold lexicon (see Table 2), we intersect its word material with that of TargetMT and TargetPred. We then compute the correlation of both TargetPred and TargetMT with the gold standard. This analysis was conducted on the respective training sets because substituting TargetPred for TargetMT is only feasible for entries known at training time. Table 6 presents the results of this comparison averaged across all gold lexicons. As hypothesized, the TargetPred lexicons show, on average, stronger agreement with human judgments than the TargetMT lexicons, indicating that the word emotion model functions as a value-adding post-processor that partially corrects rating inconsistencies introduced by straightforward translation of the lexicons. This pattern holds for every individual emotion variable, with especially pronounced gains for Arousal, where the post-processed TargetPred lexicons outperform the translation-only TargetMT lexicons by an average of 14 percentage points. This suggests that lexical Arousal is less stable across translational equivalents than other emotional meaning dimensions such as Valence and Sadness, which appear more resilient to translation effects.

We now return to the three major issues presented in Section 1. In 4.1 we present evidence that SG score is dissociated from perplexity. In 4.2 we argue that model architecture accounts for larger gains in SG score than amount of training data. And in 4.3 we show that this cross-architecture difference is due largely to variance on a handful of key test suites. Figure 2 shows the relationship between SG score and perplexity on the BLLIP test set across models and training set sizes. As expected, n-gram models never rise appreciably above chance in SG score. Among neural models, GPT-2 achieves both the worst (BLLIP-XS and BLLIP-SM) and best (BLLIP-MD and BLLIP-LG) performance; the impressive performance of these latter models comes with the caveat that the sub-words come from the pre-trained GPT-2 model, tacitly importing information from a larger training dataset (see further discussion in Section 4.5). For the remaining neural models, there is no simple relationship between perplexity and SG score, especially once training dataset size is controlled for (comparing points in Figure 2 of the same color). For example, there is a remarkable amount of variance in the SG score of models trained on BLLIP-LG not explained by perplexity. This suggests that targeted syntactic evaluation can reveal information that may be orthogonal to perplexity.

metadata.title: A Systematic Assessment of Syntactic Generalization in Neural Language Models

text: We now return to the three central issues introduced in Section 1. In 4.1 we provide evidence that SG score is dissociated from perplexity. In 4.2 we argue that model architecture yields larger improvements in SG score than does the volume of training data. In 4.3 we demonstrate that this cross-architecture variation is largely attributable to variance on a small set of key test suites. Figure 2 plots the relation between SG score and perplexity on the BLLIP test set across models and training set sizes. As anticipated, n-gram models never rise substantially above chance in SG score. Among neural models, GPT-2 attains both the lowest (BLLIP-XS and BLLIP-SM) and the highest (BLLIP-MD and BLLIP-LG) performance; the strong performance of these latter models must be qualified by the fact that the sub-words derive from the pre-trained GPT-2 model, thereby implicitly importing information from a larger training corpus (see further discussion in Section 4.5). For the remaining neural models, no straightforward relation holds between perplexity and SG score, particularly when training dataset size is controlled (comparing points of the same color in Figure 2). For instance, there is a notable degree of variance in the SG score of models trained on BLLIP-LG that perplexity does not explain. This indicates that targeted syntactic evaluation can uncover information that may be orthogonal to perplexity.

Here, a bed was prepared, without loss of time, in which Mr. Brownlow saw his young charge carefully and comfortably deposited; and here, he was tended with a kindness and solicitude that knew no bounds.

The coach rattled off, covering nearly the exact stretch of road Oliver had traveled when he first arrived in London in the Dodger’s company; upon reaching the Angel at Islington, it veered down an alternate route, finally pulling to a stop outside a neat house on a quiet, leaf-shaded street close to Pentonville. A bed was readied without a moment’s delay here, and Mr. Brownlow watched as his young charge was settled carefully and comfortably into it; he was tended to with a warmth and thoughtfulness that knew no limits.

“I heard your dog howling and scratching at the door of the hut when I came to the milking (it was so lucky, Daisy’s milking is almost over for the season, and I shall not come here after this week or the next). The dog saw me, and jumped over to me, and laid hold of my skirt. I came across and looked round the hut the very first thing to see if the slides were closed. My uncle has a hut like this one, and I have heard him tell his shepherd not to go to sleep without leaving a slide open. I opened the door, and there you were like dead. I threw the milk over you, as there was no water, forgetting it was warm, and no use.”

“I heard your hound wailing and clawing at the cabin door when I arrived for the evening milking—such a mercy that Daisy’s flow is nearly done for the year, and I shan’t need to come this way beyond a week or two more. The beast caught sight of me, bounded over, and seized the hem of my dress. I crossed the yard at once and circled the hut, my first thought being to check whether the vents were shut. My own uncle keeps a cabin much like this one, and I’ve heard him warn his shepherd never to sleep without a vent cracked open. I drew back the door, and there you lay as still as a corpse. I dashed the milk over you, there being no water to hand, forgetting in my haste that it was warm and would do nothing.”

Thus far the reddleman had been tolerably successful in his rude contrivances for keeping down Wildeve’s inclination to rove in the evening. He had nipped in the bud the possible meeting between Eustacia and her old lover this very night. But he had not anticipated that the tendency of his action would be to divert Wildeve’s movement rather than to stop it. The gambling with the guineas had not conduced to make him a welcome guest to Clym; but to call upon his wife’s relative was natural, and he was determined to see Eustacia. It was necessary to choose some less untoward hour than ten o’clock at night. “Since it is unsafe to go in the evening,” he said, “I’ll go by day.”

So far, the reddleman’s rough precautions had worked well enough to curb Wildeve’s urge to wander after dark. That very night, he had cut short any chance encounter between Eustacia and her former lover before it could bloom. Yet what he had not foreseen was that his interference would only shift Wildeve’s course, not halt it. The dice game over the guineas had hardly made him a welcome figure in Clym’s eyes, but a visit to his wife’s kinswoman was perfectly plausible, and he was resolved to see Eustacia. He needed to pick a less awkward hour than ten at night. “If it’s risky to go in the evening,” he told himself, “I’ll go by daylight.”

To harbor a secret was contrary to Arthur’s every instinct—especially one involving a complex ethical dilemma—but he recognized that Beatrice and Professor Vance were ill-equipped to entertain with composure a matter that, in a more serene setting, would be a compelling philosophical problem. Yet he was not made for silence; he was made for the explicit declaration.

"No one in England really seems to have any suspicion what is going on in Belgium. You hear stories--People tell them in Holland. It takes your breath away. They have set out just to cow those Belgians. They have started in to be deliberately frightful. You do not begin to understand.... Well.... Outrages. The sort of outrages Americans have never heard of. That one doesn't speak of.... Well.... Rape.... They have been raping women for disciplinary purposes on tables in the market-place of Liège. Yes, sir. It's a fact. I was told it by a man who had just come out of Belgium. Knew the people, knew the place, knew everything. People over here do not seem to realise that those women are the same sort of women that you might find in Chester or Yarmouth, or in Matching's Easy for the matter of that. They still seem to think that Continental women are a different sort of women--more amenable to that sort of treatment. They seem to think there is some special Providential law against such things happening to English people. And it's within two hundred miles of you--even now. And as far as I can see there's precious little to prevent it coming nearer...."

Hardly a soul in Britain seems to harbor any inkling of the nightmare unfolding across the Channel. One hears the accounts—they circulate in the Dutch borderlands—and they leave one reeling. The invaders have embarked upon a campaign to systematically terrorize the populace; they have embraced a philosophy of cold, calculated horror. You cannot conceive of it... Truly... Violations. Cruelties of a nature that would stun a New Yorker. Things one simply does not mention... Well... Defilement. They have been despoiling women as a method of discipline, out in the open on the very merchant stalls of Louvain. It is an absolute fact. I had it from a gentleman who fled the region himself; he knew the families, he knew the terrain, he saw the wreckage. Our neighbors here fail to recognize that those victims are the same flesh and blood as the women in York or Hastings, or even here in Little Waltham for that matter. There remains this stubborn belief that the women of the Continent are of a separate, more resilient cast—better suited to endure such savagery. They cling to the notion that some celestial decree renders the English immune to such calamity. And yet, it is occurring not sixty leagues from where we stand. As far as I can tell, there is little enough to stop that shadow from stretching toward our own threshold.

“Very well, then let me see. I tell you how we’ll manage,” said the other, who perhaps felt it would be extremely awkward to offend his leading man just at this time. “I won’t tell ’em anything about your keeping silence; go on with the piece and say nothing, doing what you can by a judicious wink now and then, and a few indomitable nods in the heroic places, you know. They’ll never find out that the speeches are omitted.”

“Alright then, let me think. I’ve got the perfect way we can make this work,” the other man said, likely well aware that crossing his leading actor right now would be deeply awkward. “I won’t say a single thing to anyone about you staying silent. Just carry on with the performance as usual, don’t utter a line, toss in a well-timed wink every now and again, and a few resolute, grand nods during the heroic beats, you know what I mean. They’ll never catch on that all your speeches are cut.”

The enterprising lady followed the mumming company through the gate in the white paling, and stood before the open porch. The house was encrusted with heavy thatchings, which dropped between the upper windows; the front, upon which the moonbeams directly played, had originally been white; but a huge pyracanth now darkened the greater portion.

The resourceful woman trailed the troupe of mummers past the gate set in the pale fence and halted before the open porch. Thick layers of thatch burdened the cottage, sagging between the upper casements; the façade, where the moonlight struck full, had once been white, though a sprawling firethorn now shadowed most of it.

The only signs of the terrible sorrow Boldwood had been combating through the night, and was combating now, were the want of colour in his well-defined face, the enlarged appearance of the veins in his forehead and temples, and the sharper lines about his mouth. The horse bore him away, and the very step of the animal seemed significant of dogged despair. Gabriel, for a minute, rose above his own grief in noticing Boldwood’s. He saw the square figure sitting erect upon the horse, the head turned to neither side, the elbows steady by the hips, the brim of the hat level and undisturbed in its onward glide, until the keen edges of Boldwood’s shape sank by degrees over the hill. To one who knew the man and his story there was something more striking in this immobility than in a collapse. The clash of discord between mood and matter here was forced painfully home to the heart; and, as in laughter there are more dreadful phases than in tears, so was there in the steadiness of this agonized man an expression deeper than a cry.

The only hints of the crippling grief Boldwood had fought all night, and was still fighting in that moment, showed in the drained, pale cast of his sharp-featured face, the bulging veins standing out across his forehead and temples, and the newly harsh edges of the lines framing his mouth. The horse carried him off, and even the creature’s plodding gait felt heavy with the same stubborn, unyielding despair clinging to its rider. For a few seconds, Gabriel set his own heartache aside to watch Boldwood’s distress unfold. He tracked the broad, square-shouldered form sitting rigidly upright in the saddle, head held straight without glancing left or right, elbows fixed firmly at his sides, the brim of his hat sitting perfectly even as he glided forward, until the sharp, distinct outline of Boldwood’s figure slowly dipped out of sight over the crest of the hill. For anyone who knew Boldwood and what he’d been through, this rigid stillness hit far harder than any public breakdown would have. The jarring mismatch between his outward composure and inner turmoil cut straight to the chest with a sharp, painful clarity; just as there are strains of laughter far more terrible than sobs, the unshakable calm of this man raw with agony held a weight of emotion far more profound than any screamed outburst.

Multiplication vs. addition In Equation 2, we calculated the weighing scores by multiplying the self-attention scores by the distance to the parent token. Multiplication is, in fact, the standard way to weight values (e.g., the gating mechanism of LSTMs and GRUs). In our case, it introduces sparseness in the attention scores for non-parent tokens. Moreover, it weights gradients in backpropagation: Let x and y be the attention score and dependency weight, respectively. Consider a loss l = f (z) where z = xy and dl/dx = df (z)/dz * y. The attention score receive gradients more on dependent pairs (larger y) than non-dependent ones (smaller y), which is sound for dependency information. In contrast, addition cannot obtain such an effect because it does not affect gradients: dl/dx = df (z)/dz when z = x + y. For completeness, we trained our best NC11 models replacing multiplication by addition. We find that BLEU scores still improve upon the baseline, meaning that our approach is robust, but find them to be slightly lower (−0.2) than with multiplication.

metadata.title: Enhancing Machine Translation with Dependency-Aware Self-Attention

text: In Equation 2, the weighing scores are obtained by multiplying the self-attention scores with the distance to the parent token. Multiplication serves as the conventional operation for weighting values, as exemplified by the gating mechanisms in LSTMs and GRUs. In our setting, this operation induces sparsity in the attention scores assigned to non-parent tokens. Furthermore, it modulates gradient flow during backpropagation: let an attention score be denoted by \(x\) and a dependency weight by \(y\). For a loss \(l = f(z)\) with \(z = xy\), the gradient is \(dl/dx = df(z)/dz \cdot y\). Consequently, dependent token pairs (where \(y\) is larger) receive stronger gradient signals than non-dependent ones (where \(y\) is smaller), which aligns naturally with the goal of encoding dependency information. By contrast, addition fails to produce such an effect because it leaves gradients unchanged: when \(z = x + y\), we have \(dl/dx = df(z)/dz\). For completeness, we retrained our best NC11 models with addition replacing multiplication. The resulting BLEU scores still surpass the baseline, confirming the robustness of our approach, yet they are marginally lower (\(-0.2\)) than those achieved with multiplication.

We present Torch-Struct, a library for deep structured prediction. The library achieves modularity through its adoption of a generic distributional API, completeness by utilizing CRFs and semirings to make it easy to add new algorithms, and efficiency through core optimizations to vectorize important dynamic programming steps. In addition to the problems discussed so far, Torch-Struct also includes several other example implementations including supervised dependency parsing with BERT, unsupervised tagging, structured attention, and connectionist temporal classification (CTC) for speech. Code demonstrates that the model is able to replicate standard deep learning results, although we focus here on the fidelity and implementation approach of the core library. The full library is available at https: //github.com/harvardnlp/pytorch-struct.

metadata.title: Torch-Struct: Deep Structured Prediction Library

text: We introduce Torch-Struct, a library for deep structured prediction. The library achieves modularity through a generic distributional API, completeness by leveraging CRFs and semirings to make it straightforward to add new algorithms, and efficiency through core optimizations that vectorize key dynamic programming operations. Beyond the problems discussed above, Torch-Struct also includes several additional example implementations, such as supervised dependency parsing with BERT, unsupervised tagging, structured attention, and connectionist temporal classification (CTC) for speech. The code shows that the model can reproduce standard deep learning results, although here we emphasize the fidelity and implementation strategy of the core library. The full library is available at https://github.com/harvardnlp/pytorch-struct.

Mr. Giles directed an appealing glance at the tinker; but he had suddenly fallen asleep. The women were out of the question.

As he spoke, Mr. Hale glanced over at Toby, but that soft-spoken young man, naturally modest to a fault, likely considered himself far too unimportant to be the intended recipient of the question, so he assumed the inquiry could not possibly be meant for him; in any case, he offered no reply whatsoever. Mr. Hale then turned an imploring look toward the itinerant tinker, but the man had suddenly fallen fast asleep. Asking any of the women in the room was entirely out of the question.

So Mr. Britling was out of conceit with volunteering, and instead he went and was duly sworn and entrusted with the badge of a special constable. The duties of a special constable were chiefly not to understand what was going on in the military sphere, and to do what he was told in the way of watching and warding conceivably vulnerable points. He had also to be available in the event of civil disorder. Mr. Britling was provided with a truncheon and sent out to guard various culverts, bridges, and fords in the hilly country to the north-westward of Matching's Easy. It was never very clear to him what he would do if he found a motor-car full of armed enemies engaged in undermining a culvert, or treacherously deepening some strategic ford. He supposed he would either engage them in conversation, or hit them with his truncheon, or perhaps do both things simultaneously. But as he really did not believe for a moment that any human being was likely to tamper with the telegraphs, telephones, ways and appliances committed to his care, his uncertainty did not trouble him very much. He prowled the lonely lanes and paths in the darkness, and became better acquainted with a multitude of intriguing little cries and noises that came from the hedges and coverts at night. One night he rescued a young leveret from a stoat, who seemed more than half inclined to give him battle for its prey until he cowed and defeated it with the glare of his electric torch....

So Mr. Britling had grown disenchanted with volunteering, and instead he went and was duly sworn in and entrusted with the insignia of a special constable. The business of a special constable consisted chiefly in not understanding what was happening in the military world, and in doing what he was told, in the matter of keeping watch and guard over points that might conceivably be vulnerable. He was also to be available in the event of civil disturbance. Mr. Britling was supplied with a truncheon and sent out to protect various culverts, bridges, and fords in the hilly country to the north-west of Matching’s Easy. It was never quite plain to him what he was supposed to do if he came upon a motor-car full of armed enemies busied in weakening a culvert, or maliciously deepening some strategic ford. He imagined that he would either strike up a conversation with them, or hit them with his truncheon, or perhaps do both at once. But since he did not for a moment believe that any human creature was likely to meddle with the telegraphs, telephones, roads, and appliances entrusted to his care, his uncertainty did not much disturb him. He ranged the lonely lanes and paths by night, and came to know better a host of odd little cries and rustlings that rose from the hedges and coverts after dark. One night he rescued a young leveret from a stoat, which seemed more than half disposed to make battle for its prey until he overawed and routed it with the beam of his electric torch....

He walked at the other’s heels with a swing to his shoulders, and his legs spread unwittingly, as if the level floors were tilting up and sinking down to the heave and lunge of the sea. The wide rooms seemed too narrow for his rolling gait, and to himself he was in terror lest his broad shoulders should collide with the doorways or sweep the bric-a-brac from the low mantel. He recoiled from side to side between the various objects and multiplied the hazards that in reality lodged only in his mind. Between a grand piano and a centre-table piled high with books was space for a half a dozen to walk abreast, yet he essayed it with trepidation. His heavy arms hung loosely at his sides. He did not know what to do with those arms and hands, and when, to his excited vision, one arm seemed liable to brush against the books on the table, he lurched away like a frightened horse, barely missing the piano stool. He watched the easy walk of the other in front of him, and for the first time realized that his walk was different from that of other men. He experienced a momentary pang of shame that he should walk so uncouthly. The sweat burst through the skin of his forehead in tiny beads, and he paused and mopped his bronzed face with his handkerchief.

He tramped at the other man’s heels, shoulders swinging loosely, his legs unconsciously splayed as if the flat floorboards were rolling and dipping with the ocean’s heave and surge. The spacious rooms felt far too cramped for his rolling gait, and he was privately terrified his broad shoulders would bash against doorframes or send the bric-a-brac clattering off the low mantelpiece. He flinched left and right around assorted furnishings, inflating risks that existed entirely in his own head. The gap between a grand piano and a central table stacked high with books was wide enough for half a dozen people to walk side by side, yet he edged through it with skittish dread. His heavy arms hung limp at his sides; he had no idea what to do with those gangly limbs, and when his overwrought gaze spotted his arm brushing perilously close to the volumes stacked on the table, he lurched away like a spooked horse, barely clearing the piano stool. He watched the easy, unselfconscious stride of the man ahead, and for the first time it hit him that his walk was nothing like other men’s. A sharp, fleeting stab of shame pricked him at how uncouth his gait was. Tiny beads of sweat burst across his forehead, and he stopped short, dabbing at his bronzed face with his handkerchief.

Phonology: A wide range of phonological assimilation processes interplay with the morphological processes described above and obfuscate morpheme boundaries. These can concern voicing, nasality and vowel quality, among other features. As an example of morphological and phonological processes working together, consider the realization of pronomial possession in Australian language Wembawembda (Laughren, 2009) . Unlike English, which expresses this feature with pronouns his/her/its, Wembawemba expresses it with a suffix on the noun it modifies, e.g. wutyupuk '(his/her/its) stomach'. The form of the suffix, however, depends on the ending of the noun it attaches to and can vary greatly as shown in Table 3 . Indonesian language Abui, for example, literally translates into "pistol's ear", but a more fitting translation would be "trigger" (Peguševs, 2017) .

metadata.title: PuzzLing Machines: A Challenge on Learning From Small Data

text: Phonology: A broad range of phonological assimilation processes interacts with the morphological mechanisms discussed above and thereby obscures morpheme boundaries. These processes may affect voicing, nasality, vowel quality, and related features. As an illustration of the joint operation of morphological and phonological phenomena, consider the expression of pronominal possession in the Australian language Wembawembda (Laughren, 2009). In contrast to English, which encodes this feature with possessive pronouns such as his, her, and its, Wembawembda marks possession with a suffix attached to the noun it modifies, e.g., wutyupuk ‘(his/her/its) stomach’. However, the phonological shape of the suffix is conditioned by the final segment of the noun to which it is attached and may vary substantially, as illustrated in Table 3. A related example comes from the Indonesian language Abui, whose literal rendering is “pistol’s ear,” although a more appropriate translation would be “trigger” (Peguševs, 2017).

There’s little Tom Dacre, who cried when his head,
That curled like a lamb’s back, was shaved; so I said,
‘Hush, Tom! never mind it, for, when your head’s bare,
You know that the soot cannot spoil your white hair.’

metadata.title: Songs of Innocence and of Experience

text:
There’s little Tom Dacre, who wept as his head,
That curled like a lamb’s wool, was clipped and stripped bare;
So I told him, “Be still now, Tom, it doesn’t matter at all,
For once your head is bare, the soot can’t stain your white hair.”

“Well, never mind arguing—never mind. One thing is sure: you were all but mine, and now you are not nearly mine. Everything is changed, and that by you alone, remember. You were nothing to me once, and I was contented; you are now nothing to me again, and how different the second nothing is from the first! Would to God you had never taken me up, since it was only to throw me down!”

“Well, let us say no more of it—no more. One thing is certain: you were almost mine, and now you are scarcely mine at all. All is altered, and altered by you alone, remember that. Once you meant nothing to me, and I was content; now you mean nothing to me again, and how unlike this second nothing is to the first! Would to God you had never raised me up, if only to cast me down!”

He again placed the letter on the mantelpiece, and sat down to think of the circumstances attending it by the light of Gabriel’s information.

Soon after leaving Gabriel, the solitary, reserved man went back home to breakfast, troubled by pangs of shame and regret for having so openly revealed his feelings in those heated questions to a stranger. He once more set the letter on the mantelpiece and sat down to consider the circumstances surrounding it in the light of Gabriel’s information.

To evaluate our model, we compare it with two others from the literature in two different settings. Table 1 compiles an overview of the evaluation results. A confusion matrix for our model is found in Table 2 in the appendix. Our model achieves 96% accuracy over all classes. Mapped to binary decisions between paragraphs and non-paragraphs, the accuracy goes up to 98%. The recall on the paragraph class is 93% (see Table 2 ). The majority class are quotations with 33%, followed by patches with 16%. Paragraphs come in at 11%. Note that the patch class is overrepresented not because we sampled primarily patch emails, but because patches tend to be longer than normal emails. Still, we achieve an overall high accuracy on all classes. A typical segmentation is provided as an example in Figure 3 . To test the model's ability to generalize to unseen data, we annotated 300 emails from the Enron corpus, whose class distribution differs significantly from mailing lists: The emails are much shorter and most lines belong to paragraphs (36%) or empty lines (26%). Quotations account for 8% and code or patches are non-existent. Though significantly lower, our model still shows an acceptable accuracy of about 88%. The excessive use of inline headers containing multiple lines of forwarding addresses appears to be the main challenge for our model, which is expected considering that forwarding emails to dozens of recipients is rare on mailing lists. Furthermore, the proprietary Enron mail user agent had an unusual forwarding and quotation style quite unlike the more common Thunderbird, GMail, or Outlook notations.

metadata.title: Crawling and Preprocessing Mailing Lists At Scale for Dialog Analysis

text: To assess our model, we benchmark it against two existing approaches from the literature under two distinct conditions. Table 1 summarizes the evaluation outcomes. A confusion matrix for our model is provided in Table 2 in the appendix. Our model attains 96% overall accuracy across all classes. When reduced to binary classification between paragraphs and non-paragraphs, accuracy rises to 98%. Recall for the paragraph class reaches 93% (see Table 2). The majority class consists of quotations at 33%, followed by patches at 16%; paragraphs account for 11%. It is worth noting that the patch class is overrepresented not because we preferentially sampled patch-heavy emails, but because patches tend to be longer than ordinary messages. Even so, we obtain consistently high accuracy across all classes. A representative segmentation is shown in Figure 3. To probe the model’s capacity to generalize to unseen data, we annotated 300 emails from the Enron corpus, whose class distribution diverges markedly from that of mailing lists: the messages are considerably shorter, and most lines belong either to paragraphs (36%) or to empty lines (26%). Quotations make up 8%, while code or patches are absent. Although performance drops noticeably, our model still yields an acceptable accuracy of roughly 88%. The principal difficulty appears to be the heavy use of inline headers containing multiple lines of forwarding addresses, which is to be expected given that forwarding emails to dozens of recipients is uncommon on mailing lists. In addition, the proprietary Enron mail client employed an idiosyncratic forwarding and quotation style quite unlike the more widespread conventions of Thunderbird, Gmail, or Outlook.

“And now, as we are about to lay his deserted body in the earth, let not our perceptions be dulled by the constant repetition in this world of death and burial. At this hour our friend is no longer aged; wrinkles and furrows, trembling limbs and snowy locks he has left behind him, and he knows, we believe, to-day, more than the wisest philosopher on earth. We may study and argue, all our lives, to discover the nature of life, or the form it takes beyond the grave; but in one moment of swift transition the righteous man may learn it all. We differ widely one from another, here, in mental power. A slight hardening of some tissue of the brain might have left a Shakspeare an attorney's clerk. But, in the brighter world, no such impediments prevent, I believe, clear vision and clear expression; and differences of mind that seem world-wide here, may vanish there. When the spirit breaks its earthly prison and flies away, who can tell how bright and free the humblest of us may come to be! There may be a more varied truth than we commonly think, in the words,--'The last shall be first.'

“And now, as we prepare to lower his long-worn frame into the cool dark soil, let us not let our senses grow numb to this moment, jaded though we may be by the world’s endless cycle of loss and interment. Right now, our old neighbor Elias is no longer frail or weathered; the deep lines scored across his brow, the shake in his knotted hands, the frost of his silver hair he has shed entirely, and we hold faith that this day he understands more than the sharpest living scholar walking the earth. We might spend every year of our mortal lives poring over texts, debating in halls, trying to pin down the true essence of being, or what shape existence takes once we leave this flesh behind; but in that single swift, sacred instant of crossing, the good and gentle soul grasps every last piece of that puzzle. Here, the gulf between our intellectual capacities can feel uncrossable. A tiny blockage in a single brain vessel, a faint scarring of neural tissue, might have turned a Dante into a scribe copying tax records for a local magistrate. But in that luminous realm that awaits us, I truly believe no such flaws stand in the way of clear sight and true speech; the chasms between minds that seem to stretch across continents here may dissolve entirely when we stand on that shore. When the soul bursts free of its clay cage and rises to meet that light, who can say how radiant and unshackled even the quietest, most overlooked among us might become? There is a deeper, more vivid truth than most of us ever dare to imagine in the old promise: ‘The last shall be first.’”

At last, swooping at a street corner by a fountain, one of its wheels came to a sickening little jolt, and there was a loud cry from a number of voices, and the horses reared and plunged.

With a furious rattling and clanging, and a kind of reckless disregard that is hard to imagine in these times, the carriage hurtled through the streets and swung round the corners, while women shrieked ahead of it and men seized one another and dragged children aside to escape its path. At last, as it swept around a corner beside a fountain, one of the wheels gave a small, dreadful jolt, and a cry rose from several voices, while the horses reared and struggled.

“I do, and I do not,” he said with a mischievous air. “That is to say, I have my times and seasons. At one moment you are too tall, at another too idle; one time too melancholy, another too dark, and another I scarcely know what, except that you are not the whole world to me that you once were, my dear. But you are a pleasant lady to know and agreeable to meet, and I dare say as sweet as ever—almost.”

“Way behind,” he said. “Got to work after supper.” And after supper they worked until ten o’clock, under the blazing electric lights, until the last piece of under-clothing was ironed and folded away in the distributing room. It was a hot California night, and though the windows were thrown wide, the room, with its red-hot ironing-stove, was a furnace. Martin and Joe, down to undershirts, bare armed, sweated and panted for air.

“We’re way behind on orders,” he grunted. “Have to put in hours after supper.” Once the meal was cleared away, they labored straight through to ten o’clock beneath the searing glow of overhead electric bulbs, not pausing until the final piece of underclothing had been pressed crisp and tucked away in the distribution room. It was a scorching California night, and though every window had been flung wide to the dark, the small workroom, heated by the red-glowing ironing stove, was nothing short of a furnace. Leo and Rafe, stripped down to thin cotton undershirts, bare-armed, dripped with sweat and panted for even the smallest wisp of cool air.

We propose a language-neutral, fine-grained definition of cross-linguistic morphosyntactic divergences (CLMD) that allows for their extraction using a syntactically annotated, content-wordaligned parallel corpus. Concretely, we classify CLMD based on the edge labels on the dependency paths between corresponding pairs of content words ( § 3.2). See Figure 1 for an example. 1 We further conduct a detailed corpus study, manually aligning content words in a subset of the PUD corpus (Zeman et al., 2017) over five language pairs-English-French (En-Fr), English-Russian (En-Ru), English-Chinese (En-Zh), English-Korean (En-Ko), and English-Japanese (En-Jp) ( § 3.1)-and analyze the prevalence of divergences by types ( § 4). The resulting resource can be useful for MT research, by guiding the creation of challenge sets focusing on particular constructions known to be cross-linguistically divergent, as well as by guiding preprocessing of source-side sentences for better MT performance. 2 The emerging CLMD provide information not only on the macro-structure of the grammar (e.g., whether the language is pro-dropping), but also on parameters specific to certain lexical classes (e.g., modal verbs) and probabilistic tendencies (e.g., Japanese tends to translate sequences of events, expressed in English using coordinating conjunctions, with subordinate clauses). See § 5.

metadata.title: Fine-Grained Analysis of Cross-Linguistic Syntactic Divergences

text: We introduce a language-agnostic, fine-grained characterization of cross-linguistic morphosyntactic divergences (CLMD) that enables their extraction from syntactically parsed, content-word-aligned parallel corpora. Specifically, we categorize CLMD according to the edge labels along dependency paths linking aligned content-word pairs ( § 3.2); Figure 1 provides an illustration. We further perform an extensive corpus analysis, manually aligning content words across five language pairs—English–French (En–Fr), English–Russian (En–Ru), English–Chinese (En–Zh), English–Korean (En–Ko), and English–Japanese (En–Jp)—in a subset of the PUD corpus (Zeman et al., 2017) ( § 3.1), and examine the distribution of divergence types ( § 4). The resulting dataset can inform machine translation research, both by steering the construction of challenge sets that target constructions known to diverge cross-linguistically, and by directing source-side preprocessing strategies to improve MT output. The derived CLMD reveal not only macro-grammatical properties (e.g., whether a language is pro-drop), but also parameters tied to particular lexical classes (e.g., modal verbs) and probabilistic patterns (e.g., Japanese preferentially renders event sequences—expressed in English via coordinating conjunctions—using subordinate clauses). See § 5.

But she was too busy in her mind, carving out a career for him that would at least be possible, to ask what the ultimate something was which he had hinted at. There was no career for him in literature. Of that she was convinced. He had proved it to-day, with his amateurish and sophomoric productions. He could talk well, but he was incapable of expressing himself in a literary way. She compared Tennyson, and Browning, and her favorite prose masters with him, and to his hopeless discredit. Yet she did not tell him her whole mind. Her strange interest in him led her to temporize. His desire to write was, after all, a little weakness which he would grow out of in time. Then he would devote himself to the more serious affairs of life. And he would succeed, too. She knew that. He was so strong that he could not fail—if only he would drop writing.

She was too preoccupied with mapping out a feasible path for him to bother asking about that vague ambition he’d alluded to. Literature held no future for him; of that, she felt certain. His clumsy, adolescent efforts earlier that day had only confirmed it. Though he was a gifted talker, he lacked the ability to translate those thoughts into a literary form. When she measured him against the likes of Tennyson, Browning, or her most beloved prose stylists, he fell embarrassingly short. Still, she held back her full judgment. Her unusual fascination with him prompted her to stall for time. This urge to write was merely a trivial flaw, a phase he would eventually move past. Once he did, he could focus on the more substantial pursuits of the world. She was confident he would thrive then. His inner strength was so profound that failure seemed impossible—provided he abandoned his pen.

Wildeve had been brooding ever since they started on the mean estimation in which he was held by his wife’s friends; and it cut his heart severely. As the minutes passed he had gradually drifted into a revengeful intention without knowing the precise moment of forming it. This was to teach Mrs. Yeobright a lesson, as he considered it to be; in other words, to show her if he could that her niece’s husband was the proper guardian of her niece’s money.

Ever since their departure, Wildeve had been fixated on the low opinion his wife’s kin harbored toward him, a slight that galled him to his core. With each passing mile, he found himself sliding toward a retaliatory scheme, though he could not have said exactly when the impulse first took root. His aim was to provide Mrs. Yeobright with what he deemed a necessary lesson: to prove that, as her niece’s husband, he was the only rightful custodian of the woman’s fortune.

She then told him the circumstances. All that he had known of the affair before he returned from Paris was that there had existed an attachment between Thomasin and Wildeve, which his mother had at first discountenanced, but had since, owing to the arguments of Thomasin, looked upon in a little more favourable light. When she, therefore, proceeded to explain all he was greatly surprised and troubled.

She then related the circumstances to him. All he had known of the matter before his return from Paris was that there had once been an attachment between Thomasin and Wildeve, which his mother had at first disapproved of, but had afterwards, under Thomasin’s persuasions, come to regard with somewhat more favour. When she went on, therefore, to explain everything, he was much surprised and deeply troubled.

The third draught that I craved from the fount of knowledge was enlightenment concerning the character known as A Man About Town. He was more vague in my mind than a type should be. We must have a concrete idea of anything, even if it be an imaginary idea, before we can comprehend it. Now, I have a mental picture of John Doe that is as clear as a steel engraving. His eyes are weak blue; he wears a brown vest and a shiny black serge coat. He stands always in the sunshine chewing something; and he keeps half-shutting his pocket knife and opening it again with his thumb. And, if the Man Higher Up is ever found, take my assurance for it, he will be a large, pale man with blue wristlets showing under his cuffs, and he will be sitting to have his shoes polished within sound of a bowling alley, and there will be somewhere about him turquoises.

The third sip I desired from the wellspring of understanding was illumination regarding the figure called A Man About Town. He lingered in my thoughts more indistinctly than any archetype should. We must possess a tangible notion of a thing, even an invented one, before we can truly grasp it. For instance, I carry a mental image of John Doe as crisp as an etched copperplate. His gaze is a watery blue; he sports a tan waistcoat and a glossy black serge jacket. He is forever poised in sunlight, chewing on something, his pocket knife half-closed and flicked open again with his thumb. And should the Man Higher Up ever be discovered, you may rely on my word—he will be a tall, pallid gentleman with blue wristbands peeking beneath his sleeves, seated to have his boots buffed within earshot of a skittle alley, and somewhere near him there will be turquoises.

Thus has a week passed away, while I have listened to the strangest tale that ever imagination formed. My thoughts and every feeling of my soul have been drunk up by the interest for my guest which this tale and his own elevated and gentle manners have created. I wish to soothe him, yet can I counsel one so infinitely miserable, so destitute of every hope of consolation, to live? Oh, no! The only joy that he can now know will be when he composes his shattered spirit to peace and death. Yet he enjoys one comfort, the offspring of solitude and delirium; he believes that when in dreams he holds converse with his friends and derives from that communion consolation for his miseries or excitements to his vengeance, that they are not the creations of his fancy, but the beings themselves who visit him from the regions of a remote world. This faith gives a solemnity to his reveries that render them to me almost as imposing and interesting as truth.

A full week has slipped by as I have absorbed the most extraordinary story ever spun by mortal fancy. My mind and every pulse of my soul have been consumed by the fascination for my visitor which his narrative and his own lofty, tender bearing have awakened. I long to comfort him, yet dare I urge a being so fathomlessly wretched, so stripped of any hope of solace, to endure? Ah, no! The sole happiness left to him now will arrive when he lays his fractured spirit to rest in stillness and oblivion. Still, he possesses one strange consolation, born of solitude and fevered vision; he trusts that when in sleep he converses with his companions and draws from that fellowship either relief for his sorrows or fuel for his revenge, they are no phantoms of his own invention, but the very souls who visit him from the borders of some distant sphere. This conviction lends his trances a gravity that makes them, to my perception, nearly as majestic and compelling as reality.

By this time stupor had given place to anxiety, and anxiety began to make room for hunger and thirst. A form now appeared upon the rise on the other side of the swamp, half-hidden by the mist, and came towards Bathsheba. The woman—for it was a woman—approached with her face askance, as if looking earnestly on all sides of her. When she got a little further round to the left, and drew nearer, Bathsheba could see the newcomer’s profile against the sunny sky, and knew the wavy sweep from forehead to chin, with neither angle nor decisive line anywhere about it, to be the familiar contour of Liddy Smallbury.

By that point, the dazed stupor she’d been trapped in had given way to quiet anxiety, and that anxiety soon made room for sharp, insistent pangs of hunger and thirst. Just then, a figure appeared on the low rise on the far side of the swamp, half-shrouded in mist, and began moving toward Bathsheba. It was a woman, approaching with her head tilted askance, as if she was carefully scanning every part of the area around her. When she veered a little further left and drew closer still, Bathsheba could make out the newcomer’s profile outlined against the bright, sunlit sky. She knew that soft, flowing curve stretching from forehead to chin, with no sharp angles or defined harsh lines anywhere along it, instantly: it was the familiar contour of Liddy Smallbury.

We assemble a large number of test suites inspired by the methodology of experimental sentenceprocessing and psycholinguistic research. Each test suite contains a number of ITEMS (typically between 20 and 30), and each item appears in several CONDITIONS: across conditions, a given item will differ only according to a controlled manipulation designed to target a particular feature of grammatical knowledge. Each test suite contains at least one PREDICTION, which specifies inequalities between surprisal values at pairs of regions/conditions that should hold if a model has learned the appropriate syntactic generalization.

metadata.title: A Systematic Assessment of Syntactic Generalization in Neural Language Models

text: We construct a large set of test suites inspired by methods from experimental sentence processing and psycholinguistic research. Each test suite includes a number of items, typically between 20 and 30, and each item appears in multiple conditions: across conditions, a given item differs only in a controlled manipulation intended to target a specific aspect of grammatical knowledge. Each test suite contains at least one prediction, which states inequalities between surprisal values at pairs of regions or conditions that should hold if a model has learned the relevant syntactic generalization.

Mrs. Morse drove by Martin on the street one day, and smiled and nodded. He smiled back and lifted his hat. The episode did not affect him. A month before it might have disgusted him, or made him curious and set him to speculating about her state of consciousness at that moment. But now it was not provocative of a second thought. He forgot about it the next moment. He forgot about it as he would have forgotten the Central Bank Building or the City Hall after having walked past them. Yet his mind was preternaturally active. His thoughts went ever around and around in a circle. The centre of that circle was “work performed”; it ate at his brain like a deathless maggot. He awoke to it in the morning. It tormented his dreams at night. Every affair of life around him that penetrated through his senses immediately related itself to “work performed.” He drove along the path of relentless logic to the conclusion that he was nobody, nothing. Mart Eden, the hoodlum, and Mart Eden, the sailor, had been real, had been he; but Martin Eden! the famous writer, did not exist. Martin Eden, the famous writer, was a vapor that had arisen in the mob-mind and by the mob-mind had been thrust into the corporeal being of Mart Eden, the hoodlum and sailor. But it couldn’t fool him. He was not that sun-myth that the mob was worshipping and sacrificing dinners to. He knew better.

Mrs. Leland passed Julian one afternoon in the street, smiled, and dipped her head. He answered with a smile and a lift of his hat. It meant nothing to him. A month earlier it might have repelled him, or at least stirred his curiosity and sent him wondering what went on behind her eyes at that instant. Now it provoked no second reflection. The next moment it was gone from his mind. He forgot it as he might have forgotten the granite post office or the courthouse after walking by them. And yet his mind was in unnatural motion. His thoughts wheeled and circled without rest. At the center of that circle was “work accomplished”; it gnawed at his brain like an immortal worm. He woke to it in the morning. It haunted his sleep at night. Everything in life that came to him through the senses was instantly referred to “work accomplished.” He followed the pitiless chain of logic to its end and found himself reduced to no one, to nothing. Jules Eden, the rough, and Jules Eden, the seaman, had been real, had been himself; but Julian Eden, the celebrated author, did not exist. Julian Eden, the celebrated author, was a mist rising in the public mind and by that public mind imposed upon the flesh of Jules Eden, rough and sailor. But it could not deceive him. He was not that sun-born idol to whom the crowd prayed and for whom it sacrificed its dinners. He knew better.

In the room from which the conversation proceeded Bathsheba and her servant-companion, Liddy Smallbury, were to be discovered sitting upon the floor, and sorting a complication of papers, books, bottles, and rubbish spread out thereon—remnants from the household stores of the late occupier. Liddy, the maltster’s great-granddaughter, was about Bathsheba’s equal in age, and her face was a prominent advertisement of the light-hearted English country girl. The beauty her features might have lacked in form was amply made up for by perfection of hue, which at this winter-time was the softened ruddiness on a surface of high rotundity that we meet with in a Terburg or a Gerard Douw; and, like the presentations of those great colourists, it was a face which kept well back from the boundary between comeliness and the ideal. Though elastic in nature she was less daring than Bathsheba, and occasionally showed some earnestness, which consisted half of genuine feeling, and half of mannerliness superadded by way of duty.

Inside the room where the voices originated, Bathsheba and her companion-servant, Liddy Smallbury, could be found seated on the floor. They were sorting through a chaotic jumble of documents, books, vials, and discarded items—the leftover belongings of the former resident. Liddy, the great-granddaughter of the maltster, was nearly the same age as Bathsheba, her face a striking portrait of a spirited English country girl. Any lack of refinement in her features was easily overlooked because of her magnificent complexion; in the winter air, her full, round face held a gentle flush similar to those seen in the works of Terburg or Gerard Douw. Like the figures in those old Dutch paintings, her beauty was grounded and pleasant, stopping well short of the sublime. Though she was resilient, she wasn't as adventurous as Bathsheba, and she occasionally exhibited a seriousness that was equal parts genuine sentiment and a dutiful sense of decorum.

And not feel that same pain wake?

“We must try to track ’em, since we can’t hear ’em,” said Jan, fumbling in his pockets. He struck a light, and held the match to the ground. The rain had been heavier here, and all foot and horse tracks made previous to the storm had been abraded and blurred by the drops, and they were now so many little scoops of water, which reflected the flame of the match like eyes. One set of tracks was fresh and had no water in them; one pair of ruts was also empty, and not small canals, like the others. The footprints forming this recent impression were full of information as to pace; they were in equidistant pairs, three or four feet apart, the right and left foot of each pair being exactly opposite one another.

“We’ve got to follow their trail now that we can’t pick up any sound of them,” Jan said, fumbling through his pockets. He struck a match and held its wavering flame low to the earth. The rain had fallen harder in this spot, so every footprint and horse hoof print left before the storm had been eroded and blurred by the falling drops, reduced to tiny shallow pools of water that glinted back the match’s flame like watching eyes. Only one set of tracks was fresh, with no water pooled inside its indentations; a single pair of wagon ruts was also dry, rather than the narrow little water channels the older ruts had turned into. Those recent footprints held clear clues about the pace of the person who’d left them: they fell in evenly spaced pairs, three or four feet between each set, the right and left foot of each pair perfectly aligned side by side.

Then—oh, then—if you still stood on one foot, with your hot hand clutching the three moist dollars in your pocket, and hoarsely proclaimed your hideous and culpable poverty, nevermore would Mrs. Parker be cicerone of yours. She would honk loudly the word “Clara,” she would show you her back, and march downstairs. Then Clara, the coloured maid, would escort you up the carpeted ladder that served for the fourth flight, and show you the Skylight Room. It occupied 7×8 feet of floor space at the middle of the hall. On each side of it was a dark lumber closet or storeroom.

And then—if you still wavered there, your feverish hand clutching three soggy dollars as you rasped out the truth of your miserable poverty—Mrs. Parker would no longer be your guide. She would bray a loud call for “Clara,” present you with her back, and march down the stairs. Then Clara, the maid, would lead you up the steep, carpeted ladder of the fourth floor to reveal the Skylight Room. It took up a mere seven-by-eight feet in the middle of the landing, squeezed between two pitch-black storage closets.

After some mental debating of the point, he came to the conclusion that it would be as well to get all the preliminaries done with, and they could then arrange at their leisure whether he should give her his hand a week or two before Michaelmas Term, or in the little Christmas vacation between it and Hilary.

Mr. Stryver, having firmly decided on that noble conferring of good fortune upon the Doctor’s daughter, determined to let her know of her happiness before he departed from town for the Long Vacation. After some inward discussion of the matter, he concluded that it would be best to settle all the preliminaries at once, and that they could afterwards decide at their leisure whether he should offer her his hand a week or two before Michaelmas Term, or during the short Christmas vacation between that and Hilary.

As the circumstances of his marriage illustrate his character, I cannot refrain from relating them. One of his most intimate friends was a merchant who, from a flourishing state, fell, through numerous mischances, into poverty. This man, whose name was Beaufort, was of a proud and unbending disposition and could not bear to live in poverty and oblivion in the same country where he had formerly been distinguished for his rank and magnificence. Having paid his debts, therefore, in the most honourable manner, he retreated with his daughter to the town of Lucerne, where he lived unknown and in wretchedness. My father loved Beaufort with the truest friendship and was deeply grieved by his retreat in these unfortunate circumstances. He bitterly deplored the false pride which led his friend to a conduct so little worthy of the affection that united them. He lost no time in endeavouring to seek him out, with the hope of persuading him to begin the world again through his credit and assistance.

Since the particulars of his union lay bare the core of his nature, I cannot hold myself back from recounting them. One of his dearest confidants was a trader who, once at the height of commercial success, had been reduced to penury by a string of unforeseen calamities. This man, Theron by name, carried a stubborn, unyielding streak, and refused to scrape by in obscurity and want in the very nation where he had once been celebrated for his status and lavish generosity. After settling every last one of his debts with scrupulous integrity, he retreated with his only daughter to the city of Innsbruck, where he passed his days unrecognized and mired in hardship. My father cherished Theron with the most sincere of bonds, and was profoundly aggrieved that his friend would flee in the face of such misfortune. He railed against the misplaced pride that had driven Theron to act in a manner so unworthy of the deep affection that bound the two men together. He wasted no time in trying to track him down, hoping to convince him to rebuild his livelihood with the support of his connections and financial backing.

All our pilgrims looked on and commented--on the expert way in which the whip was handled. They were too much hardened by lifelong everyday familiarity with slavery to notice that there was anything else in the exhibition that invited comment. This was what slavery could do, in the way of ossifying what one may call the superior lobe of human feeling; for these pilgrims were kind-hearted people, and they would not have allowed that man to treat a horse like that.

Every pilgrim among us watched and remarked on the deftness with which the whip was wielded. Their sensibilities had been so calloused by a lifetime of daily, unthinking exposure to slavery that nothing else in the scene seemed to them worthy of note. Such was slavery’s power to petrify what might be called the higher chambers of human sympathy; for these were, at heart, kindly souls, and they would never have stood by while a man treated a horse in such a fashion.

For this reason, it has become common to modify the language model's output probability distribution to increase the chance of sampling tokens with high likelihood according to the language model. Top-k random sampling, where low-likelihood words are restricted from being generated, is one such method. A language model that is only permitted to produce high-likelihood words is less likely to make a poor choice and create the type of mistakes that are easy for humans to detect. Since humans are not proficient at identifying when a model subtly favors some utterances more often than a human author would, they don't notice the over-representation of high-likelihood words in the generated text. In contrast, automatic systems excel at identifying statistical anomalies and struggle to build deeper semantic understanding. Top-k in particular creates text that is easy for machines to detect but very hard for humans. Thus, we observe the general trend: as the number of unlikely words available to be chosen is increased, humans get better at detecting fakes while automatic systems get worse.

metadata.title: Automatic Detection of Generated Text is Easiest when Humans are Fooled

text: For this reason, it has become common to modify the language model’s output probability distribution so as to increase the likelihood of sampling tokens with high model probability. Top-k random sampling, which restricts the generation of low-probability words, is one such method. A language model that is allowed to produce only high-probability words is less likely to make a poor selection and thereby produce the kinds of errors that humans can easily detect. Because humans are not adept at recognizing when a model subtly favors certain utterances more often than a human author would, they do not notice the overrepresentation of high-probability words in generated text. By contrast, automatic systems are highly effective at detecting statistical anomalies but struggle to develop deeper semantic understanding. Top-k, in particular, produces text that is easy for machines to detect but very difficult for humans. Thus, we observe the general trend that, as the number of unlikely words available for selection increases, humans become better at detecting fakes while automatic systems become worse.

Eustacia watched his shadowy form till it had disappeared. She placed her hand to her forehead and breathed heavily; and then her rich, romantic lips parted under that homely impulse—a yawn. She was immediately angry at having betrayed even to herself the possible evanescence of her passion for him. She could not admit at once that she might have overestimated Wildeve, for to perceive his mediocrity now was to admit her own great folly heretofore. And the discovery that she was the owner of a disposition so purely that of the dog in the manger had something in it which at first made her ashamed.

Genevieve followed his retreating shadow until it vanished into the night. She rested a hand against her brow and exhaled a long, weighted breath; then, those plush, poetic lips gave way to a vulgar necessity—a yawn. She felt an immediate sting of anger at having signaled, if only to her own soul, the waning of her fire. To confess to Julian’s mediocrity at this late hour was to acknowledge the depth of her own previous delusions. Moreover, the dawning sense that her heart was ruled by the spiteful spirit of the dog in the manger left her, for a moment, profoundly ashamed.

She had written ignoring his silence and absence, or rather treating it as if it were an incident of no particular importance. Apparently she had not called upon the patient and devoted Oliver as she had threatened; at any rate, there were no signs of Oliver in her communication. But she reproached Mr. Britling for deserting her, and she clamoured for his presence and for kind and strengthening words. She was, she said, scared by this war. She was only a little thing, and it was all too dreadful, and there was not a soul in the world to hold her hand, at least no one who understood in the slightest degree how she felt. (But why was not Oliver holding her hand?) She was like a child left alone in the dark. It was perfectly horrible the way that people were being kept in the dark. The stories one heard, "_often from quite trustworthy sources_," were enough to depress and terrify any one. Battleship after battleship had been sunk by German torpedoes, a thing kept secret from us for no earthly reason, and Prince Louis of Battenberg had been discovered to be a spy and had been sent to the Tower. Haldane too was a spy. Our army in France had been "practically _sold_" by the French. Almost all the French generals were in German pay. The censorship and the press were keeping all this back, but what good was it to keep it back? It was folly not to trust people! But it was all too dreadful for a poor little soul whose only desire was to live happily. Why didn't he come along to her and make her feel she had protecting arms round her? She couldn't think in the daytime: she couldn't sleep at night....

She had written as though his quiet withdrawal were a trifle, a mere oversight unworthy of remark. Evidently she had not summoned the steadfast and besotted Oliver as she had vowed; at least, his name did not stain the page. Instead, she chided Mr. Britling for abandoning her, crying out for his nearness, his tender and fortifying phrases. The war, she confessed, had frightened her. She was a frail creature, and the whole affair was too monstrous, and there was not a single being left to clasp her hand—no one who grasped in the faintest measure what she endured. (But why had Oliver’s hand not found hers?) She was a child forsaken in the blackness. It was utterly wicked how truths were hidden. The rumours one caught, “_often from lips beyond reproach,_” were enough to crush and horrify anyone. Vessel after vessel had been swallowed by German torpedoes, a fact veiled from us for no sane cause, and Prince Louis of Battenberg had been unmasked as a traitor and cast into the Tower. Haldane, too, was a traitor. Our forces in France had been “all but _bartered away_” by the French. Nearly every French commander took German coin. The censors and the journals smothered it all, yet what purpose did smothering serve? It was madness not to confide in the people! Still, it was all too hideous for a pitiful little spirit whose sole wish was to dwell in peace. Why would he not hasten to her and wrap her in the shelter of his arms? By day her thoughts would not settle; by night sleep fled.
